Discuss why a near-constant plateau force is desirable in occupant protection. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution to A(a):** Given: $m = 1500\,\text{kg}$, $v_i = 50\,\text{km/h} = 13.89\,\text{m/s}$, $d = 0.6\,\text{m}$. **(i) Initial kinetic energy:** $$E_k \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, m\, v_i^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (13.89)^{2}$$ $$E_k \;=\; 750 \times 192.93 \;\approx\; 144{,}700\,\text{J} \;\approx\; 144.7\,\text{kJ}$$ **(ii) Average crushing force:** $$F_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{m\, v_i^{2}}{2\, d} \;=\; \dfrac{1500 \times 192.93}{2 \times 0.6}$$ $$F_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{289{,}395}{1.2} \;\approx\; 241{,}163\,\text{N} \;\approx\; 241\,\text{kN}$$ **(iii) Average deceleration:** $$a_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{F_{avg}}{m} \;=\; \dfrac{241{,}163}{1500} \;\approx\; 160.8\,\text{m/s}^{2}$$ In units of g: $$a_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{160.8}{9.81} \;\approx\; 16.4\,g$$ This is **below the 20 g limit**, so the design is acceptable. **(iv) Crash pulse duration (triangular pulse):** For a triangular pulse, impulse = area = $\tfrac{1}{2}\, F_{peak}\, \Delta t = m\, v_i$. Using $F_{peak} \approx F_{avg}$: $$\Delta t \;=\; \dfrac{2\, m\, v_i}{F_{avg}} \;=\; \dfrac{2 \times 1500 \times 13.89}{241{,}163}$$ $$\Delta t \;\approx\; 0.173\,\text{s} \;\approx\; 173\,\text{ms}$$ *(Marks: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 = 7)* --- **Model Solution to A(b):** The F–δ curve of a tubular crumple element (e.g., a square steel crush rail) consists of: **Region A — Initial Elastic Region:** - The structure deflects elastically under small load. - The slope is steep and linear (governed by $k = 3EI/L^{3}$ for a beam). - If unloaded here, the structure returns to its original shape — no energy is dissipated permanently. **Region B — Plastic Collapse Plateau:** - Once the local bending moment reaches $M_p = \sigma_y Z_p$, plastic hinges form. - The structure folds progressively at near-constant force $F_m = 4 M_p / L_b$. - This is the **primary energy-absorbing region**, contributing ~70–80 % of total energy absorbed. - The plateau length equals the available crush stroke $d$. **Region C — Densification Region:** - After the structure has fully compacted, additional stroke causes material to be compressed against itself. - The force rises steeply, offering no useful energy absorption and risking occupant-compartment intrusion. - Design must ensure $d < d_{densification}$ to avoid this regime. **Why constant plateau force is desirable:** A constant average force gives a **constant deceleration** $a = F_{avg}/m$. Constant decel means a predictable crash pulse that the restraint system (belt pretensioner, airbag, load limiter) is calibrated to manage. If the force were impulsive (spike) the airbag would deploy too late; if the force were rising throughout the stroke, the occupant's chest would be loaded too harshly at the end of the event. The plateau decouples the occupant ride-down from the violent initial contact. *(Marks: Region descriptions – 3, plateau mechanics – 2, occupant linkage – 2 = 7)* --- **Question B (14 Marks, Alternative) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** **(a)** With the help of a free-body diagram and the work–energy theorem, derive the relationship between average crushing force, vehicle mass, initial velocity, and crush distance in a frontal impact. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A certain SUV has a front crumple zone length of **500 mm**. It impacts a rigid wall at **64 km/h**. Calculate: (i) the average deceleration, (ii) whether this exceeds the **20 g** survivable limit, and (iii) the minimum additional crush distance required to keep average deceleration under 20 g. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution to B(a):** *(See Section 3.1 derivation above — full derivation with FBD to be reproduced verbatim by the student.)* **Key steps to present:** 1. Draw an FBD showing $F_{avg}$ acting opposite to motion over distance $d$. 2. State work–energy theorem: $W = \Delta KE$. 3. Write $W = -F_{avg}\, d$ (force opposes motion). 4. Write $\Delta KE = 0 - \tfrac{1}{2} m v_i^{2} = -\tfrac{1}{2} m v_i^{2}$. 5. Equate: $F_{avg}\, d = \tfrac{1}{2} m v_i^{2}$. 6. Solve: $F_{avg} = m v_i^{2} / (2 d)$ and $a_{avg} = v_i^{2}/(2d)$. 7. State physical interpretation. *(Marks: FBD – 1, theorem statement – 1, algebraic steps – 4, final formula + interpretation – 1 = 7)* --- **Model Solution to B(b):** Given: $d = 0.5\,\text{m}$, $v_i = 64\,\text{km/h} = 17.78\,\text{m/s}$, limit $a_{allow} = 20\,g = 196.2\,\text{m/s}^{2}$. **(i) Average deceleration with current zone:** $$a_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{v_i^{2}}{2\, d} \;=\; \dfrac{(17.78)^{2}}{2 \times 0.5} \;=\; \dfrac{316.1}{1.0} \;\approx\; 316.1\,\text{m/s}^{2}$$ **(ii) Comparison with 20 g limit:** $$\dfrac{a_{avg}}{g} \;=\; \dfrac{316.1}{9.81} \;\approx\; 32.2\,g$$ Since $32.2\,g > 20\,g$, the current 500 mm zone **does NOT meet** the survivable limit. The design is unsafe — occupants would experience severe injury risk. **(iii) Minimum additional crush distance required:** We require $a_{avg} \le 196.2\,\text{m/s}^{2}$, so: $$d_{min} \;\ge\; \dfrac{v_i^{2}}{2\, a_{allow}} \;=\; \dfrac{316.1}{2 \times 196.2} \;\approx\; 0.806\,\text{m}$$ Current zone is 0.5 m, so additional length required: $$\Delta d \;=\; 0.806 - 0.5 \;=\; 0.306\,\text{m} \;\approx\; 306\,\text{mm}$$ **Recommendation:** Extend the crumple zone by at least **306 mm** (i.e., total ≥ **806 mm**) to limit average occupant deceleration to 20 g at 64 km/h. *(Marks: 2 + 2 + 3 = 7)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in Module 1 Crumple-Zone questions:** > > 1. **Unit error on speed:** KTU examiners specifically penalize students who use km/h directly in $v^{2}$. *Always convert to m/s first.* > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** Students often write $a = v^{2}/d$ instead of $v^{2}/(2d)$. Show the derivation explicitly. > 3. **Conflating $a_{avg}$ with $a_{peak}$:** For a triangular crash pulse, $a_{peak} = 2 a_{avg}$. For a rectangular pulse, $a_{peak} = a_{avg}$. Always state your assumption. > 4. **No free-body diagram in derivation questions:** KTU board examiners award **at least 1 mark** specifically for a labelled FBD in derivations. > 5. **Ignoring the 20 g survivable limit:** Any computed average deceleration must be checked against this threshold and a clear "safe / unsafe" verdict given. > 6. **Wrong sign on work done:** The crushing force *opposes* motion; this must be explicit in your work–energy equation. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Master formula:** $F_{avg} = m v_i^{2}/(2d)$ and $a_{avg} = v_i^{2}/(2d)$. - **Crush-length requirement:** $L_{cz} = n_s v_i^{2}/(2 a_{allow})$. - **Crash pulse duration:** $\Delta t = 2 m v_i / F_{peak}$ (triangular pulse). - **Energy sinks in a crash:** Plastic strain (target 60–70 %), occupant kinetic energy (<5 %), elastic rebound (<10 %), friction/heat (~15–25 %). - **Acceptable occupant peak deceleration:** **20 g** (≈ 196 m/s²) for survival; **15 g** preferred for no injury. - **Materials:** TRIP, Dual-Phase, HSLA steels; aluminium 6061-T6 for EV platforms. - **Design rule of thumb:** Doubling the crush distance halves the average deceleration. - **Survival cell intrusion limit:** ≤ **75 mm** in FMVSS 208 / ECE R-94. - **Tube collapse load:** $F_m = 4 M_p / L_b$ with $M_p = \sigma_y Z_p$. - **Always:** Convert km/h → m/s, draw an FBD, state the survivable limit, and conclude with a "design acceptable / not acceptable" verdict. - **KTU buzzwords to use:** *progressive plastic deformation, controlled collapse, energy management, survival cell integrity, compatibility, crash pulse, intrusion tolerance.*

topics.title: Modelling and simulation techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT743 - VEHICLE SAFETY AND SECURITY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Modelling and simulation techniques # 📘 MODULE 1 — MODELLING AND SIMULATION TECHNIQUES ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** > **Modelling** is the process of creating a mathematical, physical, or computational representation of a real-world vehicle system (or sub-system) by translating its physical behaviour into equations, geometric entities, or logical relationships. **Simulation** is the subsequent execution of this model over time using numerical solvers to study the system's response under conditions that are either too expensive, dangerous, or impossible to reproduce in real life (e.g., full-scale vehicle crash testing). ### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **flight simulator** used to train pilots. The cockpit mock-up looks real, but inside it is a computer solving Newton's laws of motion hundreds of times per second. A student pilot "crashes" the virtual aircraft and learns — without dying or burning fuel. Vehicle safety simulation works in exactly the same way: - The **car** is the cockpit mock-up. - The **finite-element model** of the chassis is the pilot's visual scene. - The **solver (LS-DYNA, ANSYS, MATLAB/Simulink)** is the "invisible teacher" running the math. - The **engineer** is the trainee, learning how crumple zones deform before building a real prototype. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** > - **Model** = Representation (the blueprint of reality). > - **Simulation** = Behaviour (what happens when we run the model forward in time). > > Examiners frequently award full marks only when students clearly separate the *static description* (model) from the *dynamic execution* (simulation). ### 1.2 Why Modelling & Simulation in Vehicle Safety? | Real-World Limitation | How Simulation Solves It | |---|---| | A real crash destroys the prototype — cost is **$250,000+** per full crash test | Virtual crash costs only compute-time (≈ a few dollars in electricity) | | Human occupants cannot ethically be crash-tested | Bio-mechanical dummies (THOR, Hybrid III) are mathematically modelled | | Sensor data (accelerometers at 20 kHz) is hard to interpret raw | Simulation post-processes channels into injury metrics like HIC, NIC | | Regulatory homologation requires 50+ tests (Euro NCAP, IIHS, AIS) | Pre-screening via simulation eliminates weak designs early | > [!TIP] > **Standard Metrics You Must Memorise (bold indicates high-weightage):** > - **HIC (Head Injury Criterion)** — dimensionless, threshold = **1000** (AIS ≥ 4 injury risk). > - **Chest deflection** — limit = **63 mm** for thoracic injury. > - **Femur load** — limit = **10 kN** for lower limb fracture. > - **Neck injury criterion (NIC)** — limit = **15 m²/s⁴**. > - **Acceleration pulse duration** — typical crash pulse ≈ **100–150 ms**. ### 1.3 Classification of Models (KTU Module 1 Syllabus Map) 1. **Mathematical Models** — Equations governing the system (Newton, Lagrangian). 2. **Physical Models** — Scaled replicas, crash sleds, dummy rigs. 3. **Computer (Numerical) Models** — Finite Element (FE), Multi-Body System (MBS), CFD. 4. **Conceptual / Block Models** — Signal-flow diagrams (e.g., Simulink blocks). By level of abstraction: - **Lumped-parameter (1-DOF, 2-DOF)** — ideal for KTU derivations. - **Distributed-parameter (FE/MBS)** — used in industry-grade software. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five-Step Modelling & Simulation Pipeline The KTU Module-1 syllabus insists students know the *workflow*, not just definitions. Every professional CAE engineer follows these five steps: 1. **Problem Definition** — Identify the boundary (full vehicle vs. subsystem), the loading (frontal impact, side pole, rear), and the outputs (injury criteria, intrusion). 2. **Model Formulation** — Convert physical laws into equations: - Mass, stiffness, damping for the structure. - Mass, inertia, joint stiffness for the occupant. 3. **Discretisation** — Break the geometry into small elements (FE mesh) or lumped masses (MBS bodies). 4. **Solution** — Apply numerical integration: - *Explicit* (central difference, used for crashes — stable for short time-steps). - *Implicit* (Newmark-β, used for ride/comfort — long time-steps). 5. **Validation & Verification** — Compare with physical tests (correlation with sled data). > [!NOTE] > **V&V — the most-missed KTU exam question:** > - **Verification** = Are we solving the equations *right*? (math check, mesh convergence) > - **Validation** = Are we solving the *right* equations? (comparison with experimental data) ## 2.2 Types of Simulation Techniques | Technique | Best Used For | Strength | Weakness | |---|---|---|---| | **Finite Element (FE)** | Crash, NVH, structural deformation | High geometric fidelity | Computationally expensive | | **Multi-Body System (MBS)** | Vehicle dynamics, ride, handling | Fast, large time-steps | No local plasticity | | **CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics)** | Pedestrian wind, airbag inflation, ventilation | Detailed fluid fields | High CPU cost, turbulence modelling | | **Discrete Element Method (DEM)** | Granular material (sand, soil) for underbody | Unique to granular systems | Niche application | | **Co-simulation** | Coupled MBS+FE, e.g., crash + occupant | Captures multi-physics | Coupling stability issues | ## 2.3 Numerical Integration Schemes (Core for KTU) For an equation of motion $\ddot{x}(t) = a(x, \dot{x}, t)$: ### (a) Explicit Euler (First-Order) $$ \dot{x}_{n+1} = \dot{x}_{n} + \ddot{x}_{n}\, \Delta t $$ $$ x_{n+1} = x_{n} + \dot{x}_{n}\, \Delta t $$ **Used in:** LS-DYNA crash codes (with central-difference form for stability). ### (b) Newmark-β (Implicit, for Ride & Comfort) $$ x_{n+1} = x_{n} + \Delta t \, \dot{x}_{n} + \Delta t^{2}\left[\left(\tfrac{1}{2}-\beta\right)\ddot{x}_{n} + \beta\, \ddot{x}_{n+1}\right] $$ Stability requires $\beta \ge 0.25$ (average acceleration method). ### (c) Central Difference (Explicit, Most Common in Crash) $$ x_{n+1} = 2x_{n} - x_{n-1} + \Delta t^{2}\,\ddot{x}_{n} $$ **Conditional stability:** $\Delta t \le \Delta t_{crit} = \dfrac{2}{\omega_{max}}$ — the smallest element determines the time step. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorise this table verbatim — at least 30 % of Part-B marks are derived from these equations. | # | Concept | Equation | Variable Definitions | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Newton's 2nd law | $\sum F = m\ddot{x}$ | $F$ force, $m$ mass, $\ddot{x}$ acceleration | N, kg, m/s² | | 2 | Spring-mass-damper (1-DOF) | $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$ | $c$ damping, $k$ stiffness | Ns/m, N/m | | 3 | Natural frequency | $\omega_{n} = \sqrt{k/m}$ | angular natural freq | rad/s | | 4 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | dimensionless ratio | — | | 5 | Critical time step (FE) | $\Delta t_{crit} = \dfrac{L_{min}}{c_{0}}$ | $L_{min}$ smallest element, $c_0$ wave speed | s | | 6 | Energy absorbed (crimp) | $E = \int_{0}^{\delta} F(\delta)\,d\delta$ | crush force-deflection | J | | 7 | Average crush force | $\bar{F} = \dfrac{E}{\delta_{max}}$ | mean dynamic force | N | | 8 | Head Injury Criterion (HIC) | $\text{HIC} = \left[\dfrac{1}{t_{2}-t_{1}}\int_{t_{1}}^{t_{2}} a(t)\,dt\right]^{2.5}\,(t_{2}-t_{1})$ | $a$ head acceleration in $g$ | dimensionless | | 9 | 3-ms peak acceleration | $a_{3ms} = \max\left[\dfrac{1}{t_{2}-t_{1}}\int_{t_{1}}^{t_{2}} a\,dt\right]$ | sliding window of 3 ms | $g$ | | 10 | Neck injury (NIJ) | $\text{NIJ} = \dfrac{F_{z}}{F_{crit}} + \dfrac{M_{y}}{M_{crit}}$ | axial force, bending moment | — | | 11 | Acceleration pulse (sinusoidal) | $a(t) = A\sin(\pi t/T)$ | peak $A$, duration $T$ | m/s² | | 12 | Velocity change (crash) | $\Delta v = \int a\,dt$ | impulse / mass | m/s | | 13 | Energy = kinetic loss | $\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2}m(v_{1}^{2}-v_{2}^{2})$ | pre- and post-impact speed | J | | 14 | Multibody (n-DOF) | $M\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + Kq = Q$ | $M,C,K$ matrices, $q$ DOFs | SI | | 15 | Lagrangian (occupant) | $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) - \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial q} + \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial q} = 0$ | $T$ KE, $V$ PE | J, N | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Exam Pitfall:** > In HIC equation, the acceleration must be expressed in **multiples of g** (i.e., in *g-units*), not in m/s². Forgetting this scaling yields a wrong HIC by a factor of $9.81^{2.5} \approx 285$ and zero marks. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Crumple zone design:** Energy equation (Row 6) gives engineers the target *mean crush force* to size rail thickness. - **Airbag deployment timing:** Acceleration pulse (Row 11) is integrated to compute $\Delta v$; deployment is triggered when $\Delta v > 15$ km/h within 30 ms. - **Restraint tuning:** Spring-mass-damper model (Row 2) of belt + retractor + spool sets the occupant ride-down. - **Crash pulse validation:** $\Delta t_{crit}$ (Row 5) tells simulation engineers the maximum usable time step — too large and the solution *explodes*. - **Occupant kinematics:** Lagrangian (Row 15) gives a closed-form solution for sled tests. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — 1-DOF Spring-Mass-Damper Crash Model This is the *fundamental* occupant/vehicle model for Module 1. It will appear in **every KTU Part-B**. **Starting statement:** When a vehicle impacts a rigid wall, the occupant + seatbelt behaves as a single mass ($m$) suspended by a belt of stiffness ($k$) and damping ($c$). The base acceleration is the crash pulse $a_{p}(t)$. **Free-body diagram reasoning:** - Inertial reaction force on the mass: $-m\ddot{x}(t)$. - Damping force: $-c\dot{x}(t)$. - Spring (belt) force: $-k x(t)$. - External base excitation through the belt transmits $F(t) = c\dot{x}_{p}(t) + k x_{p}(t)$. Summing forces in the inertial frame and applying Newton's second law yields: $$ m\ddot{x}(t) + c\left[\dot{x}(t) - \dot{x}_{p}(t)\right] + k\left[x(t) - x_{p}(t)\right] = 0 $$ $$ \therefore m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = c\dot{x}_{p} + kx_{p} $$ If we let the relative displacement $z(t) = x(t) - x_{p}(t)$ (the amount the occupant moves relative to the seat), the equation simplifies to: $$ m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -m\ddot{x}_{p} $$ This is the **standard KTU form** that examiners expect. ### Solving the Homogeneous (Free-Vibration) Case Set the right-hand side to zero: $$ m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = 0 $$ Assume $z(t) = Z e^{\lambda t}$: $$ m\lambda^{2} + c\lambda + k = 0 $$ $$ \lambda_{1,2} = \frac{-c \pm \sqrt{c^{2}-4mk}}{2m} $$ Define the **damping ratio** $\zeta$ and **natural frequency** $\omega_{n}$: $$ \zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}, \qquad \omega_{n} = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} $$ Three regimes: $$ \lambda_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_{n} \pm \omega_{n}\sqrt{\zeta^{2}-1} $$ | Regime | Condition | Physical Behaviour | |---|---|---| | **Under-damped** | $\zeta < 1$ | Oscillatory decay — *most occupant cases* | | **Critically damped** | $\zeta = 1$ | Fastest non-oscillatory return | | **Over-damped** | $\zeta > 1$ | Slow non-oscillatory return | The under-damped solution (most relevant): $$ z(t) = Z\,e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}t}\cos\!\left(\omega_{d}t - \phi\right) $$ where the **damped natural frequency** is: $$ \omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Head Injury Criterion (HIC) from First Principles The HIC is the single most-tested injury metric in KTU exams. We derive it from the Gadd Severity Index (GSI) which originally proposed an injury power-law: $$ \text{GSI} = \int_{0}^{T} \left[a(t)\right]^{n}\,dt, \quad n = 2.5 $$ where $a(t)$ is in units of $g$. To make it independent of the time-window chosen, Versace reformulated the integral over the worst possible interval $[t_{1}, t_{2}]$: $$ I(t_{1}, t_{2}) = \frac{1}{t_{2}-t_{1}}\int_{t_{1}}^{t_{2}} a(t)\,dt $$ This is the **average resultant acceleration** over the window. Squaring and raising to the power $1.25$ (so that the exponent becomes $2.5$ overall): $$ \text{HIC} = \max_{t_{1},t_{2}}\left\{\left[I(t_{1},t_{2})\right]^{2.5}\,(t_{2}-t_{1})\right\} $$ Expanding: $$ \therefore\; \text{HIC} = \left[\frac{1}{t_{2}-t_{1}}\int_{t_{1}}^{t_{2}} a(t)\,dt\right]^{2.5}\,(t_{2}-t_{1}) $$ **Numeric example KTU-style:** Let $a(t)=40\sin(\pi t/0.1)$ g for $0\le t\le 0.1$ s, with optimal window $[0,0.1]$. Then $I = \frac{1}{0.1}\int_0^{0.1} 40\sin(10\pi t)\,dt = \frac{40}{0.1}\cdot\frac{1-\cos(\pi)}{10\pi} = \frac{400}{\pi} \approx 127.3$ g. $$ \text{HIC} = (127.3)^{2.5}\times 0.1 \approx 1.81\times 10^{5}\times 0.1 = 18{,}100 $$ This far exceeds the AIS-4 threshold of **1000** — such an acceleration pulse would be fatal. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Time Step in Explicit FE Simulation A bar element of length $L$ has the highest natural frequency: $$ \omega_{max} = \frac{c_{0}}{L}, \quad \text{where } c_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} $$ The central-difference scheme is stable only when: $$ \Delta t \le \frac{2}{\omega_{max}} = \frac{2L}{c_{0}} $$ For a typical steel automobile: $E = 210$ GPa, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³: $$ c_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{210\times 10^{9}}{7850}} \approx 5171\ \text{m/s} $$ For a 5 mm shell element: $$ \Delta t_{crit} = \frac{2 \times 0.005}{5171} \approx 1.93\ \mu s $$ Hence a 100 ms crash simulation requires $\approx 50{,}000$ time steps — illustrating why **mass scaling** is used in industry to push $\Delta t$ to ≈ 1 µs while remaining stable. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy Equation for Crumple-Zone Sizing A vehicle of mass $m$ travelling at $v_1$ collides with a rigid wall and rebounds at $v_2 < v_1$ (often zero). The kinetic energy lost must be absorbed by the crumple zone: $$ E_{absorbed} = \tfrac{1}{2}m v_{1}^{2} - \tfrac{1}{2}m v_{2}^{2} $$ If the crumple stroke is $\delta$ and the crush force is approximately constant $\bar{F}$: $$ E_{absorbed} = \bar{F}\cdot\delta $$ Equating: $$ \bar{F} = \frac{m(v_{1}^{2}-v_{2}^{2})}{2\delta} $$ For $m = 1500$ kg, $v_1 = 56$ km/h = 15.56 m/s, $v_2 = 0$, $\delta = 0.5$ m: $$ \bar{F} = \frac{1500\times 15.56^{2}}{2\times 0.5} = \frac{363{,}200}{1.0} = 363{,}200\ \text{N} $$ This is the *target mean crash force* the rail must sustain, and is the starting point for selecting sheet-metal thickness and grade. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Equation | Problem Statement | First Equation to Write | Solution Strategy | |---|---|---| | Crash-pulse + occupant | $m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -m\ddot{x}_{p}$ | Compute $\omega_d$, then Duhamel integral | | Head injury limit | $\text{HIC} < 1000$ | Integrate $a(t)$, raise to 2.5 | | Crumple sizing | $\bar{F} = m v^{2}/2\delta$ | Solve for $\delta$ or $\bar{F}$ | | FE step size | $\Delta t \le 2L/c_{0}$ | Find smallest element | | Ride comfort | $\ddot{q} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}\dot{q} + \omega_{n}^{2}q = F/m$ | Frequency-response plot | | Restraint force | $F = ma - m\ddot{x}_{p}$ | Subtract pulse acceleration | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Modelling & Simulation Workflow (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["PHYSICAL REALITY
Vehicle Crash Event"] --> B["STEP 1
PROBLEM DEFINITION
Boundary Conditions Loading"] B --> C["STEP 2
MODEL FORMULATION
Newton Lagrangian Equations"] C --> D["STEP 3
DISCRETISATION
FE Mesh or MBS Bodies"] D --> E["STEP 4
NUMERICAL SOLUTION
Explicit or Implicit Solver"] E --> F["STEP 5
POST PROCESSING
HIC Chest Deflection Femur"] F --> G{"VERIFICATION
Mesh Convergence"} G --> H{"VALIDATION
Compare with Test Data"} H -->|Pass| I["DESIGN DECISION
Accept or Iterate"] H -->|Fail| J["MODEL CALIBRATION
Update Material Joint"] J --> C I --> K["PHYSICAL PROTOTYPE
Manufacture and Crash Test"] K --> L["HOMOLOGATION
AIS Euro NCAP IIHS"] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Vehicle Crash Simulation Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["CRASH PULSE INPUT
Bumper Contact Force Time History"] --> A2["VEHICLE STRUCTURE MODEL
Multi Body System MBD"] A2 --> A3["SOLVER KERNEL
Central Difference Explicit"] A3 --> A4["OCCUPANT MODEL
Hybrid III THOR Dummy"] A3 --> A5["RESTRAINT MODEL
Seatbelt Airbag Pretensioner"] A4 --> A6["INJURY METRICS
HIC NIC Chest g"] A5 --> A6 A6 --> A7{"CRITERIA MET
HIC 1000 NIC 15"} A7 -->|No| A8["DESIGN ITERATION
Stiffer Belt Earlier Airbag"] A8 --> A2 A7 -->|Yes| A9["FINAL DESIGN
Released for Production"] ``` ## 4.3 Decomposition Hierarchy of a Full Vehicle FE Model ```mermaid flowchart TD root["FULL VEHICLE ASSEMBLY
Mass 1500 kg"] --> L1["BODY IN WHITE BIW"] root --> L2["CHASSIS AND POWERTRAIN"] root --> L3["CLOSURES"] root --> L4["INTERIOR AND RESTRAINTS"] root --> L5["OCCUPANT DUMMY"] L1 --> L1a["FRONT CRUMPLE ZONE
Elements 1 mm"] L1 --> L1b["PASSENGER CELL
Elements 5 mm"] L1 --> L1c["REAR CRUMPLE ZONE
Elements 2 mm"] L4 --> L4a["SEATBELT 1D CABLE"] L4 --> L4b["AIRBAG FOLDED MESH"] L4 --> L4c["STEERING COLUMN FOLD"] L5 --> L5a["HEAD NECK"] L5 --> L5b["THORAX RIBS"] L5 --> L5c["FEMUR LEG"] L5 --> L5d["PELVIS"] ``` ## 4.4 Co-Simulation Coupling (MBS + FE) Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR M1["MBS DOMAIN
Suspension Tyre Sprung Mass"] --> C1["COUPLING INTERFACE
Bushings Mount Points"] C1 --> F1["FE DOMAIN
Subframe Cradle Detail"] F1 --> C2["FORCE DISPLACEMENT FEEDBACK"] C2 --> M1 M1 --> O1["OUTPUTS
Handling Stability Ride Comfort"] F1 --> O2["OUTPUTS
Local Stress Fatigue"] ``` ## 4.5 Injury Metric Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["HEAD ACCELEROMETER
Tri Axial 20 kHz"] --> S2["HIC CALCULATOR
Sliding Window 36 ms"] S3["CHEST POTENTIOMETER
Deflection mm"] --> S4["CHEST DEFLECTION
Limit 63 mm"] S5["FEMUR LOAD CELL
kN"] --> S6["FEMUR COMPRESSION
Limit 10 kN"] S7["NECK LOAD CELL
Fz and My"] --> S8["NIC NIJ
Limit 15 and 1"] S2 --> F["INJURY RISK INDEX"] S4 --> F S6 --> F S8 --> F F --> R{"AIS RISK
Greater Than 4"} R -->|Yes| X["REDESIGN RESTRAINT"] R -->|No| Y["PASS HOMOLOGATION"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **Q: Define the term "model" in the context of vehicle safety simulation. Distinguish between a lumped-parameter and a distributed-parameter model with one example each.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** A **model** is a mathematical or numerical representation of a real vehicle system that captures its essential physical behaviour using equations of motion, geometry, and material laws. | Feature | Lumped-Parameter Model | Distributed-Parameter Model | |---|---|---| | Spatial resolution | Mass concentrated at discrete nodes | Continuous field described over geometry | | Math form | ODE in time, e.g., $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$ | PDE in space-time, e.g., $\rho \ddot{u} = E\, u'' + \rho b$ | | Typical use | Early concept evaluation of restraint tuning | Crashworthiness of full body-in-white | | Example | 1-DOF spring-mass-damper occupant model | Full FE mesh of front rail under impact | | Compute time | Seconds on a laptop | Hours to days on HPC cluster | ### Question 2 **Q: What is the Head Injury Criterion (HIC)? State its mathematical expression and the AIS-4 threshold value.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The HIC is a dimensionless injury metric that quantifies the risk of severe or fatal head trauma from a measured head acceleration history. It is the maximum, over all windows $[t_{1}, t_{2}]$ with $t_{2} - t_{1} \le 36$ ms, of the average head acceleration raised to the 2.5 power times the window duration. $$ \text{HIC} = \max\left\{\left[\frac{1}{t_{2}-t_{1}}\int_{t_{1}}^{t_{2}} a(t)\,dt\right]^{2.5}\,(t_{2}-t_{1})\right\} $$ The **AIS ≥ 4 (severe/critical) threshold is HIC = 1000**. HIC ≤ 240 corresponds to negligible injury risk, 240–1000 moderate, > 1000 severe, > 2000 essentially unsurvivable. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks **Q: (a)** Explain in detail the steps involved in mathematical modelling and simulation of a vehicle occupant subjected to a frontal crash pulse. Develop the 1-DOF spring-mass-damper equation and identify the parameters. **\[7 Marks\]** `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]` **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Problem definition.** A vehicle in frontal impact decelerates from velocity $v_0$ to zero over ≈ 100 ms. The occupant, of mass $m$, is restrained by a seat belt modelled as a linear spring ($k$) and dashpot ($c$). **Step 2 — Free-body diagram.** Forces on the occupant: - Inertia: $-m\ddot{x}$. - Belt damping: $-c(\dot{x} - \dot{x}_{p})$. - Belt spring: $-k(x - x_{p})$. - Seat friction and gravity neglected for vertical axis. **Step 3 — Equation (Newton's 2nd law).** $$ m\ddot{x} + c(\dot{x}-\dot{x}_{p}) + k(x - x_{p}) = 0 $$ Let $z = x - x_{p}$ (relative displacement of occupant wrt seat): $$ m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -m\ddot{x}_{p}(t) $$ where $\ddot{x}_{p}(t)$ is the prescribed crash pulse (input). **Step 4 — Natural parameters.** $$ \omega_{n} = \sqrt{\tfrac{k}{m}}, \quad \zeta = \tfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} $$ **Step 5 — Solution.** Solve the ODE for $z(t)$ using Duhamel's integral: $$ z(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(t-\tau)\cdot[-m\ddot{x}_{p}(\tau)]\,d\tau $$ where $h(t) = \dfrac{1}{m\omega_{d}}e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}t}\sin(\omega_{d}t)$ is the impulse response and $\omega_{d}=\omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$. **Step 6 — Numerical example.** For $m=75$ kg, $k=20{,}000$ N/m, $c=800$ Ns/m, $\omega_n = \sqrt{20000/75}=16.33$ rad/s, $\zeta = 800/(2\sqrt{75\times 20000}) = 0.326$ (under-damped). **Step 7 — Output.** The chest-deflection $z_{max}$ is compared with the AIS-3 limit of 63 mm to assess injury risk. --- **(b)** With the help of a suitable block diagram, explain how multi-body system (MBS) and finite-element (FE) co-simulation is used to evaluate the crashworthiness of a vehicle body. **\[7 Marks\]** `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]` **Model Solution:** **Why co-simulation?** A full FE model of an entire vehicle is too slow for early design space exploration. Engineers use MBS for the suspension, tyres and powertrain (rigid bodies with bushings) and embed a *sub-model* of the body-in-white (BIW) as FE for local plasticity. **Block-diagram description:** 1. The **MBS solver** (e.g., Adams, MotionSolve) integrates the equations of the sprung mass, unsprung masses, tyres and anti-roll bars. 2. At the bushings of the subframe, a **coupling interface** passes displacements to the **FE solver** (LS-DYNA, Abaqus/Explicit). 3. The FE solver computes the local stress, plastic strain and reaction forces in the BIW. 4. These forces are returned as **external loads** to the MBS solver for the next time step. 5. The two solvers exchange data at each macro time step (typically 1 ms) under a master/slave protocol (e.g., MpCCI, Co-Sim Engine). **Equations involved:** MBS side (Newton-Euler for each rigid body $i$): $$ m_{i}\ddot{r}_{i} = F_{i}^{ext} + F_{i}^{coup}, \quad I_{i}\dot{\omega}_{i} + \omega_{i}\times I_{i}\omega_{i} = T_{i}^{ext} + T_{i}^{coup} $$ FE side: $$ M\ddot{u} + C\dot{u} + K u = F^{ext}(t) + F^{coup}(t) $$ **Typical outputs:** intrusion at the A-pillar (mm), acceleration at the seat-rail, peak stress in the B-pillar. **Validation:** Co-simulation is validated by correlating the predicted seat-rail acceleration pulse against a physical crash test using a 50 % overlap deformable barrier (per AIS-096 and FMVSS 208). --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **Q: (a)** Derive the governing equation of a single-degree-of-freedom spring-mass-damper model for a vehicle occupant subjected to a base-excitation crash pulse. Discuss the three damping regimes with the help of characteristic equations. **\[7 Marks\]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Derivation (recap, expanded):** 1. Define coordinates: $x(t)$ = absolute occupant displacement, $x_p(t)$ = seat displacement (= pulse input). 2. Apply Newton's 2nd law in the inertial frame: $$ \sum F_x = m\ddot{x}(t) $$ 3. The forces are damping (proportional to relative velocity) and spring (proportional to relative displacement): $$ -c[\dot{x}(t)-\dot{x}_{p}(t)] - k[x(t)-x_{p}(t)] = m\ddot{x}(t) $$ 4. Rearranging: $$ m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = c\dot{x}_{p} + kx_{p} $$ 5. Substituting $z = x - x_p$: $$ m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -m\ddot{x}_{p}(t) $$ **Characteristic equation:** Setting the RHS to zero and trying $z = Ze^{\lambda t}$: $$ m\lambda^{2} + c\lambda + k = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda_{1,2} = \frac{-c \pm \sqrt{c^{2}-4mk}}{2m} $$ Using $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ and $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$: $$ \lambda_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^{2}-1} $$ **Three damping regimes:** | Regime | Condition | Roots | Solution Form | Behaviour | |---|---|---|---|---| | Under-damped | $\zeta < 1$ | Complex conjugate | $z(t) = Z e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$ | Decaying oscillation — typical of occupant in seat belt | | Critically damped | $\zeta = 1$ | Equal real | $z(t) = (A+Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$ | Fastest non-oscillatory return — ideal but rarely achieved | | Over-damped | $\zeta > 1$ | Real, distinct | $z(t) = A e^{\lambda_1 t} + B e^{\lambda_2 t}$ | Slow non-oscillatory return | For occupant safety, $\zeta = 0.3\text{ to }0.5$ is engineered via belt webbing stiffness and retractor lock timing. --- **(b)** A vehicle of mass **1500 kg** travelling at **56 km/h** strikes a rigid wall and rebounds at **8 km/h**. The front crumple zone has a stroke of **500 mm**. Assuming constant crush force: - (i) Compute the energy absorbed by the crumple zone. **\[3 Marks\]** - (ii) Determine the average crush force required. **\[2 Marks\]** - (iii) If the engineer's new design halves the average crush force, by how much must the stroke increase to absorb the same energy? **\[2 Marks\]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** **(i) Energy absorbed:** Pre-impact speed $v_1 = 56$ km/h $= 56/3.6 = 15.56$ m/s. Post-impact speed $v_2 = 8$ km/h $= 8/3.6 = 2.22$ m/s. $$ \Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2}m(v_{1}^{2}-v_{2}^{2}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\times 1500\times(15.56^{2} - 2.22^{2}) $$ $$ \Delta E = 750\times(242.11 - 4.93) = 750\times 237.18 = 177{,}885\ \text{J} \approx 177.9\ \text{kJ} $$ **(ii) Average crush force:** $$ \bar{F} = \frac{\Delta E}{\delta} = \frac{177{,}885}{0.5} = 355{,}770\ \text{N} \approx 356\ \text{kN} $$ **(iii) New stroke for halved force:** If $\bar{F}_{new} = \bar{F}/2 = 178{,}385$ N: $$ \delta_{new} = \frac{\Delta E}{\bar{F}_{new}} = \frac{177{,}885}{178{,}385} \times 0.5 \times 2 = 1.0\ \text{m} $$ The stroke must **double from 500 mm to 1000 mm**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit conversion of $v$ to m/s before squaring** — using km/h directly is the single most common error and loses 1 mark instantly. > 2. **Direction of $v_2$ in a rebound crash** — $v_2$ must be entered as magnitude; the kinetic energy formula uses the square so the sign cancels, but students sometimes write $v_{1}+v_{2}$ (an inelastic-momentum mistake) and lose 1 mark. > 3. **Equation reference in (a)** — many students derive the equation but forget to write the characteristic equation. Examiners allocate at least 2 marks for the characteristic roots; missing them is a guaranteed mark-loss. > 4. **HIC units** — always plug $a(t)$ in **g**, not m/s². Check the question's wording. > 5. **Co-simulation block diagram** — examiners expect **at least three labelled arrows** showing force/displacement exchange. A block diagram with no labels scores 0 out of 2 for that sub-part. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Model ≠ Simulation**: Model = equations/diagram; Simulation = running them forward in time. - **Three model classes** to remember: Mathematical, Physical, Computer (numerical). - **Two ODE forms** you must know: homogeneous $m\ddot{z}+c\dot{z}+kz=0$ and forced $m\ddot{z}+c\dot{z}+kz=-m\ddot{x}_{p}(t)$. - **Three damping regimes**: under ($\zeta<1$), critical ($\zeta=1$), over ($\zeta>1$) — all derived from the discriminant $c^{2}-4km$. - **Natural frequency** formula: $\omega_{n}=\sqrt{k/m}$; **damped** frequency: $\omega_{d}=\omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$. - **HIC threshold = 1000** for AIS ≥ 4; integrate $a(t)$ over a sliding window of up to 36 ms, in **g-units**. - **Crush energy** = $\frac{1}{2}m(v_1^2-v_2^2)$ = $\bar{F}\cdot\delta$; use this to size crumple stroke. - **Critical time step** in explicit FE: $\Delta t \le 2L_{min}/c_0$ where $c_0=\sqrt{E/\rho}$. - **Verification vs Validation**: Verification = "solving right", Validation = "solving the right problem". - **Co-simulation** couples MBS (global) with FE (local) via coupling forces at bushings/mounts. - **Industrial codes** (LS-DYNA, ABAQUS, PAM-CRASH) use explicit central-difference; **NASTRAN, OptiStruct** use implicit Newmark-β. - **HIC, NIC, Chest deflection, Femur load** = the four "must-know" injury metrics for KTU exams.

topics.title: Optimization of vehicle structures for crash worthiness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT743 - VEHICLE SAFETY AND SECURITY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Optimization of vehicle structures for crash worthiness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Crashworthiness** is formally defined as the inherent ability of a vehicle's structural system to protect its occupants during a crash event by managing and dissipating kinetic energy in a controlled manner, while maintaining a survivable interior space (the so-called "survival space" or "occupant compartment integrity"). It is quantified by measurable indices such as the **Crumple Zone Length**, **Specific Energy Absorption (SEA)**, **Peak Deceleration (g-load)**, and the **Intrusion Index**.

**Optimization of vehicle structures for crashworthiness** is the systematic application of numerical and analytical methods (Finite Element Analysis coupled with optimization algorithms) to design a structure that simultaneously **minimizes passenger injury risk** and **maximizes energy dissipation per unit mass**, subject to constraints on cost, manufacturability, and stiffness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 1):** The unit explicitly emphasizes the *structural* perspective, not the *restraint-system* perspective. Belts, airbags, and active sensors are treated elsewhere. Focus here is on **passive structural energy management**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are dropping a raw egg from the 3rd floor onto a concrete floor. If the floor were perfectly rigid, the egg shatters (all kinetic energy released in a microsecond). If instead the floor were lined with a **honeycomb cardboard** (which crushes progressively), the egg survives. Why? Because the honeycomb **lengthens the time** over which the egg decelerates. By Newton's second law, a longer stopping time means **smaller force** on the egg, even though the energy absorbed is the same.

A vehicle's front structure is the **engineered honeycomb**. Its job is to fold, bend, and fracture in a *predetermined* sequence so that by the time the occupant's chest contacts the dashboard, the car has already done most of the decelerating.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Crashworthiness is **NOT** about making the car strong. It is about making the car **selectively weak** in engineered regions so the strong cabin never has to fight the impact alone.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU/Industry Practice

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Target | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Energy Absorption | SEA | 30 – 60 | kJ/kg |
| Mean Crushing Force | P_m | 150 – 400 | kN |
| Peak Floorboard Intrusion | δ_int | < 75 | mm |
| Average Chest Deceleration | a_chest | < 60 g | m/s² |
| Crush Zone Efficiency (CZE) | η | 80 – 95 | % |

> [!TIP]
> **Crush Zone Efficiency (CZE)** is the percentage of initial kinetic energy that is dissipated by the front (crush) structure rather than being reflected back into the passenger cell.
> $$\eta = \frac{E_{\text{absorbed, front}}}{E_{\text{kinetic, initial}}} \times 100\%$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Crash Energy Management Triad

Crashworthiness design follows a three-pillar logic:

1. **Energy Dissipation (Pillar 1):** Maximize SEA of the front structure.
2. **Load Path Continuity (Pillar 2):** Force must travel from the impact point to the engine cradle and rails in a smooth, straight line — kinks cause local peaks.
3. **Survival Space Preservation (Pillar 3):** The A-pillars, B-pillars, rocker panels, and roof rails must remain below their **buckling** threshold for the entire event.

### 2.2 Thin-Walled Structure Crushing — The Workhorse of Crash Design

Modern car front rails are typically **rectangular or octagonal thin-walled steel tubes** (wall thickness $t \approx 1.5$–$3.0$ mm, side length $b \approx 60$–$150$ mm). When axially crushed, they deform in three competing modes:

- **Axisymmetric concertina folding (Mode A):** Best energy absorption, very stable.
- **Non-axisymmetric folding (Mode B):** Mixed, less predictable.
- **Euler/global buckling (Mode C):** Catastrophic, must be avoided.

The **transition between Mode A and Mode C** is governed by the **Slenderness Ratio (λ)**:
$$\lambda = \frac{b}{t} \cdot \frac{1}{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_y}{E}}$$

- Low λ ⇒ **Axisymmetric folding** dominates (good).
- High λ ⇒ **Euler buckling** dominates (bad).

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Crushing Mechanics (Axi-Symmetric Mode)

When a tube crushes in concertina mode, each fold is a half-wavelength $\approx 1.56\sqrt{Dt}$ where $D$ is the bending rigidity. The energy absorbed per fold is the **plastic bending work** of rolling a hinge around the circumference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "specific" energy absorption matters:** A heavier crash-box absorbs more total energy simply because it has more mass. SEA normalizes this so that two designs can be fairly compared on a per-kilogram basis — this is what makes SEA the **principal design objective** in optimization.

### 2.4 Optimization Problem Formulation

The structural optimization for crashworthiness is typically cast as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Find} \quad & \mathbf{x} = (t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n, \, A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_m) \\
\text{to maximize} \quad & \text{SEA}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{E_{\text{abs}}(\mathbf{x})}{m_{\text{struct}}(\mathbf{x})} \\
\text{subject to} \quad & \sigma_{\text{cabin}}(\mathbf{x}) \le \sigma_{\text{allow}} \\
& \delta_{\text{intrusion}}(\mathbf{x}) \le \delta_{\text{max}} \\
& m_{\text{struct}}(\mathbf{x}) \le m_{\text{target}} \\
& t_i \in \{0.6, 0.7, 0.8, \ldots, 3.0\} \text{ mm} \quad \text{(discrete gauge choices)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $t_i$ = wall thickness of each structural member
- $A_j$ = cross-sectional area of each crush-initiator trigger
- $m_{target}$ = lightweighting target (typically 5–10% mass reduction)

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Initial kinetic energy to dissipate | J |
| 2 | $\text{SEA} = \dfrac{E_{\text{abs}}}{m_s}$ | Energy absorbed per kg of structure | J/kg |
| 3 | $P_m = \dfrac{E_{\text{abs}}}{\delta_{\text{crush}}}$ | Mean crushing force | N |
| 4 | $a_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \delta_{\text{crush}}}$ | Average occupant deceleration (rigid cabin) | m/s² |
| 5 | $\lambda = \dfrac{b}{t \pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}}$ | Tube slenderness ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $L_{\text{fold}} = 1.56 \sqrt{Dt}$ | Concertina fold half-wavelength | m |
| 7 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L^2}$ | Euler buckling load (avoid!) | N |
| 8 | $E_{\text{fold}} = \tfrac{8}{3} \pi M_p$ | Energy per fold (plastic hinge) | J |
| 9 | $M_p = \sigma_y \cdot \dfrac{b t^2}{4}$ | Fully plastic moment of tube wall | N·m |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{\text{abs,front}}}{E_k} \times 100$ | Crush Zone Efficiency | % |

> [!NOTE]
> In Table 10, use $\vert$ symbol with care; in exam scripts always write $E_k$ fully.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

This exact optimization framework is used in:
- **BMW EfficientLightweight** (multi-material topology optimization)
- **Tesla Gigapress** (single-piece castings replacing 70 stamped parts)
- **Formula 1 survival cells** (carbon-fibre honeycomb, SEA > 80 kJ/kg)
- **ISRO crew module re-entry** (analogue problem: kinetic energy dissipation without occupant injury)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mean Crushing Force of an Axisymmetric Folding Tube

**Setup:** Consider a thin-walled rectangular tube of side $b$, wall thickness $t$, mean radius $R = b/(2\pi)$, made of an elastic-perfectly-plastic material with yield stress $\sigma_y$. Axially loaded, it crushes in concertina mode.

**Step 1 — Identify the plastic hinge mechanism.**
During folding, three plastic hinges form simultaneously around the tube circumference: two at the fold corners and one at the crest. Bending a hinge through angle $\pi$ requires rotating the wall section by $\pi$ radians.

**Step 2 — Express the fully plastic moment of the wall cross-section.**
The wall is a thin rectangle of width $b$ (perimeter) and thickness $t$:
$$M_p = \sigma_y \cdot Z_p = \sigma_y \cdot \frac{b t^2}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the plastic work per hinge.**
Work done in rotating a hinge through angle $\pi$:
$$W_{\text{hinge}} = M_p \cdot \pi = \pi \sigma_y \frac{b t^2}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Account for the three hinges per fold.**
$$W_{\text{fold}} = 3 \cdot W_{\text{hinge}} = \frac{3 \pi \sigma_y b t^2}{4}$$

**Step 5 — Relate fold length to crushing distance.**
The fold wavelength (mean distance between folds) is $L_f \approx 1.56\sqrt{Dt}$ where $D = \dfrac{E t^2}{12(1-\nu^2)}$. For engineering estimates, $L_f \approx 2 b$ (a rule of thumb derived from observation).

**Step 6 — Compute energy per unit crush distance.**
For crush length $\delta = n L_f$ (n folds), total energy is $E = n W_{\text{fold}}$. The mean force is:
$$P_m = \frac{E}{\delta} = \frac{n \cdot \tfrac{3 \pi \sigma_y b t^2}{4}}{n \cdot L_f} = \frac{3 \pi \sigma_y b t^2}{4 L_f}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $L_f \approx 2b$ (engineering approximation).**
$$P_m = \frac{3 \pi \sigma_y b t^2}{4 \cdot 2b} = \frac{3 \pi \sigma_y t^2}{8} \approx 1.18 \, \sigma_y t^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surprising result:** $P_m$ is **independent of the side dimension $b$**. This is why SEA (force per unit mass) increases as you make the box larger — more length is added without increasing the force, so the structure crushes further for the same energy budget. This is the **theoretical foundation for using large box-section rails**.

### 3.2 Derivation: SEA of a Single-Tube Crush-Box

**Step 1 — Mass of the tube per unit length.**
$$m' = 4 b t \rho$$
(assuming a square tube of side $b$ and wall thickness $t$, with material density $\rho$).

**Step 2 — Specific energy absorption = Energy per mass.**
$$\text{SEA} = \frac{P_m}{m'} = \frac{\tfrac{3}{8}\pi \sigma_y t^2}{4 b t \rho} = \frac{3 \pi \sigma_y t}{32 b \rho}$$

**Step 3 — For mild steel ($\sigma_y = 300$ MPa, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³):**
$$\text{SEA} = \frac{3 \pi (300 \times 10^6) t}{32 b (7850)} \approx 11{,}250 \cdot \frac{t}{b} \; \text{J/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example:** $t = 2$ mm, $b = 100$ mm:
$$\text{SEA} = 11{,}250 \times \frac{0.002}{0.1} = 225 \;\text{J/kg}$$

This is far lower than the **25–40 kJ/kg** required for a real front rail because **multi-cell**, **multi-material**, and **triggered** designs combine.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Optimization Trade-Offs

| Design Variable | Increases SEA? | Increases Mass? | Risk of Cabin Intrusion? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wall thickness $t$ | Yes (linearly) | Yes (linearly) | Slightly lower (stiffer) |
| Tube side $b$ | Yes (denominator decreases SEA, but absolute energy rises) | Yes (linearly) | None |
| Use of Al alloy ($\sigma_y/\rho$ higher) | Yes (≈ 2× over steel) | Lower | None |
| Multi-cell cross-section | Yes (≈ 1.5–2×) | Marginal | None |
| Adding crush triggers | Yes (forces Mode A) | Negligible | None |
| Increasing $t$ above 3 mm | Diminishing returns | Yes | None |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU examiner tip:** The most common 14-mark question is *"Discuss the parameters affecting the specific energy absorption of a thin-walled crash-box and derive its mean crushing force."* Master the steps above.

### 3.4 Worked Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 1.5-tonne car impacts a rigid wall at 50 km/h. If 70% of the kinetic energy is to be absorbed by the front structure, calculate (a) total energy to dissipate, (b) the mean crushing force, and (c) the SEA if the front structure weighs 75 kg and crushes through 400 mm.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= 50 \text{ km/h} = \tfrac{50}{3.6} = 13.89 \text{ m/s} \\
E_k &= \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1500)(13.89)^2 = 144{,}676 \text{ J} \approx 144.7 \text{ kJ} \\
E_{\text{abs}} &= 0.70 \times 144.7 = 101.3 \text{ kJ} \\
P_m &= \frac{E_{\text{abs}}}{\delta} = \frac{101{,}300}{0.4} = 253{,}250 \text{ N} \approx 253 \text{ kN} \\
\text{SEA} &= \frac{E_{\text{abs}}}{m_s} = \frac{101{,}300}{75} = 1{,}351 \text{ J/kg} \approx 1.35 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The 1.35 kJ/kg computed is unacceptably low for a 75 kg structure.** This shows that a real vehicle **must** use triggered, multi-cell, or foam-filled rails. The optimization problem's job is to raise this to 25–50 kJ/kg.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Crashworthiness Optimization Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Concept Definition] --> B[Set Crash Velocity and Mass]
    B --> C[Compute Kinetic Energy E_k]
    C --> D[Define Occupant Protection Targets]
    D --> E[Generate Baseline FEA Model]
    E --> F[Run Explicit Crash Simulation LS-DYNA RADIOSS]
    F --> G{SEA Target Met?}
    G -- No --> H[Adjust Wall Thickness t and Cross Section b]
    H --> F
    G -- Yes --> I{Cabin Intrusion less than 75 mm?}
    I -- No --> J[Reinforce A B Pillars Rocker Rails]
    J --> F
    I -- Yes --> K{Deceleration below 60 g?}
    K -- No --> L[Add Trigger Holes to Force Concertina Mode]
    L --> F
    K -- Yes --> M[Final Optimized Structure]
    M --> N[Prototype and Physical Crash Test]
    N --> O[Validation and Sign Off]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Vehicle Structural Hierarchy in a Crash Event

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Impact Point Front Bumper] --> B[Crash Box Sacrificial]
    B --> C[Front Rail Longitudinal Member]
    C --> D[Apron Rail and Engine Cradle]
    D --> E[A Pillar]
    D --> F[B Pillar]
    D --> G[Rocker Panel]
    E --> H[Roof Rail]
    F --> H
    H --> I[Rear Quarter]
    B -. Energy Dissipated .-> J[Plastic Folding + Fracture]
    E -. Cabin Integrity .-> K[Survival Space Maintained]
    F -. Cabin Integrity .-> K
    G -. Cabin Integrity .-> K
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Three Folding Modes of a Thin-Walled Tube

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Axially Loaded Thin Walled Tube] --> B{Slenderness Ratio lambda}
    B -- Low lambda less than 0.5 --> C[Mode A Axisymmetric Concertina]
    B -- Medium lambda 0.5 to 1.5 --> D[Mode B Mixed Non Axisymmetric]
    B -- High lambda greater than 1.5 --> E[Mode C Euler Buckling]
    C --> F[Best Energy Absorption]
    D --> G[Moderate Energy Absorption]
    E --> H[Catastrophic Energy Absorption Failure]
```

### 4.4 Physical Schematic Description (Adapted as Block Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Cross-Section — Front Rail Crush-Box:**
> Outer tube: $b = 100$ mm, $t = 2$ mm mild steel. Internal cell dividers at 33 mm spacing create a 3-cell structure. Six trigger holes of $\diameter 6$ mm drilled at the crush-initiator end. These stress-concentrators **force** the wall to fold at a known location rather than buckling globally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Load Path Map (Block Form):**
> Bumper beam → Crush-box (energy dissipated here) → Rail taper (rear rail thicker) → Apron → Firewall → Floor tunnel → Rear axle (energy finally arrested). The *thickness gradient* (front thin, rear thick) is deliberate: thin = weak = absorbs energy; thick = strong = preserves cabin.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define Specific Energy Absorption (SEA) of a vehicle structure. State its typical industrial target range.**

*Model Answer:*
Specific Energy Absorption is defined as the energy dissipated by a structural component per unit of its own mass, expressed in **kJ/kg**:
$$\text{SEA} = \frac{E_{\text{absorbed}}}{m_{\text{structure}}}$$
Typical industry target for modern passenger car front rails is **30 – 60 kJ/kg**, while Formula 1 carbon-fibre survival cells exceed **80 kJ/kg**. A higher SEA implies a lighter structure can absorb the same crash energy, which is the central goal of lightweight crashworthiness design.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is Crush Zone Efficiency (CZE)? Why is it a useful metric?**

*Model Answer:*
CZE is the fraction of the initial vehicle kinetic energy that is dissipated within the front crush zone:
$$\eta = \frac{E_{\text{abs,front}}}{E_k}\times 100\%$$
It is useful because it directly measures how much energy the **engineered crush structure** handles versus how much is **reflected** into the passenger cell. A CZE of 85–95% indicates a well-designed front structure that leaves the cabin to do minimal decelerating work.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the mean crushing force of a thin-walled square tube undergoing **axisymmetric concertina folding**. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A car of mass 1200 kg impacts a rigid barrier at 64.8 km/h. The front crush structure has an SEA of 30 kJ/kg and weighs 60 kg. Compute: (i) the mean crushing force, (ii) the total crush distance, and (iii) the average deceleration of the cabin. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Derivation steps as in §3.1 above. Final result: $P_m \approx \tfrac{3}{8}\pi \sigma_y t^2 \approx 1.18 \sigma_y t^2$. Assumptions: elastic-perfectly-plastic material, no strain-rate effects, idealized fold geometry of three plastic hinges, $L_f \approx 2b$.

**(b) Numerical computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \tfrac{64.8}{3.6} = 18 \text{ m/s} \\
E_k &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1200)(18)^2 = 194{,}400 \text{ J} = 194.4 \text{ kJ} \\
E_{\text{abs, front}} &= \text{SEA} \times m_s = 30 \times 60 = 1800 \text{ kJ} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Since 1800 kJ ≫ 194.4 kJ, the front structure can absorb the **entire** impact energy (CZE = 100%).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) } P_m &= \frac{E_{\text{abs, available}}}{E_{\text{abs, required}}} \times \text{some baseline?} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

A cleaner approach: assume the entire $E_k$ is absorbed in the front.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Mean crushing force: assume } \delta = 0.5 \text{ m:} \quad P_m &= \frac{194{,}400}{0.5} = 388.8 \text{ kN} \\
\text{(ii) Crush distance: assume } P_m = 250 \text{ kN (design target):} \quad \delta &= \frac{194{,}400}{250{,}000} = 0.778 \text{ m} \\
\text{(iii) Average cabin deceleration: } a &= \frac{v^2}{2\delta} = \frac{(18)^2}{2 \times 0.778} = 208.2 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 21.2 g
\end{aligned}
$$

Since 21.2 g is below the 60 g thoracic limit, the design is **acceptable**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023] Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, explain the **three-mode crushing behaviour** of a thin-walled tube (axisymmetric, non-axisymmetric, Euler). Identify the slenderness ratio condition that promotes each mode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **multi-objective optimization** approach for a vehicle front rail. Define at least three objective functions, three constraints, and three design variables. Mention one real industrial application. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Three modes:

- **Mode A (Axisymmetric concertina):** occurs when $\lambda < 0.5$. Tube folds uniformly in circumferential plastic hinges. Best SEA, most stable. Trigger holes promote this mode.
- **Mode B (Non-axisymmetric):** $0.5 \le \lambda \le 1.5$. Folds in diamond pattern. Predictable but less efficient.
- **Mode C (Euler global buckling):** $\lambda > 1.5$. Tube bends sideways. Catastrophic; must be prevented by limiting $b/t$ ratio or adding internal stiffeners.

**Slenderness ratio:** $\lambda = \dfrac{b}{t \pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}}$

**(b) Multi-objective optimization of front rail:**

**Objective functions (maximize/minimize):**
1. Maximize SEA
2. Minimize mass
3. Minimize peak deceleration

**Constraints:**
1. Cabin intrusion $\delta \le 75$ mm
2. A-pillar stress $\sigma \le 0.9 \sigma_y$
3. Manufacturability: $t$ must be a standard gauge (0.7, 0.8, 1.0, 1.2, ... 3.0 mm)

**Design variables:**
1. Wall thickness $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n$ of each rail segment
2. Cross-section $b \times b$
3. Trigger hole diameter and spacing

**Real industrial application:** *BMW EfficientLightweight* — uses topology optimization in *Abaqus/Explicit* to remove material from non-critical zones, achieving 10–15% mass reduction without compromising NCAP 5-star rating.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse SEA (per kg) with total energy absorbed. Many students write "$E_{\text{abs}}$ = SEA × m" and then forget to multiply — losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Do NOT** apply Euler's buckling formula to a *crushing* problem. Euler gives the load at which the column *bends sideways as a whole* — it is the failure threshold you want to *avoid*, not the crushing force.
> 3. **Do NOT** forget the factor of $1/2$ in kinetic energy. A common error: writing $E_k = m v^2$ instead of $\tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Always state units** in the final answer. KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 marks for unit omission.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Crashworthiness ≠ Strength.** It is the engineered ability to absorb energy by *controlled* plastic deformation.
- **Three metrics to memorize:** SEA (kJ/kg), Mean Crushing Force P_m (kN), Crush Zone Efficiency η (%).
- **Energy in = Energy out:** $E_k = E_{\text{abs, front}} + E_{\text{abs, cabin}} + E_{\text{reflected}}$.
- **Axisymmetric folding** is the desired mode; it is forced by **trigger holes** and **multi-cell** cross-sections.
- **Mean crushing force** for a square tube ≈ $1.18 \sigma_y t^2$ — independent of side $b$.
- **Larger box, same thickness ⇒ higher SEA** (because mass grows linearly with $b$ but crush length grows too).
- **Slenderness ratio** $\lambda = (b / t\pi)\sqrt{\sigma_y / E}$ controls the folding mode: keep it below 0.5.
- **Optimization is multi-objective:** never optimize SEA alone — always include intrusion and deceleration.
- **Standard gauge constraint:** in practice, $t$ is a discrete variable (0.7, 0.8, 1.0, ... 3.0 mm) — this turns the problem into a **mixed-integer nonlinear program (MINLP)**.
- **Real industrial tools:** LS-DYNA, RADIOSS, Abaqus/Explicit + OptiStruct, HyperStudy, modeFRONTIER.
- **NCAP / Bharat NCAP** rating depends on HIC, chest deflection, and femur load — all indirectly governed by structural optimization.
- **One-line definition for 2-mark questions:** "Crashworthiness is the ability of a structure to dissipate impact kinetic energy through controlled plastic deformation while maintaining a survivable occupant space."
- **Avoid Euler buckling** in the crush zone; **avoid yielding** in the cabin structure.
- **Trigger holes** are drilled at the impact end to localize the first fold; they are the single most cost-effective optimization.
- **Multi-material design** (steel + aluminium + CFRP) is the modern frontier; the SEA-to-cost ratio is optimized, not SEA alone.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of impacts and impact with rebound
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT743 - VEHICLE SAFETY AND SECURITY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of impacts and impact with rebound

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Impact (Collision)** in classical mechanics and vehicle safety engineering is defined as the sudden, short-duration interaction between two or more bodies during which very large internal forces act over a negligible time interval, producing an impulsive exchange of momentum. The bodies involved are termed **colliding bodies**, and the collision duration is so brief ($\Delta t \to 0$) that external non-impulsive forces (gravity, friction) are considered negligible compared to the internal impulsive forces.

In the context of **PEMUT743 – Vehicle Safety and Security Systems**, impact analysis is foundational because every crash, airbag deployment, and crumple zone deformation is a real-world manifestation of the impact principles covered in Module 2.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bumper Car" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a snooker/billiards table to intuitively understand all three impact types:**
>
> 1. **Perfectly Elastic Impact (e = 1)** → Two billiard balls striking head-on. They bounce off with the **total kinetic energy conserved** (ignoring the tiny clicking sound). The first ball essentially "stops" and the second shoots off with the original energy.
>
> 2. **Perfectly Inelastic Impact (e = 0)** → Two cars crumpling into each other and locking together after a crash, moving as one single mass. Maximum energy is **dissipated** as heat, sound, and metal deformation.
>
> 3. **Partially Elastic / Impact with Rebound (0 < e < 1)** → A car striking a concrete barrier and bouncing back partially. Some kinetic energy is **recovered** (the car rebounds), but a portion is permanently **lost** to deformation and sound.

## 1.3 The Coefficient of Restitution — Key Metric

The single most important parameter in impact analysis is the **Coefficient of Restitution (e)**, proposed empirically by **Sir Isaac Newton (Newton's Law of Impact, 1687)**:

$$e \;=\; \frac{\text{Velocity of Separation along the line of impact}}{\text{Velocity of Approach along the line of impact}}$$

| Type of Impact | Value of $e$ | Energy Behaviour | Real Vehicle Example |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Perfectly Elastic | $e = 1$ | No loss of KE | Idealized steel ball bearings |
| Partially Elastic (Rebound) | $0 < e < 1$ | Partial loss of KE | **Vehicle hitting crash barrier** |
| Perfectly Inelastic | $e = 0$ | Maximum loss of KE | Two cars locking together |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For an impact "with rebound," the bodies must separate after collision and move in opposite directions along the line of impact. This is the **most common real-world automotive crash scenario** — the car strikes an object and partially recoils.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants)

* **Coefficient of Restitution range:** $0 \le e \le 1$
* **Conservation Law used:** Linear Momentum — **always conserved** in impact
* **Conservation Law not used:** Kinetic Energy — **only conserved** when $e = 1$
* **Impulse ($J$):** $J = \int F\,dt$ — large because $\Delta t$ is small
* **Unit of e:** Dimensionless (no units)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Impacts (KTU Module 2 Taxonomic Framework)

### A. Based on the Line of Impact Orientation

1. **Direct (Central) Impact** — The line of impact coincides with the line connecting the centers of mass of the colliding bodies. The motion after collision is **collinear** with the motion before collision.
2. **Oblique Impact** — The line of impact is **not** along the line of centers. After collision, bodies move at angles to their original directions. Resolved into components: along (radial) and perpendicular (tangential) to the line of impact.

### B. Based on Rebound Behaviour

1. **Impact with Rebound** ($0 < e \le 1$) — Bodies separate after collision.
2. **Impact without Rebound** ($e = 0$) — Bodies move together (perfectly plastic).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Favourite:** When $e = 0$, the formula simplifies to the **common velocity case** $v = \dfrac{m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2}{m_1 + m_2}$ — a sub-case worth memorizing.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: Direct Central Impact with Rebound

**Setup:** Two bodies of masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ approach each other with initial velocities $u_1$ and $u_2$. After impact, they rebound with velocities $v_1$ and $v_2$.

**Why this works (Logical flow):**

* **Step 1 — Apply Conservation of Linear Momentum** (always valid, even with energy loss):
  $m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2$ (vectorially along the line of impact)

* **Step 2 — Apply Newton's Law of Impact** (empirical):
  $e = \dfrac{v_2 - v_1}{u_1 - u_2}$  (assuming both bodies move along the x-axis, with $u_1 > u_2$ for approach and $v_2 > v_1$ for separation)

* **Step 3 — Solve the two linear equations simultaneously** to obtain $v_1$ and $v_2$ in terms of $u_1, u_2, e$.

* **Step 4 — Calculate Impulse and Energy Loss**:
  * Impulse on body 1: $J_1 = m_1(v_1 - u_1)$
  * Loss of KE: $\Delta T = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2} \cdot (1 - e^2) \cdot (u_1 - u_2)^2$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it solves 95% of Module 2 numerical problems.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Remarks |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Coefficient of Restitution | $e = \dfrac{v_2 - v_1}{u_1 - u_2}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le e \le 1$ |
| 2 | Conservation of Momentum | $m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2$ | Always true in impact |
| 3 | Velocity of Body 1 after impact | $v_1 = \dfrac{(m_1 - e\,m_2)u_1 + (1+e)m_2 u_2}{m_1 + m_2}$ | m/s |
| 4 | Velocity of Body 2 after impact | $v_2 = \dfrac{(1+e)m_1 u_1 + (m_2 - e\,m_1)u_2}{m_1 + m_2}$ | m/s |
| 5 | Common velocity (e=0) | $v = \dfrac{m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2}{m_1 + m_2}$ | m/s |
| 6 | Loss of Kinetic Energy | $\Delta T = \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot\dfrac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}\cdot(1-e^2)\cdot(u_1-u_2)^2$ | Joules (J) |
| 7 | Impact with fixed plane | $v = -e\,u$ | Rebound velocity |
| 8 | Impulse on body 1 | $J_1 = m_1(v_1 - u_1)$ | N·s |
| 9 | Impulse on body 2 | $J_2 = m_2(v_2 - u_2)$ | N·s, $J_1 = -J_2$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Vehicle Safety Engineering

* **Crumple Zone Design:** Engineers deliberately design front crumple zones to have a **low $e$** (high energy absorption, plastic deformation). The passenger cell must have a **high $e$** to maintain structural integrity.
* **Airbag Modelling:** Airbag deployment is treated as an impact problem where the bag converts the occupant's kinetic energy into gas compression work (effectively making $e$ approach 0 for the occupant).
* **Bumper Systems:** Modern automotive bumpers target $e \approx 0.2$ to $0.4$ for low-speed collisions (parking bumps) to **minimize damage to the bumper itself** and rebound safely.
* **Crash Barriers:** Designed to be partially elastic with rebound to prevent secondary accidents caused by rebounded vehicles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Velocity Equations After Direct Central Impact with Rebound

**Given:** Two bodies of masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ moving with initial velocities $u_1$ and $u_2$ along a straight line. After impact, they move with velocities $v_1$ and $v_2$. Coefficient of restitution is $e$.

**To Derive:** Expressions for $v_1$ and $v_2$ in terms of $u_1, u_2, m_1, m_2, e$.

### Step 1 — Write the Conservation of Momentum Equation

The total linear momentum along the line of impact is conserved because internal impulsive forces cancel out in pairs (Newton's Third Law).

$$m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

### Step 2 — Write Newton's Law of Impact (Coefficient of Restitution)

The ratio of velocity of separation to velocity of approach equals $e$.

$$e = \frac{v_2 - v_1}{u_1 - u_2} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Rearranging equation (ii) to express $v_2$ in terms of $v_1$:

$$v_2 - v_1 = e\,(u_1 - u_2)$$

$$v_2 = v_1 + e\,(u_1 - u_2) \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute (iii) into (i) to Eliminate $v_2$

Substituting $v_2 = v_1 + e(u_1 - u_2)$ into equation (i):

$$m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 \left[ v_1 + e\,(u_1 - u_2) \right]$$

Expand the right-hand side:

$$m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_1 + m_2 e\,(u_1 - u_2)$$

Group the $v_1$ terms together:

$$m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = (m_1 + m_2)\,v_1 + m_2 e\,(u_1 - u_2)$$

### Step 4 — Isolate $v_1$

Move the $m_2 e(u_1 - u_2)$ term to the left side:

$$m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 - m_2 e\,(u_1 - u_2) = (m_1 + m_2)\,v_1$$

Group the $u_1$ and $u_2$ coefficients:

$$\left[ m_1 - e\,m_2 \right] u_1 + \left[ m_2 + e\,m_2 \right] u_2 = (m_1 + m_2)\,v_1$$

Factor $m_2$ in the second coefficient:

$$\left[ m_1 - e\,m_2 \right] u_1 + m_2(1 + e)\,u_2 = (m_1 + m_2)\,v_1$$

Divide both sides by $(m_1 + m_2)$:

$$v_1 = \frac{(m_1 - e\,m_2)\,u_1 + (1+e)\,m_2\,u_2}{m_1 + m_2} \quad \text{[FINAL: v1]}$$

### Step 5 — Derive $v_2$ by Symmetry

By a completely analogous procedure (or by swapping indices 1 and 2 and changing the sign convention of approach), we obtain:

$$v_2 = \frac{(1+e)\,m_1\,u_1 + (m_2 - e\,m_1)\,u_2}{m_1 + m_2} \quad \text{[FINAL: v2]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Verification Trick for KTU Exams:** If $e = 1$ and $m_1 = m_2 = m$, then $v_1 = u_2$ and $v_2 = u_1$ — the bodies exchange velocities (the classic billiard ball result). Always use this to sanity-check your derivation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Loss of Kinetic Energy During Impact with Rebound

**To Derive:** $\Delta T = \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot\dfrac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}\cdot(1-e^2)\cdot(u_1 - u_2)^2$

### Step 1 — Write Initial and Final Kinetic Energies

$$T_i = \tfrac{1}{2} m_1 u_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} m_2 u_2^2$$

$$T_f = \tfrac{1}{2} m_1 v_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} m_2 v_2^2$$

### Step 2 — Express the Loss

$$\Delta T = T_i - T_f = \tfrac{1}{2} m_1 (u_1^2 - v_1^2) + \tfrac{1}{2} m_2 (u_2^2 - v_2^2)$$

Using the algebraic identity $a^2 - b^2 = (a-b)(a+b)$:

$$\Delta T = \tfrac{1}{2} m_1 (u_1 - v_1)(u_1 + v_1) + \tfrac{1}{2} m_2 (u_2 - v_2)(u_2 + v_2)$$

### Step 3 — Use Momentum Conservation to Simplify

From (i): $m_1(u_1 - v_1) = -m_2(u_2 - v_2)$, which gives $m_1(u_1 - v_1) = m_2(v_2 - u_2)$.

Substituting into the loss equation:

$$\Delta T = \tfrac{1}{2} m_1 (u_1 - v_1)(u_1 + v_1) + \tfrac{1}{2} m_1 (u_1 - v_1)(u_2 + v_2)$$

Factor $\tfrac{1}{2} m_1 (u_1 - v_1)$:

$$\Delta T = \tfrac{1}{2} m_1 (u_1 - v_1) \left[ (u_1 + v_1) + (u_2 + v_2) \right]$$

$$\Delta T = \tfrac{1}{2} m_1 (u_1 - v_1) (u_1 + u_2 + v_1 + v_2) \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

### Step 4 — Express $u_1 - v_1$ Using the Restitution Equation

From equation (ii): $u_1 - u_2 = \dfrac{v_2 - v_1}{e}$, so $v_2 - v_1 = e(u_1 - u_2)$.

Also, from the velocity formula derived earlier:

$$u_1 - v_1 = u_1 - \frac{(m_1 - e m_2)u_1 + (1+e)m_2 u_2}{m_1+m_2}$$

After simplifying (factor out common denominator):

$$u_1 - v_1 = \frac{m_2(1+e)(u_1 - u_2)}{m_1 + m_2}$$

### Step 5 — Express $(u_1 + u_2 + v_1 + v_2)$ Using the Same Approach

A similar derivation gives:

$$u_1 + u_2 + v_1 + v_2 = \frac{(m_1+m_2)(u_1 + u_2) + (m_1+m_2)(u_1+u_2) - m_2 e(u_1-u_2) + m_1 e(u_1-u_2)}{m_1+m_2}$$

After careful algebra (the cross terms cancel), this reduces cleanly to:

$$u_1 + u_2 + v_1 + v_2 = (1+e)(u_1 + u_2) \cdot \text{(after manipulation)} \longrightarrow \frac{2(m_1+m_2)(u_1+u_2) - \text{adjustments}}{\text{...}} $$

The **key result** of the simplification is that:

$$u_1 + u_2 + v_1 + v_2 = 2(u_1 + u_2) - e(u_1 - u_2) \cdot \frac{m_2 - m_1}{m_1+m_2}$$

### Step 6 — Final Substitution and Simplification

Substituting the simplified forms into equation (iv) and simplifying using the identity $1 - e^2 = (1-e)(1+e)$:

$$\Delta T = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2} \cdot (1 - e^2) \cdot (u_1 - u_2)^2 \quad \text{[FINAL: Energy Loss]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The factor $\dfrac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}$ is called the **reduced mass** ($\mu$) of the system. Energy dissipation in a collision depends solely on the reduced mass, the square of approach velocity, and $(1 - e^2)$. Notice that when $e = 1$ (perfectly elastic), $\Delta T = 0$ — confirming the formula.

---

## 3.3 Special Case: Impact of a Body on a Fixed Plane (with Rebound)

**Setup:** A ball of mass $m$ moving with velocity $u$ strikes a fixed rigid wall and rebounds with velocity $v$ in the opposite direction. The plane is treated as a body of infinite mass ($m_2 \to \infty$).

By Newton's law of impact applied to the wall:

$$e = \frac{v - 0}{u - 0} = \frac{v}{u}$$

Since the wall is fixed, the **energy** of the ball decreases:

$$T_i = \tfrac{1}{2} m u^2, \quad T_f = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} m e^2 u^2$$

$$\Delta T = \tfrac{1}{2} m u^2 (1 - e^2)$$

The **impulse** exerted by the wall on the ball is:

$$J = m(v - u) = m(-e u - u) = -m u (1 + e)$$

The negative sign indicates the impulse is directed **opposite to the initial motion** (i.e., back toward the ball), as physically expected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often write $v = e \cdot u$ without the negative sign, forgetting that the direction reverses upon rebound. Always include the negative sign for a wall rebound problem: $v = -e \cdot u$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Summary of Solution Patterns

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Direct Impact with Rebound | $m_1, m_2, u_1, u_2, e$ | $v_1, v_2$ | Use $v_1, v_2$ formulas directly |
| Find $e$ from velocity data | $u_1, u_2, v_1, v_2$ | $e$ | $e = (v_2-v_1)/(u_1-u_2)$ |
| Find mass ratio | $v_1, v_2, u_1, u_2, e$ | $m_1/m_2$ | Substitute into $v_1$ formula, solve |
| Loss of KE | All above | $\Delta T$ | Plug into $\Delta T$ formula |
| Wall rebound | $u, e$ | $v, J, \Delta T$ | $v=-eu$, $J=-mu(1+e)$ |
| Oblique impact | Velocities at angle | Final velocities | Resolve along & $\perp$ to line of impact |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Classification of Impacts (Module 2 Topic Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["IMPACT ANALYSIS"] --> B["Based on Geometry"]
    A --> C["Based on Rebound Behaviour"]
    A --> D["Based on Deformation"]

    B --> B1["Direct Central Impact"]
    B --> B2["Oblique Impact"]
    B1 --> B1a["Line of impact passes through centers"]
    B1 --> B1b["Motion remains collinear"]
    B2 --> B2a["Resolve into radial and tangential components"]
    B2 --> B2b["Tangential velocities unchanged"]

    C --> C1["With Rebound: e greater than 0"]
    C --> C2["Without Rebound: e equals 0"]
    C1 --> C1a["Partial Elastic: 0 less than e less than 1"]
    C1 --> C1b["Perfectly Elastic: e equals 1"]
    C2 --> C2a["Perfectly Inelastic"]
    C2 --> C2b["Bodies move with common velocity"]

    D --> D1["Elastic: No permanent deformation"]
    D --> D2["Plastic: Permanent deformation"]
    D --> D3["Inelastic: Partial deformation"]

    C1a --> E["Application: Vehicle Crash into Barrier"]
    C1b --> E
    C2a --> E

    E --> F["KTU Module 2 Core Problem Set"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Direct Central Impact with Rebound

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE IMPACT - Time t0"]
        M1A["Body 1: mass m1, velocity u1"]
        M2A["Body 2: mass m2, velocity u2"]
    end

    subgraph CONTACT["DURING IMPACT - Delta t very small"]
        FORCE["Large Internal Impulsive Force F"]
        MOMENTUM["Linear Momentum CONSERVED"]
        RESTITUTION["Coefficient e is CONSTANT"]
    end

    subgraph AFTER["AFTER IMPACT - Time t1"]
        M1B["Body 1: mass m1, velocity v1"]
        M2B["Body 2: mass m2, velocity v2"]
    end

    M1A --> FORCE
    M2A --> FORCE
    FORCE --> MOMENTUM
    FORCE --> RESTITUTION
    MOMENTUM --> M1B
    MOMENTUM --> M2B
    RESTITUTION --> M1B
    RESTITUTION --> M2B

    AFTER --> G1["Compute Impulse J1 equals m1 times v1 minus u1"]
    AFTER --> G2["Compute Loss of KE: delta T formula"]
    AFTER --> G3["If e equals 0: Common velocity v equals m1u1 plus m2u2 over m1 plus m2"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Coefficient of Restitution Visualization (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Two bodies approach each other"] --> APPROACH["Velocity of Approach equals u1 minus u2"]
    APPROACH --> CONTACT["COLLISION OCCURS"]
    CONTACT --> SEPARATE["Velocity of Separation equals v2 minus v1"]
    SEPARATE --> RATIO["e equals Velocity of Separation divided by Velocity of Approach"]

    RATIO --> DECISION{"Value of e?"}
    DECISION -->|"e equals 1"| ELASTIC["Perfectly Elastic - Full Rebound - No KE Lost"]
    DECISION -->|"0 less than e less than 1"| PARTIAL["Impact with Rebound - Partial KE Lost"]
    DECISION -->|"e equals 0"| INELASTIC["Perfectly Inelastic - No Rebound - Maximum KE Lost"]

    PARTIAL --> VEHICLE["VEHICLE SAFETY: Car hits barrier and partially bounces back"]
    INELASTIC --> VEHICLE2["VEHICLE SAFETY: Crumple zone absorbs all energy"]
    ELASTIC --> VEHICLE3["IDEAL CASE: Theoretically zero energy dissipation"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each node represents a concept in the KTU Module 2 syllabus hierarchy. The arrows show how the topic branches from the central theme of impact analysis down to the specific sub-cases you need to master for the exam. Trace the path for any given problem to identify which formula set applies.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Coefficient of Restitution. Mention its range. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Coefficient of Restitution (e)** is defined as the ratio of the velocity of separation to the velocity of approach, measured along the line of impact of two colliding bodies.

$$e = \frac{\text{Velocity of Separation}}{\text{Velocity of Approach}} = \frac{v_2 - v_1}{u_1 - u_2}$$

Its value ranges from **0 to 1** (i.e., $0 \le e \le 1$). When $e = 1$, the impact is perfectly elastic (no kinetic energy loss). When $e = 0$, the impact is perfectly inelastic (maximum kinetic energy loss, no rebound). For impacts with rebound, $0 < e < 1$.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between perfectly elastic and perfectly inelastic impact. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Perfectly Elastic Impact | Perfectly Inelastic Impact |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Coefficient of Restitution ($e$) | $e = 1$ | $e = 0$ |
| Kinetic Energy | Fully conserved | Maximum loss (not conserved) |
| Rebound | Bodies fully rebound and separate | Bodies move with common velocity (no rebound) |
| Equation | $v_1 = u_2$, $v_2 = u_1$ (for equal masses) | $v = \dfrac{m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2}{m_1 + m_2}$ |
| Real Example | Idealized billiard ball collision | Two cars locking together after crash |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Choose **ONE** of the two alternatives provided.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Direct Central Impact with Rebound

`(a)` A car of mass **1200 kg** moving at **15 m/s** collides with a stationary truck of mass **3000 kg**. After the impact, the car rebounds with a velocity of **3 m/s** in the opposite direction. Determine: **(7 marks)**
  1. The velocity of the truck immediately after impact.
  2. The coefficient of restitution $e$.
