`(b)` Using the values obtained in part (a), calculate: **(7 marks)**
  1. The impulse exerted on the car.
  2. The total loss of kinetic energy due to the impact.

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a):**

**Given:** $m_1 = 1200$ kg (car), $m_2 = 3000$ kg (truck), $u_1 = +15$ m/s, $u_2 = 0$, $v_1 = -3$ m/s (rebound, so negative).

**Step 1 — Apply Conservation of Momentum:**

$$m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2$$

$$1200 \times 15 + 3000 \times 0 = 1200 \times (-3) + 3000 \times v_2$$

$$18000 = -3600 + 3000\,v_2$$

$$3000\,v_2 = 18000 + 3600 = 21600$$

$$\boxed{v_2 = 7.2 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Coefficient of Restitution:**

$$e = \frac{v_2 - v_1}{u_1 - u_2} = \frac{7.2 - (-3)}{15 - 0} = \frac{10.2}{15}$$

$$\boxed{e = 0.68}$$

**Part (b):**

**Step 3 — Impulse on the Car:**

$$J_1 = m_1 (v_1 - u_1) = 1200 \times (-3 - 15) = 1200 \times (-18)$$

$$\boxed{J_1 = -21600 \text{ N·s} = -21.6 \text{ kN·s}}$$

The negative sign indicates the impulse is directed opposite to the initial motion of the car (acting backward on the car).

**Step 4 — Loss of Kinetic Energy:**

Initial KE: $T_i = \tfrac{1}{2}(1200)(15)^2 + 0 = 135000$ J = 135 kJ

Final KE: $T_f = \tfrac{1}{2}(1200)(-3)^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}(3000)(7.2)^2 = 5400 + 77760 = 83160$ J = 83.16 kJ

$$\Delta T = T_i - T_f = 135000 - 83160 = \boxed{51840 \text{ J} \approx 51.84 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Cross-verification using the standard formula:**

$$\Delta T = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2} \cdot (1 - e^2) \cdot (u_1 - u_2)^2$$

$$\Delta T = 0.5 \times \frac{1200 \times 3000}{4200} \times (1 - 0.68^2) \times (15)^2$$

$$\Delta T = 0.5 \times 857.143 \times (1 - 0.4624) \times 225$$

$$\Delta T = 0.5 \times 857.143 \times 0.5376 \times 225 = \boxed{51840 \text{ J}} \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Impact on a Fixed Plane & Energy Loss

`(a)` A steel ball of mass **0.5 kg** is dropped from a height of **3.2 m** onto a rigid horizontal steel plate. It rebounds to a height of **1.8 m**. Determine: **(7 marks)**
  1. The velocity of the ball just before impact and just after rebound.
  2. The coefficient of restitution between the ball and the plate.

`(b)` Calculate: **(7 marks)**
  1. The impulse imparted by the plate to the ball during impact.
  2. The percentage of the initial kinetic energy lost in the impact.

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Velocity Just Before Impact (downward direction is positive):**

Using kinematics: $v^2 = u^2 + 2gh$, with $u = 0$ (dropped from rest):

$$v_1^2 = 0 + 2 \times 9.81 \times 3.2 = 62.784$$

$$\boxed{v_1 = 7.924 \text{ m/s (downward)}}$$

**Step 2 — Velocity Just After Rebound (the ball travels back up to $h = 1.8$ m):**

Using $v^2 = u^2 - 2gh$ at the top of rebound (where final velocity is zero):

$$0 = v_2^2 - 2 \times 9.81 \times 1.8$$

$$v_2^2 = 35.316 \implies \boxed{v_2 = 5.943 \text{ m/s (upward, opposite to } v_1)}$$

**Step 3 — Coefficient of Restitution:**

The plate is fixed, so $u_{\text{plate}} = v_{\text{plate}} = 0$. The ball approaches with velocity $v_1$ downward and leaves with velocity $v_2$ upward.

Using the velocity-of-approach / velocity-of-separation framework (with the convention that approach is positive):

$$e = \frac{v_{\text{separation}}}{v_{\text{approach}}} = \frac{v_2}{v_1} = \frac{5.943}{7.924}$$

$$\boxed{e \approx 0.75}$$

**Part (b):**

**Step 4 — Impulse on the Ball:**

Taking downward as positive, the ball arrives with $v_1 = +7.924$ m/s and leaves with $v_2 = -5.943$ m/s.

$$J = m(v_2 - v_1) = 0.5 \times (-5.943 - 7.924) = 0.5 \times (-13.867)$$

$$\boxed{J = -6.9335 \text{ N·s}}$$

The negative sign indicates the impulse is directed **upward** (plate pushes ball up), which is physically correct.

**Step 5 — Percentage Loss of Kinetic Energy:**

Initial KE (just before impact): $T_i = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 0.5 \times (7.924)^2 = 15.7$ J

Final KE (just after rebound): $T_f = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 0.5 \times (5.943)^2 = 8.83$ J

Energy lost: $\Delta T = 15.7 - 8.83 = 6.87$ J

Percentage loss:

$$\%\text{Loss} = \frac{\Delta T}{T_i} \times 100 = \frac{6.87}{15.7} \times 100 \approx 43.75\%$$

**Cross-verification:** Since $T_f/T_i = e^2 = (0.75)^2 = 0.5625$, the fractional loss is $1 - 0.5625 = 0.4375 = 43.75\%$. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN THIS TOPIC:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder (–2 to –3 marks):** When a body rebounds, the velocity vector **reverses direction**. Always assign +ve to one direction (e.g., initial motion) and use negative sign for rebound. Forgetting this leads to wrong $e$ values.
>
> 2. **Confusing $v_1$ and $v_2$ in the Restitution Formula (–2 marks):** In $e = (v_2 - v_1)/(u_1 - u_2)$, the indices are body-1 and body-2 specific. Swapping them gives an incorrect $e$ (and $e$ can come out > 1 or < 0, which is unphysical).
>
> 3. **Applying Conservation of KE Instead of Momentum (–3 to –4 marks):** Kinetic energy is conserved **only** when $e = 1$. For all other cases (especially "impact with rebound" where $0 < e < 1$), students must use momentum conservation + the restitution equation. Mixing the two laws is a fatal error.
>
> 4. **Units and Significant Figures (–1 mark):** Final answers in m/s, N·s, and Joules (J). Express large energies in kJ. Keep 3 significant figures minimum.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the Wall/Fixed-Plane Special Case (–2 marks):** For impact on a fixed plane, the **impulse is NOT zero**, even though the wall's velocity is zero. Use $J = -mu(1+e)$, not $J = 0$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Types of Impacts & Impact with Rebound**

* ✅ **Two Universal Laws of Impact Analysis:**
  * **Law 1:** Linear momentum is **always conserved** (along the line of impact).
  * **Law 2:** Coefficient of restitution $e$ is **constant** for a given pair of materials (Newton's experimental law).

* ✅ **Coefficient of Restitution — Master Definition:**
  $e = \dfrac{\text{Velocity of Separation}}{\text{Velocity of Approach}}$ ; range $[0, 1]$.

* ✅ **Three Master Formulas You Must Memorize:**
  1. $v_1 = \dfrac{(m_1 - e m_2)u_1 + (1+e)m_2 u_2}{m_1 + m_2}$
  2. $v_2 = \dfrac{(1+e)m_1 u_1 + (m_2 - e m_1)u_2}{m_1 + m_2}$
  3. $\Delta T = \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot\dfrac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}\cdot(1-e^2)\cdot(u_1-u_2)^2$

* ✅ **Special Case: Fixed Wall Rebound** → $v = -e \cdot u$ ; Impulse $J = -m u(1+e)$.

* ✅ **Special Case: $e = 0$ (No Rebound)** → Common velocity $v = \dfrac{m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2}{m_1 + m_2}$.

* ✅ **Special Case: $e = 1$ (Perfectly Elastic)** → Velocities exchange for equal masses; KE conserved.

* ✅ **Energy is NOT conserved** in any impact with rebound (i.e., $e < 1$). Energy is dissipated as heat, sound, and permanent deformation.

* ✅ **Vehicle Safety Application:** Crumple zones are designed for low $e$ (high energy absorption); bumpers use moderate $e$ (partial rebound to prevent damage at low speeds).

* ✅ **Common Exam Traps:** Sign errors, wrong index in $e$ formula, mixing up momentum and energy equations, ignoring the rebound direction.

* ✅ **Quick Sanity Check:** For any impact with rebound problem, verify that the final velocities satisfy $e \in [0, 1]$. If $e > 1$ or $e < 0$, the sign convention is wrong.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Movable barrier tests
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT743 - VEHICLE SAFETY AND SECURITY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Movable barrier tests

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Movable Barrier (MB) Test** is a standardized full-scale crashworthiness evaluation procedure in which a propelled barrier (rigid or deformable) of prescribed mass, geometry, and impact surface characteristics is driven into a stationary target vehicle (or vice versa) to simulate vehicle-to-vehicle collision scenarios. The test is governed by global regulations such as **FMVSS 214 (Side Impact)**, **FMVSS 301 (Rear Impact Fuel System Integrity)**, **ECE R95 (Uniform Provisions Concerning the Protection of the Occupants of the Saloon)**, and the **Euro NCAP Adult Occupant Protection Protocol**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a **Moving Deformable Barrier (MDB)** consists of an aluminum honeycomb face backed by a rigid trolley of defined mass, engineered to replicate the kinetic-energy absorption behavior of a real passenger vehicle's front or rear end during a crash event.

The most common test variants encountered in syllabus contexts are:

1. **Side-Impact MDB Test** – simulates a vehicle being struck from the side by another car.
2. **Rear-Impact MDB Test** – simulates a vehicle being struck from behind.
3. **Oblique-Impact MDB Test** – evaluates angled crash loadings (used by IIHS and Euro NCAP).
4. **Pole Side-Impact Test** – a deformable pole strikes the side of the vehicle.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two bumper cars on an amusement-park ride. Instead of testing the bumper car's actual front structure, engineers build a **replica of the front of an average car** mounted on a heavy trolley, then ram it into the side of the test vehicle at a precisely controlled speed. The barrier's face is made of a **"metal sponge" (aluminum honeycomb)** that crushes the same way a real car's crumple zone would. By studying the dummy inside the struck car, engineers can tell whether the door, B-pillar, and airbags keep occupants safe — without ever needing to crash two real cars together.

> [!NOTE]
> The aluminum honeycomb is the **hero of the test**: it ensures repeatability, because real cars deform differently based on their design, but the honeycomb always behaves identically. This is why a single MDB test can fairly compare different car models.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bolded Key Constants)

- **MDB mass (Side Impact, ECE R95): 950 kg ± 20 kg**
- **Impact velocity (ECE R95): 50 km/h ± 1 km/h** (≈ 13.89 m/s)
- **MDB mass (FMVSS 301 Rear): 1814 kg**
- **Impact velocity (FMVSS 301): 80.5 km/h ± 1.6 km/h**
- **IIHS Side MDB mass: 1500 kg** (updated from 1361 kg)
- **IIHS Side MDB velocity: 60 km/h (32 mph)**
- **Test temperature: 20.5 °C ± 3.5 °C**
- **Honeycomb block specifications:** Crush strength of approximately **0.34 MPa to 1.7 MPa** depending on test variant.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Sequence of a Movable Barrier Test

The test is executed in a strict sequence to guarantee repeatability and validity:

- **Step 1: Vehicle Preparation**
  - Target vehicle is fitted with measurement instrumentation.
  - Anthropomorphic Test Devices (ATDs) — Hybrid-III 50th percentile male dummies in front and rear seats — are positioned and instrumented.
  - High-speed cameras, accelerometers, load cells, and pressure sensors are calibrated.

- **Step 2: Barrier Preparation**
  - The MDB is fitted with the prescribed aluminum honeycomb face block.
  - Honeycomb is preconditioned at 20.5 °C ± 3.5 °C for ≥ **4 hours** to standardize its energy-absorption curve.

- **Step 3: Test Track Alignment**
  - The barrier is accelerated by a hydraulic or pneumatic propelling system to the target velocity.
  - Laser-based speed gates confirm the impact velocity within tolerance.

- **Step 4: Impact Event**
  - The barrier strikes the target vehicle (or vice versa).
  - Data acquisition runs at ≥ **10 kHz** sampling rate.
  - Cameras capture at ≥ **1000 frames/second**.

- **Step 5: Post-Impact Analysis**
  - Dummy injury metrics are computed from sensor data.
  - Vehicle structural deformation is measured.
  - Comparisons are made with the regulatory injury reference values (IRVs).

### 2.2 Why the Honeycomb Face? — The Engineering Rationale

The aluminum honeycomb face replicates the **average stiffness of a production car's front structure**. A real car's front-end energy absorption depends on the specific model, but the honeycomb is **regulatorily fixed** so that every vehicle is tested against the same impact "enemy." This guarantees **fair comparison** across different vehicle types.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The honeycomb's load–deflection curve closely mimics the bilinear shape of a typical car crush zone: a **low initial force** (engine compartment collapses) followed by a **higher secondary force** (engine block resists), making the test both realistic and reproducible.

### 2.3 Injury Assessment Criteria

The dummies carry sensors that produce time-history data, from which injury metrics are calculated:

- **HIC (Head Injury Criterion)** – assesses skull and brain injury risk.
- **Thoracic Trauma Index (TTI)** – measures chest/rib injury risk.
- **Femur Load** – measures lower-extremity risk.
- **Pelvis Acceleration** – assesses hip injury risk.
- **Viscous Criterion (VC)** – soft-tissue injury indicator.
- **Lower Spine Index** – assesses lumbar injury.

> [!NOTE]
> In Euro NCAP, side-impact testing reports a **chest deflection** value (in mm) and **HIC** values; in FMVSS 214, the critical metrics are **TTI**, **Pelvic Force**, and **HIC**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Kinetic Energy of Barrier | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | J (Joules) | Quantifies total energy to be absorbed. |
| Equivalent Mean Deceleration | $\bar{a} = \tfrac{v^2}{2 d}$ | m/s² | Average decel over crush distance $d$. |
| HIC (Head Injury Criterion) | $\mathrm{HIC} = \max_{t_1,t_2}\left[ \tfrac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \left( \displaystyle\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt \right)^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1) \right]$ | dimensionless | Skull/brain injury risk. |
| Thoracic Trauma Index | $\mathrm{TTI} = \tfrac{g_{R} + g_{L}}{2}$ where $g_{R}, g_{L}$ are rib accelerations (g) | dimensionless | Chest injury risk. |
| Viscous Criterion | $\mathrm{VC}(t) = \tfrac{V(t)\cdot C(t)}{1.5}$ | dimensionless | Soft-tissue injury. |
| Equivalent Energy Speed (EES) | $\mathrm{EES} = \sqrt{\tfrac{2 E_k}{m_{ref}}}$ | m/s | Energy-speed equivalence. |
| Crush Distance | $d = \tfrac{m v^2}{2 F_{mean}}$ | m | Predicted deformation depth. |
| Pseudo-Work (Force-Deflection) | $W = \int F(\delta)\, d\delta$ | J | Energy absorbed by honeycomb. |
| Deceleration Ratio (R) | $R = \tfrac{a_{peak}}{\bar{a}}$ | dimensionless | Crash pulse severity indicator. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Isolation Rule:** When a formula contains absolute value, use $\vert x \vert$ instead of $|x|$ to avoid breaking the markdown table.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

Movable barrier tests are the **foundation of vehicle safety star ratings worldwide**. Euro NCAP, IIHS, ASEAN NCAP, and Global NCAP all rely on MDB-style protocols. The data generated directly drives:
- Vehicle platform redesign (B-pillar reinforcement, side-curtain airbag tuning).
- Insurance industry ratings.
- Regulatory compliance certification.
- Consumer awareness ("5-star safety" advertising).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Crushed Length vs. Barrier Mass and Velocity

The governing energy-balance equation for an inelastic moving-barrier impact, where the barrier of mass $m_b$ impacts a stationary target of mass $m_t$ at velocity $v_b$, is derived below.

**Step 1: Conservation of Linear Momentum**

At the moment of impact, momentum is conserved (assuming a fully plastic collision during primary engagement):

$$m_b \, v_b = (m_b + m_t)\, v_{co}$$

where $v_{co}$ is the common velocity immediately after impact.

**Step 2: Solve for the Common Velocity**

$$v_{co} = \frac{m_b \, v_b}{m_b + m_t}$$

**Step 3: Kinetic Energy Before Impact**

$$E_{k,\text{before}} = \tfrac{1}{2} m_b v_b^2$$

**Step 4: Kinetic Energy After Impact**

$$E_{k,\text{after}} = \tfrac{1}{2} (m_b + m_t)\, v_{co}^2$$

**Step 5: Energy Dissipated (Absorbed by Deformation)**

The deformation work $W$ equals the kinetic energy lost:

$$W = E_{k,\text{before}} - E_{k,\text{after}}$$

**Step 6: Substitute the Common Velocity**

$$W = \tfrac{1}{2} m_b v_b^2 - \tfrac{1}{2} (m_b + m_t)\left(\frac{m_b v_b}{m_b + m_t}\right)^2$$

**Step 7: Simplify the Square**

$$\left(\frac{m_b v_b}{m_b + m_t}\right)^2 = \frac{m_b^2 v_b^2}{(m_b + m_t)^2}$$

**Step 8: Multiply Through**

$$W = \tfrac{1}{2} m_b v_b^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}\frac{m_b^2 v_b^2}{m_b + m_t}$$

**Step 9: Factor Out the Common Term $\tfrac{1}{2} m_b v_b^2$**

$$W = \tfrac{1}{2} m_b v_b^2 \left[ 1 - \frac{m_b}{m_b + m_t} \right]$$

**Step 10: Simplify the Bracket**

$$1 - \frac{m_b}{m_b + m_t} = \frac{m_t}{m_b + m_t}$$

**Step 11: Final Compact Form**

$$\boxed{\, W = \tfrac{1}{2} m_b v_b^2 \cdot \frac{m_t}{m_b + m_t} \,}$$

This result is the **generalized Lumped-Parameter (LP) energy partition formula** for an inelastic MDB impact.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (ECE R95 Side Impact)

**Given Data:**
- Barrier mass $m_b = 950$ kg
- Target vehicle mass $m_t = 1500$ kg
- Impact velocity $v_b = 50 \text{ km/h} = 13.89$ m/s

**Step 12: Convert the Velocity**

$$v_b = \frac{50 \times 1000}{3600} = 13.89 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 13: Compute Initial Kinetic Energy**

$$E_{k,\text{before}} = \tfrac{1}{2}(950)(13.89)^2 = 91{,}653 \text{ J} \approx 91.65 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 14: Compute Common Velocity**

$$v_{co} = \frac{950 \times 13.89}{950 + 1500} = \frac{13{,}196}{2450} = 5.386 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 15: Compute Energy After Impact**

$$E_{k,\text{after}} = \tfrac{1}{2}(2450)(5.386)^2 = 35{,}533 \text{ J}$$

**Step 16: Energy Absorbed by Structure**

$$W = 91{,}653 - 35{,}533 = 56{,,120 \text{ J}} \approx 56.12 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 17: Equivalent Mean Deceleration (assuming effective crush distance $d \approx 0.45$ m)**

$$\bar{a} = \frac{v_b^2}{2 d} = \frac{(13.89)^2}{2 \times 0.45} = \frac{192.9}{0.9} = 214.3 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 21.86 \text{ g}$$

**Step 18: Peak Load Estimate (using peak-to-mean ratio $R = 1.8$ for honeycomb)**

$$F_{peak} = R \cdot \bar{a} \cdot m_{eff}$$

where $m_{eff} = \frac{m_b \cdot m_t}{m_b + m_t} = \frac{950 \cdot 1500}{2450} = 581.6$ kg.

$$F_{peak} = 1.8 \times 214.3 \times 581.6 \approx 224{,}317 \text{ N} \approx 224 \text{ kN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is consistent with the regulatory peak forces measured in the FMVSS 214 and ECE R95 side impact tests, validating the simplified LP model.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Test Variants Compared

| Parameter | ECE R95 | FMVSS 214 (old) | FMVSS 214 (new) | FMVSS 301 (Rear) | IIHS (2018+) | Euro NCAP |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Barrier Mass | **950 kg** | 1367 kg | 1814 kg | **1814 kg** | 1500 kg | 1400 kg |
| Velocity | 50 km/h | 53.1 km/h | 62 km/h | 80.5 km/h | 60 km/h | 60 km/h |
| Impact Angle | 90° | 90° | 90° | 0° (rear) | 90° | 90° |
| Deformable Face | Honeycomb | Honeycomb | Honeycomb | Rigid rear | Honeycomb | Honeycomb |
| Dummies | ES-2 / SID-IIs | SID | THOR / SID-IIs | ES-2 hybrid | THOR | WorldSID |
| Primary Injury Metric | HIC, Rib Defl. | TTI, PF, HIC | HIC, Rib Defl. | Fuel Leakage | HIC, Pelvis | HIC, Rib Defl. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MDB Test Architecture (Functional Flow Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hydraulic Propeller Unit] --> B[Accelerator Guide Rail]
    B --> C[Speed Gates 1 and 2]
    C --> D[Moving Deformable Barrier Trolley]
    D --> E[Aluminum Honeycomb Impact Face]
    E --> F[Target Vehicle Under Test]
    F --> G[ATD Dummy 1 - Front Driver]
    F --> H[ATD Dummy 2 - Rear Passenger]
    G --> I[Dummy Instrumentation Sensors]
    H --> I
    I --> J[High Speed Data Acquisition System]
    K[High Speed Cameras 1000 fps] --> J
    L[Temperature Conditioning Unit] --> E
    J --> M[Injury Metric Calculation Engine]
    M --> N[Report and Compliance Decision]
```

### 4.2 Honeycomb Energy-Absorption Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Impact Load Applied] --> B{Honeycomb Cell Orientation}
    B --> C[Cell Wall Bending]
    B --> D[Cell Wall Buckling]
    B --> E[Cell Wall Plastic Hinge Formation]
    C --> F[Energy Dissipated by Plastic Deformation]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Stable Plateau Force Maintained]
    G --> H[Densification Begins]
    H --> I[Sharp Force Rise - End of Energy Absorption]
```

### 4.3 Data Acquisition and Injury Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Accelerometer Channel 1] --> X[Analog to Digital Converter]
    A2[Accelerometer Channel 2] --> X
    A3[Load Cell Signal] --> X
    A4[Displacement LVDT Signal] --> X
    X --> Y[Digital Time History Data]
    Y --> Z1[HIC Computation Block]
    Y --> Z2[TTI Computation Block]
    Y --> Z3[Viscous Criterion Block]
    Y --> Z4[Femur Load Integration Block]
    Z1 --> Q[Final Injury Report]
    Z2 --> Q
    Z3 --> Q
    Z4 --> Q
```

### 4.4 Test Variants Decision Tree

```mermaid

    Start[Select Movable Barrier Test Type] --> Q1{Collision Direction?}

    S1 --> T4[Euro NCAP - 1400 kg at 60 km/h]
    R2 --> U1[FMVSS 301 - 1814 kg at 80.5 km/h]
    R3 --> V1[Euro NCAP ODB - 1400 kg at 64 km/h]

```

> [!NOTE]

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define a Moving Deformable Barrier (MDB) test. State the standard test mass and impact velocity as per ECE R95.**

**Model Answer:**
A Moving Deformable Barrier (MDB) test is a crashworthiness procedure in which a barrier of prescribed mass fitted with a deformable aluminum honeycomb face is propelled into a stationary vehicle to simulate a vehicle-to-vehicle side collision. As per **ECE R95**, the standard barrier mass is **950 kg ± 20 kg** and the impact velocity is **50 km/h ± 1 km/h**. The honeycomb face is engineered to replicate the energy-absorption characteristics of a typical passenger car front structure. The test is used to evaluate occupant protection by measuring dummy injury metrics such as HIC, TTI, and rib deflection. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is aluminum honeycomb used as the impact face of a Movable Deformable Barrier instead of a real car body?**

**Model Answer:**
Aluminum honeycomb is used because it provides a **standardized, repeatable energy-absorption profile** that is independent of any specific car model. A real vehicle's front structure varies in stiffness across manufacturers, which would make test-to-test comparisons unreliable. The honeycomb crushes in a controlled bilinear manner — a low initial force plateau followed by a higher densification phase — closely mimicking real crash behavior. This allows regulators (ECE, FMVSS, Euro NCAP, IIHS) to evaluate the **target vehicle's** side structure and occupant protection, while the **impact condition** remains identical for all vehicles. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the components of a Moving Deformable Barrier test setup, including the dummy types, instrumentation, and test preparation procedure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A typical MDB test setup consists of the following components:

1. **Barrier Trolley (950 kg for ECE R95):** A rigid steel trolley with a flat impact face onto which the deformable honeycomb is bolted. It carries ballast masses to achieve the required total mass.

2. **Aluminum Honeycomb Face Block:** A bolted-on deformable element with specified cell size, foil thickness, and crush strength. The honeycomb is preconditioned at 20.5 °C ± 3.5 °C for at least 4 hours before the test.

3. **Propulsion System:** A hydraulic or pneumatic winch accelerates the barrier to the prescribed impact velocity. The barrier releases on a guide rail and is timed by laser speed gates.

4. **Target Vehicle:** Fitted with regulated dummies in the driver and rear passenger seats. Driver-side uses **ES-2** or **WorldSID** dummies; rear uses **SID-IIs** (5th percentile female) in Euro NCAP.

5. **Instrumentation:**
   - Tri-axial accelerometers in dummy head, chest, pelvis, and femurs.
   - Load cells in femurs and upper neck.
   - Rib displacement potentiometers.
   - Vehicle accelerometers at B-pillar, sill, and floorpan.
   - High-speed cameras at 1000+ fps.

6. **Data Acquisition System:** Samples at ≥10 kHz with anti-alias filtering; records dummy and vehicle channels simultaneously.

7. **Test Procedure:**
   - Vehicle is preconditioned, weighed, photographed.
   - Honeycomb is conditioned thermally.
   - Dummies are instrumented and seated per H-point machine.
   - Barrier is accelerated and released.
   - Post-impact, injury metrics (HIC, TTI, VC, rib deflection, femur load) are computed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a sketch, describe the energy-absorption mechanism of the aluminum honeycomb face during a side-impact MDB test. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The aluminum honeycomb face absorbs crash energy through three sequential deformation mechanisms, observable in its load–deflection curve:

1. **Initial Elastic Region (Region I):** The cell walls bend elastically. Force rises steeply with small deflection. Energy stored reversibly is minimal.

2. **Plateau Region (Region II):** As load exceeds the elastic limit, cell walls **buckle plastically** and form plastic hinges, causing a near-constant plateau force. This is where the bulk of crash energy is dissipated. The plateau force is dictated by the cell wall thickness, foil alloy, and cell size, and is regulatorily specified.

3. **Densification Region (Region III):** When the cells are fully crushed together, the material behaves like a solid block. The force rises sharply with little further deformation. This region is **avoided** in a well-designed test because it transfers excessive load to the target vehicle.

The plateau force $F_p$ for a regular hexagonal honeycomb is given by:

$$\frac{F_p}{\sigma_{ys}} = C \left( \frac{t}{l} \right)^{5/3}$$

where $\sigma_{ys}$ is the yield stress, $t$ is the foil thickness, $l$ is the cell size, and $C$ is a geometry constant. The total energy absorbed per unit volume is approximately $F_p \cdot \varepsilon_D$, where $\varepsilon_D$ is the densification strain (typically 0.5–0.8).

A typical load-deflection curve shows the **three-region shape**: rise → plateau → densification, with the test designed to terminate in the plateau region for an idealized crash pulse. **(7 marks)**

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Compare the ECE R95 and FMVSS 214 side-impact MDB test procedures. Highlight the key differences in barrier mass, velocity, dummy, and injury criteria. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | ECE R95 | FMVSS 214 (New) |
|---|---|---|
| Barrier Mass | 950 kg | 1814 kg |
| Impact Velocity | 50 km/h | 62 km/h |
| Barrier Face | Deformable (honeycomb) | Deformable (honeycomb) |
| Driver Dummy | ES-2 | THOR (advanced) |
| Rear Dummy | SID-IIs | SID-IIs |
| Primary Injury Metrics | HIC, Rib Deflection, Femur Force | HIC, Rib Deflection, Pelvic Force, TTI |
| Effective Test Year | 1995+ | 2018+ (US) |

**Key Differences:**

1. **Higher Mass Barrier in FMVSS 214:** The US regulation uses a heavier barrier (1814 kg vs 950 kg) to better represent the modern heavier fleet, which is more conservative for older platforms.

2. **Higher Velocity:** FMVSS 214's 62 km/h vs ECE R95's 50 km/h increases the total crash energy, raising the test severity.

3. **THOR Dummy:** The newer THOR dummy used by FMVSS 214 has biofidelity advantages over the ES-2, especially in chest and shoulder injury assessment.

4. **Injury Criteria:** FMVSS 214 limits are stricter on the pelvis, reflecting the modern understanding of pelvic injuries in side crashes.

5. **Global Harmonization:** Euro NCAP and IIHS have also adopted heavier barriers (1400–1500 kg) to converge on a more realistic global standard. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 950 kg MDB strikes a stationary 1500 kg vehicle at 50 km/h in a fully inelastic side impact. Calculate: (i) the common velocity after impact, (ii) the kinetic energy lost in the deformation, and (iii) the equivalent mean deceleration assuming a 0.45 m crush distance for the target vehicle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $m_b = 950$ kg, $m_t = 1500$ kg, $v_b = 50 \text{ km/h} = 13.89$ m/s.

**(i) Common Velocity (Conservation of Momentum):**

$$v_{co} = \frac{m_b v_b}{m_b + m_t} = \frac{950 \times 13.89}{2450} = \frac{13{,}196}{2450} = 5.386 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Kinetic Energy Lost:**

$$E_{k,\text{before}} = \tfrac{1}{2} m_b v_b^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} (950)(13.89)^2 = 91{,}653 \text{ J}$$

$$E_{k,\text{after}} = \tfrac{1}{2} (m_b + m_t) v_{co}^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} (2450)(5.386)^2 = 35{,}533 \text{ J}$$

$$W = E_{k,\text{before}} - E_{k,\text{after}} = 91{,}653 - 35{,}533 = 56{,}120 \text{ J} \approx 56.12 \text{ kJ}$$

**(iii) Equivalent Mean Deceleration:**

$$\bar{a} = \frac{v_b^2}{2 d} = \frac{(13.89)^2}{2 \times 0.45} = \frac{192.9}{0.9} = 214.3 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 21.86 \text{ g}$$

**Final Results:**
- $v_{co} = 5.39$ m/s
- $W = 56.12$ kJ
- $\bar{a} \approx 21.9 \text{ g}$ **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion trap:** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s is the single most common mark-losing error. Always show $v = \frac{V_{km/h} \times 1000}{3600}$.
> 2. **Mistaking barrier mass for total system mass:** Several students incorrectly set $m_b + m_t$ as a single mass in the KE-before term. The energy balance must distinguish between pre- and post-impact KE.
> 3. **Using $g$ vs $g_{c}$:** When expressing accelerations in $g$, use $1g = 9.81$ m/s². Don't mix SI and gravimetric units in the same equation.
> 4. **HIC formula sign errors:** HIC is calculated over a sliding time window; writing it without the max operator loses one full mark.
> 5. **Honeycomb terminology:** Don't call the impact face "cardboard" or "plastic foam" — write "aluminum honeycomb" specifically.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Movable Barrier Test** = a propelled barrier strikes a stationary target vehicle to simulate vehicle-to-vehicle crash scenarios.
- **ECE R95 standard parameters:** 950 kg barrier at 50 km/h side impact.
- **FMVSS 301 rear impact:** 1814 kg at 80.5 km/h.
- **IIHS modern test:** 1500 kg at 60 km/h; uses THOR dummy.
- **Honeycomb face** provides reproducible, fair impact conditions.
- **Energy balance** for inelastic MDB impact: $W = \tfrac{1}{2} m_b v_b^2 \cdot \tfrac{m_t}{m_b + m_t}$.
- **Common velocity:** $v_{co} = \tfrac{m_b v_b}{m_b + m_t}$.
- **Mean deceleration:** $\bar{a} = \tfrac{v^2}{2d}$.
- **Dummy injury metrics:** HIC, TTI, rib deflection, femur load, pelvis acceleration, viscous criterion.
- **Honeycomb regions:** Elastic → Plateau → Densification.
- **ATD dummies used:** ES-2, SID-IIs, THOR, WorldSID.
- **Sampling rates:** ≥10 kHz data, ≥1000 fps cameras.
- **Pre-conditioning:** Honeycomb held at 20.5 °C ± 3.5 °C for ≥4 hours.
- **Memorize the formula table** — it is a high-yield KTU question topic.
- **Distinguish MDB (deformable)** from a **rigid moving barrier** used in some older rear-impact tests.
- **Euro NCAP** uses 1400 kg barriers and WorldSID dummies; **IIHS** uses 1500 kg and THOR.
- Always write the **units** and **velocity conversion** step explicitly in numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Roll over crash tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT743 - VEHICLE SAFETY AND SECURITY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Roll over crash tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Rollover Crash Test** is a standardized passive-safety evaluation procedure in which a complete production or prototype vehicle is subjected to a controlled quasi-static or dynamic loading regime that simulates a real-world tip-over or trip-over event, in order to verify the structural integrity of the occupant survival space, the strength of the roof superstructure, the retention of glazing, and the efficacy of restraint systems (seat belts, airbags, and rollover curtains) when the vehicle departs from its normal upright attitude.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-2 Highlight:** Rollover tests are categorised into (a) **Static Roof-Crush Tests** (e.g., FMVSS 216, IIHS Roof Strength), (b) **Tilt-Table / Quasi-Static Tip-Over Tests** (e.g., UN ECE R66, ECE R29), and (c) **Dynamic Rollover Tests** (e.g., FMVSS 208 dolly rollover, ARL free-rolling tip-up). Each test addresses a different aspect of rollover protection.

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The “Empty Cardboard Box” Test**
> Imagine stacking five heavy textbooks on a soft cardboard box and slowly sliding it off the edge of a table. The box will either **crush downward** (roof crush) or **tumble and split open** (superstructure collapse). A rollover crash test is the automotive version of this experiment: instead of a cardboard box, a 1.5–2.5 tonne vehicle is loaded in a controlled way until its roof buckles or its superstructure intrudes into the passenger cabin. Engineers measure two things — (i) *how much force* the roof resists before crushing by 127 mm, and (ii) *whether the passenger survival space* (an imaginary bubble around the occupants) remains unviolated.

### Key Standardised Metrics

- **Test Loading Platen Dimensions:** **300 mm × 300 mm** rigid steel platen (FMVSS 216).
- **Standard Crush Limit:** **127 mm (5 inches)** of vertical roof deformation.
- **Tilt-Table Reference Angles:** **20° to 50°** from horizontal, depending on UN ECE R66 vehicle class.
- **Static Stability Factor (SSF) Threshold:** **SSF ≥ 1.20** for low rollover risk classification.
- **Test Mass Basis:** **Kerb Mass (M_kerb)** plus **75 kg dummy** per seating position (UN ECE R66 / R29 convention).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Rollover Tests Are Necessary

Rollover crashes represent only **~3 % of all passenger-vehicle crashes** in India and the OECD, yet they account for **~20–30 % of all occupant fatalities** in single-vehicle crashes. The reasons are:
1. **High vertical deceleration** through roof impact (occupants' heads strike the inner roof panel).
2. **Ejection risk** if door latches fail or glazing is lost.
3. **Multiple impacts** (a rollover typically generates 2–4 quarter-turns, each producing an impact).
4. **High fatality per crash** — the deadliest crash mode per event.

### 2.2 Taxonomy of Rollover Tests

| S.No. | Test Type | Standard | Primary Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Static Roof Crush | **FMVSS 216** / AIS-029 (India) | Crush force vs. deformation curve; **F ≥ 1.5 × W (≤ 2722 kg)** or **3.0 × W (> 2722 kg)** at 127 mm |
| 2 | Quasi-Static Tilt-Table | **UN ECE R66** (buses) / **R29** (truck cabs) | Tipping angle & residual survival space |
| 3 | Dynamic Dollying (Sideways Trip) | **FMVSS 208 § S10** / Euro NCAP | Occupant Injury Criteria (HIC, NIC, ThPC) |
| 4 | ARL Free-Tip (Curbside Trip-Over) | Research / NHTSA ARL | Roof intrusion, dummy kinematics |
| 5 | IIHS Roof Strength | **IIHS Good/Acceptable/Marginal/Poor** | Strength-to-Weight Ratio (SWR) |
| 6 | Dynamic Rollover on Soil | NHTSA Research | Real-world trip-over simulation |

### 2.3 Physics of Static Roof Crush (FMVSS 216)

The test plate is positioned **254 mm** forward of the rearmost point of the windshield header, inclined **5°** rearward from vertical, and pressed downward at **≤ 13 mm/s**. Force is measured continuously. The two-pass criterion is:

$$
F_{\text{crush}} \; \geq \; 1.5 \cdot M_{\text{kerb}} \cdot g \quad \text{for } M_{\text{kerb}} \leq 2722 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
F_{\text{crush}} \; \geq \; 3.0 \cdot M_{\text{kerb}} \cdot g \quad \text{for } M_{\text{kerb}} > 2722 \text{ kg}
$$

### 2.4 Physics of Quasi-Static Tilt-Table (UN ECE R66)

The vehicle is placed on a **rigid tilting platform** with the centre of gravity (CG) vertically above the platform's pivot axis. The platform rotates slowly (≤ 5°/s) until the desired test angle **α** is reached (or until the vehicle would tip). For class I and II buses, **α = 20°** minimum; for class III, it is **higher**.

The gravitational potential energy gained by the CG that must be **dissipated** by the superstructure during a potential rollover is:

$$
\Delta E \;=\; M \cdot g \cdot \Delta h_{CG}
$$

where **Δh_CG** is the vertical rise of the centre of gravity from the horizontal rest position to the on-the-edge position over the lower-side rail.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All symbols use **SI units** unless otherwise noted. Absolute values are typeset with `\vert` to preserve Markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Tipping Angle (Static)** | $\alpha_{\text{tip}} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{T}{2H}\right)$ | When track **T** and CG height **H** are known |
| 2 | **Static Stability Factor (SSF)** | $SSF = \dfrac{T}{2H}$ | NHTSA rollover risk rating |
| 3 | **Roof Crush Force (Light Vehicle)** | $F_{c} = 1.5 \, M_{k} \, g$ | FMVSS 216 limit, $M_k \leq 2722$ kg |
| 4 | **Roof Crush Force (Heavy Vehicle)** | $F_{c} = 3.0 \, M_{k} \, g$ | FMVSS 216 limit, $M_k > 2722$ kg |
| 5 | **IIHS SWR** | $SWR = \dfrac{F_{\text{peak}}}{M_{k} \, g}$ | IIHS Good ≥ 4.0, Acceptable ≥ 3.25 |
| 6 | **Tilt-Table Energy** | $\Delta E = M \, g \, H \, (1 - \cos\alpha)$ | UN ECE R66 collapse-energy estimate |
| 7 | **Required Crush Limit** | $\delta_{\max} = 127 \text{ mm} \;(5 \text{ in})$ | FMVSS 216 stopping criterion |
| 8 | **Height of CG from H-Point** | $H_{CG} = H_{\text{H-point}} + \Delta h$ | Inertia-measurement by tilt method |
| 9 | **Pendulum CG Inversion** | $T_{\text{osc}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{M g a}}$ | Compound pendulum test |
| 10 | **Trip-Over Induced Roll** | $\omega_{\text{roll}} = \dfrac{v_{y}}{R_{\text{trip}}}$ | Lateral velocity / trip-arm kinematics |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Body-Structure Design:** Higher yield-strength boron-steel A-pillars, B-pillars, and roof rails are sized to satisfy the 1.5×W rule.
- **Active Safety Calibration:** Electronic Stability Control (ESC) thresholds (LTR – Load Transfer Ratio) are tuned using **SSF** values.
- **Insurance & NCAP:** IIHS Good rating in roof strength lowers insurance premiums and improves consumer trust.
- **Bus & Coach Certification:** Mandatory UN ECE R66 certification is required for type approval of all new inter-city and tourist buses in Europe, India (AIS-052), and ASEAN.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Tipping Angle α_tip

Consider a rigid vehicle of mass **M** with its centre of gravity (CG) at height **H** above the ground, and a track width **T** (wheel-to-wheel lateral distance). The vehicle is quasi-statically tilted about the lower-side wheel-ground contact point.

> **Step 1 — Free Body Setup**
> At a tilt angle **α** from horizontal, the gravitational force **W = M g** continues to act vertically downward through the CG. The reaction force at the upper-side wheel becomes zero exactly at the tipping point, leaving only the **lower-side wheel pivot** as the support.

> **Step 2 — Moment Equilibrium about the lower wheel**
> Let the CG lie on the line connecting the centre of the lower wheel to the centre of the vehicle (i.e., centred laterally). When tilted by **α**, the moment arm of **W** about the lower-wheel contact point is:
> - **Perpendicular horizontal distance** = $(T/2)\cos\alpha - H\sin\alpha$ → this must equal **zero at tipping** (the CG is directly above the pivot).

> **Step 3 — Setting the Condition**
> Equating the two opposing moment contributions to zero:

$$
\left(\frac{T}{2}\right)\cos\alpha_{\text{tip}} \;-\; H \sin\alpha_{\text{tip}} \;=\; 0
$$

> **Step 4 — Solving for α_tip**
> Rearrange by dividing through by $\cos\alpha_{\text{tip}}$ (which is non-zero for finite angles):

$$
\frac{T}{2} \;=\; H \tan\alpha_{\text{tip}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha_{\text{tip}} \;=\; \arctan\!\left(\frac{T}{2H}\right)\;}
$$

> **Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation**
> The Static Stability Factor emerges directly as the ratio **T / (2H)**, which is **tan(α_tip)**. A wider track and lower CG both increase the SSF and reduce rollover propensity.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Roof-Crush Force Requirement (FMVSS 216)

> **Step 1 — Define the curb mass and gravitational force**
> $W = M_{\text{kerb}} \cdot g$. For a typical 1500 kg passenger car, $W = 1500 \times 9.81 = 14{,}715$ N (≈ 14.7 kN).

> **Step 2 — Apply the 1.5×W multiplier (light-vehicle branch)**
> $F_{c} = 1.5 \, M_{kerb} \, g$

> **Step 3 — Numerical Illustration**
> For $M_{\text{kerb}} = 1500$ kg:

$$
F_{c} = 1.5 \times 1500 \times 9.81 = 22{,}072.5 \text{ N} \approx 22.07 \text{ kN}
$$

> **Step 4 — Interpretation**
> The vehicle roof must therefore resist at least **22.07 kN** of vertical platen force at the prescribed platen location before allowing **127 mm** of crush. This is the *minimum* structural strength — better designs achieve 30–40 kN.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Quasi-Static Tilt-Table Energy (UN ECE R66)

> **Step 1 — Initial CG Position**
> At rest, the CG is at height **H** above the tilting platform's pivot axis (when the platform is horizontal).

> **Step 2 — Tilted CG Position**
> When the platform is inclined by angle **α**, the CG rotates about the pivot. Its new height **H′** is:
> $H' = H \cos\alpha + (T/2) \sin\alpha$
> For typical R66 setups where $T/2 \ll H$, we approximate $H' \approx H \cos\alpha$.

> **Step 3 — Vertical Rise of the CG**
> $\Delta h_{CG} = H' - H = H(\cos\alpha - 1) = -H(1 - \cos\alpha)$
> The magnitude of the rise is $H(1 - \cos\alpha)$.

> **Step 4 — Gravitational Potential Energy Gained**
> $\Delta E = M \cdot g \cdot H (1 - \cos\alpha)$

> **Step 5 — Numerical Illustration for a 12-tonne coach, α = 20°, H = 1.2 m**

$$
\Delta E = 12{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 1.2 \times (1 - \cos 20°)
$$

$$
\cos 20° = 0.9397 \;\Rightarrow\; 1 - 0.9397 = 0.0603
$$

$$
\Delta E = 12{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 1.2 \times 0.0603 \approx 8517.4 \text{ J} \approx 8.52 \text{ kJ}
$$

> **Step 6 — Interpretation**
> The superstructure must absorb **at least 8.52 kJ** of collapse energy while preventing any intrusion into the prescribed survival volume.

### 3.4 Load-Transfer Ratio (LTR) Derivation (Dynamic Rollover)

> **Step 1 — Define the wheel loads**
> Left wheel vertical load $W_L$ and right wheel vertical load $W_R$. The total weight is $W = W_L + W_R$.

> **Step 2 — Define LTR**
> $LTR = \dfrac{\vert W_L - W_R \vert}{W_L + W_R}$

> **Step 3 — Rollover Threshold**
> Rollover is imminent when **LTR → 1.0** (all load on the outer wheel). ESC intervenes at **LTR ≈ 0.6–0.8**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Rollover Test Programme

```mermaid

    A[vehicleUnderTest] --> B[preTestInspection]
    B --> C[massAndCGMeasurement]
    C --> D{testTypeSelection}
    D --> D1[FMVSS216RoofCrush]
    D --> D2[ECER66TiltTable]
    D --> D3[DollyRolloverFMVSS208]
    D1 --> E1[applyPlatenLoad5deg]
    E1 --> F1[recordForcevsCrush]
    F1 --> G1{Fc_ge_15W}
    G1 -- yes --> H1[pass]
    G1 -- no  --> H2[fail_redesignA_pillar]
    D2 --> E2[tiltToAngleAlpha]
    E2 --> F2[checkSurvivalSpace]
    F2 --> G2{intrusions_present}
    G2 -- no  --> H1
    G2 -- yes --> H2
    D3 --> E3[dollyPropulsion]
    E3 --> F3[freeRollOntoSoil]
    F3 --> G3[HIC_NIC_ThPC_check]
    G3 -- withinLimits --> H1
    G3 -- exceedLimits --> H2

```

```mermaid

    A[platformPivotAxis] --> B[lowerSideWheelContact]
    B --> C[CGatHeightH]
```
