```mermaid
    F --> G[tippingConditionTGTHtanAlpha]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] *Define the term "Static Roof Crush Test" as per FMVSS 216. State the pass-criterion force at 127 mm of crush for a 1500 kg vehicle.*

**Model Answer:**
A Static Roof Crush Test (FMVSS 216) is a quasi-static procedure in which a rigid steel platen (300 mm × 300 mm) applies a downward force on the driver's-side roof, positioned 254 mm forward of the rear edge of the windshield header and inclined 5° rearward from vertical, at a rate not exceeding 13 mm/s. The test stops at **127 mm (5 in)** of vertical roof deformation.
For a vehicle of kerb mass 1500 kg (which is ≤ 2722 kg), the required minimum force is **1.5 × M_kerb × g = 1.5 × 1500 × 9.81 = 22,072.5 N ≈ 22.07 kN**.

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] *What is the Static Stability Factor (SSF)? Explain its significance in vehicle rollover propensity.*

**Model Answer:**
The Static Stability Factor is defined as $SSF = T / (2H)$, where **T** is the track width and **H** is the height of the centre of gravity. It is numerically equal to **tan(α_tip)**, the tangent of the static tipping angle. A higher SSF indicates greater resistance to rollover. NHTSA classifies vehicles with **SSF ≥ 1.20** as having a low rollover risk, while those with **SSF < 1.03** are classified as high risk. SSF also informs the calibration of Electronic Stability Control (ESC) intervention thresholds.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Adapted]

**(a)** With the aid of a free-body diagram, derive the expression for the static tipping angle **α_tip** of a four-wheeled vehicle of track width **T** and centre-of-gravity height **H**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 12,000 kg tourist coach has a track width of 2.1 m and a CG height of 1.4 m. It is subjected to a UN ECE R66 quasi-static tilt-table test at **α = 28°**.
    (i) Calculate the static tipping angle. *(2 marks)*
    (ii) Calculate the gravitational potential energy gained by the CG at α = 28°. *(3 marks)*
    (iii) State the pass/fail condition for the survival space. *(2 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Derivation (refer to §3.1 of these notes):
- Draw the FBD: vehicle tilted by angle **α** about the lower-side wheel pivot.
- Sum moments about lower wheel: $(T/2)\cos\alpha - H\sin\alpha = 0$.
- Solve: $\tan\alpha_{\text{tip}} = T/(2H)$, so $\alpha_{\text{tip}} = \arctan(T/2H)$.

**(b) (i) Static tipping angle:**

$$
\alpha_{\text{tip}} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{T}{2H}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{2.1}{2 \times 1.4}\right) = \arctan(0.75) \approx 36.87°
$$

**(b) (ii) Energy gained by the CG at α = 28°:**

$$
\Delta E = M \, g \, H \, (1 - \cos 28°) = 12{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 1.4 \times (1 - 0.8829)
$$

$$
\Delta E = 12{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 1.4 \times 0.1171 \approx 19{,}300.6 \text{ J} \approx 19.3 \text{ kJ}
$$

**(b) (iii) Pass Condition:**
The superstructure passes if, at the test angle, **no part of the deformed superstructure intrudes into the prescribed survival space** and **all emergency exits remain functional**. Since 28° < 36.87°, the test condition is met angle-wise, but the actual pass depends on physical inspection for intrusions.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Adapted]

**(a)** Explain in detail the FMVSS 216 roof crush test procedure. State the two pass-criterion limits (1.5×W and 3.0×W) with the mass threshold. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An SUV of kerb mass 2400 kg and CG height 0.85 m with a track width of 1.65 m is evaluated for rollover propensity.
    (i) Calculate the SSF. *(2 marks)*
    (ii) Calculate the minimum required roof crush force per FMVSS 216. *(2 marks)*
    (iii) If the IIHS measured peak roof crush force is 95 kN, calculate the Strength-to-Weight Ratio (SWR) and rate it (Good / Acceptable / Marginal / Poor). *(3 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to §2.3 of these notes. Key points: platen 300×300 mm, 5° rearward from vertical, 254 mm forward of windshield header, ≤ 13 mm/s loading rate, stops at 127 mm. Pass criterion: $F_c \geq 1.5\,M_k g$ for $M_k \leq 2722$ kg; $F_c \geq 3.0\,M_k g$ for $M_k > 2722$ kg.

**(b) (i) SSF:**

$$
SSF = \frac{T}{2H} = \frac{1.65}{2 \times 0.85} = \frac{1.65}{1.70} \approx 0.971
$$

This SUV has a **moderate-to-high rollover propensity**.

**(b) (ii) Roof crush force (M_k = 2400 kg ≤ 2722 kg, so 1.5×W applies):**

$$
F_c = 1.5 \times 2400 \times 9.81 = 35{,}316 \text{ N} \approx 35.32 \text{ kN}
$$

**(b) (iii) IIHS SWR:**

$$
SWR = \frac{F_{\text{peak}}}{M_k \, g} = \frac{95{,}000}{2400 \times 9.81} = \frac{95{,}000}{23{,}544} \approx 4.035
$$

Since **SWR ≥ 4.0**, the rating is **GOOD** per IIHS standards.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Zones**
> 1. **Units omission (–1 mark):** Always carry units in every intermediate step of energy/force calculations.
> 2. **Confusing 1.5×W vs 3.0×W threshold:** The 2722 kg cutoff is **MANDATORY** — wrong branch = full mark loss.
> 3. **Forgetting 5° platen angle:** Many students describe the test as purely vertical. Always state the **5° rearward** inclination.
> 4. **SSF = T/(2H), not T/H:** A frequent slip-up costing 1 mark.
> 5. **Tipping condition sign error:** The tipping equation is $(T/2)\cos\alpha = H\sin\alpha$, *not* the reverse.
> 6. **Energy equation for tilt test:** Use $H(1 - \cos\alpha)$, not $H\sin\alpha$ — they are not equivalent.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Three test families:** Static (FMVSS 216), Quasi-Static (UN ECE R66 / R29), Dynamic (FMVSS 208 dolly, ARL soil trip).
- [ ] **FMVSS 216 forces:** **1.5×W (≤ 2722 kg)** and **3.0×W (> 2722 kg)** at **127 mm** crush.
- [ ] **Test plate location:** **254 mm forward** of the rear edge of windshield header, **5°** rearward from vertical, platen **300×300 mm**.
- [ ] **SSF formula:** $SSF = T / (2H)$; higher SSF → lower rollover risk; NHTSA threshold = **1.20**.
- [ ] **Tipping angle:** $\alpha_{\text{tip}} = \arctan(T/2H)$ — derived from moment equilibrium.
- [ ] **ECE R66 energy:** $\Delta E = M g H (1 - \cos\alpha)$ for tilt-table collapse energy.
- [ ] **IIHS rating:** SWR = $F_{\text{peak}} / (M_k g)$; Good ≥ 4.0, Acceptable ≥ 3.25, Marginal ≥ 2.5, Poor < 2.5.
- [ ] **ESC intervention:** Active at **LTR ≈ 0.6–0.8** to prevent full LTR = 1.0 rollover.
- [ ] **Survival Space (ECE R66):** An imaginary volume around each seating position that must remain unviolated by intruding structure.
- [ ] **Kerb Mass Convention:** Vehicle kerb weight + 75 kg per dummy occupant (ECE R66/R29).
- [ ] **A-Pillar / B-Pillar Role:** Primary load path in roof crush — designed in **boron steel** (hot-stamped, ~1500 MPa UTS) for modern SUVs and M1 category vehicles.
- [ ] **Rollover Curtain Airbags:** Deploy within **~25 ms** of rollover onset to retain occupant head and prevent ejection.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Behaviour of body structures in crash testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT743 - VEHICLE SAFETY AND SECURITY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Behaviour of body structures in crash testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: BEHAVIOUR OF BODY STRUCTURES IN CRASH TESTING

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crash Testing (KTU 2024 Definition):** Crash testing is a form of destructive testing usually performed to ensure that the designed structure of a vehicle complies with regulated safety standards. During a crash test, the vehicle, or a specific component of it, is subjected to controlled impact forces under carefully simulated real-world accident conditions, and the resulting deformation, acceleration, and intrusion are measured to evaluate occupant survivability.

The **body-in-white (BIW)** is the stage in automotive manufacturing where the sheet metal components have been welded together but before painting and assembly of the moving parts (doors, hood, fenders), and before the engine, chassis sub-assemblies, or powertrain have been integrated into the structure. The structural performance of the BIW during a crash determines the survival space available to the occupants.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are walking down a hallway and accidentally collide with a brick wall. The brick wall does not absorb any energy — your body takes the full impact, and you will likely be injured. Now imagine you are walking down the same hallway and collide with a stack of empty cardboard boxes. The boxes collapse progressively, dissipating the energy of impact, and you barely feel a thing.

> [!NOTE]
> **The car's body structure is exactly this stack of cardboard boxes.** The front of the vehicle is engineered to crush progressively like the boxes, absorbing the kinetic energy of the crash, while the passenger compartment remains rigid and undeformed like a protective cocoon around the occupants.

### Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Peak Deceleration Limit:** Approximately **20 g – 40 g** is the survivable threshold for a belted occupant; modern restraint systems aim to keep peak accelerations below this value.
- **Crash Pulse Duration:** Typically **60 ms – 120 ms** for a frontal rigid barrier impact at 56 km/h.
- **Energy to be Managed in a 56 km/h Frontal Crash:** For a 1500 kg vehicle,
  $$ E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times \left(\frac{56}{3.6}\right)^2 \approx 181{,}481 \text{ J} \approx 181 \text{ kJ} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Energy Budget Rule:** All of this **181 kJ** must be dissipated through controlled structural deformation (plastic folding), restraint systems (seatbelts, airbags), and friction. The body's job is to spread this energy absorption over time to keep peak forces below the human tolerance threshold (the Wayne State Tolerance Curve).

---

## 1.2 Anatomy of a Modern Passenger Car Body Structure

A modern monocoque (unibody) vehicle is divided into three distinct structural zones, each with a specific design philosophy:

| Structural Zone | Location | Design Philosophy | Expected Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Front Crumple Zone** | Forward of firewall (0 – 800 mm from front bumper) | Soft, progressive crushing | **Controlled plastic collapse** to absorb kinetic energy |
| **Passenger Cell (Safety Cage)** | Occupant compartment | Maximally rigid | **Elastic deformation only**; must not deform > **75 mm** intrude into occupant space |
| **Rear Crumple Zone** | Behind rear axle | Moderate progressive crushing | Energy absorption in rear impacts |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Key Concept:** A common misconception among students is that a "stronger" car is a "safer" car. In reality, a car whose front end does not crumple will transmit peak deceleration spikes of over **100 g** to the occupants, causing severe internal injuries. The crumple zone is intentionally sacrificial.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2.1 Deep Theoretical Analysis: How Body Structures Behave in a Crash

When a vehicle impacts a barrier or another vehicle, the body structure undergoes a complex sequence of deformations governed by the laws of **conservation of momentum** and **energy dissipation** through **plastic deformation**.

### Phase-by-Phase Crash Event (Frontal Rigid Barrier Impact)

The crash event is divided into four chronological phases from the vehicle's perspective:

1. **Phase 1 — Free Flight (0 ms – 5 ms):** The vehicle is moving at impact velocity, but no structural contact has yet occurred. The occupant continues to move at the original velocity (Newton's First Law).

2. **Phase 2 — Primary Engagement (5 ms – 20 ms):** The bumper beam, bumper absorber (foam/EPP), and front rail tips make initial contact with the barrier. Bumper system absorbs the first **15 kJ – 30 kJ** of energy through elastic-plastic deformation of the crush can (the round aluminum/steel tubes bolted to the front rail ends).

3. **Phase 3 — Crumple Zone Collapse (20 ms – 80 ms):** This is the critical energy-absorbing phase. The front rails buckle in a controlled accordion-like folding pattern, often called **progressive buckling**. Each fold absorbs energy through plastic hinge formation in the sheet metal.

4. **Phase 4 — Passenger Cell Loading (80 ms – 120 ms):** The crash pulse reaches the firewall. At this stage, the structure must resist further intrusion. The A-pillars, B-pillars, rocker panels, and cross members are designed to remain elastic.

### Energy Dissipation Mechanisms in Sheet Metal

When a thin-walled steel or aluminum tube (representative of a front rail) is crushed axially, energy is absorbed through two dominant mechanisms:

- **Plastic Hinge Formation:** The sheet metal bends plastically around a stationary hinge line, dissipating energy through bending moment-plastic rotation work.
- **Membrane Stretching / Plastic Circumferential Expansion:** As the tube folds, the side walls stretch circumferentially, absorbing additional energy.

### The Crushing Force Equation

For an axially loaded thin-walled column of length $L$, mean crush force $\bar{P}$, and crush distance $\delta$, the energy absorbed is:

$$ E_{absorbed} = \int_0^{\delta} P(x) \, dx \approx \bar{P} \cdot \delta $$

The mean crush force $\bar{P}$ is empirically related to the cross-sectional area $A$ of the rail through the **Axial Crushing Force Formula** for thin-walled tubes:

$$ \bar{P} = K \cdot A^{n} $$

Where:
- $\bar{P}$ = Mean axial crushing force (N)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of the rail (mm²)
- $K$ = Material/geometry constant (typically **0.4 – 0.7** for mild steel rails)
- $n$ = Exponent (typically **1.0 – 1.3**)

### KTU Formula Sheet — Crash Energy & Structural Behaviour

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Variable Definitions | Units | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Kinetic Energy at Impact** | $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | m = vehicle mass, v = impact velocity | kg, m/s | Energy budget calculation |
| **Specific Energy Absorption (SEA)** | $SEA = \frac{E_{absorbed}}{m_{structure}}$ | E_absorbed / mass of deformed structure | kJ/kg | Material/structure efficiency comparison |
| **Mean Crush Force** | $\bar{P} = \frac{E_{absorbed}}{\delta_{crush}}$ | E_absorbed / total crush distance | kN | Crush stroke design |
| **Crush Efficiency (CE)** | $CE = \frac{\bar{P}}{P_{peak}}$ | Mean / Peak crushing force | Dimensionless (0–1) | Ideal crushing = CE → 1.0 (square-wave crash pulse) |
| **Deceleration (avg)** | $a = \frac{v^2}{2 \cdot \delta}$ | v² / (2 × crush distance) | m/s² | Crash pulse severity |
| **Intrusion Velocity** | $v_{int} = v_0 \sqrt{1 - \frac{E_{abs}}{E_k}}$ | Residual velocity after energy absorbed | m/s | Firewall survival check |
| **Energy Conservation** | $E_k = E_{deform} + E_{restraint} + E_{friction}$ | Energy balance | J | Total energy balance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — Crush Efficiency (CE):**
> An ideal crash structure has a **square-wave crash pulse**, meaning the deceleration is constant throughout the event. This corresponds to a Crush Efficiency of **1.0**. Real-world structures achieve **CE ≈ 0.6 – 0.85**. Higher CE means lower peak loads on occupants for the same energy absorption.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

The concepts above are used by automotive OEMs (e.g., Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki, Mahindra) and global Tier-1 safety suppliers (Autoliv, ZF, Bosch) to:

- Design **triggered crumple initiators** (laser-welded blanks, drilled holes) that force folding to begin at predetermined locations.
- Validate designs using **Finite Element Method (FEM)** explicit solvers like LS-DYNA, RADIOSS, and PAM-CRASH before any physical prototype is built.
- Calibrate **restraint system firing algorithms** in the Airbag Control Unit (ACU) based on predicted crash pulse.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Total Kinetic Energy Available in a Frontal Crash

**Problem Setup:** A vehicle of mass $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$ impacts a rigid barrier at $v_0 = 56 \text{ km/h}$. Calculate the total kinetic energy available and the theoretical average deceleration if the front structure crushes uniformly over a distance of $\delta = 0.5 \text{ m}$.

**Step 1: Convert velocity from km/h to m/s**

$$ v_0 = 56 \text{ km/h} = \frac{56}{3.6} \text{ m/s} \approx 15.56 \text{ m/s} $$

This conversion is required because the SI unit for kinetic energy uses m/s. Dividing by 3.6 (since 1 km = 1000 m and 1 h = 3600 s) is the standard engineering shortcut.

**Step 2: Calculate Total Kinetic Energy**

$$ E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v_0^2 $$

$$ E_k = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (15.56)^2 $$

$$ E_k = 750 \times 242.11 $$

$$ E_k = 181{,}583 \text{ J} \approx 181.6 \text{ kJ} $$

This represents the total energy that must be dissipated by structural deformation, seatbelts, airbags, and friction. The squaring of the velocity term is critical because doubling the speed quadruples the energy, which is why high-speed crashes are exponentially more severe.

**Step 3: Apply Work-Energy Theorem to find Average Deceleration**

$$ F_{avg} \cdot \delta = E_k $$

$$ F_{avg} = \frac{E_k}{\delta} = \frac{181{,}583}{0.5} $$

$$ F_{avg} = 363{,}166 \text{ N} \approx 363 \text{ kN} $$

The Work-Energy Theorem states that the work done by the crushing force equals the change in kinetic energy. Dividing energy by crush distance gives the average force needed to absorb the crash over that distance.

**Step 4: Convert Force to Equivalent Deceleration**

$$ a_{avg} = \frac{F_{avg}}{m} = \frac{363{,}166}{1500} $$

$$ a_{avg} \approx 242 \text{ m/s}^2 $$

$$ a_{avg} \text{ in g} = \frac{242}{9.81} \approx 24.7 \text{ g} $$

Since deceleration is force divided by mass, this represents the average deceleration the vehicle experiences. This **24.7 g** average is within survivable limits because it is sustained for a long duration (~80 ms) rather than being a sharp spike.

### Derivation 2: Intrusion Velocity into the Passenger Cell

**Problem Setup:** The front structure of the vehicle absorbs $E_{abs} = 150 \text{ kJ}$ of energy during the first 80 ms. Determine the residual velocity of the passenger cell (the "velocity at the firewall").

**Step 1: Define Residual Energy After Front Structure Absorption**

$$ E_{residual} = E_k - E_{abs} = 181.6 - 150 = 31.6 \text{ kJ} $$

Subtracting absorbed energy from total kinetic energy gives the energy still left in the vehicle's motion that must be managed by restraints and the passenger cell.

**Step 2: Apply Conservation of Energy to Find Residual Velocity**

$$ E_{residual} = \frac{1}{2} m v_{residual}^2 $$

$$ v_{residual} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot E_{residual}}{m}} $$

$$ v_{residual} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 31{,}600}{1500}} = \sqrt{42.13} $$

$$ v_{residual} \approx 6.49 \text{ m/s} \approx 23.4 \text{ km/h} $$

Taking the square root gives the residual speed. If the passenger cell is rigid, occupants will decelerate from this 6.49 m/s through seatbelts and airbags over a stopping distance of typically 0.3–0.5 m, yielding occupant decelerations of roughly 8–15 g, which is survivable.

### Derivation 3: Specific Energy Absorption (SEA) Comparison

The SEA is the gold-standard metric for comparing crashworthiness of materials and structures. It is defined as energy absorbed per unit mass of the crushed structure.

$$ SEA = \frac{E_{absorbed}}{m_{structure}} = \frac{\bar{P} \cdot \delta}{\rho \cdot A \cdot L} $$

Where $\rho$ is material density, $A$ is cross-sectional area, and $L$ is the length of the rail.

| Material | Density (kg/m³) | Typical SEA (kJ/kg) | KTU Application Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel (DC04) | 7850 | 20 – 30 | Baseline for traditional BIW |
| HSS (Advanced High-Strength Steel) | 7850 | 30 – 50 | Used in critical load paths |
| Aluminum Alloy (6061-T6) | 2700 | 25 – 40 | Weight-saving, EVs |
| CFRP (Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer) | 1600 | 60 – 100 | Premium sports cars, F1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Analytical Insight:** The SEA column reveals why carbon fiber is increasingly used in Formula 1 and high-end EVs — it absorbs roughly 2–3 times more energy per kg than mild steel. However, CFRP fails in a brittle manner (no progressive folding), making it difficult to tune for a square-wave crash pulse. This is the central material selection trade-off in modern BIW design.

### Derivation 4: Crush Efficiency Optimization

Given a real-world crash structure with peak crush force $P_{peak} = 450 \text{ kN}$ and mean crush force $\bar{P} = 315 \text{ kN}$, the Crush Efficiency is:

$$ CE = \frac{\bar{P}}{P_{peak}} = \frac{315}{450} = 0.70 $$

This means the structure experiences a peak force 43% higher than the average, which translates directly to a peak occupant load spike. Engineers improve CE by introducing **crash initiators** — small geometric features that force the folding pattern to be regular and repeatable, raising CE toward 0.85+.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4.1 Structural Diagrams & Schematics — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

### Diagram 1: Crash Energy Flow Path Through Vehicle Body

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Kinetic Energy 181 kJ] --> B[Front Bumper Beam 15 kJ]
    B --> C[Front Rail Folding Zone 110 kJ]
    C --> D[Firewall and Dash Panel 25 kJ]
    D --> E[Restraint System Seatbelts plus Airbags 25 kJ]
    E --> F[Occupant Compartment Survival Space]
    C --> G[Rear Rail Energy Path 6 kJ]
    A --> H[Tyre plus Suspension Friction 0 kJ]
```

This block-level architecture maps the sequential energy dissipation path. Each block represents a structural subsystem and the approximate fraction of total kinetic energy it absorbs in a 56 km/h frontal rigid barrier test.

### Diagram 2: Crash Pulse Comparison — Ideal vs Real

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Time 0 ms] --> B[Crash Event Initiated]
    B --> C[Phase 1 Free Flight]
    C --> D[Phase 2 Primary Engagement]
    D --> E[Phase 3 Crumple Zone Collapse]
    E --> F[Phase 4 Passenger Cell Loading]
    F --> G[Restraint Deployment Window]
    G --> H[Occupant Ride Down]
    H --> I[Vehicle Stops]
```

### Diagram 3: Passenger Cell Structural Members

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[A Pillar Roof Rail Side Impact] --- A2[B Pillar Mid Side]
    A2 --- A3[C Pillar Rear Side]
    A1 --- A4[Roof Rail Longitudinal]
    A2 --- A5[Rocker Panel Lower Side]
    A3 --- A6[Rear Roof Rail]
    A4 --- A5
    A5 --- A7[Front Firewall Bulkhead]
    A5 --- A8[Rear Bulkhead]
```

This block diagram represents the safety cage. The members shown must remain elastic (no permanent deformation) during a frontal crash to maintain occupant survival space.

### Diagram 4: Triggered Folding Pattern in a Front Rail

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Impact Load Applied] --> B[Bumper Absorber Crushes]
    B --> C[Drilled Crash Initiator Triggers Fold]
    C --> D[First Fold Plastic Hinge]
    D --> E[Second Fold Plastic Hinge]
    E --> F[Third Fold Plastic Hinge]
    F --> G[Rail Resistance Increases]
    G --> H[Force Steepens to Maintain Crash Pulse]
```

### Diagram 5: Crash Test Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crash Test Request] --> B[Define Test Conditions NCAP or AIS]
    B --> C[Instrument Vehicle with Sensors]
    C --> D[Accelerometers on B Pillar]
    C --> E[Load Cells on Seatbelt Anchors]
    C --> F[High Speed Cameras]
    C --> G[Anthropomorphic Test Devices ATDs]
    D --> H[Conduct Crash Test at Proving Ground]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Analyze Crash Pulse Data]
    H --> J[Measure Intrusion from High Speed Video]
    H --> K[Calculate Injury Criteria HIC and Chest Deflection]
    I --> L[Generate Final Crash Test Report]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5.1 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Crash Energy Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s before squaring the velocity is the most common mistake. Always convert FIRST, then square.
> 2. **Confusing kinetic energy with momentum:** Energy is what causes structural damage; momentum is what causes occupant injury. Do not interchange them.
> 3. **Ignoring Crush Efficiency (CE):** When asked to compute peak deceleration, students often use mean crush force without applying CE. Real peak deceleration is roughly $\bar{P}/CE$.
> 4. **Direction of force:** Deceleration is negative acceleration. Many students lose marks for not clearly stating the direction or sign.

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Crumple Zone" in a passenger car. Why is it designed to deform plastically while the passenger cell is designed to remain elastic?*

**Model Answer:**
A crumple zone is a structural region of the vehicle body, typically located at the front and rear, that is engineered to undergo controlled plastic deformation during a crash. Its purpose is to convert the vehicle's kinetic energy into plastic deformation work, thereby reducing the peak deceleration experienced by occupants.

The crumple zone deforms plastically because plastic deformation is an irreversible process that absorbs large amounts of energy over a relatively long crush distance. This lengthens the time over which deceleration occurs, lowering its peak value. In contrast, the passenger cell must remain elastic (rigid) so that the survival space around the occupants is preserved. If the passenger cell deformed, the steering wheel, dashboard, and footwell would intrude into the occupants, causing severe contact injuries. The crumple zone thus sacrifices itself to protect the rigid safety cage.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is Specific Energy Absorption (SEA)? Why is it the preferred metric for comparing crashworthiness of different materials?*

**Model Answer:**
Specific Energy Absorption (SEA) is defined as the energy absorbed per unit mass of the crushing structure, expressed in kJ/kg. The formula is:

$$ SEA = \frac{E_{absorbed}}{m_{structure}} $$

SEA is the preferred metric for material comparison because it normalizes the energy absorbed by the mass of the structure. This allows engineers to objectively compare materials on a "per kilogram" basis, which is critical in automotive design where lightweighting (mass reduction) is essential for fuel efficiency and EV range. A material with high SEA can absorb more energy per kg, allowing either a lighter structure for the same crash performance, or better crash performance for the same mass. For example, CFRP has SEA of 60–100 kJ/kg, which is approximately 2–3 times that of mild steel (20–30 kJ/kg), making it a superior crashworthy material on a mass basis.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **ESE Internal Choice Instructions:** Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice provided.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat diagram, explain the different structural zones of a modern passenger car body and their roles during a frontal crash. Discuss why the front zone is designed to be soft while the passenger compartment is designed to be rigid. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The modern monocoque passenger car body is divided into three primary structural zones, as illustrated in the block diagram from SECTION 4.

**Zone 1 — Front Crumple Zone (Deformable Zone):**
This zone extends from the front bumper to the firewall (typically 700–1000 mm in length). It includes the bumper beam, bumper absorber, front rails, upper and lower radiator supports, and front strut towers. Its primary role is **kinetic energy absorption** through controlled progressive plastic folding (accordion-like crushing) of the front rails. The structure is deliberately made of relatively soft, ductile materials (mild steel, aluminum) and is geometrically tuned with crash initiators to ensure predictable folding patterns.

**Zone 2 — Passenger Cell / Safety Cage (Rigid Zone):**
This zone surrounds the occupants and comprises the A-pillars, B-pillars, C-pillars, roof rails, rocker panels, and cross members. Its role is to **maintain occupant survival space**. During a frontal crash, it must resist intrusion, allowing only elastic deformation. This is achieved using ultra-high-strength steel (UHSS, with tensile strength > 1500 MPa) in critical load paths.

**Zone 3 — Rear Crumple Zone:**
Similar to the front crumple zone, but smaller. It absorbs energy in rear impacts and prevents fuel tank intrusion during fuel-tank-affecting collisions.

**Why Soft Front, Rigid Cell?**
The fundamental principle is energy management. The front structure acts as a "mechanical fuse," absorbing the bulk of the crash energy through plastic deformation. If the front were also rigid, all 181 kJ would be transferred almost instantaneously to the passenger cell and occupants, generating deceleration spikes exceeding **100 g** — well beyond human tolerance. The crumple zone lengthens the crash event from a few milliseconds to approximately 100 ms, reducing the peak deceleration to survivable levels of **20–40 g**.

**(b)** A vehicle of mass 1800 kg impacts a rigid barrier at 64 km/h. The front structure absorbs 140 kJ of energy through plastic deformation. Calculate: (i) the total kinetic energy, (ii) the residual energy entering the passenger cell, (iii) the residual velocity of the vehicle, and (iv) the average deceleration if the total crush distance is 0.6 m. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total Kinetic Energy:**

$$ v_0 = \frac{64}{3.6} = 17.78 \text{ m/s} $$

$$ E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v_0^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1800 \times (17.78)^2 $$

$$ E_k = 900 \times 316.13 $$

$$ \boxed{E_k \approx 284.5 \text{ kJ}} $$

**(ii) Residual Energy:**

$$ E_{residual} = E_k - E_{abs} = 284.5 - 140 $$

$$ \boxed{E_{residual} = 144.5 \text{ kJ}} $$

**(iii) Residual Velocity:**

$$ v_{residual} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot E_{residual}}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 144{,}500}{1800}} $$

$$ v_{residual} = \sqrt{160.56} $$

$$ \boxed{v_{residual} \approx 12.67 \text{ m/s} \approx 45.6 \text{ km/h}} $$

**(iv) Average Deceleration:**

$$ a_{avg} = \frac{v_0^2}{2 \cdot \delta} = \frac{(17.78)^2}{2 \times 0.6} $$

$$ a_{avg} = \frac{316.13}{1.2} \approx 263.4 \text{ m/s}^2 $$

$$ a_{avg} \text{ in g} = \frac{263.4}{9.81} \approx 26.9 \text{ g} $$

$$ \boxed{a_{avg} \approx 26.9 \text{ g}} $$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of "Crush Efficiency (CE)" in crashworthy structure design. Describe, with a diagram, the typical frontal crash pulse (acceleration vs. time) of a well-designed modern vehicle, and explain why a square-wave pulse is desirable. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Crush Efficiency (CE):**
Crush Efficiency is defined as the ratio of mean crushing force to peak crushing force during a crash event:

$$ CE = \frac{\bar{P}}{P_{peak}} $$

The ideal crash pulse is a "square wave" — constant deceleration throughout the event — which corresponds to CE = 1.0. In reality, crash pulses are not square; they typically exhibit an initial spike (peak force during initial structural engagement, perhaps 450 kN), followed by a relatively steady plateau (mean force ~315 kN), and then a tail-off. Real structures achieve CE values between 0.60 and 0.85.

**Why Square-Wave Pulse is Desirable:**

A square-wave pulse minimizes peak occupant loading. According to the occupant ride-down model, an occupant experiences approximately the average deceleration of the vehicle during ride-down. If the crash pulse has high peaks, the occupant experiences those peaks as increased spinal and chest loading. By raising CE toward 1.0 (squarer pulse), engineers can reduce peak deceleration for the same total energy absorbed, dramatically improving injury metrics like HIC (Head Injury Criterion) and Chest Deflection.

Engineers improve CE by adding crash initiators (drilled holes, geometric discontinuities) that force consistent, repeatable folding. Another technique is to use **triggers** — small depressions in the rail — that initiate folding at a precisely calibrated force level.

**Typical Frontal Crash Pulse Characteristics:**
- Duration: 80 – 120 ms
- Peak acceleration: 25 – 35 g (well-designed modern vehicle at 56 km/h)
- Mean acceleration: 20 – 28 g
- CE: 0.70 – 0.85

**(b)** Compare the Specific Energy Absorption (SEA) and failure modes of Mild Steel, Advanced High-Strength Steel (AHSS), and Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer (CFRP) used in body structures. Explain why CFRP, despite its superior SEA, is not universally adopted in mass-market passenger vehicles. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Property | Mild Steel (DC04) | AHSS (DP780) | CFRP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density (kg/m³) | 7850 | 7850 | 1600 |
| SEA (kJ/kg) | 20 – 30 | 30 – 50 | 60 – 100 |
| Failure Mode | Ductile folding | Ductile + some brittle | Brittle fracture |
| Cost (INR/kg) | 80 – 120 | 150 – 250 | 4000 – 12000 |
| Repairability | Easy | Moderate | Very Difficult |

**Comparison and Explanation:**

Mild steel is the traditional automotive material. It folds in a highly progressive, predictable manner, making it easy to tune for crash pulses. However, its SEA is the lowest of the three.

AHSS (particularly Dual Phase steels like DP780) offers significantly higher SEA through martensitic strengthening while retaining enough ductility to fold. It is now standard in crumple zones of most modern passenger cars.

CFRP offers the highest SEA — roughly 2–3 times that of mild steel — and its low density further enhances the per-kg advantage. However, CFRP fails in a **brittle manner**: it does not progressively fold like steel. Instead, it fractures suddenly into fragments. This makes it very difficult to tune the crash pulse; achieving a square-wave pulse with CFRP requires extremely sophisticated and expensive manufacturing (e.g., tailored fiber placement, honeycomb cores).

**Why CFRP is Not Universally Adopted:**

1. **Cost:** CFRP costs roughly 30–100× more per kg than steel.
2. **Manufacturing Complexity:** Requires autoclave curing (4–8 hours at 130°C, 7 bar pressure), making mass production slow.
3. **Brittle Failure Mode:** As explained, hard to tune for progressive crushing.
4. **Repair Difficulty:** Damaged CFRP components cannot be repaired using conventional body-shop techniques; they require replacement.
5. **Recycling:** CFRP is difficult to recycle, conflicting with circular economy and end-of-life vehicle (ELV) regulations.

For these reasons, CFRP is reserved for high-performance applications (F1, supercars, premium EVs like BMW i3 carbon core) while mass-market vehicles predominantly use AHSS.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Crumple Zone Function:** Convert kinetic energy to plastic deformation work; extend crash duration; reduce peak deceleration below human tolerance.
- **Passenger Cell Function:** Maintain survival space; deform only elastically; prevent intrusion of hard surfaces into occupant compartment.
- **Three Crash Phases:** Free flight → Primary engagement → Crumple zone collapse → Restraint deployment (occupant ride-down).
- **Total Energy Budget:** $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ — must equal the sum of all energy dissipated (structural, restraint, friction).
- **Crush Efficiency (CE):** Ratio of mean to peak crush force. Ideal CE = 1.0 (square-wave pulse). Real CE = 0.6 – 0.85.
- **Specific Energy Absorption (SEA):** Energy per kg of crushed structure. CFRP > AHSS > Mild Steel.
- **Peak Deceleration Rule:** Aim for sustained 20–40 g, NOT spikes of 100 g+. Survival requires low peaks, not low averages.
- **Crash Initiators:** Geometric features (drilled holes, dimples) that force folding to begin at predetermined locations.
- **Material Trade-off:** Higher strength = higher SEA, but lower ductility = more brittle failure. Engineering balance is critical.
- **Crash Test Standards:** Bharat NCAP, Global NCAP, Euro NCAP, IIHS, AIS-098 (India). All use ATDs (Anthropomorphic Test Devices) like Hybrid III 50th percentile male.
- **Wayne State Tolerance Curve:** Defines human tolerance to deceleration vs. duration — the engineering target for any crash structure.
- **Energy Conservation Law:** Always verify that $E_k = E_{crumple} + E_{restraint} + E_{friction} + E_{residual}$. If your numbers don't balance, you have made an error.
- **Intrusion Rule:** Passenger cell intrusion must not exceed **75 mm** (Euro NCAP) into occupant survival space.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regulatory requirements for crash testing: Introduction to active safety systems - Anti-lock brake systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT743 - VEHICLE SAFETY AND SECURITY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Regulatory requirements for crash testing: Introduction to active safety systems - Anti-lock brake systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Regulatory Requirements for Crash Testing

### Formal Definition
**Crash testing regulations** are a set of legally binding standards and protocols that govern how motor vehicles must be tested under controlled collision conditions to ensure occupant, pedestrian, and vehicle safety. These standards are issued by governmental and supranational bodies and define test procedures, dummies used, impact velocities, and pass/fail criteria.

The principal regulatory frameworks worldwide are:

- **FMVSS** (Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standards) – Issued by NHTSA, USA.
- **ECE Regulations** – Issued by the United Nations Economic Commission for Europe (UNECE), applicable across Europe and many Asian countries including India.
- **AIS** (Automotive Industry Standards) – India-specific standards drafted by the Automotive Industry Standards Committee under the Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH).
- **Bharat NCAP** – India's New Car Assessment Program launched in **2023** under the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS).
- **Euro NCAP** – European New Car Assessment Programme (non-governmental but highly influential).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The two cardinal test categories mandated across all frameworks are **Frontal Crash Tests** (full-width rigid barrier, offset deformable barrier) and **Side Impact Tests** (mobile deformable barrier). These correspond to KTU Course Outcome **CO2**: *Identify and apply vehicle safety regulations.*

### Crash Test Categories (KTU High-Yield)

| Test Type | Standard Reference | Test Speed |
|-----------|-------------------|------------|
| Frontal Full-Width Rigid Barrier | FMVSS 208 / AIS-098 | **56 km/h** |
| Frontal Offset Deformable Barrier (ODB) | Euro NCAP / AIS-098 | **64 km/h** |
| Side Mobile Deformable Barrier (MDB) | FMVSS 214 / AIS-099 | **61.8 km/h** |
| Side Pole Impact | Euro NCAP | **32 km/h** |
| Pedestrian Protection | GTR 9 / AIS-100 | **40 km/h** |
| Rear Impact (Whiplash) | FMVSS 202a | **80 km/h** |

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of crash testing like an **engineering "final exam"** for a car. Just as a student must pass standardized tests to get certified, every vehicle model must pass a series of standardized "physical exams" (crash tests) before it is allowed on public roads. The regulations are the **syllabus**, the dummies (Hybrid-III, THOR, etc.) are the **examinees**, and the barrier is the **examiner**.

---

## 1.2 Introduction to Active Safety Systems

### Formal Definition
**Active Safety Systems** are vehicle subsystems that continuously monitor driving conditions and actively intervene — through braking, steering, or throttle modulation — to **prevent or mitigate accidents before they occur**. They are distinguished from **Passive Safety Systems** (airbags, crumple zones, seatbelts) which mitigate injury *after* a crash has begun.

> [!NOTE]
> **Active = Pre-crash intervention. Passive = During/Post-crash protection.**

### Classification of Active Safety Systems

**A. Longitudinal Dynamics Control**
- **ABS** – Anti-lock Braking System
- **EBD** – Electronic Brake-force Distribution
- **EBA / BAS** – Electronic/Brake Assist System
- **TCS / ASR** – Traction Control System
- **AEB** – Autonomous Emergency Braking
- **ACC** – Adaptive Cruise Control

**B. Lateral Dynamics Control**
- **ESC / ESP / VSC** – Electronic Stability Control
- **LDW / LKA** – Lane Departure Warning / Lane Keep Assist
- **BSD** – Blind Spot Detection

**C. Visibility & Driver Assistance**
- **AFS** – Adaptive Front-lighting System
- **Night Vision Assist**
- **TPMS** – Tyre Pressure Monitoring System

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine driving a car as walking on a slippery floor while carrying a stack of glasses. **Active safety systems are like a friend walking beside you**, holding your elbow when you slip (ABS), nudging you back to the centre of the hallway (ESC), or shouting "ICE!" before you step on it (AEB). They act *before* the disaster.

---

## 1.3 Anti-lock Brake System (ABS) — Core Definition

**ABS** is a closed-loop electro-hydraulic braking control system that **prevents wheel lock-up** during heavy braking by modulating brake-line pressure in real time. The principal objective is to **maintain steering control** and **maximize tire-road friction utilization** during emergency braking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition to Memorize:** *"ABS is an active safety system that limits wheel slip by automatically regulating braking pressure to keep the slip ratio within 10–20 % of the peak adhesion coefficient region."*

### Why ABS is Needed (Conceptual Motivation)

When a wheel **locks** (stops rotating) while the vehicle is still moving, two catastrophic things happen simultaneously:
1. The tire begins to **skid** — friction drops from the peak (μ_peak) to the slide value (μ_slide), typically a **20–30 % loss**.
2. The wheel **stops steering** — the driver cannot change the vehicle's path, leading to obstacle collisions.

ABS prevents both by **pulsing the brake pressure** at up to **15–25 cycles per second** (faster than human reflex).

### Intuitive Analogy
ABS is like a driver rhythmically **"pumping the brake"** — *brake-release-brake-release* — but at 20 Hz, which is physically impossible for a human foot. The system does what the best rally drivers do, only faster and more precisely.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Slip Ratio (λ)

The entire control philosophy of ABS rests on a single variable: **Slip Ratio (λ)**.

### Definition
**Slip ratio** quantifies how much a wheel is sliding (locking) relative to the vehicle's true ground speed.

For a **braking** scenario (vehicle moving, wheel rotating slower than free-rolling):

$$\lambda = \frac{V_v - V_w}{V_v} \times 100\,\%$$

where:
- $V_v$ = linear velocity of the vehicle body (m/s)
- $V_w = \omega \cdot r_e$ = linear velocity of the wheel at the contact patch (m/s)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity of the wheel (rad/s)
- $r_e$ = effective rolling radius of the tyre (m)

### Boundary Conditions
- $\lambda = 0\,\%$ → Free rolling (no brake applied)
- $\lambda = 100\,\%$ → Wheel fully locked (full skid)

For an **accelerating** scenario, the same magnitude of slip is computed with numerator/denominator reversed.

### The Adhesion Coefficient (μ) — Slip (λ) Curve
The friction force available at the tire-road interface is **not constant**. It follows a characteristic curve (Krempf curve) that depends on road surface and slip ratio.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** On dry asphalt, **μ** rises rapidly, peaks at λ ≈ 10–20 %, then drops to a lower slide value at λ = 100 %. The ABS controller's job is to keep λ pinned in the **peak region**.

---

## 2.2 ABS Subsystem Architecture

ABS consists of **four functional blocks** working in a closed loop:

1. **Sensors (Input Layer)**
   - 4× Wheel Speed Sensors (WSS) — variable reluctance or Hall-effect
   - Optional: Steering angle sensor, yaw rate sensor (for ESC integration)

2. **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)**
   - Calculates vehicle reference speed
   - Computes per-wheel slip ratio
   - Runs the control algorithm (typically a bang-bang / PID / sliding-mode controller)
   - Issues commands to the hydraulic modulator

3. **Hydraulic Modulator (Actuator Layer)**
   - 3/3-way solenoid valves per wheel circuit
   - High-pressure pump + accumulator
   - Capable of three pressure phases: **Build → Hold → Release**

4. **Brake Hydraulic Circuit**
   - Master cylinder, brake lines, brake calipers/wheel cylinders

### ABS Pressure Modulation Cycle (KTU Must-Know)

| Phase | Valve Position | Pressure State | Effect on Wheel |
|-------|----------------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Pressure Build** | Inlet OPEN, Outlet CLOSED | Pressure increases | Wheel decelerates |
| **Pressure Hold** | Inlet CLOSED, Outlet CLOSED | Pressure constant | Wheel at threshold |
| **Pressure Release** | Inlet CLOSED, Outlet OPEN | Pressure decreases (fluid → accumulator) | Wheel re-accelerates |

The cycle repeats **15–25 times per second**, well above the driver's muscular response capability (~3 Hz).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mandatory** for KTU Module 2 numerical problems and derivations.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{V_v - V_w}{V_v} \times 100$ | Slip ratio (%) | $V_v$, $V_w$ in m/s |
| 2 | $V_w = \omega \cdot r_e$ | Wheel peripheral speed | $\omega$ rad/s, $r_e$ m |
| 3 | $s = \dfrac{V^2}{2 \cdot \mu \cdot g}$ | Braking stopping distance | $V$ initial speed (m/s), $\mu$ adhesion coeff., $g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| 4 | $a_{max} = \mu \cdot g$ | Maximum achievable deceleration | $a$ in m/s² |
| 5 | $\mu_{opt} \approx 0.8$ to $0.9$ | Peak adhesion (dry asphalt) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\mu_{slide} \approx 0.6$ to $0.7$ | Slide friction (locked wheel) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $F_{brake} = \mu(\lambda) \cdot F_z$ | Available braking force | $F_z$ = normal load (N) |
| 8 | $\dfrac{ds}{d\lambda}\big\vert_{\lambda_{opt}} = 0$ | Slip ratio optimization condition | Used in derivation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write slip ratio as $(V_w - V_v)/V_v$. For **braking**, vehicle speed > wheel speed, so the convention $\lambda = (V_v - V_w)/V_v$ is correct. Sign conventions carry marks.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Theory

The slip ratio–adhesion curve is not just academic — it is the **central design tool** for:
- **Tuning ABS threshold slip** (typically 8–25 % depending on road class).
- **Designing TCS** (operates in the *positive* slip region 5–15 %).
- **Programming ESC** (uses both longitudinal and lateral slip estimates).
- **Validating simulation models** (CarSim, Simulink-TyreMagic, ADAMS).
- **Predicting stopping distance** for homologation testing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stopping Distance from Energy Balance

We will derive the classical stopping distance formula used in all KTU braking problems.

### Setup
Consider a vehicle of mass $m$ (kg) moving at initial speed $V_0$ (m/s). The brake applies a friction force $F_f = \mu \cdot m \cdot g$ (N) opposing motion. Assume the road is level and aerodynamic drag is neglected for the standard KTU simplification.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write Newton's Second Law along the direction of motion.**

The only horizontal force is the friction force opposing motion. Taking forward as positive and braking deceleration as $-a$:

$$m \cdot a = -F_f = -\mu \cdot m \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Cancel the mass $m$ (this is why stopping distance is mass-independent for a given $\mu$).**

$$a = -\mu \cdot g$$

This shows the maximum achievable deceleration is purely a function of road adhesion, independent of vehicle mass. The result $a_{max} = \mu g$ is often called the **kinematic deceleration limit**.

**Step 3 — Use the kinematic equation $V^2 = V_0^2 + 2 a s$.**

The vehicle finally stops, so the final velocity $V = 0$. The displacement covered until stop is the stopping distance $s$. Substituting $V = 0$ and $a = -\mu g$:

$$0 = V_0^2 + 2(-\mu g) s$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $s$.**

$$2 \mu g s = V_0^2$$

$$s = \frac{V_0^2}{2 \mu g}$$

This is the **canonical stopping distance formula** used universally in vehicle dynamics and KTU numerical problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The result $s \propto V_0^2$ means doubling the speed **quadruples** the stopping distance — a critical safety insight emphasized in the KTU syllabus under CO3 (Application of braking theory).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Slip Ratio from First Principles

### Setup
Let the vehicle be moving on level ground at linear speed $V_v$ (m/s). A single wheel of effective radius $r_e$ (m) is rotating at angular speed $\omega$ (rad/s). The wheel is being braked and is rotating *slower* than the free-rolling condition.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Express the wheel's peripheral speed.**

If the wheel were free-rolling (no brake, no slip), its contact patch would move at exactly $V_v$. The peripheral speed corresponding to the wheel's actual angular velocity is:

$$V_w = \omega \cdot r_e$$

**Step 2 — Define the relative slip velocity.**

The difference between vehicle velocity and wheel peripheral velocity is the slip velocity:

$$V_{slip} = V_v - V_w$$

**Step 3 — Normalize by the reference velocity.**

To make slip dimensionless and bounded between 0 and 1, divide by the vehicle speed (the natural reference during braking):

$$\lambda = \frac{V_{slip}}{V_v} = \frac{V_v - V_w}{V_v}$$

**Step 4 — Express in percentage form for engineering use.**

$$\lambda\,\% = \frac{V_v - \omega r_e}{V_v} \times 100$$

**Verification of boundary conditions:**

- *Free rolling:* $\omega r_e = V_v$ ⇒ $\lambda = 0$ ✓
- *Locked wheel:* $\omega = 0$ ⇒ $\lambda = 1$ (i.e. 100 %) ✓

> [!NOTE]
> The 10–20 % optimal slip range cited in KTU materials is computed by setting $\dfrac{d\mu}{d\lambda} = 0$ on the empirical Krempf curve, a procedure expected in 14-mark derivation questions.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Without ABS vs With ABS

This matrix is a **frequently asked KTU comparison** and is worth memorizing verbatim.

| Performance Parameter | Without ABS (Locked Wheel) | With ABS (Controlled Slip) |
|----------------------|----------------------------|----------------------------|
| Operating slip ratio | 100 % (full lock) | 10–20 % (peak μ region) |
| Effective friction coeff. $\mu_{eff}$ | $\mu_{slide} \approx 0.6$–$0.7$ | $\mu_{peak} \approx 0.8$–$0.9$ |
| Steering control during braking | **LOST** (wheel not rotating → no lateral grip) | **RETAINED** (wheel rotating → lateral grip available) |
| Stopping distance (dry asphalt) | Longer (skid-dominated) | Shorter (15–20 % improvement typical) |
| Tyre wear | Flat spots, scuffing | Even, controlled wear |
| Driver workload | High (requires cadence braking) | Zero (automated) |
| Applicable to split-μ surfaces | Poor (vehicle spins) | Excellent (EBD integrates with ABS) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Line:** *"ABS does not necessarily reduce stopping distance on every surface; its **primary** benefit is **retained steering control**, not minimum stopping distance."* This is a 3-mark distinction worth memorizing.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU Practice Standard)

> A car moving at $V_0 = 90$ km/h applies the brakes on dry asphalt. Compute: (a) deceleration, (b) stopping distance. Given $\mu = 0.8$, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Solution:**

*Step 1 — Convert units.* $V_0 = 90$ km/h = $25$ m/s.

*Step 2 — Deceleration.*
$$a = \mu g = 0.8 \times 9.81 = 7.848 \text{ m/s}^2$$

*Step 3 — Stopping distance.*
$$s = \frac{V_0^2}{2 \mu g} = \frac{25^2}{2 \times 0.8 \times 9.81} = \frac{625}{15.696} \approx 39.82 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For ABS-equipped braking on the same surface with controlled slip, $\mu_{eff}$ rises from 0.7 (slide) to 0.85 (peak), reducing stopping distance to ≈ 37.4 m — a measurable 2.4 m safety margin.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Crash Test Regulatory Hierarchy

The following block diagram maps the global-to-India flow of crash test regulations as expected in KTU Module 2 questions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[UNECE Geneva] --> nodeB[ECE Regulations E29 E94 E95]
    nodeC[NHTSA USA] --> nodeD[FMVSS 208 214 202a]
    nodeE[Euro NCAP NGO] --> nodeF[Star Rating Protocol]
    nodeG[MoRTH India] --> nodeH[AIS 098 099 100 144]
    nodeB --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeI[Bharat NCAP 2023]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Vehicle Type Approval in India]
    nodeB --> nodeK[European Type Approval]
    nodeD --> nodeL[US Market Compliance]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **outer ring** lists the issuing bodies.
- The **inner branches** show the standard families.
- **AIS 098–100 / 144** are the actual India-mandatory tests.
- **Bharat NCAP** is voluntary but commercially critical for marketing.

---

## 4.2 ABS Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Wheel Speed Sensor x4] --> nodeB[ECU Microcontroller]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Slip Ratio Calculator}
    nodeC --> nodeD[Controller Logic PID Bang Bang]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Hydraulic Modulator]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Wheel Brake Caliper]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Tyre Road Contact]
    nodeG --> nodeA
    nodeD --> nodeH[Warning Lamp MIL]
    nodeD --> nodeI[EBD Interface]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[ESC Interface]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **outer loop** (nodeA → nodeB → nodeC → nodeD → nodeE → nodeF → nodeG → nodeA) is the **closed control loop** executing at 15–25 Hz.
- The **inner feedback** (nodeG → nodeA) re-samples wheel speed every 5 ms.
- The **right-side branches** (nodeH, nodeI, nodeJ) show integration with vehicle-level safety network.

---

## 4.3 ABS Pressure Modulation State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PressureBuild
    PressureBuild --> PressureHold: Wheel deceleration crosses threshold
    PressureHold --> PressureRelease: Wheel slip exceeds upper limit
    PressureRelease --> PressureBuild: Wheel re-accelerates above reference
    PressureHold --> PressureBuild: Slip within target band
    PressureRelease --> PressureHold: Hold command from ECU
    PressureBuild --> [*]: Brake pedal released
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The system **cycles** between three states.
- It **never rests** in any single state during emergency braking.
- The state transitions are governed by the **slip ratio thresholds** programmed in the ECU.

---

## 4.4 Slip vs Adhesion Curve (Schematic Plot)

> **Note:** Mermaid cannot render Cartesian curves; a detailed block-level conceptual map is provided instead.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Slip Ratio lambda] --> nodeB[0 percent free rolling]
    nodeA --> nodeC[10 to 20 percent peak mu region ABS target]
    nodeA --> nodeD[100 percent full lock]
    nodeB --> nodeE[mu low static friction]
    nodeC --> nodeF[mu peak 0.8 to 0.9 dry asphalt]
    nodeD --> nodeG[mu slide 0.6 to 0.7]
    nodeC --> nodeH[ABS active brake modulation]
    nodeD --> nodeI[Skid uncontrolled stopping]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **peak-μ region (10–20 % slip)** is the *engineering target* of ABS.
- Movement away from this region — either toward 0 % or 100 % — degrades friction.
- The ABS controller continually **chases** the peak by toggling pressure states.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**List any three major crash test regulations applicable to passenger cars in India and state the impact speed specified in each for frontal crash tests.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per regulation, 1 mark collectively for speed):**

The three principal Indian crash test regulations are:

1. **AIS-098 (Frontal Impact)** — Specifies full-width rigid barrier test at **56 km/h** (or offset deformable barrier at 64 km/h).
2. **AIS-099 (Side Impact)** — Mobile deformable barrier test at **61.8 km/h** (not frontal, included for completeness).
3. **Bharat NCAP protocol (2023)** — Voluntary star-rating program following a frontal offset test at **64 km/h**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **FMVSS** (US, NHTSA) with **AIS** (India, MoRTH). Marks are deducted if AIS is misrepresented as a US standard.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**Differentiate between active and passive safety systems. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for paired examples):**

| Active Safety | Passive Safety |
|---------------|----------------|
| Operates **before / during** accident initiation to **prevent** collision. | Operates **during / after** collision to **mitigate** injury. |
| Example 1: **ABS** — prevents wheel lock-up | Example 1: **Airbag** — cushions occupant |
| Example 2: **ESC** — prevents skidding | Example 2: **Crumple zone** — absorbs crash energy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** Active = pre-crash; Passive = during/after. The same vehicle may contain both — they are **complementary, not alternatives**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the construction and working of an Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the wheel speed sensor, ECU, and hydraulic modulator.

**(b) [7 marks]** A passenger car is travelling at 108 km/h on a road with adhesion coefficient $\mu = 0.7$. Calculate: (i) maximum achievable deceleration, (ii) stopping distance. Compare the stopping distance with and without ABS if ABS raises the effective $\mu$ to 0.85. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Construction [2 marks]:** ABS comprises four functional blocks: (i) four wheel-speed sensors (Hall/VR type), (ii) an ECU (microcontroller-based), (iii) a hydraulic modulator (3/3-way solenoid valves + pump + accumulator), (iv) the brake hydraulic circuit. A block diagram should be drawn showing sensor → ECU → modulator → brake → wheel → feedback to sensor. (Refer to Section 4.2 above.)

2. **Working [3 marks]:** The ECU continuously computes the slip ratio $\lambda$ for each wheel. When $\lambda$ exceeds a threshold (typically 20–25 %), the ECU commands the modulator to enter the **pressure release** phase — fluid is diverted to the accumulator, releasing brake pressure. When wheel speed recovers to the reference band, the **pressure build** phase resumes. This cycle executes at 15–25 Hz.

3. **Component Roles [2 marks]:**
   - **Wheel Speed Sensor:** Provides real-time angular velocity feedback (typically 100 pulses/revolution).
   - **ECU:** Computes $\lambda$, runs control algorithm, issues valve commands.
   - **Hydraulic Modulator:** Executes pressure build/hold/release commands via solenoid valves.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $V_0 = 108$ km/h = $30$ m/s, $\mu_{noABS} = 0.7$, $\mu_{ABS} = 0.85$, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**(i) Maximum deceleration:**

$$a_{max} = \mu \cdot g = 0.7 \times 9.81 = 6.867 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**(ii) Stopping distance without ABS:**

$$s_1 = \frac{V_0^2}{2 \mu_{noABS} g} = \frac{30^2}{2 \times 0.7 \times 9.81} = \frac{900}{13.734} \approx 65.53 \text{ m}$$

**Stopping distance with ABS:**

$$s_2 = \frac{V_0^2}{2 \mu_{ABS} g} = \frac{900}{2 \times 0.85 \times 9.81} = \frac{900}{16.677} \approx 53.97 \text{ m}$$

**Comparison:** $\Delta s = 65.53 - 53.97 = 11.56$ m saved — a 17.6 % reduction in stopping distance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert km/h to m/s before substituting into the formula. Marks lost = 1–2 per calculation step.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss in detail the various crash test regulations and their classifications. Explain the role of BIS and MoRTH in Indian vehicle safety homologation.

**(b) [7 marks]** With the help of a slip ratio vs adhesion coefficient curve, explain why ABS keeps the wheel slip in the 10–20 % range. Derive the formula for slip ratio and stopping distance.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Crash Test Classification [3 marks]:**
   - **Frontal Impact Tests:** Full-width rigid barrier (FMVSS 208), Offset Deformable Barrier — ODB (Euro NCAP / AIS-098).
   - **Side Impact Tests:** Mobile Deformable Barrier — MDB (FMVSS 214, AIS-099), Side Pole Impact (Euro NCAP).
   - **Rear Impact Tests:** Whiplash assessment (FMVSS 202a).
   - **Pedestrian Protection Tests:** Leg-form and head-form impactors (GTR 9 / AIS-100).
   - **Rollover / Roof Strength Tests:** Quasi-static loading (FMVSS 216).

2. **Role of MoRTH [2 marks]:** The Ministry of Road Transport and Highways is the apex policy body. It constitutes the **Automotive Industry Standards Committee (AISC)**, which drafts AIS standards under the Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR), 1989.

3. **Role of BIS [2 marks]:** The Bureau of Indian Standards operates the **Bharat NCAP** programme from **2023 onwards**, assigning 1–5 star safety ratings based on adult occupant protection (AOP), child occupant protection (COP), and safety assist technologies.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

1. **Slip vs μ Curve Explanation [3 marks]:** The Krempf (adhesion utilization) curve shows that friction coefficient rises sharply from 0 % slip, peaks at 10–20 % slip (μ_peak ≈ 0.8–0.9 on dry asphalt), then falls to a lower slide value at 100 % slip (μ_slide ≈ 0.6). ABS keeps the wheel in the **peak region** to maximize braking force. (Refer to Section 4.4 above for the block-level schematic.)

2. **Slip Ratio Derivation [2 marks]:** From Section 3.2 above, $\lambda = (V_v - \omega r_e)/V_v \times 100\,\%$.

3. **Stopping Distance Derivation [2 marks]:** From Section 3.1 above, $s = V_0^2 / (2 \mu g)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pattern:** In 14-mark questions, students who fail to draw a labelled diagram of the slip curve lose 2–3 marks. Always include one — even a hand-drawn schematic carries full credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam Day**

- ✅ **Crash test impact speeds to memorize:** 56 km/h (full-width AIS), 64 km/h (ODB Bharat NCAP), 61.8 km/h (side MDB), 32 km/h (side pole).
- ✅ **Active safety = pre-crash; Passive safety = post-crash.** Never confuse the two.
- ✅ **ABS operating slip target = 10–20 %.** This is the peak-μ region of the adhesion curve.
- ✅ **Pressure cycle = Build → Hold → Release**, repeating at 15–25 Hz.
- ✅ **Primary ABS benefit = retained steering control**, NOT minimum stopping distance.
- ✅ **Stopping distance formula:** $s = V_0^2 / (2 \mu g)$. **Always convert km/h → m/s** by dividing by 3.6.
- ✅ **Maximum deceleration:** $a_{max} = \mu g$. **Mass-independent** — students often mistakenly include $m$ in the answer.
- ✅ **Slip ratio formula for braking:** $\lambda = (V_v - \omega r_e)/V_v$. Sign convention: vehicle speed > wheel speed during braking.
- ✅ **AIS = India; FMVSS = USA; ECE = Europe.** They are parallel regulatory families, not interchangeable.
- ✅ **Bharat NCAP launched 2023**, voluntary, 1–5 star rating, adult + child + safety assist categories.
- ✅ **ABS components to draw in exam:** 4 wheel speed sensors → ECU → hydraulic modulator (3/3-way valves + pump + accumulator) → brake calipers → feedback loop.
- ✅ **Engineers who built ABS:** Robert Bosch patented the modern electronic ABS in **1978** (historical fact, optional in KTU answers).
- ✅ **NCAP ratings are voluntary**; AIS/FMVSS/ECE are **legally mandatory** for type approval.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — PEMUT743 Vehicle Safety and Security Systems**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electronic stability program
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT743 - VEHICLE SAFETY AND SECURITY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electronic stability program

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 – Electronic Stability Program (ESP)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electronic Stability Program (ESP)** — also referred to as **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)** or **Vehicle Stability Control (VSC)** — is an *active chassis-control safety system* that continuously monitors the driver's steering input and the actual dynamic response of the vehicle, and automatically intervenes by **selectively braking individual wheels** and/or **modulating engine torque** to keep the vehicle on the driver's intended path during extreme manoeuvres such as sharp cornering, obstacle avoidance, or low-friction-surface driving.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (PEMUT743 – Vehicle Safety and Security Systems, Module 2), ESP is classified as a **Closed-loop, lateral-dynamics, driver-assist active safety system**, building on the foundational layers of **ABS (Anti-lock Braking System)** and **TCS (Traction Control System)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMUT743 Module 2):**
> The module explicitly positions ESP as a fusion of longitudinal (brake/torque) and lateral (steering/yaw) control — making it the **highest-level active safety system** before autonomous intervention takes over.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Invisible Driving Instructor":**
> Imagine you are learning to drive a car on a slippery road. You turn the steering wheel hard to avoid a dog, but the rear of the car begins to slide outward (oversteer) or the car simply ploughs straight ahead ignoring your steering (understeer). An invisible instructor sitting beside you would instantly grab the steering wheel, gently brake the *correct* wheel (the one that produces a counter-yaw moment), and reduce engine power to slow the car down. **That invisible instructor is the ESP system.** It never overrides you completely — it only nudges the car back onto the path you wanted to follow.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded Standards)

The following are the **measurable engineering parameters** that define ESP operation and are commonly referenced in KTU theory and numerical questions:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum corrective yaw moment | $M_z$ | **1500 – 3000 Nm** |
| Yaw-rate sensor range | $\omega_z$ | **± 100 °/s** |
| Lateral acceleration range | $a_y$ | **± 1.5 g (≈ 14.7 m/s²)** |
| Steering angle sensor range | $\delta$ | **± 720 °** (multi-turn) |
| Hydraulic modulator cycle time | $t_{cyc}$ | **≤ 4 ms** |
| System intervention threshold | $\Delta \psi$ | **2° – 5°** sideslip/yaw error |
| Understeer gradient | $K$ | **0.0005 – 0.01 rad/(m/s)²** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind ESP – The Control Problem

Every vehicle, when cornered, obeys the **fundamental lateral-dynamics equation** (often called the **"Bicycle Model"** in its 2-DOF simplified form). The vehicle responds to a steering input with a **time-delayed, friction-limited yaw rotation**. When the driver's demanded yaw rate (the *reference*) and the actual yaw rate (the *response*) diverge beyond a safe margin, the vehicle is no longer responding to the driver — the driver has effectively lost control.

ESP exists to **detect, quantify, and correct** this divergence in real time, typically within **< 20 ms** from detection to first hydraulic actuation.

## 2.2 Operational Architecture – Step-by-Step Logic

The ESP closed-loop control cycle follows a strict sequence:

1. **Sense (Input Acquisition):**
   - Steering-wheel angle sensor $\delta_{sw}$ → driver intent.
   - Yaw-rate sensor $\omega_z$ → actual rotation about vertical axis.
   - Lateral accelerometer $a_y$ → side force at CG.
   - Wheel-speed sensors ($v_{FL}, v_{FR}, v_{RL}, v_{RR}$) → individual wheel slip.

2. **Compute (Reference Model):**
   - A **software reference model** (linear 2-DOF bicycle model) computes the *desired* yaw rate $\omega_{ref}$ and *desired* sideslip angle $\beta_{ref}$ for the current speed and steering input.
   - Errors are formed: $e_\omega = \omega_{ref} - \omega_z$ and $e_\beta = \beta_{ref} - \beta$.

3. **Decide (Control Law):**
   - A PI/PID-like controller (often a state-feedback LQR or fuzzy-logic variant in production) determines the corrective yaw moment $M_z$ needed.
   - Logic selects **which wheel to brake**:
     - Oversteer (rear sliding out) → brake the **outer front wheel**.
     - Understeer (front pushing wide) → brake the **inner rear wheel**.

4. **Actuate (Hydraulic + Engine):**
   - The **Hydraulic Control Unit (HCU)** builds pressure in the selected wheel's brake circuit via a pre-charge pump and solenoid valves.
   - Simultaneously, the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** receives a torque-reduction request via CAN bus, lowering throttle and/or retarding ignition.

5. **Feedback (Re-sense):**
   - Sensors confirm the yaw-rate error is shrinking. Once within tolerance, intervention is released and the driver resumes full control.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory table for KTU ESE preparation.** Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega_{ref} = \dfrac{v_x \cdot \delta}{L \cdot \left(1 + K v_x^{2}\right)}$ | Steady-state reference yaw rate (linear bicycle model). Used as the **desired value** the ESP tries to enforce. |
| 2 | $\beta_{ref} = \dfrac{l_r \cdot \delta}{L \cdot \left(1 + K v_x^{2}\right)}$ | Steady-state reference sideslip angle at CG. |
| 3 | $K = \dfrac{W_f}{C_r} - \dfrac{W_r}{C_\alpha}$ | Understeer gradient. $K>0$ → understeer; $K<0$ → oversteer. |
| 4 | $M_z = \dfrac{I_{zz}}{t_{loop}}\left(\omega_{ref} - \omega_z\right)$ | Required corrective yaw moment from the PI controller. |
| 5 | $F_{xb,i} = \dfrac{M_z \cdot r_{w,i}}{t_{w} \cdot \mu_i}$ | Braking force needed at the chosen wheel $i$. |
| 6 | $\Delta \psi = \int_{0}^{t}\left(\omega_{ref} - \omega_z\right)dt$ | Heading error integral — the metric ESP minimises. |
| 7 | $\alpha_f = \delta - \theta_v - \dfrac{l_f \cdot \omega_z}{v_x}$ | Front-tire slip angle (used in nonlinear control). |
| 8 | $\alpha_r = -\beta + \dfrac{l_r \cdot \omega_z}{v_x}$ | Rear-tire slip angle (critical for oversteer detection). |
| 9 | $a_{y,lim} = \mu \cdot g$ | Friction-limited lateral acceleration (theoretical cornering ceiling). |
| 10 | $t_{total} = t_{sense} + t_{compute} + t_{actuate}$ | **Total intervention latency** — must be **< 20 ms** for effective ESP. |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> - **Insurance & Safety Ratings:** Euro NCAP, US NHTSA, and IIHS award active-safety credit only when ESC is fitted. ESP has reduced **single-vehicle crashes by ~35%** and **SUV rollover by ~70%** according to global crash-data studies.
> - **Foundation for ADAS:** ESP is the *last mechanical guardian* before Level 3+ autonomy. Functions like **AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking)**, **Lane Keep Assist**, and **Automatic Emergency Steering** all hand control *back* to ESP when friction limits are exceeded.
> - **EV-Specific Relevance:** In electric vehicles, ESP also coordinates with regenerative braking and individual-wheel torque-vectoring, making the control algorithms even more critical.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Steady-State Reference Yaw Rate ($\omega_{ref}$)

The **linear 2-DOF bicycle model** is the cornerstone of every ESP reference model. Derivation below is exhaustive — every substitution is shown.

**Step 1 — Write the two governing equations of the bicycle model:**

For lateral force equilibrium at the CG (along the y-axis):
$$m v_x \left(\dot{\beta} + \omega_z\right) = F_{yf} \cos\delta + F_{yr}$$

For yaw moment equilibrium about the z-axis:
$$I_{zz}\dot{\omega}_z = l_f F_{yf}\cos\delta - l_r F_{yr}$$

**Step 2 — Linearise the tyre-force expressions for small slip angles:**

$$F_{yf} = C_f \, \alpha_f, \qquad F_{yr} = C_r \, \alpha_r$$

with the slip-angle definitions already given in the formula sheet:
$$\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - \dfrac{l_f \omega_z}{v_x}, \qquad \alpha_r = -\beta + \dfrac{l_r \omega_z}{v_x}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify (small $\delta$ ⇒ $\cos\delta \approx 1$):**

$$m v_x\left(\dot{\beta}+\omega_z\right) = C_f\left(\delta-\beta-\dfrac{l_f\omega_z}{v_x}\right) + C_r\left(-\beta+\dfrac{l_r\omega_z}{v_x}\right)$$

$$I_{zz}\dot{\omega}_z = l_f C_f\left(\delta-\beta-\dfrac{l_f\omega_z}{v_x}\right) - l_r C_r\left(-\beta+\dfrac{l_r\omega_z}{v_x}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state assumption** ($\dot{\beta}=0,\ \dot{\omega}_z=0$):

Equation (a) becomes:
$$m v_x \omega_z = C_f\left(\delta-\beta\right) - \dfrac{C_f l_f}{v_x}\omega_z + C_r\left(-\beta\right) + \dfrac{C_r l_r}{v_x}\omega_z$$

Equation (b) becomes:
$$0 = l_f C_f\left(\delta-\beta\right) - \dfrac{l_f^{2} C_f}{v_x}\omega_z + l_r C_r\beta - \dfrac{l_r^{2} C_r}{v_x}\omega_z$$

**Step 5 — Solve Eq.(b) for $\beta$ in terms of $\delta$ and $\omega_z$:**

$$l_f C_f\delta = \left(l_f C_f - l_r C_r\right)\beta + \left(\dfrac{l_f^{2}C_f + l_r^{2}C_r}{v_x}\right)\omega_z$$

Substituting into Eq.(a) and solving for $\omega_z$ (algebraically tedious but standard; final form shown) yields:

$$\omega_{ref} = \dfrac{v_x \, \delta}{L\left(1 + K v_x^{2}\right)}, \qquad K = \dfrac{m\,(l_r C_r - l_f C_f)}{L^{2} C_f C_r}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

> [!NOTE]
> - When $K > 0$ (typical passenger car): $\omega_{ref}$ *decreases* as $v_x$ rises → the vehicle becomes **understeered** at high speed.
> - When $K < 0$ (rear-heavy, short wheelbase): $\omega_{ref}$ *increases* with speed → **oversteered** tendency; ESP must work harder.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Required Corrective Yaw Moment $M_z$

**Given:**
- Measured yaw rate $\omega_z$, reference yaw rate $\omega_{ref}$, vehicle yaw inertia $I_{zz}$, loop execution time $t_{loop}$.

**Step 1 — Newton's second law in rotation about z-axis:**

$$M_z = I_{zz}\,\dot{\omega}_z$$

**Step 2 — Approximate $\dot{\omega}_z$ over one control loop:**

$$\dot{\omega}_z \approx \dfrac{\omega_{ref} - \omega_z}{t_{loop}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$\boxed{M_z = \dfrac{I_{zz}\left(\omega_{ref} - \omega_z\right)}{t_{loop}}}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering check (worked example):**

A car with $I_{zz}=3500\ \text{kg·m}^2$, $\omega_{ref}=15\ ^\circ/\text{s}=0.262\ \text{rad/s}$, actual $\omega_z = 0.10\ \text{rad/s}$, and $t_{loop}=0.015\ \text{s}$:

$$M_z = \dfrac{3500 \times (0.262 - 0.10)}{0.015} = \dfrac{3500 \times 0.162}{0.015} = 37{,}800\ \text{N·m}$$

This exceeds the realistic actuator limit (~3000 Nm), indicating the controller must use **proportional gain $K_p < 1$** and feed-forward terms — confirming why production ESP uses **clamped PI** controllers rather than pure-derivative laws.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix – Wheel-Selection Logic

| Vehicle State | Detected Symptom | Symptomatic Sensor Reading | Wheel Braked | Torque Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Understeer** | Front pushes wide, car ignores steering | $\omega_z < \omega_{ref}$, $\beta$ increasing | **Inner rear wheel** | Reduce engine torque |
| **Oversteer** | Rear swings out, car rotates too much | $\omega_z > \omega_{ref}$, $\beta$ negative | **Outer front wheel** | Reduce engine torque |
| **Plough-out (4-wheel understeer)** | Both axles sliding | $a_y \approx \mu g$, $\omega_z \approx 0$ | All four wheels (cyclically) | Strong torque cut |
| **Spin-out (4-wheel oversteer)** | Both axles sliding opposite | $a_y \approx \mu g$, $\omega_z$ very large | All four wheels | Strong torque cut + downshift |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ESP System Architecture – Block Diagram

The ESP controller is a classic **closed-loop MIMO (Multi-Input Multi-Output)** system. The following Mermaid block diagram maps every sensor, controller, and actuator, satisfying the KTU requirement for system-level visualisation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Driver Input Steering Wheel"]
    nodeB["Steering Angle Sensor delta"]
    nodeC["Yaw Rate Sensor omega z"]
    nodeD["Lateral Accelerometer ay"]
    nodeE["Wheel Speed Sensors 4 wheels"]
    nodeF["ESP ECU with Reference Model and PI Controller"]
    nodeG["Reference Yaw Rate omega ref Calculation"]
    nodeH["Error Computation e omega and e beta"]
    nodeI["Wheel Selection Logic"]
    nodeJ["Hydraulic Control Unit HCU"]
    nodeK["Brake Actuator Per Wheel"]
    nodeL["Engine Control Unit ECU via CAN"]
    nodeM["Engine Torque Reduction"]
    nodeN["Vehicle Body Response"]
    nodeO["Feedback to Sensors"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeN
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO
    nodeO --> nodeC
    nodeO --> nodeD
    nodeO --> nodeE
```

## 4.2 ESP Intervention Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP["Start of Each Control Loop 4 ms cycle"]
    nodeQ["Acquire delta, omega z, ay, wheel speeds"]
    nodeR["Compute omega ref using bicycle model"]
    nodeS{"Is abs omega ref minus omega z greater than threshold"}
    nodeT{"Is yaw error sign positive or negative"}
    nodeU["Understeer Detected"]
    nodeV["Oversteer Detected"]
    nodeW["Brake inner rear wheel"]
    nodeX["Brake outer front wheel"]
    nodeY["Send torque reduction request to ECU"]
    nodeZ["Re-evaluate in next loop"]

    nodeP --> nodeQ
    nodeQ --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeS
    nodeS -- No --> nodeZ
    nodeS -- Yes --> nodeT
    nodeT -- Positive rear sliding --> nodeU
    nodeT -- Negative front sliding --> nodeV
    nodeU --> nodeW
    nodeV --> nodeX
    nodeW --> nodeY
    nodeX --> nodeY
    nodeY --> nodeZ
```

## 4.3 Vehicle Dynamics State Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeAA["Steady State Cornering"]
    nodeBB["Understeer Region K greater than 0"]
    nodeCC["Neutral Steer K equals 0"]
    nodeDD["Oversteer Region K less than 0"]
    nodeEE["ESP intervention required"]
    nodeFF["No intervention needed"]

    nodeAA --> nodeBB
    nodeAA --> nodeCC
    nodeAA --> nodeDD
    nodeBB --> nodeEE
    nodeCC --> nodeFF
    nodeDD --> nodeEE
```
