## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define a Parking Assist System. List any four components used in it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

A **Parking Assist System (PAS)** is an advanced driver assistance system (ADAS) that uses sensors, cameras, and electronic control units to help a driver safely park a vehicle by detecting obstacles, measuring parking space, and either warning the driver or autonomously controlling steering and braking.

**Four components (1/2 mark each):**
1. **Ultrasonic sensors** — mounted on bumpers for distance measurement
2. **Rear-view camera** — provides visual feedback
3. **Parking ECU** — processes sensor data and generates control commands
4. **Electric Power Steering (EPS) actuator** — executes autonomous steering

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected]**

**Q2. With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of an ultrasonic parking sensor using the Time-of-Flight (ToF) method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

The ultrasonic parking sensor works on the principle of **Time-of-Flight (ToF) measurement**. The ECU sends a **trigger pulse** to the piezoelectric element in the sensor, which vibrates and emits an **ultrasonic wave at ~58 kHz** into the air. This wave travels at the **speed of sound (c = 343 m/s)**, strikes an obstacle, and reflects back as an **echo** to the same sensor. The ECU measures the **round-trip time (t)** and computes the distance using the formula:

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}
$$

This distance is then mapped to appropriate **visual and audible warnings** for the driver. A simple block sketch should be drawn showing: **ECU → Trigger → Sensor → Wave emission → Obstacle → Echo → Sensor → ECU → Display**.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Actual Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a parking assist system** with a block diagram. Describe the **search, measure, warn, and intervene** phases in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of an **ultrasonic sensor** used in parking assist systems. Explain the **Time-of-Flight formula** and compute the distance when the round-trip time is **1.5 ms**. Take $c = 343$ m/s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**(a) Working Principle of PAS (7 marks):**

The parking assist system operates in **four sequential phases**:

**Phase 1 — Space Search (2 marks):**
- Activated when vehicle speed < 30 km/h and reverse gear engaged.
- Side-mounted ultrasonic sensors emit lateral pulses.
- ECU records echo times as the vehicle passes potential parking spots.
- Integrates velocity over time to compute the space length.
- Compares with $L_{req} = L_{veh} + 1.0$ m. If satisfied, recommends the space.

**Phase 2 — Distance Measurement (2 marks):**
- All 8–12 sensors emit periodic ultrasonic pulses.
- Each ECU channel measures the **round-trip time (t)**.
- Distance calculated as $d = c \cdot t / 2$.
- Updated 10–20 times per second.

**Phase 3 — Warning Generation (1.5 marks):**
- Visual: Bird's-eye view on infotainment screen with colour zones (green/yellow/red).
- Audible: Variable-frequency beeps (slow → fast → continuous).
- Haptic: Steering wheel vibration (in premium systems).

**Phase 4 — Intervention (1.5 marks):**
- In semi-automatic mode: System controls steering via EPS.
- In full-automatic mode: System also controls accelerator (via drive-by-wire) and brake (via ESP).
- Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB-P) applies full brake if obstacle detected < 0.3 m and driver is non-responsive.

**Block Diagram** (1 mark): Must show Sensors → ECU → Actuators → HMI as drawn in Section 4.1.

---

**(b) Ultrasonic Sensor Construction, Working & Numerical (7 marks):**

**Construction (2 marks):**
- **Housing**: Sealed plastic capsule (PBT or ABS) mounted in bumper cut-outs.
- **Piezoelectric element**: Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT) disc that converts electrical energy to mechanical vibration and vice versa.
- **Membrane**: Protects the piezo element from water/dust while allowing ultrasonic transmission.
- **Connector**: 3-pin connector (power, ground, signal) wired to the Parking ECU via CAN/LIN.
- **Sealing**: IP6K7 rated for weather resistance.

**Working (2.5 marks):**
- ECU sends a **trigger pulse** (~10–20 V, 0.2 ms wide).
- The piezo element vibrates at its **resonant frequency (~58 kHz)**, emitting an ultrasonic wave.
- The wave travels at **c = 343 m/s**, strikes an obstacle, and reflects.
- The same piezo element receives the echo and converts it back to a small AC voltage (~mV range).
- The ECU amplifies, filters, and timestamps this echo.
- The **ToF formula** $d = (c \cdot t)/2$ is applied.

**Numerical (2.5 marks):**

Given: $t = 1.5$ ms $= 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ s, $c = 343$ m/s.

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{343 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2}
$$

$$
d = \frac{0.5145}{2} = 0.25725 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{d \approx 0.257 \text{ m} \approx 25.7 \text{ cm}}
$$

**Interpretation**: The obstacle is 25.7 cm away. This is in the **critical near zone (< 30 cm)**, so the system will trigger a **continuous warning tone** and may apply **automatic emergency braking** in AEB-P equipped vehicles.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** List and explain the **major components of a parking assist system** with their functions and typical specifications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **passive (PDC), semi-automatic (SAP), and fully automatic (FAP) parking assist systems**. Explain with a real-world example of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**(a) Major Components of PAS (7 marks):**

*(At least 8 components must be covered for full marks. Each component: 0.75 mark.)*

1. **Ultrasonic Sensors** (Front + Rear, 4–12 nos.): Emit and receive ~58 kHz ultrasonic waves; range 0.25–5 m; beam cone 60°.
2. **Rear-View Camera**: Mounted above number plate; provides wide-angle (130°) video to infotainment screen.
3. **Surround-View Cameras** (4 nos.): Located at front, rear, and both side mirrors; generate 360° bird's-eye view via image stitching.
4. **Corner Radar (SRR)**: 24 GHz or 77 GHz; detects cross-traffic and cyclists in blind spots; range ~30 m.
5. **Parking ECU**: Master processor; runs AUTOSAR OS; performs sensor fusion, path planning, and decision logic.
6. **Electric Power Steering (EPS)**: Provides torque overlay capability > 5 Nm for autonomous steering.
7. **ESP/Brake Actuator**: Enables automatic braking intervention during AEB-P.
8. **HMI Display**: 7"–12.3" TFT/LCD; shows bird's-eye view, distance bars, and warning icons.
9. **CAN Bus Interface**: 500 kbps backbone for inter-ECU communication.
10. **Wheel Speed Sensors & Gear Position Sensor**: Provide vehicle speed and reverse-gear status.

---

**(b) PDC vs. SAP vs. FAP (7 marks):**

| Feature | **PDC (Park Distance Control)** | **SAP (Semi-Auto Parking)** | **FAP (Fully Auto Parking)** |
|---------|--------------------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Driver Input** | Full control | Steering auto; pedals manual | All controls auto |
| **Activation** | Reverse gear engaged | Button press + reverse | Button press + auto |
| **Steering Control** | Driver only | System controls | System controls |
| **Pedal Control** | Driver only | Driver controls | System controls (brake + throttle) |
| **Gear Shifting** | Driver only | Driver manually shifts | System auto-shifts |
| **System Complexity** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Cost** | Low (₹5,000–15,000) | Medium (₹30,000–80,000) | High (₹1,00,000+) |
| **Examples** | Maruti Suzuki Alto, base Hyundai i20 | Hyundai Venue, Tata Nexon | Tesla Autopark, BMW Parking Assistant Plus, Mercedes Active Parking Assist |
| **SAE Level** | Level 0 (warning only) | Level 1 (steering assist) | Level 2 (combined control) |
| **Driver Monitoring** | Required at all times | Required (pedals) | Can momentarily relax |

**Real-world example (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **PDC Example**: A Maruti Suzuki Swift reversing into a tight spot — the system beeps faster as the rear bumper approaches the wall. The driver must steer and brake manually.

2. **SAP Example**: A Hyundai Creta driver parallel parks using the system — the steering wheel turns automatically while the driver controls the accelerator and brake.

3. **FAP Example**: A Tesla Model 3 driver double-taps the Autopark button — the system automatically detects a space, shifts gears, steers, accelerates, and brakes, with the driver ready to take over.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Park Assist" with "Park Pilot"** — Park Assist actively controls steering, while Park Pilot only provides warnings.
> 2. **Wrong unit of speed of sound** — Always use **c = 343 m/s at 20°C** in SI units; do not substitute in cm/s unless explicitly asked.
> 3. **Forgetting to divide by 2 in ToF formula** — The wave travels to **and from** the obstacle, so the total path is $2d$, not $d$. This is the **single most common error** in KTU numerical answers.
> 4. **Conflating "lateral" and "longitudinal" distances** — Lateral sensors measure the **gap width** (perpendicular to vehicle), not the gap length.
> 5. **Missing the activation conditions** — Always state the **speed threshold (< 30 km/h)** and **reverse gear condition** in descriptive answers.
> 6. **Incorrect ToF numerical rounding** — Final answers must be in **metres (m)** with 2–3 decimal places; avoid writing "20 cm" without showing the calculation.
> 7. **Confusing LiDAR, Radar, and Ultrasonic ranges** — Ultrasonic: < 5 m, Radar: 30–250 m, LiDAR: up to 200 m. Examiners deduct marks for mixing these up.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision — Must Memorize for KTU Board Exam:**

- ✅ **Full form**: PAS = Parking Assist System; PDC = Park Distance Control; SAP = Semi-Automatic Parking; FAP = Fully Automatic Parking; AEB-P = Automatic Emergency Braking - Pedestrian; SRR = Short Range Radar.
- ✅ **Activation threshold**: Vehicle speed **< 30 km/h** (or **< 15 km/h** in some OEM implementations) + reverse gear engaged OR PAS button pressed.
- ✅ **Time-of-Flight formula** (MUST memorize): $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ where $c = 343$ m/s, $t$ in seconds, $d$ in metres.
- ✅ **Ultrasonic frequency range**: **40 kHz – 70 kHz** (standard = **58 kHz**).
- ✅ **Ultrasonic detection range**: **0.25 m to 5.0 m**; minimum object size ~3 cm vertical pole.
- ✅ **Sensor count on a modern PAS**: **8 to 12 ultrasonic sensors** (4 front, 4 rear, 2 side optional) + **1 rear camera** + **4 surround-view cameras** (in premium).
- ✅ **Beam cone angle**: ~**60°** for typical ultrasonic sensor.
- ✅ **Sensor placement**: Flush-mounted in front and rear bumpers, side mirrors, and rear tailgate.
- ✅ **Warning zones**:
   - **Green zone**: > 1.5 m → No warning
   - **Yellow zone**: 0.5 m – 1.5 m → Slow beep
   - **Orange zone**: 0.25 m – 0.5 m → Fast beep
   - **Red zone**: < 0.25 m → Continuous tone + AEB intervention
- ✅ **Major OEMs** (for examples): Bosch ParkPilot, Valeo Park4U, Continental, Hyundai Smart Parking Assist, Tesla Autopark, BMW Parking Assistant Plus, Mercedes Active Parking Assist.
- ✅ **Standards to mention**: **SAE J3016** (AD levels), **ISO 15006**, **ISO 17386** (parking aids), **Euro NCAP** (reversing AEB scoring).
- ✅ **Four layers of PAS**: **Sensing → Processing → Actuation → HMI**.
- ✅ **Communication protocol**: **CAN bus (500 kbps)** for inter-ECU communication; some sensors use **LIN bus (19.2 kbps)** for cost reduction.
- ✅ **Mandatory components** (for 7-mark component question): Ultrasonic sensor, Rear-view camera, Parking ECU, EPS, Brake actuator, HMI display, Wheel speed sensor, Gear position sensor.
- ✅ **Why ultrasonic, not radar, for parking**: Cost-effectiveness at short range, robustness against weather (rain, dust), and ability to detect small objects (poles, kerbs) at < 5 m.
- ✅ **Difference between camera, radar, ultrasonic, and LiDAR** (commonly asked in 3-mark comparison):
   - **Camera**: 2D image, weather-sensitive, recognizes signage.
   - **Radar**: Long range, weather-immune, measures velocity.
   - **Ultrasonic**: Short range, cheap, measures distance only.
   - **LiDAR**: 3D point cloud, very accurate, expensive.
- ✅ **Path planning algorithms** (for 14-mark answer): Trigonometric arc-based, Bezier curve, polynomial trajectory, RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree) in research-level systems.
- ✅ **Failure modes to mention** (gains extra marks): Sensor blocked by mud/snow, ECU failure, EPS failure, CAN bus communication loss, software bug — system must fail safely with driver alert.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Driver assistance systems in automobiles: Definitions and terminology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT743 - VEHICLE SAFETY AND SECURITY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Driver assistance systems in automobiles: Definitions and terminology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Driver Assistance Systems (DAS / ADAS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Driver Assistance Systems (DAS)**, more formally known as **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)**, are a category of electronic and electromechanical systems embedded within a road vehicle that assist the human driver in the dynamic driving task. They use **sensors, actuators, controllers and human–machine interfaces (HMI)** to perceive the environment, interpret driving conditions, and either **warn the driver** or **actively intervene** in vehicle control to improve **safety, comfort and efficiency**.

According to the **SAE International Standard J3063** and the **European Commission’s GEAR 2030 strategy**, ADAS are defined as systems that:
- Continuously **sense** the vehicle’s internal state and the external driving environment.
- **Process** this information using on-board electronic control units (ECUs).
- **Act** on the vehicle’s longitudinal (throttle/brake) and lateral (steering) dynamics — or warn the driver through visual, auditory, or haptic cues.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Co-Pilot" Intuition

Imagine a professional race car driver sitting beside you while you drive. He is **not** holding the steering wheel. Instead, he:
- **Watches** the road ahead, the blind spots, and the dashboard gauges.
- **Warns** you ("Caution! Car in blind spot!").
- Sometimes **physically pushes your hand** to correct the steering, or **taps the brake** for you to prevent a crash.

That race driver is your **ADAS** — a vigilant co-pilot that senses, decides, and acts (or warns) to keep you safe. The human driver remains legally and primarily responsible for the vehicle.

## 1.3 Fundamental Terminology — Safety Taxonomy

A clear understanding of ADAS requires mastery of the surrounding safety vocabulary. The table below introduces the **core terminology** that KTU examiners expect students to confidently define.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Pillars of Vehicle Safety**:
> - **Passive Safety** — protects occupants *during and after* a crash (seat belts, airbags, crumple zones).
> - **Active Safety** — *prevents* the crash from occurring (ADAS, ABS, ESC).

| Term | Full Form | One-line Definition |
|------|-----------|---------------------|
| **ADAS** | Advanced Driver Assistance System | Electronic systems aiding the driver to improve safety |
| **HMI** | Human–Machine Interface | Interface between driver and ADAS (display, buzzer, steering feedback) |
| **DDT** | Dynamic Driving Task | Real-time operational and tactical functions required to drive |
| **OEDR** | Object and Event Detection and Response | Sub-task of DDT — perceive, recognize, classify, respond |
| **LCA** | Lane Centering Assist | Keeps vehicle within lane centerline |
| **LKA** | Lane Keeping Aid/Assist | Applies corrective steering to prevent lane departure |
| **LDW** | Lane Departure Warning | Warns driver if vehicle crosses lane markings |
| **FCW** | Forward Collision Warning | Alerts driver of imminent frontal collision |
| **AEB** | Autonomous Emergency Braking | Automatically applies brakes to avoid/reduce impact |
| **ACC** | Adaptive Cruise Control | Cruise control that adapts speed to preceding vehicle |
| **BSD** | Blind Spot Detection | Detects vehicles in driver’s blind zones |
| **NV** | Night Vision | Enhances driver visibility in low-light conditions |
| **ESC** | Electronic Stability Control | Selective braking to maintain directional control |
| **ABS** | Anti-lock Braking System | Prevents wheel lock-up during hard braking |
| **TCS** | Traction Control System | Prevents wheel spin during acceleration |
| **EPS** | Electric Power Steering | Stealing-by-wire system enabling automated steering |
| **V2X** | Vehicle-to-Everything | Communication of vehicle with other entities |
| **HAD** | Highly Automated Driving | Driving without driver attention in defined ODD |

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Module 3 of PEMUT743 is the **definitional foundation** for nearly all subsequent modules. Examiners frequently ask **2-mark and 3-mark short-answer questions** that demand **precise definitions** and **category-mapping** of ADAS subsystems. Marks are awarded for **terminology accuracy**, not for lengthy explanations.

**Key Standard Metrics Students Must Memorize**:
- **NHTSA / Euro NCAP scoring** uses star ratings (**5** = safest).
- **Euro NCAP AEB Pedestrian** test speed range: **5 km/h to 60 km/h**.
- **Forward Collision Warning (FCW) typical activation time**: **2.7 seconds** before impact.
- **LDW activation threshold**: lateral position deviation > **0.3 m to 0.5 m**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Driver Assistance Systems

Driver assistance systems are classified by **the function they perform**, the **degree of vehicle control they assume**, and the **level of driving automation** (per SAE J3016). Understanding this classification is essential for KTU short-answer and long-answer questions.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Function (Most Important for KTU)

The European Union’s **PReVENT**, **INTERSAFE-2**, and **AdaptIVe** research frameworks classify ADAS into five functional clusters:

1. **Sensing and Perception Systems** — Environment data acquisition.
2. **Warning Systems** — Alert driver without taking control.
3. **Comfort and Convenience Systems** — Reduce driver workload.
4. **Active Control / Intervention Systems** — Take partial longitudinal/lateral control.
5. **Cooperative and Connected Systems** — Use V2X communication.

### 2.1.2 Classification by Degree of Automation (SAE J3016)

> [!NOTE]
> The **SAE J3016 standard** defines **6 levels of driving automation** (0 to 5). The driver and the system share responsibility differently at each level.

| SAE Level | Name | Driver Role | System Role | Example ADAS |
|-----------|------|-------------|-------------|--------------|
| **0** | No Automation | Full control | None | Conventional car |
| **1** | Driver Assistance | Full attention, shared control | Steer **OR** brake/accelerate | **ACC, LKA** |
| **2** | Partial Automation | Must monitor | Steer **AND** brake/accelerate | **Tesla Autopilot, GM Super Cruise** |
| **3** | Conditional Automation | May disengage attention | Full DDT within ODD | **Traffic Jam Pilot (Audi A8)** |
| **4** | High Automation | No attention within ODD | Full DDT, no fallback | **Robotaxis in geofenced zones** |
| **5** | Full Automation | No driver needed | All conditions, everywhere | **Theoretical full autonomy** |

### 2.1.3 Classification by Vehicle Dynamics Domain

- **Longitudinal Control Systems** — Govern the **forward/backward** motion along the vehicle’s travel axis. Examples: **ACC, AEB, ACC Stop & Go, Intelligent Speed Assistance (ISA)**.
- **Lateral Control Systems** — Govern the **side-to-side** motion (steering). Examples: **LKA, Lane Centering, Auto-Steering, Parking Assist**.
- **Combined Longitudinal-Lateral Systems** — Control both axes simultaneously. Examples: **Highway Chauffeur, Traffic Jam Assist, Self-Parking**.

### 2.1.4 Classification by Level of Intervention

| Intervention Type | Definition | KTU-Relevant Example |
|-------------------|------------|----------------------|
| **Informative** | Displays info to driver | Speed limit sign recognition |
| **Warning (Advisory)** | Alerts driver without action | **LDW, FCW, BSD** |
| **Active Intervention (Corrective)** | Modifies driver input briefly | **ESC, AEB, LKA** |
| **Continuous Assistance** | Sustained control with driver oversight | **ACC, Highway Assist** |
| **Automated Driving** | Driver disengaged (Level 3+) | **Traffic Jam Pilot** |

## 2.2 Operational Logic of a Generic ADAS — Six Sequential Stages

Every ADAS, regardless of complexity, follows the same **sense → plan → act** cycle (popularized by the **DARPA Grand Challenge** architecture). This is a high-yield topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 6-Stage ADAS Operational Cycle** (Frequently tested in 7-mark sub-questions):
> 1. **Sensing / Perception** — Radar, lidar, camera, ultrasonic, GPS, IMU gather raw data.
> 2. **Sensor Fusion** — Combine multi-sensor data using **Kalman filter** or **Bayesian networks** to form a unified world model.
> 3. **Object Detection and Classification** — Detect, classify, and track road users (vehicles, pedestrians, cyclists).
> 4. **Situation Assessment** — Evaluate risk, time-to-collision (TTC), and driver attention.
> 5. **Decision Making** — Choose appropriate action (warn, brake, steer) using rule-based or AI-based algorithms.
> 6. **Actuation / HMI** — Send commands to throttle, brake, steering, or display.

## 2.3 Core Sensing Modalities — Terminology and Trade-offs

| Sensor | Range | Strength | Weakness | KTU-Standard Use |
|--------|-------|----------|----------|------------------|
| **Radar** (24 / 77 GHz) | 0.2 – 250 m | Weather-robust, measures velocity directly | Low angular resolution | **ACC, AEB, BSD** |
| **Lidar** | Up to 200 m | High-resolution 3D point cloud | Cost, weather sensitivity | **Highway Pilot, Robotaxis** |
| **Camera (Mono/Stereo)** | 50 – 250 m | Reads signs, lanes, lights | Affected by glare/darkness | **LDW, TSR, FCW** |
| **Ultrasonic** | 0.1 – 5 m | Cheap, robust at low speed | Short range | **Parking Assist** |
| **GPS / GNSS** | Global | Absolute position | Tunnels, urban canyons | **Navigation, V2X** |
| **IMU (Inertial)** | Local | High-frequency motion | Drifts over time | **Dead reckoning, ESC** |

## 2.4 Operational Domain and the Concept of ODD

> [!NOTE]
> **ODD — Operational Design Domain**: The specific operating conditions (weather, road type, time of day, speed range, geographic area) under which an ADAS is designed to function. SAE J3016 explicitly requires ODD declaration for Levels 3–5 systems.

For example, an **AEB system** may have its ODD defined as:
- **Speed range**: 5–80 km/h
- **Road**: Dry/wet paved roads
- **Targets**: Passenger cars, pedestrians, cyclists
- **Exclusions**: Off-road, snow-covered surfaces

## 2.5 Terminology — Driver-in-the-Loop Concepts

- **Hands-on Detection** — Steering wheel torque/capacitive sensors detect driver grip (used in Level 2 systems like **BMW Driving Assistant Plus**).
- **Eyes-on Road Monitoring** — Driver monitoring camera (DMS) ensures driver attention (used in Level 2+ systems).
- **Take-over Request (TOR)** — System-initiated prompt to the human driver to resume control in Level 3 (e.g., **Audi AI Traffic Jam Pilot**).
- **Minimal Risk Maneuver (MRM)** — The fallback strategy executed when the driver fails to take over; the vehicle slows to a safe stop, often activating hazard lights.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Although this topic is heavily conceptual, certain **quantitative relationships** appear in numerical questions. The most important ones are summarized below.

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | **$TTC = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta v}$** | Time-to-Collision = distance gap / relative velocity | seconds |
| 2 | **$a_{req} = \dfrac{(\Delta v)^2}{2 \cdot \Delta d}$** | Required deceleration to avoid collision | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| 3 | **$S_{safe} = v \cdot t_{react} + \dfrac{v^2}{2 \cdot a_{max}}$** | Safe following distance (with reaction time) | meters |
| 4 | **$R_{lat} = \dfrac{v^2}{a_{y,max}}$** | Curve radius limited by lateral acceleration | meters |
| 5 | **$v_{lat,lim} = \sqrt{a_{y,max} \cdot R}$** | Maximum lateral speed in a curve | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 6 | **$\dot{\psi}_{max} = \dfrac{a_{y}}{v}$** | Maximum yaw rate for vehicle stability | $\text{rad/s}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the formulas above, the boundary conditions are:
> - $\Delta d$ = distance between vehicles (m)
> - $\Delta v$ = relative speed (m/s)
> - $t_{react}$ = driver reaction time ≈ **1.0–1.5 s** (standard KTU assumption)
> - $a_{max}$ = maximum achievable deceleration ≈ **8 m/s²** on dry asphalt

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

These definitions are not academic trivia. They are the **engineering contract** between:
- **OEMs** (Original Equipment Manufacturers) like **Toyota, Bosch, Continental**.
- **Tier-1 suppliers** like **Bosch, ZF, Aptiv, Mobileye, Velodyne**.
- **Regulators** like **Euro NCAP, NHTSA, IIHS, GSR (EU General Safety Regulation 2019/2144)**.

A precise definition in a **Functional Safety (ISO 26262)** document can decide whether a vehicle passes **type approval** and whether it qualifies for **5-star Euro NCAP ratings**. **From 2022, AEB, LDW, ISA, and DDA (Driver Drowsiness Attention) are mandatory** for new type approvals in the EU.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Time-to-Collision (TTC) for an ADAS Warning Trigger

The **Time-to-Collision (TTC)** is the cornerstone metric of all forward-looking ADAS. The following derivation is a frequent KTU 7-mark question.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a host vehicle (H) following a preceding vehicle (P) on a straight road. Both are moving in the same direction.

Let:
- $v_H$ = velocity of host vehicle (m/s)
- $v_P$ = velocity of preceding vehicle (m/s)
- $d$ = bumper-to-bumper distance (m)
- $d_{safe}$ = minimum safe distance (m)

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Relative Velocity**

The closing speed (relative velocity) between the two vehicles is the difference of their absolute velocities.

$$
\Delta v = v_H - v_P
$$

**Step 2 — Geometric Definition of TTC**

By definition, time-to-collision is the time remaining before the host vehicle catches up to the preceding one, assuming constant relative speed.

$$
TTC = \dfrac{\text{current gap}}{\text{closing speed}}
$$

Mathematically, this is:

$$
TTC = \dfrac{d}{\Delta v}
$$

**Step 3 — Critical Condition (Imminent Collision)**

A collision occurs if and only if the relative displacement equals the gap. Setting $d(t) = 0$ at impact gives:

$$
d(t) = d_0 - \Delta v \cdot t = 0
$$

Solving for $t$ yields the time-to-collision:

$$
t = \dfrac{d_0}{\Delta v}
$$

**Step 4 — FCW Activation Threshold**

Most production **Forward Collision Warning (FCW)** systems activate at a TTC threshold of approximately **$2.7 \, \text{s}$**. Therefore:

$$
TTC_{warn} \le 2.7 \text{ s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Warning issued}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)**

> A host vehicle traveling at $v_H = 90$ km/h approaches a slower vehicle at $v_P = 60$ km/h. The gap is $d = 45$ m. Determine (a) the closing speed, (b) the TTC, and (c) whether the FCW will trigger.

**Conversion to SI units**:

$$
v_H = 90 \, \text{km/h} = 25.0 \, \text{m/s}
$$

$$
v_P = 60 \, \text{km/h} = 16.67 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**(a) Closing speed:**

$$
\Delta v = v_H - v_P = 25.0 - 16.67 = 8.33 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**(b) Time-to-Collision:**

$$
TTC = \dfrac{d}{\Delta v} = \dfrac{45}{8.33} = 5.40 \, \text{s}
$$

**(c) FCW Trigger Decision:**

$$
TTC = 5.40 \, \text{s} \; > \; TTC_{warn} = 2.7 \, \text{s}
$$

Since the computed TTC exceeds the warning threshold, **no FCW warning is issued** — the host vehicle has sufficient time to react.

## 3.2 Derivation — Required Deceleration to Avoid Collision

The AEB system must compute the deceleration required to bring the relative velocity to zero before the gap closes.

### 3.2.1 Setup

Using the **kinematic equation of motion**:

$$
v_f^2 = v_i^2 + 2 \cdot a \cdot s
$$

For collision avoidance, we set $v_f = 0$ (relative velocity becomes zero) at the boundary of the available gap.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1** — Apply the kinematic equation with the boundary conditions.

$$
0 = (\Delta v)^2 + 2 \cdot a_{req} \cdot d
$$

**Step 2** — Solve for the required deceleration.

$$
a_{req} = -\dfrac{(\Delta v)^2}{2 \cdot d}
$$

The negative sign indicates **deceleration** (opposing motion). We usually take the magnitude:

$$
\vert a_{req} \vert = \dfrac{(\Delta v)^2}{2 \cdot d}
$$

**Step 3** — Decision Logic**

$$
a_{req} \le a_{max} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Collision avoidable by braking}
$$

$$
a_{req} > a_{max} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Collision unavoidable, mitigate by full AEB}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU Style)**

> Using the same scenario: $v_H = 25.0 \, \text{m/s}$, $v_P = 16.67 \, \text{m/s}$, $d = 45 \, \text{m}$, find the required deceleration.

$$
\Delta v = 8.33 \, \text{m/s}
$$

$$
\vert a_{req} \vert = \dfrac{(8.33)^2}{2 \cdot 45} = \dfrac{69.4}{90} = 0.77 \, \text{m/s}^2
$$

Since the required deceleration of **$0.77 \, \text{m/s}^2$** is much less than the maximum achievable braking of **$8.0 \, \text{m/s}^2$** on dry asphalt, the driver (or AEB) can comfortably avoid the collision.

## 3.3 Derivation — Minimum Safe Following Distance (Headway Distance Model)

This is a frequent KTU 3-mark definition question and 7-mark derivation.

### 3.3.1 Concept

A safe driver must maintain a distance such that, in case of an emergency stop by the leading vehicle, the host can also stop without collision. The total stopping distance is the sum of two phases.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Distance traveled during driver reaction time.**

During the reaction time $t_r$, the vehicle continues at its initial speed $v$:

$$
d_{react} = v \cdot t_r
$$

**Step 2 — Distance required to brake to a stop.**

Using $v_f^2 = v_i^2 - 2 \cdot a_{max} \cdot d_{brake}$ (with $v_f = 0$):

$$
d_{brake} = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \cdot a_{max}}
$$

**Step 3 — Total stopping distance.**

$$
d_{stop} = d_{react} + d_{brake} = v \cdot t_r + \dfrac{v^2}{2 \cdot a_{max}}
$$

**Step 4 — Safe headway with leading vehicle.**

The safe following distance $S_{safe}$ must also account for the leading vehicle’s stopping distance, but assuming the lead vehicle also brakes with the same $a_{max}$, the **net safe headway** is:

$$
S_{safe} = v \cdot t_r
$$

This is the famous **"2-second rule"** used in driver training. The **ACC headway time** is typically set between **1.0 s and 2.5 s**.

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU Style)**

> A vehicle travels at 100 km/h. Driver reaction time is 1.2 s. Maximum deceleration is 7.5 m/s². Compute the total stopping distance.

Convert speed:

$$
v = 100 \, \text{km/h} = 27.78 \, \text{m/s}
$$

Reaction distance:

$$
d_{react} = 27.78 \times 1.2 = 33.33 \, \text{m}
$$

Braking distance:

$$
d_{brake} = \dfrac{(27.78)^2}{2 \times 7.5} = \dfrac{771.6}{15} = 51.44 \, \text{m}
$$

Total stopping distance:

$$
d_{stop} = 33.33 + 51.44 = 84.77 \, \text{m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examinations frequently ask students to compare this value with **road signposting distances** (e.g., the typical 90 m warning sign distance on highways). The computed 84.77 m justifies why road signs are placed at ~90 m for 100 km/h zones.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ADAS Subsystems

This matrix is the most efficient **revision tool** for KTU 7-mark questions on “compare and contrast”.

| ADAS | Control Domain | Type | HMI Output | Sensor Used | Activates When |
|------|----------------|------|------------|-------------|----------------|
| **ACC** | Longitudinal | Continuous assistance | Visual + auditory | Radar, camera | Speed > 30 km/h, lead vehicle detected |
| **AEB** | Longitudinal | Active intervention | Visual + auditory + brake pulse | Radar, camera, lidar | $TTC \le 2.7 \, \text{s}$ or $a_{req} > a_{max}$ |
| **LDW** | Lateral (warning) | Advisory | Steering wheel vibration | Camera | Lateral offset > 0.3–0.5 m |
| **LKA** | Lateral (active) | Corrective | Steering wheel torque | Camera | LDW condition + vehicle speed > 60 km/h |
| **BSD** | Surround awareness | Advisory | Mirror indicator | Radar (24 GHz) | Vehicle in 3–5 m blind zone |
| **ESC** | Vehicle dynamics | Active intervention | Dashboard warning | Yaw rate, steering angle sensors | Yaw rate deviation > threshold |
| **ISA** | Longitudinal | Advisory or active | Visual + accelerator resistance | Camera + map | Speed > posted limit |
| **NV** | Perception | Informative | Head-up display | Infrared camera | Low ambient light |
| **PA** (Park Assist) | Longitudinal + Lateral | Continuous assistance | Audio beeps + screen | Ultrasonic | Reverse gear engaged |
| **TJA** (Traffic Jam Assist) | Longitudinal + Lateral | Continuous assistance | Visual + haptic | Radar + camera | Speed < 60 km/h, traffic jam detected |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic ADAS Functional Architecture

The diagram below illustrates the canonical **sense → perceive → plan → act** architecture used by every modern ADAS. The block structure maps each functional stage of the ADAS operational cycle introduced in Section 2.2.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ENVIRONMENT<br>Objects Road Signs Lanes] --> B[SENSOR LAYER<br>Radar Lidar Camera Ultrasonic GPS IMU]
    B --> C[SENSOR FUSION<br>Kalman Filter Bayesian Network]
    C --> D[PERCEPTION MODULE<br>Object Detection Classification Tracking]
    D --> E[SITUATION ASSESSMENT<br>TTC Risk Analysis Driver State]
    E --> F[DECISION MAKING<br>Rule Based AI Planning]
    F --> G[ACTUATION LAYER<br>Throttle Brake Steering HMI]
    G --> H[VEHICLE DYNAMICS RESPONSE<br>Longitudinal Lateral Motion]
    H --> I[DRIVER HMI FEEDBACK<br>Visual Auditory Haptic Display]
    I --> J[DRIVER RESPONSE<br>Override Accept Reject]
    J --> E
```

### 4.1.1 Block Function Description

- **ENVIRONMENT** — External world containing other vehicles, pedestrians, lane markings, and traffic signs.
- **SENSOR LAYER** — The hardware interface that converts physical phenomena into electrical signals. Multi-modal sensing ensures redundancy.
- **SENSOR FUSION** — Algorithmic stage that combines heterogeneous sensor data to overcome individual sensor weaknesses.
- **PERCEPTION MODULE** — Software that detects, classifies, and tracks objects using **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)** and **YOLO/SSD** architectures.
- **SITUATION ASSESSMENT** — Computes risk metrics like **TTC**, **Time-to-Lane-Crossing (TLC)**, and **Time Headway (THW)**.
- **DECISION MAKING** — Selects the appropriate action using rule-based finite state machines or reinforcement learning.
- **ACTUATION LAYER** — Interfaces with **drive-by-wire** systems: electronic throttle, brake-by-wire, steer-by-wire.
- **VEHICLE DYNAMICS RESPONSE** — The physical motion resulting from actuation.
- **DRIVER HMI FEEDBACK** — Closes the loop by informing the human driver of system actions.
- **DRIVER RESPONSE** — The human can override, accept, or reject the system’s action, providing a **closed human-in-the-loop** control system.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — SAE J3016 Automation Level Classification Flow

The diagram below visualizes how the **6 SAE automation levels** branch from a common root (vehicle motion) and diverge by **who is responsible** for the **Dynamic Driving Task (DDT)** and **DDT Fallback**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[VEHICLE MOTION CONTROL] --> L0[LEVEL 0<br>NO AUTOMATION<br>Driver 100 percent responsible]
    ROOT --> L1[LEVEL 1<br>DRIVER ASSISTANCE<br>System handles steering OR acceleration braking]
    ROOT --> L2[LEVEL 2<br>PARTIAL AUTOMATION<br>System handles steering AND acceleration braking<br>Driver must monitor]
    ROOT --> L3[LEVEL 3<br>CONDITIONAL AUTOMATION<br>System handles full DDT in ODD<br>Driver may disengage attention]
    ROOT --> L4[LEVEL 4<br>HIGH AUTOMATION<br>System handles full DDT in ODD<br>No driver attention needed]
    ROOT --> L5[LEVEL 5<br>FULL AUTOMATION<br>System handles all conditions<br>No driver needed]
    L1 -. Example .-> E1[Adaptive Cruise Control LKA]
    L2 -. Example .-> E2[Tesla Autopilot GM Super Cruise]
    L3 -. Example .-> E3[Audi A8 Traffic Jam Pilot]
    L4 -. Example .-> E4[Waymo Robotaxi in geofence]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — ADAS Classification by Vehicle Dynamics Domain

This block diagram groups ADAS subsystems into the **three canonical control domains** taught in KTU Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUB[ADAS SUBSYSTEMS] --> LONG[LONGITUDINAL DOMAIN<br>Forward Backward Motion]
    SUB --> LAT[LATERAL DOMAIN<br>Side to Side Steering Motion]
    SUB --> COM[COMBINED DOMAIN<br>Both Longitudinal and Lateral]
    LONG --> L1[ACC]
    LONG --> L2[AEB]
    LONG --> L3[FCW]
    LONG --> L4[ISA]
    LAT --> M1[LDW]
    LAT --> M2[LKA]
    LAT --> M3[Lane Centering]
    COM --> N1[Traffic Jam Assist]
    COM --> N2[Highway Chauffeur]
    COM --> N3[Self Parking]
    COM --> N4[Highway Pilot]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — ADAS Operational Logic (Sense Plan Act)

The diagram below distills the entire 6-stage operational cycle (Section 2.2) into a 3-block canonical control loop used universally in robotics and ADAS textbooks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENSE[SENSE<br>Radar Lidar Camera<br>Ultrasonic GPS] --> PLAN[PLAN<br>Fusion Perception<br>Risk Assessment Decision]
    PLAN --> ACT[ACT<br>Throttle Brake Steering<br>HMI Display]
    ACT --> SENSE
```

### 4.4.1 Engineering Interpretation

This loop runs at **100 Hz or higher** in production vehicles. The **latency budget** of the entire loop is:
- **Sensing**: ~10 ms
- **Perception**: ~30–50 ms
- **Planning**: ~20–30 ms
- **Actuation**: ~10 ms
- **Total loop time**: **~70–100 ms**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A 100 ms loop time corresponds to a vehicle traveling **2.78 m at 100 km/h** during a single iteration. This is the engineering reason why **look-ahead sensing ranges** must exceed **200 m** at highway speeds.

## 4.5 Block Diagram — ADAS Subsystem Interaction on a Modern Vehicle

The block diagram below shows the **CAN bus (Controller Area Network)** interconnection of ADAS ECUs. This is the most important diagram for KTU Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SR[FRONT RADAR ECU] --> CBUS[VEHICLE CAN BUS]
    SC[FRONT CAMERA ECU] --> CBUS
    SU[ULTRASONIC ECU] --> CBUS
    SV[SURROUND VIEW ECU] --> CBUS
    CBUS --> ADAS[ADAS CENTRAL ECU<br>Sensor Fusion and Planning]
    CBUS --> ECM[ENGINE CONTROL MODULE]
    CBUS --> TCM[TRANSMISSION CONTROL MODULE]
    CBUS --> BCM[BRAKE CONTROL MODULE<br>ABS ESC AEB Actuator]
    CBUS --> EPS[EPS MODULE<br>Electric Power Steering]
    CBUS --> HMI[HMI MODULE<br>Cluster HUD Infotainment]
    ADAS --> CBUS
```

### 4.5.1 Communication Insights

- The **CAN bus** typically operates at **500 kbps (CAN-C)** for chassis and **125 kbps (CAN-B)** for body.
- Newer vehicles use **CAN FD (Flexible Data-rate)** and **Automotive Ethernet (100 Mbps, 1 Gbps)** to handle the high data rate of ADAS sensors.
- The **ADAS central ECU** is the brain where **sensor fusion, perception, and decision-making** are concentrated in Level 2+ systems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS). List any four examples of ADAS used in modern automobiles.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)** are electronic systems embedded in a vehicle that use sensors, controllers, and actuators to assist the driver in the dynamic driving task, either by **warning** the driver or by **automatically intervening** in vehicle control to improve safety, comfort, and efficiency.

**Four examples of ADAS:**
1. **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** — Maintains set speed and adjusts to lead vehicle.
2. **Lane Departure Warning (LDW)** — Warns when vehicle drifts out of lane.
3. **Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB)** — Automatically applies brakes to prevent collision.
4. **Blind Spot Detection (BSD)** — Detects vehicles in the driver’s blind spot.

*(Other acceptable examples: Electronic Stability Control, Forward Collision Warning, Lane Keeping Assist, Night Vision, Traffic Sign Recognition.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Active Safety and Passive Safety in automobiles. Give two examples for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Active Safety | Passive Safety |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| **Purpose** | Prevents accidents from occurring | Protects occupants during/after a crash |
| **Operation Time** | Pre-crash (driving phase) | During and post-crash |
| **Examples (1)** | **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** | **Seat belts** |
| **Examples (2)** | **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)** | **Airbags** |

*(Further Active examples: AEB, ACC, LKA. Further Passive examples: Crumple zones, padded interiors, side-impact protection bars.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **SAE J3016 levels of driving automation** with suitable examples for each level. Discuss how the responsibility is shared between the human driver and the automated system at each level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a generic ADAS**. Explain the functions of each block in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — SAE J3016 Automation Levels

The **SAE International Standard J3016** (revised 2021) defines **six levels of driving automation**, ranging from Level 0 (no automation) to Level 5 (full automation). The classification is based on **who performs the Dynamic Driving Task (DDT)** and the **DDT fallback**.

**Level 0 — No Automation:**
- The human driver performs the entire DDT.
- The vehicle may issue warnings (e.g., blind spot warning), but does not control steering, throttle, or brakes.
- *Example:* A conventional 1990s car with reverse parking sensors.

**Level 1 — Driver Assistance:**
- The system performs **either** sustained lateral control **or** sustained longitudinal control, but not both.
- The driver is expected to perform the remaining function and monitor continuously.
- *Example 1:* **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** handles longitudinal motion (throttle + brake).
- *Example 2:* **Lane Keeping Assist (LKA)** handles lateral motion (steering).

**Level 2 — Partial Automation:**
- The system performs **both** lateral and longitudinal control simultaneously, but the driver must continuously monitor and be ready to intervene.
- The driver remains legally and operationally responsible.
- *Example:* **Tesla Autopilot, GM Super Cruise, Ford BlueCruise.**

**Level 3 — Conditional Automation:**
- The system performs the **entire DDT** within a defined Operational Design Domain (ODD).
- The driver **may divert attention** from the road (e.g., watch a video), but must be ready to take over when the system issues a **Take-Over Request (TOR)**.
- *Example:* **Audi A8 Traffic Jam Pilot (2017 release, later suspended).**

**Level 4 — High Automation:**
- The system performs the entire DDT within its ODD, and if the driver fails to take over, the system executes a **Minimal Risk Manoeuvre (MRM)** (e.g., pull over and stop).
- No driver attention is required within the ODD.
- *Example:* **Waymo robotaxis operating in geofenced urban zones of Phoenix, San Francisco.**

**Level 5 — Full Automation:**
- The system performs the entire DDT under **all conditions** that a human driver could manage.
- No steering wheel or pedals are required.
- *Example:* Currently **theoretical**; no production vehicle has achieved Level 5.

**Responsibility Sharing Summary:**

| Level | Lateral | Longitudinal | Monitoring | Fallback |
|-------|---------|--------------|------------|----------|
| 0 | Driver | Driver | Driver | Driver |
| 1 | Driver/System | Driver/System | Driver | Driver |
| 2 | System | System | Driver | Driver |
| 3 | System | System | System | Driver (within TOR time) |
| 4 | System | System | System | System |
| 5 | System | System | System | System |

#### Part (b) — Functional Architecture of a Generic ADAS

*(Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1.)*

A generic ADAS follows the **sense → perceive → plan → act** architecture comprising the following blocks:

1. **Sensor Layer** — Comprises heterogeneous sensors:
   - **Radar (24/77 GHz):** Measures range, velocity, and angle of objects.
   - **Camera:** Reads lane markings, traffic signs, lights, and object classification.
   - **Lidar:** Provides high-resolution 3D point cloud.
   - **Ultrasonic:** Detects close-range objects (parking).
   - **GNSS/IMU:** Provides position and vehicle dynamics state.

2. **Sensor Fusion Module** — Combines data using algorithms like the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**, **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)**, or **Bayesian networks** to overcome individual sensor limitations and produce a coherent world model.

3. **Perception Module** — Performs:
   - **Object detection** (using CNNs like YOLO, SSD).
   - **Object classification** (cars, pedestrians, cyclists).
   - **Object tracking** (using Kalman filter or Hungarian algorithm).

4. **Situation Assessment Module** — Computes:
   - **Time-to-Collision (TTC)**.
   - **Time Headway (THW)**.
   - **Time-to-Lane-Crossing (TLC)**.
   - **Driver attention state** (via Driver Monitoring System, DMS).

5. **Decision-Making Module** — Uses either:
   - **Rule-based finite state machines.**
   - **AI-based planners** (e.g., behavior trees, reinforcement learning).

6. **Actuation Layer** — Translates decisions into:
   - **Throttle command** (electronic throttle).
   - **Brake command** (brake-by-wire).
   - **Steering command** (steer-by-wire / EPS overlay).
   - **HMI output** (display, buzzer, steering vibration).

7. **HMI Feedback Loop** — Informs the driver of system actions, allowing override via steering, brake, or accelerator.

> **Example — Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) using this architecture:**
> - Radar senses the lead vehicle distance and speed.
> - Fusion module combines radar and camera data.
> - Perception module tracks the lead vehicle.
> - Situation assessment computes TTC and THW.
> - Decision module calculates required throttle/brake.
> - Actuation applies throttle or brake to maintain headway.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Module Choice

**(a)** Define the terms **Time-to-Collision (TTC)** and **Time Headway (THW)**. Derive expressions for **TTC** and the **safe stopping distance** for a vehicle, clearly stating the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Classify driver assistance systems based on **(i)** vehicle dynamics domain and **(ii)** the level of driver intervention. Provide two examples for each category. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — TTC and Safe Stopping Distance Derivations

**Definitions:**

> **Time-to-Collision (TTC):** The time remaining before a collision occurs between the host vehicle and a preceding object, assuming both continue at their current velocities and directions.
> **Time Headway (THW):** The time interval between the front of the host vehicle passing a fixed point on the road and the rear of the preceding vehicle passing the same point.

**Derivation of TTC:**

Let $d$ be the bumper-to-bumper gap and $\Delta v$ the closing speed.

$$
TTC = \dfrac{d}{\Delta v}
$$

*(Detailed kinematic derivation provided in Section 3.1.)*

**Derivation of Safe Stopping Distance:**

A vehicle moving at speed $v$ first travels a distance $d_{react}$ during the driver reaction time $t_r$:

$$
d_{react} = v \cdot t_r
$$

Then it decelerates at maximum rate $a_{max}$ to a stop. Using $v_f^2 = v_i^2 - 2 a_{max} d_{brake}$ with $v_f = 0$:

$$
d_{brake} = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \cdot a_{max}}
$$

Total stopping distance:

$$
d_{stop} = v \cdot t_r + \dfrac{v^2}{2 \cdot a_{max}}
$$

**Assumptions:**
- Constant velocity during the reaction time.
- Constant maximum deceleration during braking.
- Dry, level road surface.
- No aerodynamic or rolling resistance.
- Linear brake response.

**Numerical Example (KTU Typical):**

For $v = 20 \, \text{m/s}$, $t_r = 1.2 \, \text{s}$, $a_{max} = 7 \, \text{m/s}^2$:

$$
d_{react} = 20 \times 1.2 = 24 \, \text{m}
$$

$$
d_{brake} = \dfrac{20^2}{2 \times 7} = 28.57 \, \text{m}
$$

$$
d_{stop} = 24 + 28.57 = 52.57 \, \text{m}
$$

#### Part (b) — Classification of ADAS

**(i) Classification by Vehicle Dynamics Domain:**

1. **Longitudinal Control ADAS** — Governs forward/backward motion.
   - *Example 1:* **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC).**
   - *Example 2:* **Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB).**

2. **Lateral Control ADAS** — Governs side-to-side (steering) motion.
   - *Example 1:* **Lane Keeping Assist (LKA).**
   - *Example 2:* **Lane Centering Assist (LCA).**

3. **Combined Longitudinal + Lateral ADAS** — Controls both axes.
   - *Example 1:* **Traffic Jam Assist (TJA).**
   - *Example 2:* **Automated Parking Assist.**

**(ii) Classification by Level of Intervention:**

1. **Informative Systems** — Provide information only.
   - *Example 1:* **Night Vision display.**
   - *Example 2:* **Traffic Sign Recognition (TSR).**

2. **Warning / Advisory Systems** — Alert the driver.
   - *Example 1:* **Lane Departure Warning (LDW).**
   - *Example 2:* **Forward Collision Warning (FCW).**

3. **Active Intervention (Corrective) Systems** — Briefly modify driver input.
   - *Example 1:* **Electronic Stability Control (ESC).**
   - *Example 2:* **AEB intervention.**

4. **Continuous Assistance Systems** — Sustained control with driver supervision.
   - *Example 1:* **ACC.**
   - *Example 2:* **Highway Assist.**

5. **Automated Driving Systems (Level 3+)** — Driver disengagement.
   - *Example 1:* **Traffic Jam Pilot.**
   - *Example 2:* **Highway Chauffeur.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 3 (Definitions and Terminology):**
>
> 1. **Conflating Active and Passive Safety** — Many students incorrectly classify **airbags as Active Safety**. They are **Passive Safety** (they activate *during* a crash). *Always state the timing: Active = pre-crash, Passive = during/post-crash.*
>
> 2. **Confusing SAE Level 2 with Level 3** — Students often describe **Tesla Autopilot as Level 3**. It is **Level 2 (Partial Automation)** because the driver must continuously monitor. **Level 3** permits the driver to divert attention (e.g., watch a video) within the ODD.
>
> 3. **Misnaming ADAS** — Writing **“Automatic Braking”** instead of **“Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB)”** will lose marks. Use the **standardised terminology** — examiners expect full forms on first mention.
>
> 4. **Wrong unit conversion** — In TTC derivations, students forget to convert **km/h to m/s** (multiply by 5/18). This single error loses 2–3 marks.
>
> 5. **Missing the “Operational Design Domain (ODD)”** in Level 3/4 answers — KTU examiners specifically test whether students know that **Level 3+ systems are constrained to specific ODDs**. Failing to mention ODD = incomplete answer.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the human-in-the-loop** in Block Diagrams — The feedback arrow from Driver HMI back to Perception is **mandatory**. A linear (open-loop) diagram is marked down.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 3 — Driver Assistance Systems: Definitions and Terminology**

- **ADAS** = electronic systems that **sense, decide, act/warn** to assist the driver.
- **Active safety** = pre-crash prevention (ABS, ESC, ACC, AEB). **Passive safety** = during/post-crash protection (airbags, seatbelts, crumple zones).
- **SAE J3016** defines **6 levels** (0 to 5). Level 2 = driver supervises; Level 3 = driver may divert attention; Level 4 = system handles fallback; Level 5 = theoretical full automation.
- **Longitudinal ADAS** = ACC, AEB, FCW, ISA. **Lateral ADAS** = LDW, LKA, LCA. **Combined** = TJA, Parking Assist, Highway Pilot.
- **Sensors**: Radar (range/velocity), Lidar (3D), Camera (classification/lanes), Ultrasonic (parking), GPS/IMU (position/motion).
- **TTC formula**: $TTC = \dfrac{d}{\Delta v}$. Threshold: **2.7 s** for FCW.
- **Required deceleration**: $\vert a_{req} \vert = \dfrac{(\Delta v)^2}{2 d}$.
- **Safe stopping distance**: $d_{stop} = v \cdot t_r + \dfrac{v^2}{2 a_{max}}$.
- **ODD** (Operational Design Domain) = conditions in which an automated system may operate. Mandatory for Level 3+.
- **Take-Over Request (TOR)** = Level 3 prompt to driver to resume control.
- **Minimal Risk Manoeuvre (MRM)** = Level 4 fallback (e.g., safe stop).
- **EU GSR 2019/2144** mandates **AEB, LDW, ISA, DDA** for new vehicles from **July 2022/2024**.
- **Euro NCAP** rates AEB Pedestrian, AEB Cyclist, Lane Support, Speed Assist — these are the **headline test categories** for ADAS scoring.
- **Hands-on** detection and **Driver Monitoring System (DMS)** are **mandatory** for Level 2+ systems to prevent misuse.
- **Sensor fusion** uses **Kalman filter / Bayesian filter** to combine multi-sensor data.
- **Functional safety** of ADAS is governed by **ISO 26262** (ASIL A to D classification).
- **Loop time** of an ADAS = ~70–100 ms; at 100 km/h, the vehicle travels **~2.78 m per loop iteration**.
- **FCW** = advisory (warning only). **AEB** = active intervention (applies brakes). Know the difference.
- **ACC Stop & Go** is the urban version of ACC that operates down to **0 km/h** (handles traffic jam scenarios).
- **Lateral acceleration limit** for normal driving ≈ **0.3 g**; ESC intervenes above **~0.5 g** lateral acceleration on slippery surfaces.

> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Driver Assistance Systems: Definitions and Terminology**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automotive Collision Avoidance System: Types of Collision avoidance systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT743 - VEHICLE SAFETY AND SECURITY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Automotive Collision Avoidance System: Types of Collision avoidance systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Automotive Collision Avoidance System (CAS)** is an integrated active safety subsystem in a road vehicle that uses a network of on-board sensors, electronic control units (ECUs), and actuator interfaces to continuously monitor the vehicle's surrounding dynamic environment, detect imminent collision threats, and execute either autonomous mitigating interventions (braking, steering, throttle cut) or driver-warning alerts to prevent or reduce the severity of a crash.

According to the **Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) J3063** framework and **Euro NCAP 2025** safety protocols, Collision Avoidance Systems are classified under the broader umbrella of **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)** operating at SAE **Level 1 (Driver Assistance)** through **Level 3 (Conditional Automation)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** A Collision Avoidance System is *active* (it acts to prevent the crash) as opposed to a *passive* safety system (which mitigates injury after the crash, e.g., airbags, crumple zones). This distinction is a guaranteed 3-mark question.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Active Collision Avoidance

| Pillar | Function | Trigger Event |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensing** | Acquire environmental data | Continuous, **100 Hz** typical |
| **Decision** | Compute Time-To-Collision (TTC) | Threshold breach |
| **Action** | Warning or Intervention | Driver inaction or override |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sixth Sense" of the Car

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine driving with a vigilant co-passenger who has **360° eyesight**, never blinks, and can measure the exact distance to every nearby object in milliseconds. When this co-passenger senses a truck braking suddenly 40 meters ahead, they either shout **"BRAKE!"** (warning) or, if you're frozen in panic, reach over and slam the brake pedal for you (intervention). That co-passenger is the **Collision Avoidance System**. The eyes are the **sensors** (Radar, Lidar, Camera), the brain judging distance and urgency is the **ECU**, and the hands reaching for the brake are the **actuators**.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. For Module 3, the relevant Course Outcome is typically mapped as:

- **CO3:** *Analyze the architecture and functioning of modern active safety systems in automobiles.*

This directly translates to the examiner's expectation that a student can *list, classify, and describe the operational logic* of each major collision avoidance subsystem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification of Collision Avoidance Systems

Collision Avoidance Systems can be classified along **three orthogonal axes**: (a) Direction of threat, (b) Mode of action, and (c) Sensor modality.

### Axis A — By Direction of Threat

1. **Forward Collision Avoidance Systems (FCAS)**
2. **Rear Collision Avoidance Systems (RCAS)**
3. **Side/Lateral Collision Avoidance Systems (SCAS)**

### Axis B — By Mode of Action

1. **Warning-Only Systems (Advisory)**
2. **Warning + Braking Assist (Semi-Active)**
3. **Fully Autonomous Emergency Intervention (Active)**

### Axis C — By Target Vulnerable Road User (VRU)

1. **Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V)**
2. **Vehicle-to-Pedestrian (V2P)**
3. **Vehicle-to-Cyclist (V2C)**
4. **Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I)**

## 2.2 Detailed Subsystem Analysis

### 2.2.1 Forward Collision Warning (FCW)

- **Sensors:** Front-mounted 77 GHz mmWave Radar (range ~**150 m**), forward-facing monocular camera (range ~**60 m**), sometimes Lidar.
- **Logic:** ECU computes **Time-To-Collision (TTC)**. If TTC falls below a calibrated threshold (typically **2.7 s to 1.5 s**), an audiovisual alert fires.
- **Output:** Audible chime + visual HUD icon + haptic seat vibration.

### 2.2.2 Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)

- **Sensors:** Same as FCW, but with tighter sensor fusion.
- **Logic:** A two-stage intervention. **Stage 1 (Pre-fill):** Brake pads pre-positioned against rotors. **Stage 2 (Full Braking):** Hydraulic pressure applied at **8–10 MPa** achieving maximum deceleration of **0.8g–1.0g**.
- **Output:** Automatic brake actuation even if the driver is inattentive.

### 2.2.3 Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)

- **Sensors:** Long-range radar + corner radars.
- **Logic:** Maintains a user-set *time gap* (not a fixed distance) to the preceding vehicle. Typical gap settings: **1.0 s, 1.5 s, 2.0 s, 2.5 s**.
- **Output:** Modulates throttle and brake to maintain gap.

### 2.2.4 Lane Departure Warning (LDW) & Lane Keeping Assist (LKA)

- **Sensors:** Forward-facing camera tracking lane markings.
- **Logic:** Detects lane drift using **Hough Transform** image processing. Time-to-Line-Crossing (TLC) threshold ~**0.5 s**.
- **Output:** Warning (LDW) or steering torque nudge (LKA).

### 2.2.5 Blind Spot Detection (BSD) / Blind Spot Information System (BLIS)

- **Sensors:** Two mid-range corner radars mounted in the rear bumper (24 GHz or 77 GHz), each with a **150°** field of view.
- **Logic:** Monitors adjacent lanes within a **3 m × 7 m** zone. If a vehicle is present and turn signal is engaged, alert fires.
- **Output:** Mirror-mounted LED indicator.

### 2.2.6 Rear Cross Traffic Alert (RCTA)

- **Sensors:** Rear corner radars.
- **Logic:** Activated in reverse gear; detects vehicles approaching from the sides within ~**20 m**.
- **Output:** Audible chirp + visual warning in the rear-view camera display.

### 2.2.7 Pedestrian / Cyclist Detection & Emergency Steering

- **Sensors:** Camera + Radar fusion; Lidar in premium vehicles.
- **Logic:** Uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to classify VRUs. Triggers AEB or autonomous evasive steering.
- **Output:** Full emergency maneuver.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical for Numerical Problems:** The following equations are the **core math** behind all collision avoidance logic. Memorize them.

| # | Formula | Quantity | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TTC = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta v}$ | Time-To-Collision | $\Delta d$ = relative distance, $\Delta v$ = relative velocity | s |
| 2 | $d_{brake} = \dfrac{v^{2}}{2 \mu g}$ | Braking Distance | $v$ = vehicle speed, $\mu$ = friction coefficient | m |
| 3 | $a_{max} = \mu \cdot g$ | Maximum Deceleration | $g = 9.81 \ \mathrm{m/s^{2}}$ | m/s² |
| 4 | $f_{Doppler} = \dfrac{2 v_{rel}}{\lambda}$ | Doppler Frequency | $\lambda$ = radar wavelength, $v_{rel}$ = radial velocity | Hz |
| 5 | $R_{max} = \dfrac{c \cdot PRF}{2 \cdot BW}$ | Max Radar Range | $c$ = speed of light, $PRF$ = pulse repetition freq. | m |
| 6 | $SNR = \dfrac{P_t G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} R^{4} k T B F}$ | Radar SNR (Radar Equation) | $\sigma$ = radar cross-section, $k$ = Boltzmann const. | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{1.22 \lambda}{D}$ | Angular Resolution | $D$ = aperture diameter | rad |
| 8 | $TLC = \dfrac{d_{lane} - \frac{W}{2}}{v_{lat}}$ | Time-to-Line-Crossing | $W$ = vehicle width, $v_{lat}$ = lateral velocity | s |

> **Constants to Remember:** $\mu_{dry} \approx 0.7$ to $0.9$, $\mu_{wet} \approx 0.4$ to $0.6$, $g = 9.81 \ \mathrm{m/s^{2}}$, $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \ \mathrm{m/s}$, $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \ \mathrm{J/K}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Insurance Premium Reduction:** Vehicles with AEB earn up to **10%** discount in many markets.
- **Euro NCAP Rating:** AEB/FCW/LDW are mandatory for a **5-star** rating since 2020.
- **Mandates:** The **EU General Safety Regulation (GSR) 2019/2144** mandates AEB, LDW, and ISA for all new vehicles from **July 2024** onward.
- **Indian Context:** **AIS-184** (Automotive Industry Standards) issued by ARAI mandates AEB for new cars from 2026 (proposed).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time-To-Collision (TTC) and the Warning Threshold

The **Time-To-Collision** is the *single most important* quantity in any forward collision avoidance system. We derive it below in a KTU examiner-friendly stepwise format.

### 3.1.1 Given Data
- Ego vehicle velocity: $v_{e} = 20 \ \mathrm{m/s}$ (72 km/h)
- Leading vehicle velocity: $v_{l} = 15 \ \mathrm{m/s}$ (54 km/h)
- Instantaneous gap (bumper-to-bumper): $\Delta d = 30 \ \mathrm{m}$
- System reaction time: $t_{react} = 0.2 \ \mathrm{s}$
- Driver perception-brake time: $t_{pbr} = 0.8 \ \mathrm{s}$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute relative velocity.**
The relative velocity is the speed at which the gap is closing:
$$v_{rel} = v_{e} - v_{l}$$

$$v_{rel} = 20 - 15 = 5 \ \mathrm{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Time-To-Collision.**
By definition, TTC is the time remaining before the gap closes to zero, assuming both vehicles continue at constant velocity:
$$TTC = \frac{\Delta d}{v_{rel}}$$

$$TTC = \frac{30}{5} = 6 \ \mathrm{s}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the warning threshold.**
Industry standard threshold $TTC_{warn} = 2.5 \ \mathrm{s}$. Since $TTC = 6 \ \mathrm{s} > 2.5 \ \mathrm{s}$, the system remains in **NORMAL CRUISE** mode. No alert fires.

**Step 4 — Find the distance at which the warning will fire.**
The warning fires when $TTC$ drops to $2.5 \ \mathrm{s}$:
$$\Delta d_{warn} = TTC_{warn} \cdot v_{rel}$$

$$\Delta d_{warn} = 2.5 \times 5 = 12.5 \ \mathrm{m}$$

**Step 5 — Verify safe braking margin.**
Required stopping distance for the ego vehicle on dry asphalt ($\mu = 0.8$):
$$d_{brake} = \frac{v_{e}^{2}}{2 \mu g}$$

$$d_{brake} = \frac{20^{2}}{2 \times 0.8 \times 9.81} = \frac{400}{15.696} = 25.48 \ \mathrm{m}$$

Total stopping distance including reaction and perception:
$$d_{total} = v_{e} \cdot (t_{react} + t_{pbr}) + d_{brake}$$

$$d_{total} = 20 \times (0.2 + 0.8) + 25.48 = 20 + 25.48 = 45.48 \ \mathrm{m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Since the warning distance of $12.5 \ \mathrm{m}$ is *less* than the total stopping distance of $45.48 \ \mathrm{m}$, a TTC threshold of 2.5 s is **insufficient** for safe manual braking. This is exactly *why* the AEB sub-system must engage automatic braking in parallel — a key KTU design insight.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Doppler Radar Velocity Measurement

A 77 GHz automotive radar emits a continuous wave of frequency $f_0 = 77 \times 10^{9} \ \mathrm{Hz}$. A target approaches at $v_{rel} = 30 \ \mathrm{m/s}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the radar wavelength.**
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{77 \times 10^{9}} = 3.896 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{m} \approx 3.9 \ \mathrm{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Doppler equation for an automotive radar (two-way travel).**
$$f_{Doppler} = \frac{2 v_{rel}}{\lambda}$$

$$f_{Doppler} = \frac{2 \times 30}{3.896 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$f_{Doppler} \approx 15400 \ \mathrm{Hz} = 15.4 \ \mathrm{kHz}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering interpretation.**
The radar receiver mixes the transmitted and reflected signals to extract this $15.4 \ \mathrm{kHz}$ beat frequency. Because this is in the audible range, the ECU's DSP filters it cleanly. This is why $77 \ \mathrm{GHz}$ is preferred over $24 \ \mathrm{GHz}$ for modern AEB radars — the shorter wavelength gives better resolution for *small* objects (a pedestrian's torso, a child).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing All Subsystems

| System | Primary Sensor | Range (m) | Output | Reaction Time (s) | SAE Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| FCW | Radar + Camera | 150 | Audible + Visual | 0.1 | 1 |
| AEB | Radar + Camera + Lidar | 80 | Auto-brake | 0.05 | 2 |
| ACC | Long-range Radar | 200 | Throttle/Brake | 0.2 | 1 |
| LDW | Forward Camera | 60 (road) | Visual + Audio | 0.1 | 1 |
| LKA | Forward Camera | 60 | Steering Torque | 0.08 | 1 |
| BSD | Corner Radar | 40 | Mirror LED | 0.05 | 1 |
| RCTA | Rear Corner Radar | 20 | Audio + Display | 0.05 | 1 |
| Pedestrian AEB | Camera + Radar + Lidar | 60 | Full Braking | 0.1 | 2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Notice the *range* column. Long-range systems (ACC, FCW) use $77 \ \mathrm{GHz}$ long-range radar; short-range systems (BSD, RCTA) use $24 \ \mathrm{GHz}$ or short-range $77 \ \mathrm{GHz}$ SRR. This is a *guaranteed* sub-question in KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

Below, all Mermaid node IDs are alphanumeric with a letter prefix, and all labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text (no markdown formatting, no `**`, no `*`).

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Collision Avoidance System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[RADAR SENSOR 77GHZ] --> M1[SENSOR FUSION ECU]
    A2[FRONT CAMERA] --> M1
    A3[CORNER RADAR LEFT] --> M1
    A4[CORNER RADAR RIGHT] --> M1
    A5[REAR RADAR] --> M1
    A6[VEHICLE CAN BUS DATA] --> M1
    M1 --> M2[THREAT ASSESSMENT MODULE]
    M2 --> M3[TTC AND TLC COMPUTATION]
    M3 --> M4{DECISION MATRIX}
    M4 -->|TTC LESS THAN 2.5S| N1[FCW WARNING]
    M4 -->|TTC LESS THAN 1.5S| N2[AEB BRAKING]
    M4 -->|LANE DRIFT| N3[LDW WARNING]
    M4 -->|BLIND SPOT| N4[BSD INDICATOR]
    M4 -->|REAR CROSS| N5[RCTA WARNING]
    N2 --> O1[BRAKE ACTUATOR]
    N3 --> O2[STEERING ACTUATOR]
    N1 --> O3[HMI DISPLAY]
    N4 --> O3
    N5 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Operational Sequence of AEB Activation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[TIME T0 RADAR SCAN] --> S2[TIME T0 PLUS 0.05S TARGET DETECTED]
    S2 --> S3[TIME T0 PLUS 0.10S TTC COMPUTED 2.0S]
    S3 --> S4{DID DRIVER BRAKE}
    S4 -->|YES| S5[SYSTEM STANDS DOWN]
    S4 -->|NO| S6[TIME T0 PLUS 0.40S BRAKE PRE FILL]
    S6 --> S7[TIME T0 PLUS 0.60S FULL HYDRAULIC PRESSURE 8 MPA]
    S7 --> S8[VEHICLE DECELERATES AT 0.8G]
    S8 --> S9[COLLISION AVOIDED OR MITIGATED]
```

```mermaid

    D1 -->|2.5S TO 1.5S| D4[FCW AUDIO ALERT]
    D1 -->|1.5S TO 1.0S| D5[BRAKE PRE FILL]

```
