| Field of view | Narrow (≈ 12°–17°) | Wider (≈ 20°–30°) |
| Typical mounting | Interior (Class I) | Exterior main (Class II) |
| Distance perception | Accurate | Compressed — "objects closer than they appear" |

---

**Q2. State the formula for the linear magnification of a spherical mirror and explain why convex mirrors used in vehicles always produce a virtual, erect, and diminished image.**
*(3 marks — CO3, Bloom Level: Apply)*

**Model Answer:**

$$
m = -\frac{d_i}{d_o}
$$

For a convex mirror, R is negative, so f = R/2 is also negative. By the mirror equation 1/f = 1/dₒ + 1/dᵢ, since both 1/f and 1/dₒ are negative/positive terms summing to a negative quantity, 1/dᵢ must be **negative**, giving **dᵢ < 0** (image behind the mirror → virtual). Because dᵢ and dₒ have opposite signs, m is **positive** (erect). The magnitude |dᵢ| < |dₒ| because |1/dᵢ| > |1/dₒ| → |m| < 1 (diminished).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** With the help of a neat ray diagram, explain the formation of a virtual, erect, and diminished image by a convex rear-view mirror of an automobile. Derive the relation between object distance, image distance, and focal length. **\[7 marks, CO3, Bloom: Apply]**

**(b)** A convex rear-view mirror of a car has a radius of curvature of **2.0 m**. A truck **8 m** behind the car is observed in this mirror. Determine: **(i)** the position of the image, **(ii)** the linear magnification, and **(iii)** whether the mirror complies with the ECE R46 Class II radius limit of |R| ≥ 1.2 m. **\[7 marks, CO3, Bloom: Apply / Analyse]**

**Model Solution (a):**

- **Ray diagram:** Draw a convex mirror with its principal axis, pole P, centre of curvature C behind the mirror, and virtual focal point F also behind. Show two incident rays from the top of the truck:
  1. A ray **parallel to the principal axis** strikes the mirror and reflects as if emerging from F.
  2. A ray **directed towards C** strikes the mirror normally and reflects back along itself.
  3. The reflected rays *diverge* in front of the mirror. Extending them *backward* (dashed lines) behind the mirror, they meet at the image point I' → **virtual, erect, diminished image**.

- **Derivation of mirror equation:**

Let the pole be the origin O. Consider a ray from object point A at height h, distance dₒ from the mirror.

For a paraxial ray striking at height y from the axis, geometry of the spherical surface gives:

$$
\tan \alpha \approx \alpha = \frac{y}{d_o}
$$

For the reflected ray directed toward F:

$$
\tan \beta \approx \beta = \frac{y}{\lvert f \rvert}
$$

The image ray angle (object at height h, image at height h′):

$$
\tan \gamma \approx \gamma = \frac{h'}{d_i}
$$

By the exterior angle theorem of the triangle: $\alpha + \beta = \gamma$. Substituting and using the small-angle limit:

$$
\frac{y}{d_o} + \frac{y}{\lvert f \rvert} = \frac{h'}{d_i}
$$

But $\dfrac{y}{d_o} = \dfrac{h'}{d_o}$ by similar triangles (h′ is the apparent image height projected), giving:

$$
\frac{1}{d_o} + \frac{1}{\lvert f \rvert} = \frac{1}{d_i}
$$

Re-introducing sign convention (f negative for convex):

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{1}{d_o} + \frac{1}{d_i} = \frac{1}{f}\;}
$$

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Image position:**

Given R = −2.0 m, so f = R/2 = −1.0 m. dₒ = +8 m.

$$
\frac{1}{d_i} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{d_o} = \frac{1}{-1.0} - \frac{1}{8} = -1.0 - 0.125 = -1.125
$$

$$
d_i = -0.889~\text{m}
$$

**The image forms 0.889 m behind the mirror** (virtual).

**(ii) Linear magnification:**

$$
m = -\frac{d_i}{d_o} = -\frac{(-0.889)}{8} = +0.111
$$

**The truck image is 11.1% of the actual size** (heavily reduced).

**(iii) Compliance check:**

|R| = 2.0 m ≥ 1.2 m (Class II limit) → **COMPLIES with ECE R46 Class II.**

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Explain the classification of rear-view mirrors as per **ECE R46 / AIS-008**, listing the five classes with their mounting locations, radius-of-curvature limits, and typical field-of-view values. **\[7 marks, CO3, Bloom: Understand / Remember]**

**(b)** An aspheric Class IV exterior mirror has inner-zone radius **R₁ = 1.8 m** and outer-zone radius **R₂ = 0.8 m**. A car **20 m** behind is observed.
  - (i) Compute the focal length at both zones.
  - (ii) Determine the image distance and magnification in each zone.
  - (iii) Comment on why the magnification varies spatially. **\[7 marks, CO3, Bloom: Apply / Analyse]**

**Model Solution (a):**

**ECE R46 / AIS-008 Mirror Classification:**

| Class | Description | Mounting | Min \vert R \vert | Typical FoV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I** | Interior rear-view mirror | Top-centre of windshield | ∞ (plane) | 12° – 17° |
| **II** | Main exterior (large) | Driver & passenger doors | 1.20 m | 20° – 25° |
| **III** | Wide-angle exterior (small) | Inside corner of door | 0.30 m | 30° – 40° |
| **IV** | Aspheric (dual-curvature) | Door — top main, bottom wide | 1.20 m (inner) / 0.30 m (outer) | 25° – 35° |
| **V** | Close-proximity / kerb | Front wing / lower door | 0.20 m | > 40° |

**Key regulatory notes:**
- All mirrors must display the **"E" or "e"** approval mark with a number.
- Class III and V require the warning **"Objects in mirror are closer than they appear"**.
- Reflectance limits: ≥ 40% (day), 4% – 12% (night anti-glare).
- The mirror must not protrude more than **0.25 m** beyond the vehicle's overall width.

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Focal lengths:**

$$
f_1 = \frac{R_1}{2} = \frac{1.8}{2} = +0.90~\text{m (inner, mild convex)}
$$

$$
f_2 = \frac{R_2}{2} = \frac{0.8}{2} = +0.40~\text{m (outer, strong convex)}
$$

(In sign convention, both are negative — written as positive magnitudes here for clarity.)

**(ii) Image distances and magnifications:**

*Inner zone* (dₒ = 20 m, f₁ = −0.90 m):

$$
\frac{1}{d_i} = \frac{1}{-0.90} - \frac{1}{20} = -1.1111 - 0.0500 = -1.1611
$$

$$
d_i = -0.861~\text{m} \quad;\quad m_1 = -\frac{(-0.861)}{20} = +0.0431
$$

*Outer zone* (dₒ = 20 m, f₂ = −0.40 m):

$$
\frac{1}{d_i} = \frac{1}{-0.40} - \frac{1}{20} = -2.5000 - 0.0500 = -2.5500
$$

$$
d_i = -0.392~\text{m} \quad;\quad m_2 = -\frac{(-0.392)}{20} = +0.0196
$$

**(iii) Comment on spatial variation:**

The outer zone has a smaller radius of curvature → stronger convergence of reflected rays → the image is formed **closer to the mirror** (0.392 m vs 0.861 m) and is **smaller in size** (m₂ = 0.0196 vs m₁ = 0.0431). This *spatial* change in magnification is the engineering essence of the aspheric mirror:
- **Inner zone (small m reduction):** The driver sees a near-natural-size image of the directly-trailing car → **accurate distance/speed judgement**.
- **Outer zone (large m reduction):** A much wider field of view is compressed into the same mirror glass → **minimised blind spot**, even though peripheral objects appear further than they really are.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Sign convention (–2 marks):** Forgetting that f and R are **negative** for a convex mirror, and writing f = +0.6 m. The answer will then violate the mirror equation.
> 2. **Mixing up object/image distances (–1 mark):** Stating that a convex mirror produces a *real* image. It never does — the image is **always virtual**, formed behind the mirror.
> 3. **Forgetting the warning label requirement (–1 mark, part-a):** For Class III and V mirrors, ECE R46 mandates the safety warning — students often miss this.
> 4. **Using magnification as m = dᵢ/dₒ** without the negative sign (–1 mark): It should be **m = −dᵢ/dₒ** in the Cartesian sign convention.
> 5. **No unit consistency (–0.5 mark each):** If R is in mm, dₒ in m and f comes out in m⁻¹ — always convert all lengths to the same unit *before* substituting.
> 6. **Skipping the ray diagram (–2 marks):** In any derivation question, a missing ray diagram attracts 1–2 mark deduction. Always draw the principal axis, pole P, centre C, focal F, and at least two rays.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Rear-view mirror = indirect-vision device**; passive safety; provides situational awareness.
- [x] **5 classes per ECE R46 / AIS-008:** I (interior plane), II (main exterior), III (wide-angle), IV (aspheric), V (close-proximity / kerb).
- [x] **Plane mirror:** R = ∞, f = ∞, m = +1, narrow FoV, accurate distance perception.
- [x] **Convex mirror:** f < 0, image always **virtual, erect, diminished**, m < 1, wide FoV, compressed distance perception.
- [x] **Mirror equation:** $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{d_o} + \dfrac{1}{d_i}$; **f = R/2**; **m = −dᵢ/dₒ**.
- [x] **Sign convention (Cartesian):** distances measured along incident light = positive; behind mirror = negative.
- [x] **ECE R46 minimum |R|:** Class II → 1.20 m, Class III → 0.30 m, Class V → 0.20 m.
- [x] **Aspheric mirror** uses two radii: large R (inner) for natural image, small R (outer) for wide FoV.
- [x] **Assessment tests:** Field of view, radius of curvature, reflectance, distortion, impact strength.
- [x] **Reflectance limits:** Day ≥ 40%, Night 4–12% (anti-glare prismatic / electrochromic).
- [x] **Warning label** "Objects in mirror are closer than they appear" is mandatory for Class III and V.
- [x] **Approval mark** is an encircled "E" (ECE) or "e" (EEC) followed by the country code and approval number.
- [x] **Magnification trade-off:** Larger |R| → larger m, narrower FoV. Aspheric breaks this trade-off spatially.
- [x] **Image always virtual** in convex mirrors → dᵢ is negative → m is positive → object appears erect.
- [x] **Power of mirror** P = 1/f (dioptres); unit conversions: 1 m = 1000 mm → 1 D = 1 m⁻¹.
- [x] **CMS (Camera Monitor Systems)** are now legal under ECE R46 Amendment 5 (2016) as a *replacement* for Classes I–V, provided the displayed field of view and minimum object resolution match the regulatory requirements.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Warning devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT743 - VEHICLE SAFETY AND SECURITY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Warning devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Warning Devices

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **Warning Device** in an automotive context is defined as any electro-mechanical, electronic, or opto-electronic subsystem whose primary function is to **alert the driver, occupants, or surrounding road users** about a potentially hazardous condition, an abnormal vehicle operating state, or an imminent collision threat. As per the **KTU PEMUT743 (2024 Scheme)** module classification, warning devices are categorized as a **Passive-Active Hybrid Safety Subsystem** — they remain dormant during normal operation but actively communicate risk the moment a threshold parameter is violated.

In strict engineering terminology, a warning device is a **Human-Machine Interface (HMI) transducer** that converts a sensor-derived analog or digital signal (e.g., low oil pressure, seatbelt unfastened, obstacle proximity) into a perceptible cue — auditory, visual, or haptic — within the **ISO 2575 / AIS-008 / ECE R121** standardized response time of **≤ 250 milliseconds** for in-cabin critical warnings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Definition (Board-Exam Standard):**
> *"Warning devices are vehicle-mounted sensory feedback systems that monitor vehicle operating parameters and ambient conditions, and upon detecting a deviation from safe thresholds, generate controlled visual, acoustic, or tactile alerts to enable timely corrective action by the driver, thereby reducing accident probability."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are **driving in heavy Kerala monsoon rain on NH-66**. Suddenly:
- Your **tyre loses grip** (no signal to you = accident).
- But if your car had a **gentle beep + dashboard icon**, you would *know* — and react.

This is exactly what a warning device does. It is the **"voice of the car"**.

**Three intuitive analogies:**

1. **The Hospital ICU Monitor Analogy:** Just like a heart monitor beeps when a patient's vitals deviate, warning devices "beep" when the car's vitals (oil pressure, temperature, brake fluid) cross danger limits.
2. **The Smoke Detector Analogy:** A smoke detector doesn't stop the fire — it warns you so you can act. Similarly, a TPMS sensor doesn't inflate a tyre; it just tells you it's deflating.
3. **The Co-Pilot Analogy:** A warning system acts like a vigilant co-pilot constantly scanning mirrors, sensors, and gauges — flagging threats the human driver might miss due to distraction, fatigue, or blind spots.

### 1.3 Classification of Warning Devices (KTU Board-Exam Taxonomy)

Warning devices in a modern automobile are broadly classified into **three fundamental categories**, with sub-classifications:

| S.No | Category | Function | Examples |
|:----:|:---------|:---------|:---------|
| **1** | **Auditory (Acoustic) Warning Devices** | Generate sound to alert driver or pedestrians | Horn, reverse buzzer, seatbelt chime, overspeed alarm |
| **2** | **Visual (Optic) Warning Devices** | Generate light signals (dashboard / external) | Tell-tale lamps, turn indicators, brake warning, hazard lamps |
| **3** | **Haptic / Tactile Warning Devices** | Generate physical vibration feedback | Steering wheel vibration (LDW), seat vibration, brake pedal pulsation (ABS feedback) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern ADAS Integration:** Contemporary vehicles (post-2018) often fuse all three modalities — termed **Multi-Modal Warning Architecture** — for collision avoidance (e.g., AEB + LDW + BSD working together in a Toyota Hilux or Tata Nexon EV).

### 1.4 Engineering Significance in KTU Curriculum

The study of warning devices forms the **bridge between passive safety** (which mitigates injury *after* a crash) and **active safety** (which *prevents* the crash). Under **NEP 2020 outcome-based education**, this topic maps directly to:

- **CO3:** *Analyse the operation of automotive warning, lighting, and signalling subsystems.*
- **CO4:** *Evaluate the effectiveness of warning systems in real-world accident mitigation scenarios.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Working Principle of Major Warning Devices

#### 2.1.1 The Electromagnetic Horn (Auditory Warning)

The horn is the **most fundamental** warning device mandated by law globally. The KTU syllabus emphasizes its working.

**Constructional Components:**
1. **Diaphragm** (high-carbon steel disc, typically 0.6–0.8 mm thick)
2. **Electromagnetic Coil** (copper winding, ~50–100 turns, energized at 12 V DC)
3. **Contact Breaker (Make-Break Mechanism)** — analogous to a bell's interrupter
4. **Resonator / Trumpet Horn** — acoustic amplifier (length tuned to specific frequency)
5. **Core (Soft Iron)** — concentrates magnetic flux

**Working Principle — Step-by-Step:**

- **Step 1 (Press):** Driver presses the horn pad on the steering wheel.
- **Step 2 (Energization):** Battery current flows through the coil, generating a strong magnetic field.
- **Step 3 (Attraction):** The magnetic field attracts the soft-iron diaphragm downward toward the core.
- **Step 4 (Contact Break):** As the diaphragm moves, it breaks the contact-breaker circuit (just like a bell's interrupter).
- **Step 5 (Release & Oscillation):** Coil de-energizes → diaphragm springs back → contact re-makes → coil re-energizes → cycle repeats.
- **Step 6 (Sound Generation):** This rapid make-break action (typically **400 ± 80 Hz** for city horns; **500 ± 80 Hz** for high-tone horns) creates mechanical oscillations of the diaphragm.
- **Step 7 (Amplification):** The resonator trumpet amplifies the oscillations to produce a loud, directional sound.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Key Fact:** Modern vehicles use a **Relay-Operated Horn Circuit** because direct switch contacts cannot handle the inductive current (~5–8 A) without arcing. The horn button only energizes a low-current relay coil (~150 mA), which then switches the high-current horn circuit.

#### 2.1.2 Reverse Warning Buzzer / Parking Sensors

**Principle:** When reverse gear is engaged, the reverse-light switch closes, simultaneously:
- Activating reverse lights (visual cue)
- Energizing a **piezo-ceramic buzzer** (auditory cue)

**Modern Ultrasonic Parking Sensors (PT-PT Cycle):**

$$f_{transmit} = \frac{1}{T_{cycle}} \quad \text{[Hz]}$$

Where the round-trip time of an ultrasonic pulse (typically **40 kHz**) is measured:

$$d = \frac{v_{sound} \times t_{round-trip}}{2}$$

Where:
- $v_{sound} \approx 343$ m/s (at 20°C)
- $d$ = distance to obstacle
- $t_{round-trip}$ = measured echo time

The warning **frequency increases** (beeps become faster) as distance decreases:

| Distance (m) | Beep Rate | Audible Tone |
|:------------:|:---------:|:------------:|
| > 1.5 | No beep | Silent |
| 1.0 – 1.5 | Slow (1 Hz) | Beep...Beep |
| 0.5 – 1.0 | Medium (2 Hz) | Beep.Beep |
| 0.25 – 0.5 | Fast (5 Hz) | BeepBeep |
| < 0.25 | Continuous | Solid tone |

#### 2.1.3 Tell-Tale Warning Lamps (Dashboard Indicators)

These are the colored icons on the instrument cluster. KTU mandates knowledge of **standardized tell-tale colors** as per **AIS-008 (Automotive Industry Standards, India)** and **ECE R121**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Color Coding (Board-Exam Favorite):**
> - 🔴 **RED** → Critical danger (brake failure, oil pressure, coolant temp, airbag) — *immediate action required*
> - 🟡 **AMBER/YELLOW** → Caution / Non-critical fault (check engine, ABS, low fuel) — *service soon*
> - 🔵 **BLUE** → Information / System active (high-beam indicator)
> - 🟢 **GREEN** → System operating normally (turn signals, fog lamps)

#### 2.1.4 Tyre Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS)

**Two types:**
1. **Direct TPMS (dTPMS):** Pressure sensor *inside* each tyre (battery-powered, RF transmitter at 433.92 MHz). Warns when pressure drops below **75% of recommended value**.
2. **Indirect TPMS (iTPMS):** Uses **ABS wheel-speed sensors** to detect under-inflation via rolling-radius difference.

**Trigger Threshold (typical):**

$$P_{warn} = 0.75 \times P_{nominal} \quad \text{[bar]}$$

For $P_{nominal} = 2.2$ bar → $P_{warn} \approx 1.65$ bar.

#### 2.1.5 Forward Collision Warning (FCW) System

FCW uses a **front-mounted radar (77 GHz)** or **stereo camera** to compute **Time-To-Collision (TTC)**:

$$TTC = \frac{d_{relative}}{v_{relative}}$$

Where:
- $d_{relative}$ = distance between vehicles (m)
- $v_{relative}$ = closing velocity (m/s)

**Warning cascade (typical OEM tuning):**
- TTC > 2.5 s → Yellow indicator
- 1.5 s < TTC < 2.5 s → Amber + audible chime
- TTC < 1.5 s → Red + haptic brake pulse + pre-charge AEB

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in numerical sub-parts (a) of 14-mark questions.**

| S.No | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Units | Validity / Boundary |
|:----:|:------------------|:-----------------|:-----:|:--------------------|
| 1 | $d = \dfrac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{round-trip}}{2}$ | Ultrasonic obstacle distance | metres (m) | $v_{sound} = 343$ m/s @ 20°C; valid for $d \in [0.1, 5]$ m |
| 2 | $TTC = \dfrac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}}$ | Time-To-Collision (FCW / AEB) | seconds (s) | Valid when $v_{rel} > 0$ (closing only) |
| 3 | $P_{warn} = 0.75 \cdot P_{nominal}$ | TPMS alert threshold | bar or kPa | Used in dTPMS (direct sensor) |
| 4 | $f_{horn} = 400 \pm 80$ Hz | City horn frequency | Hz | AIS-010 / ECE R28 compliance |
| 5 | $f_{horn,high} = 500 \pm 80$ Hz | High-tone horn frequency | Hz | Used in tandem-horn setups |
| 6 | $L_{p} = 93 \pm 5$ dB(A) | Horn sound level @ 7 m | dB(A) | AIS-010 Type Approval |
| 7 | $t_{response} \leq 250$ ms | Driver-alert reaction window | ms | ISO 2575 / ECE R121 |
| 8 | $\Delta d_{wheel} \propto \Delta P_{tyre}$ | Indirect TPMS detection (via $\omega$ diff) | dimensionless | Uses ABS wheel-speed data |
| 9 | $V_{alert} = V_{sys} - V_{drop,coil}$ | Horn relay voltage | Volts (V) | $V_{sys} = 12$ V; $V_{drop} \approx 0.3$ V |
| 10 | $\theta_{cone} = \arctan\left(\dfrac{R_{sensor}}{d}\right)$ | Detection cone half-angle | degrees | Stereo camera / radar FOV |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Ultrasonic Parking Sensor Distance Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A car reverses toward a wall. The ultrasonic sensor emits a 40 kHz pulse, receives the echo after **5.83 ms**. Calculate the distance to the wall. (Assume $v_{sound} = 343$ m/s, temperature = 20°C.)

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the round-trip time:**

$$t_{round-trip} = 5.83 \text{ ms} = 5.83 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the kinematic distance formula:**

The pulse travels from sensor → wall → sensor. Hence total distance covered by sound = $2d$.

$$2d = v_{sound} \cdot t_{round-trip}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2d &= 343 \; \text{m/s} \times 5.83 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{s} \\
2d &= 343 \times 5.83 \times 10^{-3} \\
2d &= 1.99969 \; \text{m} \\
2d &\approx 2.00 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for one-way distance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \dfrac{2.00}{2} \; \text{m} \\
d &= 1.00 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (KTU Board expects this!):**

At $d = 1.00$ m, the parking sensor will emit **slow audible beeps (1 Hz)** and illuminate an amber warning. The driver must apply brakes.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Time-To-Collision (TTC) for FCW Warning

**Problem Statement:** Two cars are approaching each other on a two-lane road. Car A is at 60 km/h, Car B at 40 km/h, and the gap between them is 75 m. Determine the TTC and identify the FCW warning level.

**Step 1 — Convert speeds to SI units:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_A &= 60 \; \text{km/h} = \dfrac{60 \times 1000}{3600} = 16.67 \; \text{m/s} \\
v_B &= 40 \; \text{km/h} = \dfrac{40 \times 1000}{3600} = 11.11 \; \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute relative (closing) velocity:**

Since they move towards each other, velocities add:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{relative} &= v_A + v_B \\
v_{relative} &= 16.67 + 11.11 = 27.78 \; \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the TTC formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TTC &= \dfrac{d_{relative}}{v_{relative}} \\
TTC &= \dfrac{75 \; \text{m}}{27.78 \; \text{m/s}} \\
TTC &= 2.70 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Classify warning level (OEM threshold):**

- TTC = 2.70 s → falls in **YELLOW zone (caution indicator only)** — driver is informed but not yet in imminent danger.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: TPMS Pressure Threshold Trigger

**Problem Statement:** A Maruti Suzuki Swift has a recommended tyre pressure of 2.2 bar (front) and 2.0 bar (rear). Compute the TPMS warning threshold for each tyre. Determine whether a front tyre at 1.7 bar will trigger a warning.

**Step 1 — Compute front-tyre threshold:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{warn,front} &= 0.75 \times 2.2 \\
P_{warn,front} &= 1.65 \; \text{bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute rear-tyre threshold:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{warn,rear} &= 0.75 \times 2.0 \\
P_{warn,rear} &= 1.50 \; \text{bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate actual front-tyre pressure:**

Measured $P_{actual,front} = 1.7$ bar

Compare: $P_{actual} > P_{warn}$ → **No warning triggered yet**, but driver is approaching threshold.

If pressure drops to 1.6 bar (< 1.65 bar) → **TPMS warning activates**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Horn Circuit Voltage Drop Analysis

**Problem Statement:** A horn draws 6 A from a 12 V battery through a relay. The wiring resistance (total) is 0.05 Ω. Calculate the voltage actually delivered to the horn coil.

**Step 1 — Compute voltage drop across wiring:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{drop} &= I \times R_{wire} \\
V_{drop} &= 6 \times 0.05 = 0.30 \; \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute voltage across horn:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{horn} &= V_{battery} - V_{drop} \\
V_{horn} &= 12 - 0.30 = 11.70 \; \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The horn receives 11.7 V — sufficient for operation. If wiring were of poor quality ($R_{wire} = 0.5$ Ω), $V_{horn}$ would drop to **9 V**, causing weak horn output.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Warning Device Selection by Driving Scenario

| Driving Scenario | Primary Warning Device | Secondary Backup | KTU Board Exam Linkage |
|:-----------------|:----------------------|:-----------------|:------------------------|
| Reversing in tight parking | Ultrasonic parking sensor (auditory) | Reverse camera overlay | Module 3, Q on reverse buzzer |
| Tyre deflation on highway | TPMS dTPMS (visual + chime) | Indirect via ABS | Module 3, ADAS integration |
| Front collision imminent | FCW (visual + audio + haptic) | AEB auto-brake | Module 3, ADAS theory |
| Lane drift without indicator | LDW (steering vibration) | Lane Keep Assist | Module 3, ADAS theory |
| Oil pressure loss | Oil pressure tell-tale (RED lamp) | Audible chime | Module 3, dashboard warnings |
| Coolant overtemperature | Temperature gauge + RED lamp | Engine limp mode | Module 3, dashboard warnings |
| Blind-spot vehicle | BSD mirror icon | Steering vibration | Module 3, ADAS theory |
| Pedestrian in reverse path | Rear cross-traffic alert | AEB reverse | Module 3, ADAS theory |
| Driver fatigue | Driver Attention Monitor | Coffee-cup icon | Module 3, modern HMI |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern Warning Device Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Input Layer] --> B[Sensor Array Cluster]
    B --> C1[Ultrasonic Sensor 40kHz]
    B --> C2[Radar 77GHz]
    B --> C3[Camera Stereo Pair]
    B --> C4[Wheel Speed Sensor ABS]
    B --> C5[Pressure Temperature Sensor]
    B --> C6[Inertial Measurement Unit IMU]
    C1 --> D[Central Processing ECU]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    D --> E{Decision Threshold Engine}
    E -->|Normal| F[SILENT - No Warning]
    E -->|Caution Amber| G[Visual Warning Output]
    E -->|Critical Red| H[Auditory Warning Buzzer]
    E -->|Imminent Crash| I[Haptic Warning Vibration]
    G --> J[Driver Receives Cue]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Corrective Driver Action]
    K --> L[Situation Resolved]
    F --> M[Monitor Continues]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Horn Circuit Diagram (Relay-Operated)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Battery 12V] -->|Positive Cable| B[Horn Relay Coil]
    B -->|Low Current Path| C[Horn Button / Steering Pad]
    C -->|Ground| D[Chassis Earth]
    B -->|High Current Path| E[Horn Contact Switches]
    E --> F[Horn Electromagnetic Coil]
    F --> G[Diaphragm Oscillator]
    G --> H[Resonator Trumpet]
    H --> I[Acoustic Output 93 dB]
```

### 4.3 TPMS Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pressure Sensor in Tyre] -->|433.92 MHz RF Signal| B[TPMS Receiver Module]
    B --> C[Vehicle ECU]
    C --> D{Compare with 0.75 Pnom}
    D -->|Pressure OK| E[No Display Action]
    D -->|Pressure Low| F[Dashboard TPMS Icon Illumination]
    F --> G[Single Audible Chime]
    G --> H[Driver Inflation Action]
    H --> I[Pressure Restored above Threshold]
    I --> E
```

### 4.4 Multi-Modal Warning Cascade (FCW + AEB Integration)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Front Radar 77GHz Detects Object] --> B[Compute TTC]
    B --> C{TTC Threshold Check}
    C -->|TTC greater 2.5s| D[Stage 1: Yellow Visual Only]
    C -->|TTC 1.5 to 2.5s| E[Stage 2: Amber Visual plus Chime]
    C -->|TTC less 1.5s| F[Stage 3: Red Visual plus Haptic Brake Pulse]
    C -->|TTC less 0.8s| G[Stage 4: AEB Auto Brake Activation]
    D --> H[Driver Awareness]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Collision Avoided]
```

### 4.5 Dashboard Tell-Tale Lamp Standardization (ECE R121)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tell-Tale Color Code Standard] --> B[RED - Danger]
    A --> C[AMBER - Caution]
    A --> D[BLUE - High Beam Active]
    A --> E[GREEN - System Normal]
    B --> B1[Brake System]
    B --> B2[Oil Pressure]
    B --> B3[Coolant Temperature]
    B --> B4[Airbag Fault]
    C --> C1[Check Engine]
    C --> C2[ABS Fault]
    C --> C3[Low Fuel]
    C --> C4[TPMS Warning]
    D --> D1[High Beam Indicator]
    E --> E1[Turn Signal Left]
    E --> E2[Turn Signal Right]
    E --> E3[Fog Lamp]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
Explain the working of an electromagnetic horn used in automobiles. Why is a relay used in the horn circuit?

**Model Answer (3-Mark Board-Standard Response):**

The electromagnetic horn works on the principle of **electromagnetic attraction and vibration**. When the horn button is pressed, current from the battery flows through an electromagnetic coil, creating a magnetic field that attracts a soft-iron diaphragm toward the core. This movement breaks a contact-breaker (make-break switch), de-energizing the coil. The diaphragm springs back, re-makes contact, and the cycle repeats rapidly (~**400 Hz** for low-tone, **500 Hz** for high-tone), producing audible sound amplified by a resonator trumpet.

A **relay is used** because the horn draws high current (~5–8 A) which would damage the lightweight horn-pad contacts. The relay allows a low-current (~150 mA) control circuit to switch a high-current load, ensuring **contact longevity, safer operation, and reliable horn activation**. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
List any **six** dashboard tell-tale warning lamps and state their color-coding significance as per AIS-008 / ECE R121.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Tell-Tale Lamp | Color | Significance |
|---|---------------|:-----:|:-------------|
| 1 | Brake System Warning | RED | Critical — braking system fault |
| 2 | Engine Oil Pressure | RED | Critical — low pressure |
| 3 | Coolant Temperature | RED | Critical — overheating |
| 4 | Airbag (SRS) Fault | RED | Critical — airbag disabled |
| 5 | Check Engine (MIL) | AMBER | Caution — service soon |
| 6 | TPMS Warning | AMBER | Caution — tyre pressure low |
| 7 | ABS Fault | AMBER | Caution — ABS disabled |
| 8 | Turn Signal | GREEN | Information — operational |

**Significance (1 mark):** Red = immediate danger; Amber = caution; Green = system normal; Blue = high-beam active. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice)

---

## ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**Q.A (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of an electromagnetic automobile horn**. Discuss its frequency, sound-level specifications, and the role of the relay. **(7 Marks)**

**Q.A (b)** Explain the **ultrasonic parking sensor system** in detail. Derive the distance formula and describe how beep frequency changes with obstacle proximity. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Q.A (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Construction (2 Marks):**

The electromagnetic horn consists of:
- A **diaphragm** (high-carbon steel disc, ~0.7 mm thick)
- An **electromagnetic coil** wound on a former (~50–100 turns, 12 V DC)
- A **soft-iron core** to concentrate magnetic flux
- A **make-break contact breaker** (similar to an electric bell)
- A **resonator trumpet / horn** for acoustic amplification
- **Mounting bracket** and **terminal connectors**

**2. Working (3 Marks):**

- Driver presses the horn pad on the steering wheel.
- Current flows through the coil, generating a magnetic field.
- The diaphragm is attracted toward the core.
- The contact breaker opens, de-energizing the coil.
- The diaphragm springs back (restoring force).
- Contact re-makes, current flows again — cycle repeats.
- This oscillation (at **400 ± 80 Hz** for city horn) produces sound, amplified by the resonator to **93 ± 5 dB(A) at 7 m**.

**3. Role of Relay (1 Mark):**

A relay isolates the high-current horn load (~6 A) from the delicate horn-button contacts (~150 mA control), preventing contact pitting, welding, and ensuring long-term reliability.

**4. Sketch (1 Mark):** *(Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid block — equivalent manual sketch expected.)*

---

### Model Answer for Q.A (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Concept (2 Marks):**

Ultrasonic parking sensors use **piezo-ceramic transducers** mounted in the rear bumper. They emit pulses of ultrasound at **~40 kHz** (above human hearing range) and listen for echoes reflected from obstacles.

**2. Distance Formula Derivation (3 Marks):**

The pulse travels from sensor → obstacle → sensor. Total sound path = $2d$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Distance formula: } \quad d &= \dfrac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{round-trip}}{2} \\
\text{With } v_{sound} &= 343 \; \text{m/s} \text{ at 20°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** For $t_{round-trip} = 5.83$ ms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \dfrac{343 \times 5.83 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \\
d &= 1.00 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Beep-Frequency Mapping (2 Marks):**

| Distance | Beep Rate | Driver Cue |
|:--------:|:---------:|:-----------|
| > 1.5 m | None | Safe |
| 1.0–1.5 m | 1 Hz | Slow |
| 0.5–1.0 m | 2 Hz | Medium |
| 0.25–0.5 m | 5 Hz | Fast |
| < 0.25 m | Continuous | Stop! |

---

## ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Q.B (a)** Describe the **Tyre Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS)** — both direct and indirect types. Explain the threshold pressure formula and its role in safety. **(7 Marks)**

**Q.B (b)** Explain the **Forward Collision Warning (FCW)** system. Derive the Time-To-Collision (TTC) equation and describe the multi-modal warning cascade with reference to AEB integration. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Q.B (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Introduction (1 Mark):**

TPMS is a **mandatory warning device** (since 2014 in India under AIS-110 / GSR 789(E)) that continuously monitors tyre pressure and alerts the driver when pressure drops below a critical threshold, preventing accidents due to under-inflation (which causes blowouts, poor braking, and reduced fuel economy).

**2. Direct TPMS — dTPMS (3 Marks):**

- A **pressure + temperature sensor + RF transmitter** is mounted *inside* each tyre (clamped to the rim or valve-stem based).
- Sensor measures absolute pressure and broadcasts it at **433.92 MHz** (ISM band, regulated).
- A central receiver module decodes the signals and displays pressure on the dashboard or triggers a warning when:
$$P_{actual} < 0.75 \times P_{nominal}$$
- Battery life: **~7–10 years**. Sensor weighs ~30 g.

**3. Indirect TPMS — iTPMS (2 Marks):**

- No dedicated pressure sensor — uses **existing ABS wheel-speed sensors**.
- An under-inflated tyre has a **smaller rolling radius** → rotates slightly faster → ECU detects the angular-velocity difference:
$$\Delta \omega_{wheel} \propto \Delta P_{tyre}$$
- Cheaper but less accurate; cannot detect *simultaneous* under-inflation in all four tyres (no reference).

**4. Safety Role (1 Mark):**

Reduces tyre-related accidents by **~50%** (NHTSA study); improves fuel efficiency; extends tyre life.

---

### Model Answer for Q.B (b) — 7 Marks

**1. FCW Concept (2 Marks):**

FCW is an **Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS)** that uses forward-facing **77 GHz millimeter-wave radar** (Bosch / Continental / Veoneer make) and/or a **stereo camera** to detect vehicles, pedestrians, or cyclists ahead, and warn the driver of an impending collision.

**2. TTC Derivation (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TTC &= \dfrac{d_{relative}}{v_{relative}} \\
\text{where: } & \\
d_{relative} &= \text{inter-vehicle gap (m)} \\
v_{relative} &= \text{closing velocity (m/s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical:** For two cars at 60 km/h and 40 km/h, gap = 75 m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{rel} &= 16.67 + 11.11 = 27.78 \; \text{m/s} \\
TTC &= \dfrac{75}{27.78} = 2.70 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Multi-Modal Warning Cascade (2 Marks):**

| TTC Range | Warning Mode | Driver Action |
|:---------:|:-------------|:--------------|
| > 2.5 s | Yellow icon | Awareness |
| 1.5–2.5 s | Amber + chime | Brace |
| 0.8–1.5 s | Red + haptic brake pulse | Emergency brake |
| < 0.8 s | AEB full auto-brake | Automatic intervention |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. ❌ Confusing **horn frequency** (400 Hz) with **ultrasonic sensor frequency** (40 kHz) — they are **different by a factor of 100**. Horn is *audible*; sensor is *ultrasonic* (in-audible).
> 2. ❌ Using $v_{sound} = 330$ m/s instead of **343 m/s** (20°C reference) — loses 0.5 mark per occurrence.
> 3. ❌ Writing *"FCW stops the car"* — wrong! FCW only **warns**. **AEB (Automatic Emergency Braking)** is what actually brakes.
> 4. ❌ Forgetting to mention **indirect TPMS uses ABS sensors** — a favourite trick question.
> 5. ❌ Drawing horn circuit *without relay* — loses full mark for the relay sub-part.
> 6. ❌ Writing "$TTC = \dfrac{v_{rel}}{d_{rel}}$" — formula is **inverted**. Always $d/v$, not $v/d$.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — High-Yield Bullet Points:**

**Horn:**
- ✅ Electromagnetic, make-break contact principle
- ✅ Frequency: **400 ± 80 Hz** (city) / **500 ± 80 Hz** (high-tone)
- ✅ Sound level: **93 ± 5 dB(A) at 7 m**
- ✅ Always uses **relay** in modern circuits

**Reverse Warning:**
- ✅ Reverse-light switch energizes buzzer
- ✅ Piezo-ceramic or electromagnetic buzzer
- ✅ Ultrasonic sensors at **40 kHz**
- ✅ Distance formula: $d = \dfrac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{rt}}{2}$

**Tell-Tale Lamps:**
- ✅ Red = Danger, Amber = Caution, Green = Normal, Blue = High-beam
- ✅ Standardized by **AIS-008 / ECE R121**

**TPMS:**
- ✅ Direct (sensor-based) vs Indirect (ABS-based)
- ✅ Threshold: $P_{warn} = 0.75 P_{nominal}$
- ✅ RF frequency: **433.92 MHz**
- ✅ Mandatory in India since **2014 (AIS-110)**

**FCW / ADAS:**
- ✅ Uses **77 GHz radar** + stereo camera
- ✅ $TTC = \dfrac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}}$
- ✅ Multi-modal: visual + audio + haptic
- ✅ AEB activates below **TTC ≈ 0.8 s**

**Miscellaneous:**
- ✅ Seatbelt warning chime (auditory, ~7 sec)
- ✅ Door-ajar warning (visual + chime)
- ✅ Blind Spot Detection (BSD) — 24 GHz radar in rear bumper
- ✅ Lane Departure Warning (LDW) — camera-based, steering-wheel haptic
- ✅ Driver Attention Monitor (camera-based fatigue detection)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cybersecurity in automobiles: Introduction to cybersecurity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT743 - VEHICLE SAFETY AND SECURITY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cybersecurity in automobiles: Introduction to cybersecurity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cybersecurity in automobiles** refers to the disciplined practice of protecting vehicle electronic systems, communication networks, software, data, and connected services from unauthorized access, malicious manipulation, disruption, or destruction throughout the vehicle's entire lifecycle — from initial concept and design, through production, operation, maintenance, and end-of-life decommissioning.

In the context of **KTU PEMUT743**, automotive cybersecurity is treated as a **Safety-of-Intended-Functionality (SOTIF) adjacent discipline** where the integrity of the E/E (Electrical/Electronic) architecture is preserved against deliberate adversarial actions. It is formally defined under the **ISO/SAE 21434:2021** standard as:

> *"Discipline concerned with the protection of road vehicles from cyber threats, encompassing the methods, processes, and technologies used to safeguard vehicular systems from unauthorized electronic access, interference, and manipulation."*

The three foundational pillars of automotive cybersecurity, inherited from classical Information Security, are the **CIA Triad**:

| Pillar | Meaning in Automotive Context |
|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Protecting sensitive vehicle data (owner identity, GPS traces, CAN payloads) from eavesdropping. |
| **Integrity** | Ensuring that brake, steering, and acceleration commands cannot be forged or altered mid-flight. |
| **Availability** | Guaranteeing that critical real-time ECUs remain operational and responsive under attack (e.g., DoS resistance). |

> [!NOTE]
> A fourth pillar, **Authenticity** (proving that a message truly originated from a trusted ECU), is added in vehicular networks because CAN/LIN/FlexRay lack native source authentication, making spoofing a primary attack vector.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Seatbelt" Idea

Think of a modern connected car as a **high-rise office building on wheels**:

- The **ECUs** (Electronic Control Units) are the *individual offices* (engine room, finance department, mail room).
- The **CAN/LIN/Ethernet buses** are the *internal hallways and elevators* connecting them.
- The **OBD-II port, Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, 4G/5G, and V2X antennas** are the *public entrances and loading docks* where strangers can walk in.
- The **OTA update server, mobile app, and OEM cloud** are the *branch offices in another city* connected via the internet.

Traditional cars had only one entrance (the key in the ignition). A **connected car has dozens** — Bluetooth, infotainment USB ports, telematics SIMs, tire-pressure sensors, key fobs, charging plugs (for EVs), and even the diagnostic port. Just as a building needs a security desk, CCTV, access cards, and firewalls, the connected car needs layered cybersecurity controls.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic to remember pillars: "CIA + A"** → *C*onfidentiality, *I*ntegrity, *A*vailability, + *A*uthenticity.

## 1.3 Why Automotive Cybersecurity is a Critical Modern Concern

The transition from **isolated mechanical machines** to **Software-Defined Vehicles (SDVs)** has exponentially increased the attack surface. Key drivers include:

1. **External Connectivity**: 4G/5G telematics, Wi-Fi hotspots, Bluetooth, V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything), and cloud backends.
2. **Internal Complexity**: A premium car now contains **100+ ECUs** running on **100 million+ lines of code** (compared to ~10 million for a Boeing 787).
3. **ADAS & Autonomy**: SAE Level 3+ vehicles rely on cameras, radars, and LiDARs whose sensor fusion can be spoofed or jammed.
4. **OTA Updates**: Wireless firmware updates, while convenient, are now a *direct remote code execution channel* if intercepted.
5. **Regulatory Pressure**: UNECE **WP.29 R155** and **R156** regulations *mandate* a certified Cybersecurity Management System (CSMS) and Software Update Management System (SUMS) for type approval of new vehicles in 54+ countries since **July 2022**.

## 1.4 Historical Context — Landmark Automotive Cyber Incidents

| Year | Incident | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **2010** | Researchers from University of Washington & UC San Diego remotely attacked a car's telematics via the **OnStar system** and proved that physical CAN bus manipulation was possible. | First published academic proof of remote automotive attack. |
| **2015** | **Charlie Miller & Chris Valasek** executed a **remote Jeep Cherokee hack** via Sprint's cellular network, taking control of steering, brakes, and transmission at highway speeds. | Fiat Chrysler recalled **1.4 million vehicles**; the watershed moment for industry. |
| **2016** | The duo published "Advanced CAN Injection Techniques" showing how a single compromised ECU could impersonate others on the bus. | Showed the **lack of authentication** in CAN as a fundamental flaw. |
| **2017–2022** | Rise of **key fob relay attacks**, **Tesla MCU hacks**, and **Tesla key-cloning** via Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE). | Triggered mass deployment of UWB-based digital keys (IEEE 802.15.4z). |

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Terminology in Bold

- **Attack Surface**: The sum of all points (hardware interfaces, software services, network endpoints) where an unauthorized user can attempt to inject or extract data.
- **Threat Model**: A structured representation of all potential threats to a system (assets, attackers, attack vectors, vulnerabilities).
- **Vulnerability**: A weakness in a system that can be exploited (e.g., a buffer overflow in an ECU's bootloader).
- **Exploit**: Code or technique that leverages a vulnerability to achieve unintended behaviour.
- **TTP (Tactics, Techniques & Procedures)**: The pattern of behaviour used by an attacker, borrowed from the MITRE ATT&CK framework.
- **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System)**: Industry-standard **0.0 to 10.0** score quantifying severity of a software flaw.
- **TARA (Threat Analysis & Risk Assessment)**: The automotive-specific risk analysis methodology required by ISO/SAE 21434.

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, students are expected to recall at least **3 standard metrics** and **2 landmark incidents** for short-answer questions in Part A.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Attack Surface Architecture of a Modern Vehicle

The attack surface of a connected vehicle is **multi-layered** and is conventionally divided into four concentric zones:

### Layer 1 — External / Cloud Interfaces
- **Telematics Control Unit (TCU)**: Cellular (4G/5G) connection to OEM backend.
- **OTA Update Server**: Bidirectional firmware delivery channel.
- **Mobile App / Cloud API**: User authentication, remote start, location queries.
- **V2X (DSRC / C-V2X)**: Direct vehicle-to-vehicle and vehicle-to-infrastructure short-range communication at **5.9 GHz**.

### Layer 2 — External Short-Range Interfaces
- **Bluetooth / BLE** (infotainment, digital key).
- **Wi-Fi** (diagnostic / dealership flashing).
- **RFID Keyless Entry** (125 kHz / 433 MHz legacy, 2.4 GHz UWB modern).
- **TPMS Sensors** (315/433 MHz RF).
- **GNSS (GPS/Galileo)** receiver.
- **Infotainment USB / AUX** ports.

### Layer 3 — In-Vehicle Network (IVN)
- **CAN / CAN-FD** (Classical CAN at 500 kbps; CAN-FD up to 5 Mbps).
- **LIN** (Local Interconnect Network at 19.2 kbps).
- **FlexRay** (10 Mbps, time-triggered).
- **Automotive Ethernet** (100 Mbps – 1 Gbps, e.g., 100BASE-T1).
- **MOST** (Media Oriented Systems Transport) for infotainment.

### Layer 4 — Internal ECUs & Sensors
- Powertrain ECU, Body Control Module (BCM), ADAS domain controller, Radar/Camera ECUs, BMS (Battery Management System for EVs).

## 2.2 The Seven Primary Attack Vectors

| # | Vector | Mechanism | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Remote / Network Intrusion** | Exploiting TCU, OTA, or V2X over cellular/wireless. | **2015 Jeep Cherokee hack** via Sprint cellular. |
| 2 | **Physical / OBD-II Access** | Plugging into the diagnostic port under the dash. | Dealership-level ECU flashing attacks. |
| 3 | **Bluetooth/Wi-Fi Exploits** | Buffer overflows in infotainment stack. | **Tesla Model S 2016 HACK** via Wi-Fi captive portal. |
| 4 | **Key Fob Relay / Replay** | Amplifying the key fob signal to unlock/start from a distance. | Relay attacks on **Volkswagen Golf 7** (2016). |
| 5 | **Sensor Spoofing / Jamming** | Fake GPS, radar, or LiDAR signals to mislead ADAS. | **GNSS spoofing** demonstrated against Teslas (2020). |
| 6 | **Supply-Chain Compromise** | Malicious code in third-party ECU firmware during manufacturing. | NotPetya-style firmware injection (hypothetical but studied). |
| 7 | **Insider / Aftermarket Threats** | Malicious firmware in OBD dongles, insurance trackers, or racing tunes. | **"Ecomcat" OBD-II dongle vulnerability** (2019). |

## 2.3 Goals of Automotive Cybersecurity — Detailed Mapping

The cybersecurity goals are derived from the **CIA + A** triad and adapted for safety-critical real-time systems:

### 2.3.1 Confidentiality
- **Vehicle owner privacy**: GPS traces, biometric driver profiles, voice recordings.
- **OEM intellectual property**: Proprietary calibration tables, AI model weights.
- **Countermeasure**: Encryption (AES-128/256), secure key storage in HSM (Hardware Security Module).

### 2.3.2 Integrity
- **Functional safety equivalence**: A spoofed brake-by-wire command has the same consequence as a physical brake failure.
- **Countermeasure**: Message Authentication Codes (MAC), digital signatures (ECDSA), Secure Onboard Communication (SecOC) per **AUTOSAR**.

### 2.3.3 Availability
- **Real-time deadlines**: A brake message delayed by 100 ms during a CAN bus DoS attack = life-threatening.
- **Countermeasure**: Bus arbitration priority, intrusion detection systems (IDS) on CAN.

### 2.3.4 Authenticity
- **ECU identity**: Each ECU must prove its identity cryptographically before its messages are trusted.
- **Countermeasure**: Secure boot, hardware root-of-trust, TPM 2.0 chips.

## 2.4 The TARA (Threat Analysis & Risk Assessment) Workflow

TARA is the *core methodological engine* of ISO/SAE 21434. It consists of six steps:

1. **Item Definition**: Identify the system under analysis and its operational environment.
2. **Asset Identification**: Catalogue data, functions, and hardware requiring protection.
3. **Threat Scenario Identification**: Enumerate attack scenarios (e.g., "attacker spoofs V2X emergency brake message").
4. **Impact Rating**: Score the potential harm on a 1–5 scale (financial, operational, safety, privacy).
5. **Attack Feasibility Rating**: Score how *practical* the attack is (e.g., expert knowledge, time, equipment required).
6. **Risk Determination & Treatment**: Combine Impact × Feasibility into a risk matrix; select mitigations (Avoid, Reduce, Share, Accept).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield**: TARA appears frequently in 14-mark Part B questions as a process diagram with explanation.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **must-memorize reference card** for this topic. Every cell is a guaranteed scoring point.

| Concept / Term | Symbol / Notation | Definition / Value | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Attack Surface | $\mathcal{A}$ | Sum of all entry points | High — appears in every Part A |
| Threat | $\mathcal{T}$ | Potential cause of an unwanted incident | High |
| Vulnerability | $\mathcal{V}$ | Weakness exploitable by a threat | High |
| Risk Value | $R$ | $R = f(\text{Impact}, \text{Feasibility})$ | Conceptual — explain qualitatively |
| CVSS Score | $S_{cvss}$ | $0.0 \le S_{cvss} \le 10.0$ | 3-mark question favourite |
| CIA Triad Pillars | $\{C, I, A\}$ | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability | Definition-level |
| Extended Pillars | $\{C, I, A, A\}$ | Add Authenticity + Non-Repudiation | High yield |
| Primary Standard | **ISO/SAE 21434** | Published **August 2021** | Must cite year |
| UN Regulation | **WP.29 R155** | CSMS mandate from **July 2022** | Must cite year |
| TARA Steps | $n = 6$ | Item Def. → Risk Treatment | High yield |
| Common Attack Vector | CAN bus | Lacks authentication natively | Classic exam answer |
| Connected Car Code Size | $\sim 10^{8}$ LOC | ~100 million lines of code | Fun fact for introductions |
| V2X Frequency Band | $f_{v2x}$ | **5.9 GHz** | Spec-required |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners **love** process-flow answers. If asked "Explain TARA," always **draw the 6-step block diagram** before writing prose.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEM Use**: Every major OEM (Bosch, Continental, Toyota, Tesla, Tata Motors) has a Product Cybersecurity Incident Response Team (PSIRT) following ISO/SAE 21434.
- **Regulator Use**: Type approval in the EU, Japan, Korea requires an **R155-compliant CSMS audit certificate** (valid for 3 years).
- **Insurance Use**: Cybersecurity ratings are beginning to influence auto-insurance premiums (similar to ADAS safety ratings).
- **Tier-1 Supplier Use**: Components delivered to OEMs must carry a **Cybersecurity Case (CyBox)** documenting the TARA and mitigations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This module is fundamentally **conceptual**, so the "derivations" below are structured as **formal logical progressions** rather than algebraic ones. Each step explicitly states the reasoning transition. KTU 2024 examiners reward structured, numbered logical chains in 14-mark answers.

## 3.1 Derivation — The Automotive Cybersecurity Risk Equation

The ISO/SAE 21434 standard defines risk qualitatively as a function of **impact** and **attack feasibility**. The KTU examiner expects a structured derivation of the **risk value** concept:

### Step 1 — Define the two independent axes of risk

$$
\text{Risk} = f(\text{Impact}, \text{Attack Feasibility})
$$

**Logic:** A risk exists *only* if there is (a) something of value that can be harmed, and (b) a realistic way for an attacker to cause the harm. These two factors are **independent dimensions** of a 2-D risk matrix.

### Step 2 — Quantize Impact into discrete levels

Define the impact severity level $I$ on an ordinal scale:

$$
I \in \{ \text{Negligible},\ \text{Minor},\ \text{Moderate},\ \text{Major},\ \text{Catastrophic} \}
$$

In many OEM implementations, this is mapped to the **5-level SOTIF severity scale** (S0–S4) inherited from ISO 21448, where **S4 = Catastrophic (loss of life)** and **S0 = No risk**.

**Logic:** Severity is independent of the attacker's skill; even a low-skill attacker can cause catastrophic harm if the safety implication is severe (e.g., brake manipulation).

### Step 3 — Quantize Attack Feasibility into discrete levels

$$
F \in \{ \text{Unknown},\ \text{High},\ \text{Medium},\ \text{Low},\ \text{Very Low} \}
$$

Following ISO/SAE 21434 Annex G, the feasibility rating considers **four sub-factors**:

$$
F = \phi(E,\ W,\ K,\ E_t)
$$

where $E$ = elapsed time, $W$ = window of opportunity, $K$ = specialist knowledge required, $E_t$ = equipment required (the "EWKE" model).

**Logic:** A high-impact threat is still low risk if attacking it requires a clean-room lab, 10 years of expertise, and 6 months of physical access.

### Step 4 — Construct the 5 × 5 Risk Matrix

The cartesian product of $I$ and $F$ yields a 5×5 risk matrix $\mathcal{R}$:

$$
\mathcal{R} = I \times F
$$

Each cell $\mathcal{R}_{i,f}$ is assigned a qualitative risk level (Negligible → Critical):

$$
\mathcal{R}_{i,f} = \text{RiskLevel}(I_i, F_f)
$$

| | **F: Very Low** | **F: Low** | **F: Medium** | **F: High** | **F: Unknown** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I: Catastrophic** | Low | Medium | High | **Critical** | **Critical** |
| **I: Major** | Low | Medium | High | High | Critical |
| **I: Moderate** | Negligible | Low | Medium | High | High |
| **I: Minor** | Negligible | Negligible | Low | Medium | Medium |
| **I: Negligible** | Negligible | Negligible | Low | Low | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip**: In the exam, always label rows as *Impact* and columns as *Feasibility* (or vice versa) — but be **consistent**. Examiners penalize ambiguous matrix axes.

### Step 5 — Map Risk to Treatment Options

The treatment decision tree, per ISO/SAE 21434 Clause 15, is:

$$
T = \begin{cases} \text{Avoid} & \text{if } \mathcal{R} = \text{Critical} \\ \text{Reduce} & \text{if } \mathcal{R} = \text{High} \\ \text{Share} & \text{if } \mathcal{R} = \text{Medium} \\ \text{Accept} & \text{if } \mathcal{R} = \text{Low or Negligible} \end{cases}
$$

**Logic:** *Avoid* means redesigning the function out of existence (e.g., remove the vulnerable Bluetooth stack). *Reduce* means adding a control (e.g., SecOC). *Share* means transferring risk via insurance or a third-party SOC. *Accept* means documenting and signing off.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Threat Categories vs. Cybersecurity Goals

The following matrix maps each threat to the **CIA+A pillar** it most directly violates. This is a **classic KTU 14-mark diagram** that earns full marks.

| Threat Category | Confidentiality | Integrity | Availability | Authenticity |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Eavesdropping on CAN bus (e.g., OBD-II sniffer) | ✔ | — | — | — |
| ECU spoofing (fake RPM message) | — | ✔ | — | ✔ |
| CAN bus DoS / flooding | — | — | ✔ | — |
| Replay attack (re-send old brake command) | — | ✔ | — | ✔ |
| Firmware tampering via OTA | — | ✔ | ✔ | — |
| GPS spoofing of ADAS | — | ✔ | — | ✔ |
| Key fob relay attack | — | — | ✔ | ✔ |
| Ransomware locking infotainment | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | — |

**Interpretation logic**: Most physical-world attacks (relay, DoS) violate **Availability + Authenticity**, while most logical attacks (spoofing, replay) violate **Integrity + Authenticity**. Confidentiality is least often the *primary* concern in safety-critical contexts, but is critical for **owner privacy**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — ISO/SAE 21434 Clauses

| Clause # | Title | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|
| Clause 5 | Organizational Cybersecurity Management | Medium |
| Clause 6 | Project-Dependent Cybersecurity Management | High |
| Clause 7 | Distributed Cybersecurity Activities | Low |
| Clause 8 | Continual Cybersecurity Activities | Medium |
| Clauses 9–14 | **Risk Assessment (TARA)** | **Very High** |
| Clause 15 | **Cybersecurity Case** & Assessment | **Very High** |
| Clause 16 | Release for Post-Development | Medium |
| Annex G | Attack Feasibility Rating (EWKE) | High |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trivia Question:** "What does EWKE stand for?" → *Elapsed time, Window of opportunity, Knowledge, Equipment*. A 3-mark question worth a guaranteed 3 marks.

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis — Classical CAN vs. CAN-FD vs. Automotive Ethernet

A common 14-mark KTU Part B question asks to compare these buses **from a cybersecurity perspective**:

| Parameter | Classical CAN | CAN-FD | Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard Speed | 500 kbps | Up to 5 Mbps | 100 Mbps (1 Gbps emerging) |
| Payload Size | 8 bytes | 64 bytes | 1500 bytes |
| Native Authentication | ❌ None | ❌ None | ✅ Possible via MACsec (IEEE 802.1AE) |
| Native Encryption | ❌ None | ❌ None | ✅ Possible via IPsec / TLS |
| Attack Difficulty | Low (broadcast sniffing trivial) | Low (same protocol) | High (segmented, switch-based) |
| Dominant Industry Position | Legacy powertrain | Modern powertrain | ADAS domain, backbone |

**Logic:** The newer the bus technology, the more **native security primitives** are available. This is why Automotive Ethernet is the **strategic platform** for next-generation software-defined vehicles (SDVs).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Layered Attack Surface of a Connected Vehicle

The following schematic maps every entry point of a modern connected vehicle to the layer it belongs to. This is the **single most diagrammed concept** in KTU PEMUT743 Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cloud_ext[External Cloud and Connectivity Layer]
    srt[Short Range Wireless Interface Layer]
    ivn[In Vehicle Network Bus Layer]
    ecu[Internal ECU and Sensor Layer]

    telematics[Telematics Control Unit TCU]
    ota[OTA Update Server Channel]
    v2x[V2X Communication 5.9 GHz]
    mobile[OEM Mobile App and Cloud API]

    ble[Bluetooth and BLE Interface]
    wifi[WiFi Hotspot Interface]
    keyfob[Keyless Entry UWB or RFID]
    usb[Infotainment USB Port]
    gnss[GNSS GPS Receiver]
    tpms[TPMS 315 or 433 MHz RF]

    can[CAN and CAN-FD Bus]
    lin[LIN Sub-Bus]
    flex[FlexRay Time Triggered Bus]
    eth[Automotive Ethernet Backbone]

    adas[ADAS Domain Controller]
    bms[Battery Management System EV]
    bcm[Body Control Module]
    pwt[Powertrain ECU]

    cloud_ext --> telematics
    cloud_ext --> ota
    cloud_ext --> v2x
    cloud_ext --> mobile

    srt --> ble
    srt --> wifi
    srt --> keyfob
    srt --> usb
    srt --> gnss
    srt --> tpms

    ivn --> can
    ivn --> lin
    ivn --> flex
    ivn --> eth

    ecu --> adas
    ecu --> bms
    ecu --> bcm
    ecu --> pwt

    telematics -.cross-layer link.-> can
    ble -.cross-layer link.-> eth
    ota -.cross-layer link.-> pwt

    classDef extStyle fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef srtStyle fill:#4a7c2e,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef ivnStyle fill:#8a5a00,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef ecuStyle fill:#7a1a1a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class cloud_ext, telematics, ota, v2x, mobile extStyle
    class srt, ble, wifi, keyfob, usb, gnss, tpms srtStyle
    class ivn, can, lin, flex, eth ivnStyle
    class ecu, adas, bms, bcm, pwt ecuStyle
```

> [!NOTE]
> The *cross-layer link* edges (dotted) illustrate the **threat propagation path**: an attacker enters at the cloud or short-range layer, pivots into the in-vehicle network, and finally manipulates the ECUs. This is exactly the **kill-chain** observed in the 2015 Jeep Cherokee hack.

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — The TARA Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[Step 1: Item Definition]
    step2[Step 2: Asset Identification]
    step3[Step 3: Threat Scenario Identification]
    step4[Step 4: Impact Rating 1 to 5]
    step5[Step 5: Attack Feasibility EWKE]
    step6[Step 6: Risk Determination and Treatment]

    step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 --> step6
    step6 -->|Iterate if Risk = Critical| step1

    classDef stepStyle fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    class step1, step2, step3, step4, step5, step6 stepStyle
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — The CIA + Authenticity Security Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    central[Cybersecurity Goal in Connected Vehicles]

    conf[Confidentiality<br/>Protect Owner Privacy<br/>OEM IP Theft Prevention]
    integ[Integrity<br/>Prevent Brake Steering Spoofing<br/>Secure Onboard Communication]
    avail[Availability<br/>Real Time Bus Response<br/>DoS Resistance]
    auth[Authenticity<br/>ECU Identity Verification<br/>Secure Boot and HSM]

    central --> conf
    central --> integ
    central --> avail
    central --> auth

    conf -.depends on.- auth
    integ -.depends on.- auth
    avail -.depends on.- integ

    classDef goalStyle fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef pillarStyle fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    class central goalStyle
    class conf, integ, avail, auth pillarStyle
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dotted edges between pillars express the **logical dependency**: Authenticity is the *enabling* pillar — without proving identity, you cannot enforce confidentiality (encrypted to whom?), integrity (intact from whom?), or availability (prioritizing whose traffic?).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define automotive cybersecurity and explain the CIA Triad in the context of a connected vehicle.**

**Model Answer:**

> Automotive cybersecurity is the practice of protecting a vehicle's electronic systems, communication networks, software, and connected services from unauthorized access, tampering, and disruption throughout its lifecycle. It is governed by the ISO/SAE 21434 standard.

**CIA Triad in vehicular context:**

- **Confidentiality**: Preventing unauthorized reading of data such as GPS location, vehicle speed, or driver biometrics transmitted over the CAN bus, Bluetooth, or telematics link.
- **Integrity**: Ensuring that safety-critical messages (e.g., brake command, steering angle) are not modified in transit, which is achieved via Secure Onboard Communication (SecOC).
- **Availability**: Guaranteeing that ECUs can communicate on the bus without being denied service, which is critical because a 100 ms delay in a CAN message can cause a crash.

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**List and briefly explain any three primary attack vectors in modern connected cars.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Remote Network Intrusion**: Exploiting the TCU's cellular (4G/5G) link to send malicious packets into the in-vehicle CAN bus. *Example*: The 2015 Jeep Cherokee hack.
2. **Key Fob Relay Attack**: Using radio repeaters to extend the key fob's signal range, allowing thieves to unlock and start the car without physical key access.
3. **Sensor Spoofing**: Transmitting counterfeit GPS, radar, or LiDAR signals to mislead ADAS features. *Example*: GPS spoofing can misroute navigation and confuse geofenced ADAS zones.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a layered block diagram, explain the **attack surface** of a modern connected vehicle. Identify at least **eight** distinct entry points.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **CIA + Authenticity** security model as applied to automotive systems, with at least one real-world example for each pillar.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define Attack Surface (1 mark)**

> The attack surface of a connected vehicle is the *union of all hardware, software, and communication interfaces* through which an adversary could attempt to read, inject, or manipulate data. In a modern SDV (Software-Defined Vehicle), this surface has expanded from a single physical key to dozens of wireless and cloud channels.

**Step 2 — Layered Block Diagram (3 marks)**

A 4-layer model is presented:

- **Layer 1 — Cloud & External Network**: Telematics Control Unit (4G/5G), OTA update server, OEM mobile app backend, V2X communication (5.9 GHz).
- **Layer 2 — Short-Range Wireless**: Bluetooth / BLE (infotainment, digital key), Wi-Fi (diagnostic), Keyless Entry (UWB/RFID), TPMS sensors, GNSS (GPS) receiver, USB port.
- **Layer 3 — In-Vehicle Network**: Classical CAN, CAN-FD, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1).
- **Layer 4 — ECUs & Sensors**: Powertrain ECU, ADAS domain controller, Body Control Module (BCM), BMS (EV).

**Step 3 — Enumerate Entry Points (2 marks)**

Eight distinct entry points are:

1. OBD-II diagnostic port (under-dash physical access).
2. Cellular telematics antenna.
3. Bluetooth / BLE pairing interface.
4. Wi-Fi diagnostic interface.
5. Keyless entry key fob (relay attack).
6. TPMS sensor RF receiver.
7. OTA firmware update channel.
8. V2X short-range radio.

**Step 4 — Concluding Statement (1 mark)**

> The trend toward software-defined vehicles is **expanding** the attack surface linearly, which makes layered (defence-in-depth) cybersecurity a regulatory and engineering necessity.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 Marks

| Pillar | Definition | Real-World Example | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Preventing unauthorized disclosure of vehicle data. | **OBD-II sniffer** extracting a fleet operator's GPS routes via the CAN bus. | AES-128 encryption of sensitive payloads; secure storage in HSM. |
| **Integrity** | Preventing unauthorized modification of safety-critical messages. | An attacker **replaying a forged brake-by-wire CAN message** to apply brakes. | SecOC with CMAC (AES-128) per AUTOSAR. |
| **Availability** | Ensuring real-time control messages reach the bus on time. | A **CAN bus flooding attack** that starves the arbitration queue and delays ABS messages. | Intrusion Detection System (IDS) on CAN; bus load monitoring. |
| **Authenticity** | Cryptographic proof of ECU identity. | **ECU spoofing** by replacing a legitimate powertrain message with a malicious node. | Secure boot + Hardware Root of Trust (TPM 2.0). |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The CIA + Authenticity model must be applied **holistically** because in vehicular systems, a single compromised pillar can cascade into safety violations (e.g., loss of integrity → loss of vehicle control → loss of life).

---

### ❓ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is **TARA (Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment)**? Explain each of its **six** steps in detail as per ISO/SAE 21434.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **four established automotive cybersecurity standards / regulations** and state the year each was published.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):**

> TARA is the structured, ISO/SAE 21434-mandated methodology to identify, classify, and treat cybersecurity risks in a vehicle's E/E system. It produces the *Cybersecurity Case* used in type approval.

**Six Steps (each 1 mark):**

1. **Item Definition**: Identify the *item* (a function or a system at the vehicle level), its boundary, interfaces, and operational environment. *Example*: The *Adaptive Cruise Control* function interacting with radar ECU, engine ECU, and brake ECU.

2. **Asset Identification**: Catalogue all assets — data (sensor outputs, calibration tables), functions (lane keeping), and hardware (the radar sensor). Each asset receives a *cybersecurity property* (C, I, A, or Authenticity) it requires.

3. **Threat Scenario Identification**: Enumerate plausible attack scenarios using the STRIDE-like model adapted to vehicles. *Example*: *"An attacker with OBD-II access injects forged wheel-speed messages to disable ABS."*

4. **Impact Rating**: Score the worst-case harm of each scenario on a 5-level scale. *Example*: ABS disablement during highway driving = **Catastrophic (S4)**.

5. **Attack Feasibility Rating**: Score the practicality of the attack using the **EWKE** model — *Elapsed time, Window of opportunity, Knowledge, Equipment*. *Example*: OBD-II injection requires *Low* time, *High* opportunity (any garage), *Medium* knowledge, *Low* equipment → overall feasibility = **High**.

6. **Risk Determination & Treatment**: Combine Impact × Feasibility on a 5×5 matrix; assign a risk level (Negligible → Critical); and apply one of four treatments: **Avoid, Reduce, Share, Accept** per Clause 15. Document residual risk in the *Cybersecurity Case*.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** TARA is **iterative**, not a one-shot process; it must be re-executed whenever a design change introduces new attack surfaces.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 Marks

| # | Standard / Regulation | Issuing Body | Year | Scope |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **ISO/SAE 21434** | ISO + SAE | **2021** | Engineering standard for vehicle cybersecurity lifecycle. |
| 2 | **UNECE WP.29 R155** | UNECE | **2021** (in force **2022-07**) | Regulation mandating CSMS for type approval. |
| 3 | **UNECE WP.29 R156** | UNECE | **2021** (in force **2022-07**) | Regulation mandating Software Update Management System (SUMS). |
| 4 | **ISO 21448 (SOTIF)** | ISO | **2022** | Safety of Intended Functionality — closely linked to sensor attacks. |
| 5 | **AUTOSAR SecOC** | AUTOSAR consortium | **2017+** (v4.2 onwards) | Secure Onboard Communication module. |
| 6 | **IEEE 802.1AE (MACsec)** | IEEE | **2006+ (2018 rev.)** | Layer-2 encryption for Automotive Ethernet. |
| 7 | **NIST CSF 1.1** | NIST | **2018** | Generic framework adapted by some OEMs. |

> **Conclusion:** Of the above, **ISO/SAE 21434** and **UNECE R155** are the *mandatory* duo for any OEM selling vehicles in the EU/Japan/Korea since **July 2022**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**
> 1. **Wrong Year Citations**: Students frequently write *“ISO 21434 was published in 2020”* — it is **August 2021**. Off by a year → **½ mark deducted** by strict examiners.
> 2. **Conflating SOTIF and Cybersecurity**: ISO 21448 (SOTIF) handles *unintended* hazards; ISO 21434 handles *intentional* (adversarial) threats. Mixing them up is a **2-mark penalty**.
> 3. **Wrong Pillar Order in CIA**: Always list as **C, I, A** — not A, I, C. Examiners are touchy on canonical ordering.
> 4. **Forgetting Authenticity**: In vehicular networks, **Authenticity is the most important pillar** (CAN has none natively), but students often omit it. This costs full marks on the "CIA + ?" sub-parts.
> 5. **Omitting the Dotted Dependency Edges** in the CIA diagram: showing Authenticity as a *prerequisite* for the other three is what differentiates a 6-mark answer from a 7-mark one.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print Before Exam):**

- ☐ **CIA Triad + Authenticity** → 4 pillars; order: C, I, A, Auth.
- ☐ **ISO/SAE 21434** published **August 2021** — engineering standard.
- ☐ **UNECE WP.29 R155** → CSMS mandate, **July 2022**.
- ☐ **UNECE WP.29 R156** → SUMS mandate, **July 2022**.
- ☐ **TARA** = 6 steps → *Item Def. → Assets → Threats → Impact → Feasibility (EWKE) → Treatment*.
- ☐ **EWKE** = *Elapsed time, Window, Knowledge, Equipment* (Annex G of ISO 21434).
- ☐ **Attack Surface** = 4 layers → *Cloud, Short-range, In-Vehicle Network, ECU/Sensor*.
- ☐ **CAN Bus** has *no native authentication or encryption* → primary attack vector.
- ☐ **2015 Jeep Cherokee hack** = first widely-publicized remote automotive cyber-attack; via *Sprint cellular network*.
- ☐ **Connected Car Code Size** ≈ **100 million LOC** (vs Boeing 787 = 14 M).
- ☐ **V2X Frequency** = **5.9 GHz** (DSRC or C-V2X).
- ☐ **Key Fob** attacks: legacy = 125 kHz / 433 MHz; modern = **UWB (2.4 GHz)** for crypto-secure ranging.
- ☐ **CVSS** score = **0.0 to 10.0**.
- ☐ **5×5 Risk Matrix** → axes are *Impact × Feasibility*; output = *Negligible → Critical*.
- ☐ **Treatment options** = *Avoid, Reduce, Share, Accept* (per ISO 21434 Clause 15).
- ☐ **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1)** supports **MACsec** (IEEE 802.1AE) for native security.
- ☐ **Autonomous driving levels** = SAE L0–L5; cybersecurity critical from L3+ onward.
- ☐ **SecOC** = Secure Onboard Communication (AUTOSAR) using CMAC (AES-128) for CAN authentication.
- ☐ **HSM** = Hardware Security Module for storing cryptographic keys securely.
- ☐ **OTA** = Over-The-Air updates; a *direct* RCE channel if not cryptographically signed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of cybersecurity in modern vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT743 - VEHICLE SAFETY AND SECURITY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Importance of cybersecurity in modern vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive Cybersecurity** is the discipline of engineering, implementing, and continuously maintaining protective measures, cryptographic protocols, and operational policies that defend the vehicular electronic and software architecture — including Electronic Control Units (ECUs), in-vehicle networks (CAN, LIN, Ethernet, FlexRay), wireless interfaces, telematics units, and Over-The-Air (OTA) update channels — against unauthorized access, malicious manipulation, data exfiltration, and functional safety compromise.

In the context of **KTU PEMUT743 (Vehicle Safety and Security Systems)**, cybersecurity in modern vehicles is treated as a **co-engineered extension of functional safety (ISO 26262)**. It is governed internationally by the **ISO/SAE 21434** standard and mandated through the **UNECE WP.29 R155 regulation**, which requires an auditable **Cybersecurity Management System (CSMS)** for type approval of any new vehicle model sold in signatory markets (including India-aligned Bharat NCAP discussions).

The threat boundary is no longer the physical perimeter of the car — it now extends across the **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) ecosystem**, including **V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle)**, **V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure)**, **V2N (Vehicle-to-Network)**, and **V2P (Vehicle-to-Pedestrian)** communication links.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Car as a Smartphone on Wheels

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a 2010-era car is a sealed mechanical safe — you need physical access and mechanical knowledge to tamper with it. A 2024-era connected vehicle is more like a **floating laptop with a 100 km/h jet engine, four cellular radios, a Bluetooth stack, a Wi-Fi hotspot, and a global SIM card**. Anyone on the internet can attempt to "log in" while the car is driving. That is precisely why cybersecurity is no longer optional — it is a **safety-critical discipline** on par with seatbelts and airbags.

Consider this simplified mental map:

| Era | Vehicle Character | Attack Surface | Risk Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1980s | Pure mechanical | Physical access only | Negligible |
| 2000s | ECU + OBD-II | Wired diagnostic port | Low |
| 2015 | Connected infotainment + telematics | Cellular + Bluetooth + USB | Medium |
| 2024+ | ADAS + V2X + OTA + AD | Wireless, remote, chained | **High / Safety-Critical** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** Examiners expect students to clearly distinguish between **functional safety** (random hardware/software failures — addressed by ISO 26262) and **security** (intentional malicious attacks — addressed by ISO/SAE 21434). Conflating the two is a common answer-writing error.

## 1.3 Why It Matters Now — Three Driving Forces

1. **Software-Defined Vehicles (SDV):** Modern premium cars run **100–150 million lines of code** spread across **70–150 ECUs**. Attack surface scales with code volume.
2. **Regulatory Mandate:** UNECE WP.29 R155 requires OEMs to obtain a **Certificate of Compliance for CSMS** before type approval from **July 2022** onwards.
3. **Functional Safety Consequence:** A compromised ADAS sensor or braking ECU can directly cause **physical injury or death**, transforming a cyber-vulnerability into a **lethal hazard**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Attack Surface of a Modern Vehicle

The **attack surface** is the sum of all points where an unauthorized actor can attempt to inject data, extract data, or alter system behaviour. In a modern vehicle, it is distributed across at least **seven distinct domains**:

| Domain | Entry Point | Example Attack Vector |
|---|---|---|
| External wireless | Cellular, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, GNSS, RFID key | SIM-jacking, fake GPS injection |
| In-Vehicle Network | CAN, CAN-FD, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet | Bus injection, replay attacks |
| Physical ports | OBD-II, USB, diagnostic connectors | Malicious ECU flashing |
| Sensors | Camera, radar, lidar, ultrasonic | Adversarial sensor spoofing |
| Infotainment / HMI | Touchscreen, voice, companion apps | App-based privilege escalation |
| Telematics / T-Box | Remote commands, eCall, OTA | Man-in-the-Middle on OTA updates |
| Supply chain | Third-party ECU firmware, tooling | Hardware trojans, malicious DLCs |

## 2.2 Threat Modelling — The CIA Triad Adapted to Vehicles

The classical **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (CIA)** triad is extended in automotive cybersecurity to a **fourth pillar — Authenticity**, because maliciously forged messages (e.g., fake brake-by-wire commands) are often more dangerous than mere eavesdropping.

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive CIA+A:**
> - **Confidentiality:** Personal driving patterns, location, biometrics must not leak.
> - **Integrity:** Sensor data and actuation commands must not be tampered with.
> - **Availability:** Brakes, steering, ADAS must respond within hard real-time deadlines.
> - **Authenticity:** Every message on the in-vehicle bus must originate from a genuine, authorized ECU.
