## 2.3 Real-World Incidents — The "Smoking Gun" Evidence Base

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners reward answers that cite **specific, well-documented incidents**. The following are the three canonical KTU-referenced cases:

1. **Jeep Cherokee Hack (2015, Miller & Valasek):** Remote exploit of the Uconnect infotainment stack via the cellular modem → CAN bus → critical vehicle controls. Resulted in **1.4 million vehicle recall** by FCA.
2. **Tesla Model S Key Fob Relay Attack (2018):** Relay of the passive keyless entry signal to steal the vehicle in seconds.
3. **BMW Connected Drive (2015):** Vulnerable HTTPS implementation allowed remote door unlock via the BMW cloud API.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates the formulae, scoring metrics, and standard references you are expected to know for ESE.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Standard / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Risk Severity (qualitative) | $R = P \times I$ | Risk = Probability of attack $\times$ Impact | Dimensionless index |
| 2 | Attack Feasibility Rating | $AF = f(E, K, W, O, E_{a})$ | Expertise, Knowledge, Window, Opportunity, Equipment | ISO/SAE 21434 Table 7 |
| 3 | Impact Rating | $I = f(S, F, P)$ | Safety, Financial, Privacy consequence | ISO/SAE 21434 Table 8 |
| 4 | Attack Surface Cardinality | $A_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \partial \Sigma_{i} \vert$ | Sum of boundary elements of each subsystem | Unitless count |
| 5 | Hard Real-Time Deadline | $T_{r} \leq T_{deadline}$ | Response time must be bounded for safety ECUs | milliseconds (ms) |
| 6 | CAN Bus Arbitration Latency | $t_{arb} = \dfrac{L_{frame}}{R_{bus}}$ | Frame length divided by bus bit-rate | $\mu s$ |
| 7 | Message Auth Code Length | $L_{MAC} \geq 64 \text{ bits}$ | Minimum truncation for automotive MAC | bits |
| 8 | Key Length (Symmetric) | $k \geq 128 \text{ bits}$ | AES-128 baseline for automotive | bits |
| 9 | OTA Update Bandwidth | $B_{OTA} = \dfrac{S_{image}}{t_{download}}$ | Firmware size / available time | MB/s |
| 10 | Security Level (CAL) | $CAL \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ | 1=low, 4=highest threat resistance | ISO/SAE 21434 |

## 2.5 Defence-in-Depth Architecture — Layered Security

A modern vehicle applies **defence-in-depth**, meaning security is not enforced at one single layer but redundantly across seven layers:

- **Layer 1 — Hardware Root of Trust (HSM):** Hardware Security Module with secure key storage.
- **Layer 2 — Secure Boot:** Cryptographic verification of firmware at power-on.
- **Layer 3 — Network Segmentation:** Gateway ECUs isolate safety-critical CAN buses from infotainment CAN.
- **Layer 4 — Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS):** CAN-IDS watches for anomalous message frequency, payload, or source.
- **Layer 5 — Cryptographic Protocols:** TLS 1.3 for V2X, MAC for CAN-FD, SecOC for AUTOSAR Classic.
- **Layer 6 — OTA Pipeline Security:** Signed firmware bundles, rollback protection, staged deployment.
- **Layer 7 — SOC (Security Operations Center):** Vehicle SOC monitors fleets for coordinated attacks.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

This layered framework allows a B.Tech engineer to:
- Conduct a **TARA (Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment)** during concept phase.
- Map identified threats to specific **cybersecurity goals** and **claims**.
- Justify design choices to certification auditors under **WP.29 R155**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quantitative Attack Surface Estimation

The attack surface of a vehicle is not just a "list" — for board-level answers, you should be able to derive a quantifiable metric.

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** Each subsystem $\Sigma_i$ (infotainment, telematics, gateway, ADAS, powertrain, body, chassis) has an entry-point count $e_i$ and a communication-edge count $c_i$ to the central gateway.

The **Attack Surface Cardinality** is then:

$$
A_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( w_e \cdot e_i + w_c \cdot c_i \right)
$$

where $w_e$ and $w_c$ are weighting coefficients reflecting the relative exploitability of entry points vs. communication edges.

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$
A_s = w_e \cdot (e_1 + e_2 + \cdots + e_n) + w_c \cdot (c_1 + c_2 + \cdots + c_n)
$$

$$
A_s = w_e \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i \; + \; w_c \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i
$$

**Worked example** (for an answer demonstrating application):

Consider a vehicle with $n = 4$ subsystems: Infotainment, Telematics, ADAS, Powertrain.

- $e = [5, 3, 2, 1]$, $c = [4, 3, 5, 2]$
- Take $w_e = 1$ and $w_c = 2$ (edges are weighted more heavily because bus injection is harder to detect).

$$
A_s = 1 \cdot (5+3+2+1) + 2 \cdot (4+3+5+2)
$$

$$
A_s = 1 \cdot 11 + 2 \cdot 14
$$

$$
A_s = 11 + 28 = 39 \text{ (arbitrary surface units)}
$$

This scalar can be used **comparatively** across vehicle variants to argue that variant A has $\dfrac{39 - A_{s,ref}}{A_{s,ref}} \times 100\%$ larger attack surface than the reference architecture — useful in design-review board exams.

## 3.2 Derivation: Risk Determination per ISO/SAE 21434

The standard defines risk as the product of **impact** and **attack feasibility**:

$$
R_{i,k} = I_i \times AF_k
$$

where:
- $I_i$ is the impact level from Table 8 of the standard.
- $AF_k$ is the attack feasibility score from Table 7.
- Subscript $i$ indexes the impacted asset, $k$ indexes the attack path.

**Risk matrix (KTU-style compact representation):**

| Impact $\rightarrow$ / Feasibility $\downarrow$ | Severe (S3) | Major (S2) | Moderate (S1) | Negligible (S0) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| High (F4) | Critical | High | Medium | Low |
| Medium (F3) | High | Medium | Low | Low |
| Low (F2) | Medium | Low | Low | Info |
| Very Low (F1) | Low | Info | Info | Info |

**Step-by-step sample application:**

- Threat: *Remote CAN injection via cellular telematics.*
- Impact: $I = S3$ (Severe — could disable brakes).
- Attack Feasibility: $AF = F3$ (Medium — requires expert, but published PoC exists).
- From matrix: **Risk = High** → requires immediate mitigation.

## 3.3 Derivation: CAN Bus Arbitration Timing (Security-Relevant)

A bus-injection attacker must win arbitration. Time-to-bus for a single frame:

$$
t_{frame} = \dfrac{(8 \cdot L_{data} + L_{header} + L_{CRC})}{R_{bus}}
$$

For a standard CAN frame with 8 data bytes ($L_{data} = 8$, $L_{header} + L_{CRC} \approx 47$ bits) at $R_{bus} = 500 \text{ kbps}$:

$$
t_{frame} = \dfrac{8 \cdot 8 + 47}{500{,}000}
$$

$$
t_{frame} = \dfrac{64 + 47}{500{,}000} = \dfrac{111}{500{,}000}
$$

$$
t_{frame} = 2.22 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 222 \text{ }\mu s
$$

This extremely short **$222 \text{ }\mu s$** window is why physical CAN injection requires precise timing — and why **IDS detection latency must be below $200 \text{ }\mu s$** to block malicious frames.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Attack Vector vs. Defence Mechanism

| Attack Vector | Primary Defence | Secondary Defence | KTU Key Term |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cellular remote command abuse | TLS 1.3 mutual auth | Rate limiting at T-Box | Telematics hardening |
| OBD-II physical flash | Signed firmware + secure boot | Port-disable policy | Diagnostic security |
| CAN bus injection | CAN-IDS + message authentication (SecOC) | Gateway filtering | In-vehicle IDS |
| Key fob relay | UWB ranging + crypto challenge | Motion sensor in fob | PKES hardening |
| OTA malicious update | RSA/ECC signature + rollback protection | Staged canary rollout | OTA integrity |
| V2X message spoofing | PKI certificate authority (IEEE 1609.2) | Pseudonym rotation | V2X security |

## 3.5 Verification: Why Cybersecurity Is Safety-Critical

The chain of reasoning examiners expect to see:

$$
\text{Vulnerable ECU} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Exploit}}\; \text{Loss of control} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Consequence}}\; \text{Collision / Injury}
$$

Quantitatively, if $P(\text{exploit}) = 10^{-4}$ per hour of operation, and $P(\text{collision} \mid \text{exploit}) = 0.5$, then:

$$
P(\text{collision due to cyber-attack per hour}) = 10^{-4} \times 0.5 = 5 \times 10^{-5}
$$

Over a $1{,}000$ km trip at $60 \text{ km/h}$ (≈ $16.7$ hours of driving):

$$
P_{\text{trip}} = 1 - (1 - 5\times 10^{-5})^{16.7} \approx 8.3 \times 10^{-4}
$$

This is **comparable to the ASIL-D residual risk** tolerated by ISO 26262 — demonstrating quantitatively why cyber-vulnerabilities cannot be ignored by safety engineers.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Mermaid diagrams are **block-level functional architecture flows** — they map the cybersecurity topology, communication boundaries, and defensive layers of a modern connected vehicle. They comply with the Mermaid safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs, clean unformatted labels).

## 4.1 Diagram A — Modern Vehicle Attack Surface Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    attacker1[EXTERNAL ATTACKER]
    attacker2[INSIDER MALICIOUS]
    attacker3[SUPPLY CHAIN]

    cell[CELLULAR MODEM]
    bt[BLUETOOTH WIFI]
    gps[GNSS RECEIVER]
    obd[OBD II PORT]
    tbox[TBOX TELEMATICS]
    hmi[INFOTAINMENT HMI]
    adas[ADAS SENSOR STACK]
    canbus[CAN AND ETHERNET BUS]
    gateway[CENTRAL GATEWAY ECU]
    safety1[BRAKING ECU]
    safety2[STEERING ECU]
    safety3[POWERTRAIN ECU]
    hsm[HARDWARE SECURITY MODULE]
    soc[VEHICLE SOC]

    attacker1 --> cell
    attacker1 --> bt
    attacker1 --> gps
    attacker2 --> obd
    attacker3 --> hsm

    cell --> tbox
    bt --> hmi
    gps --> adas
    tbox --> gateway
    hmi --> gateway
    obd --> gateway
    adas --> gateway

    gateway --> canbus
    canbus --> safety1
    canbus --> safety2
    canbus --> safety3

    hsm -. secures .-> gateway
    soc -. monitors .-> canbus
```

## 4.2 Diagram B — Defence-in-Depth Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7[LAYER 7 VEHICLE SOC AND FLEET MONITORING]
    L6[LAYER 6 OTA PIPELINE WITH SIGNED FIRMWARE]
    L5[LAYER 5 CRYPTOGRAPHIC PROTOCOLS TLS AND SECOC]
    L4[LAYER 4 IN VEHICLE IDS AND ANOMALY DETECTION]
    L3[LAYER 3 NETWORK SEGMENTATION GATEWAY]
    L2[LAYER 2 SECURE BOOT AND FW VERIFICATION]
    L1[LAYER 1 HARDWARE ROOT OF TRUST HSM]

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6
    L6 --> L7
```

## 4.3 Diagram C — TARA Process Flow (Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DEFINE ITEM AND OPERATING CONTEXT] --> B[IDENTIFY ASSETS]
    B --> C[IDENTIFY THREAT SCENARIOS]
    C --> D[COMPUTE IMPACT I]
    D --> E[COMPUTE ATTACK FEASIBILITY AF]
    E --> F[DERIVE RISK R EQUALS I TIMES AF]
    F --> G{RISK GREATER THAN THRESHOLD}
    G -->|YES| H[DEFINE CYBERSECURITY GOAL]
    H --> I[ALLOCATE SECURITY CLAIM TO SUBSYSTEM]
    I --> J[REASSESS AFTER MITIGATION]
    G -->|NO| K[DOCUMENT AND ARCHIVE]
    J --> K
```

## 4.4 Diagram D — Cyber-to-Safety Consequence Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[VULNERABILITY IN ECU] --> B[REMOTE EXPLOIT VIA TBOX]
    B --> C[UNAUTHORIZED COMMAND INJECTED ON CAN BUS]
    C --> D[BRAKING OR STEERING ACTUATOR MALFUNCTIONS]
    D --> E[LOSS OF VEHICLE CONTROL]
    E --> F[COLLISION AND INJURY]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

### Question 1. *(Short Answer – 3 Marks)*

**"List any three attack surfaces of a modern connected vehicle with one-line examples."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **External wireless interfaces** – Cellular, Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, GNSS, and RFID key fobs are continuously exposed to remote attackers and can serve as entry points into the in-vehicle network.
2. **In-vehicle communication buses** – The CAN, CAN-FD, LIN, and Automotive Ethernet buses carry all ECU messages; an attacker who injects a malicious frame can impersonate legitimate ECUs and influence safety-critical actuators.
3. **Physical diagnostic ports** – The OBD-II port, USB sockets, and manufacturer-specific diagnostic connectors can be exploited if a vehicle is serviced at a malicious garage or if an attacker gains brief physical access to flash malicious firmware.

### Question 2. *(Short Answer – 3 Marks)*

**"Differentiate between functional safety and cybersecurity in the automotive context."**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Functional Safety (ISO 26262) | Cybersecurity (ISO/SAE 21434) |
|---|---|---|
| Origin of hazard | Random hardware/software failure | Intentional malicious attack |
| Addressed by | Hardware redundancy, diagnostics, FMEA | Cryptography, IDS, secure boot, TARA |
| Trigger condition | Component aging, EMI, software bug | Adversary with intent and capability |
| Goal | Freedom from unreasonable risk | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Authenticity |
| Process | Hazard Analysis and Risk Assessment (HARA) | Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment (TARA) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write that "functional safety covers all hazards" — that is incorrect. **Cybersecurity is now mandatory** in addition to functional safety, per UNECE WP.29 R155. Confusing these is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper Pattern, ESE Module 4]**

---

### ✅ Question 4A (Choice A) — 14 Marks

**"With the aid of a block diagram, explain the importance of cybersecurity in modern vehicles. Discuss the major attack surfaces, the layered defence-in-depth architecture, and the regulatory framework of UNECE WP.29 R155 and ISO/SAE 21434."**

**Model Answer Outline (with sub-part allocations):**

#### (a) Importance and Attack Surface Analysis — *7 Marks*

1. **Modern vehicles as connected cyber-physical systems** — a premium 2024 car contains 70–150 ECUs, runs 100+ million lines of code, and ships with cellular, Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, GNSS, and UWB radios permanently active. This connectivity has produced a large, distributed attack surface.

2. **Seven major attack surface domains:**

   - *External wireless:* cellular, Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, GNSS, RFID key.
   - *In-vehicle network:* CAN, CAN-FD, Automotive Ethernet, FlexRay, LIN.
   - *Physical ports:* OBD-II, USB, manufacturer diagnostic.
   - *Sensors:* camera, radar, lidar, ultrasonic — subject to adversarial spoofing.
   - *Infotainment/HMI:* touchscreen, voice, companion mobile app.
   - *Telematics/T-Box:* remote command channel, eCall, OTA.
   - *Supply chain:* third-party ECU firmware, dealer tooling, OBD dongles.

3. **Real-world evidence:** The 2015 Jeep Cherokee remote hack (Miller-Valasek) led to a 1.4-million-vehicle recall; the 2018 Tesla Model S key-fob relay attack enabled vehicle theft in seconds. These incidents establish the practical importance of cybersecurity beyond theoretical risk.

4. **Safety cross-link:** A compromised braking or steering ECU can directly cause physical injury. Therefore, a cyber-vulnerability is functionally equivalent to an ASIL-D-level safety hazard.

#### (b) Defence-in-Depth and Regulatory Framework — *7 Marks*

1. **Layered Defence-in-Depth Architecture** (7 layers):

   - *Layer 1 — Hardware Root of Trust:* HSM with secure key storage and cryptographic acceleration.
   - *Layer 2 — Secure Boot:* each ECU verifies signed firmware at power-on.
   - *Layer 3 — Network Segmentation:* central gateway ECU isolates safety-critical CAN buses from infotainment CAN.
   - *Layer 4 — In-Vehicle IDS:* CAN-IDS detects anomalous message frequency, payload, or source.
   - *Layer 5 — Cryptographic Protocols:* TLS 1.3 for V2X, SecOC for AUTOSAR Classic, MAC for CAN-FD.
   - *Layer 6 — OTA Pipeline:* RSA/ECC-signed firmware bundles with rollback protection and staged rollout.
   - *Layer 7 — Vehicle SOC:* continuous fleet monitoring for coordinated attack patterns.

2. **Regulatory Framework — ISO/SAE 21434:**

   - Defines a **Cybersecurity Management System (CSMS)** that must span the entire vehicle lifecycle — from concept through production, operation, and decommissioning.
   - Mandates a structured **TARA (Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment)** during the concept phase.
   - Specifies **Cybersecurity Assurance Levels (CAL 1–4)** based on impact and attack feasibility.
   - Introduces a **CSMS audit certificate** that OEMs must renew periodically.

3. **Regulatory Framework — UNECE WP.29 R155:**

   - Applicable in 54+ signatory countries including the EU, UK, Japan, Korea (and is a strong reference for India's AIS revisions).
   - **Mandatory from July 2022** for new vehicle type approvals.
   - Requires a **Certificate of Compliance for CSMS** issued by an authorized technical service.
   - Mandates a documented **post-production monitoring system** to handle vulnerabilities discovered after market launch.

4. **Conclusion:** Cybersecurity is no longer a "nice-to-have" software feature — it is a **legally mandated, safety-critical, lifecycle-wide engineering discipline** for any new vehicle sold in regulated markets.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Omitting the **TARA process** itself when discussing 21434 — examiners deduct 2 marks.
> - Writing only about software when the question asks about **cyber-physical** importance — must mention safety ECUs and physical consequences.
> - Citing "GDPR" instead of WP.29 / 21434 as the **automotive** regulatory standard — GDPR is privacy, not cybersecurity for type approval.

---

### ✅ Question 4B (Choice B) — 14 Marks

**"Explain the Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment (TARA) methodology as defined by ISO/SAE 21434. With a suitable example, show how the risk value R is computed and how cybersecurity goals are derived."**

**Model Answer Outline (with sub-part allocations):**

#### (a) TARA Methodology and Workflow — *7 Marks*

1. **Definition:** TARA is a structured, systematic, and repeatable engineering process mandated by ISO/SAE 21434 to identify cyber-threats to vehicle assets, evaluate their risk, and derive actionable cybersecurity goals and claims.

2. **TARA Workflow (7 stages):**

   - *Step 1 — Item Definition:* Define the item under analysis (e.g., "Telematics Control Unit T-Box") and its operating environment, including interfaces, network connections, and trust boundaries.
   - *Step 2 — Asset Identification:* List valuable assets — cryptographic keys, ECU firmware, vehicle position data, driver biometrics, CAN bus messages, OTA update server credentials.
   - *Step 3 — Threat Scenario Identification:* For each asset, identify *what* could go wrong, *who* the attacker is, and *how* the attack could be launched.
   - *Step 4 — Impact Rating (I):* Score each scenario using Table 8 of the standard: Severe / Major / Moderate / Negligible — based on Safety, Financial, Operational, and Privacy consequences.
   - *Step 5 — Attack Feasibility Rating (AF):* Score using Table 7, considering Expertise required, Knowledge of the item, Window of opportunity, Equipment needed, and Opportunity.
   - *Step 6 — Risk Determination:* Compute $R = I \times AF$ and look up the value in the standard's risk matrix.
   - *Step 7 — Cybersecurity Goal Derivation:* For every risk above the acceptable threshold, derive a top-level cybersecurity goal, which is then refined into claims on specific subsystems.

3. **Inputs and Outputs:**

   - *Inputs:* item definition, architectural diagrams, interface lists, prior field data.
   - *Outputs:* TARA report, list of cybersecurity goals, list of claims, CAL (Cybersecurity Assurance Level) per claim.

4. **Linkage to ISO 26262:** When a cybersecurity goal impacts functional safety, a **CFI (Cybersecurity–Functional Safety interaction)** analysis is mandatory. This is a frequently-tested concept.

#### (b) Worked Example — Risk Computation and Goal Derivation — *7 Marks*

> **Example Item:** Remote Engine Start via Mobile App.

1. *Asset:* Engine control firmware, immobilizer cryptographic key, command authentication token.
2. *Threat Scenario:* Attacker with a stolen API token remotely starts the engine while the vehicle is unattended in a closed garage, leading to **carbon monoxide poisoning** of occupants.

   $$I = \text{Severe} = S3 \quad (\text{life-threatening safety impact})$$

3. *Attack Feasibility:* Requires stolen credential (Low expertise) + remote access (Wide window) + Standard equipment.

   $$AF = \text{High} = F4$$

4. *Risk Computation:*

   $$R = I \times AF = S3 \times F4$$

   From the risk matrix in Section 3.2, this maps to **Critical** risk.

5. *Cybersecurity Goal Derived:*

   - **Goal CG-01:** The remote engine-start command shall only be accepted if the request carries a valid, non-replayable authentication token issued by the OEM backend within the last 60 seconds.

6. *Claim Allocation:*

   - **Claim CL-01.1:** Mobile app shall implement TLS 1.3 mutual authentication with the OEM server.
   - **Claim CL-01.2:** T-Box shall reject commands older than the freshness window.
   - **Claim CL-01.3:** Backend shall revoke compromised tokens within 5 minutes of report.

7. **CAL Assignment:** Because the impact is severe and the attack surface is wide, the resulting claim is assigned **CAL 3** (out of 4).

8. **Residual Risk Reassessment:** After mitigation, $AF$ drops to $F1$ (Very Low) → $R$ becomes Low → acceptable.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Skipping Step 1 (item definition) — students often jump straight to threats; 1 mark is deducted because TARA is item-scoped.
> - Forgetting to mention **Cybersecurity Assurance Level (CAL 1–4)** — a critical 21434 concept worth 2 marks.
> - Confusing **CAL** (Cybersecurity Assurance Level) with **ASIL** (Automotive Safety Integrity Level) — they are different classifications serving different standards.
> - Not showing the **reassessment loop** after mitigation — full marks require the closed-loop TARA cycle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU PEMUT743 Module 4**

- **Definition:** Automotive cybersecurity is the engineering discipline of protecting vehicular electronic systems from intentional malicious attacks.
- **Two key standards to remember:** **ISO/SAE 21434** (process standard) and **UNECE WP.29 R155** (regulation).
- **TARA** = Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment — the cornerstone methodology of 21434.
- **Risk formula:** $R = I \times AF$ — Impact multiplied by Attack Feasibility.
- **CIA + A** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, and **Authenticity** (the fourth pillar specific to vehicles).
- **Defence-in-Depth** uses 7 layers, from HSM (Layer 1) to Vehicle SOC (Layer 7).
- **Real-world case studies to memorize:** Jeep Cherokee hack (2015), Tesla key-fob relay (2018), BMW Connected Drive (2015).
- **In-vehicle network standards:** CAN, CAN-FD, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1 / 1000BASE-T1).
- **Cryptographic minimums:** AES-128 baseline, MAC $\geq 64$ bits, TLS 1.3 for V2X, SecOC for AUTOSAR.
- **Attack Surface Cardinality:** $A_s = \sum (w_e e_i + w_c c_i)$ — comparative metric for design trade-off.
- **CAN bus frame time** at 500 kbps with 8-byte payload is **$222 \text{ }\mu s$** — IDS must react faster.
- **Functional safety vs. security** — ISO 26262 addresses *random* failures; ISO/SAE 21434 addresses *intentional* attacks.
- **Regulatory trigger:** UNECE WP.29 R155 is mandatory from **July 2022** for new type approvals.
- **CSMS** = Cybersecurity Management System — the auditable lifecycle-wide process required for compliance.
- **SecOC** = Secure Onboard Communication — AUTOSAR mechanism for adding MAC to CAN/CAN-FD frames.
- **OTA security** requires signed firmware, rollback protection, and staged canary rollout to fleet.
- **Cyber-to-safety link:** cyber-vulnerabilities on safety ECUs are treated as **ASIL-equivalent** hazards in CFI analysis.
- **CAL** (Cybersecurity Assurance Level 1–4) is **not the same** as ASIL A–D — a frequent exam trap.
- **Three driving forces for current importance:** software-defined vehicles, regulatory mandate, and physical safety consequence.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic overview of global automotive cybersecurity standards: Potential threats, risks and vulnerabilities faced in automotives - keyless entry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT743 - VEHICLE SAFETY AND SECURITY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic overview of global automotive cybersecurity standards: Potential threats, risks and vulnerabilities faced in automotives - keyless entry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Global Automotive Cybersecurity Standards — Formal Definition

Automotive cybersecurity standards are internationally recognized regulatory and engineering frameworks that govern how Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs), Tier-1 suppliers, and software vendors must identify, assess, mitigate, and continuously monitor cyber-physical threats across the entire vehicle lifecycle. The three pillars of global automotive cybersecurity governance are:

- **ISO/SAE 21434** (2021) — "Road Vehicles — Cybersecurity Engineering": Defines the process framework for managing cybersecurity risks throughout the engineering lifecycle of electrical and electronic (E/E) systems.
- **UNECE WP.29 Regulation No. 155 (R155)** — "Cyber Security Management System (CSMS)": A type-approval regulation that legally mandates OEMs to operate a certified CSMS for vehicles sold in the EU, Japan, and South Korea (from July 2022).
- **SAE J3061** (2016) — "Cybersecurity Guidebook for Cyber-Physical Vehicle Systems": The pre-cursor framework that introduced the **Cybersecurity Assurance Level (CAL)** concept, conceptually similar to ISO 26262's Automotive Safety Integrity Level (ASIL).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU takeaway:** ISO/SAE 21434 is process-oriented (engineering workflow), while R155 is regulatory-oriented (legal type-approval). BOTH must be satisfied for market compliance in the EU.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a modern car is like a **floating hospital** (think Boeing 787 or Airbus A350) — full of distributed mini-computers (60–150 ECUs) that talk to each other over internal networks. The "doors" to this hospital are no longer just mechanical locks; they are now wireless — Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, cellular (4G/5G), satellite (GNSS), and a **keyless entry fob** that constantly whispers a cryptographic token to the car.

Now imagine if someone could **forge the whisper**, or **relay the whisper through a stolen satellite dish** from a parking lot in Dubai. That is precisely what automotive cybersecurity addresses — protecting the **wireless whispers** and **internal conversations** between ECUs from forgery, eavesdropping, hijacking, and replay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics You Must Memorize:**
> - **CSMS** = Cyber Security Management System (R155 mandate).
> - **TARA** = Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment (ISO/SAE 21434 Clause 15).
> - **CAL** = Cybersecurity Assurance Level (1 to 4, where 4 = highest).
> - **CVSS** = Common Vulnerability Scoring System (0.0 to 10.0).
> - **Attack Surface** = the sum of all possible entry points (digital + physical + human).

### 1.3 Automotive Cyber Threat Landscape — Definition

A **threat** is a potential cause of an unwanted incident that may result in harm to a vehicle system. A **vulnerability** is a weakness in a system that can be exploited. A **risk** is the qualitative or quantitative measure of the probability of a threat exploiting a vulnerability, multiplied by the resulting impact.

In automotive context, the **attack surface** spans seven major vectors:

| Vector | Description | Typical Attack |
|---|---|---|
| **External Connectivity** | Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, Cellular, GNSS, DSRC/C-V2X | Man-in-the-Middle, Spoofing |
| **Keyless Entry (RKE/PKE)** | Remote Keyless Entry / Passive Keyless Entry | Relay Attack, Replay Attack |
| **In-Vehicle Network** | CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet | Bus Injection, DoS |
| **Diagnostic Interface** | OBD-II, UDS over CAN/UDS over DoIP | Unauthorized flashing |
| **ECU Firmware** | OTA updates, bootloader | Malicious firmware injection |
| **Telematics / Backend** | Cloud APIs, T-Box, mobile app | API abuse, credential theft |
| **Supply Chain** | Third-party software, Tier-1 components | Hardware trojan, malicious library |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's favorite question pattern:** "List and briefly explain the attack surfaces in a modern connected vehicle." Always classify answers under **wireless, wired, internal, and human** — examiners award marks for this structured categorization.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ISO/SAE 21434 — Operational Workflow (Lifecycle Phases)

ISO/SAE 21434 structures cybersecurity into **15 clauses** distributed across the engineering lifecycle. The KTU-relevant phases are:

- **Clause 7 — Distributed Cybersecurity Activities:** Defines roles of OEM (Cybersecurity Responsible — CSO), Tier-1 supplier, and integrator.
- **Clause 8 — Continual Cybersecurity Activities:** Vulnerability monitoring, incident response, post-deployment patch management.
- **Clause 9 — Concept Phase:** Item definition, TARA preliminary.
- **Clause 15 — Risk Assessment (TARA):** The heart of the standard.

### 2.2 Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment (TARA) — Stepwise Logic

TARA is the **risk-engineering heart** of ISO/SAE 21434. The logical chain is:

1. **Item Definition** — Identify the vehicle function (e.g., keyless entry) and its boundary.
2. **Asset Identification** — List the valuable data/assets (e.g., unlock command, immobilizer PIN, ECU firmware).
3. **Threat Scenario Identification** — Describe *who* (threat actor), *what* (vulnerability), *how* (attack method), and *why* (impact).
4. **Impact Rating** — Score from **Severe, Major, Moderate, Negligible** using safety, financial, operational, and privacy impact dimensions.
5. **Attack Path Analysis** — List the sequential steps an attacker would take, mapped to a specific **Attack Potential** value (per ISO/SAE 21434 Annex G).
6. **Risk Value Determination** — Combine Impact × Attack Feasibility to get a **Risk Score**.
7. **Treatment Decision** — Avoid, Reduce, Transfer, or Accept the risk; derive Cybersecurity Goals (CG) and Claims (CC).
8. **Calibration** — Assign a **Cybersecurity Assurance Level (CAL 1 to CAL 4)** per ISO/SAE 21434 Clause 6.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CAL matters:** CAL 4 (highest) requires the most rigorous testing, formal verification, and penetration testing depth — analogous to ASIL D in functional safety. KTU examiners love to compare CAL vs ASIL.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The automotive cybersecurity risk model is governed by a single fundamental equation:

$$
Risk = f(\text{Threat}, \text{Vulnerability}, \text{Impact})
$$

In the **CVSS-aligned quantitative form** used in mature TARA implementations:

$$
Risk_{Score} = Impact_{Rating} \times AttackFeasibility_{Rating}
$$

where **Impact Rating** and **Attack Feasibility Rating** are derived from standardized matrices (typically 1–5 Likert-style scales per ISO/SAE 21434 Annex G).

| Parameter | Symbol | Range / Domain | Unit / Scale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Impact Rating | $I$ | 1, 2, 3, 4 | Negligible $\rightarrow$ Severe |
| Attack Feasibility | $AF$ | 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 | Very Low $\rightarrow$ High |
| Risk Value | $R$ | 1 to 20 | Discrete integer |
| Cybersecurity Assurance Level | $CAL$ | 1, 2, 3, 4 | Discrete (mapped from R) |
| CVSS Base Score | $S_{CVSS}$ | 0.0 to 10.0 | Continuous |
| Attack Path Length | $L_{path}$ | 1 to $n$ | Steps |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Markdown Trap Avoided:** All absolute-value and conditional notations in the table above are written without `|` to prevent breaking the markdown table parser. Use `I` for impact and `AF` for attack feasibility.

**Engineering Utility:** This Risk equation is used to **prioritize engineering effort** during the concept phase of a vehicle program. The highest $R$-values are translated into **Cybersecurity Goals (CG)**, which are then decomposed into **Cybersecurity Claims (CC)** for traceability in the design and verification phases.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In industry, the Risk equation drives the **TARA report** delivered to the OEM's cybersecurity governance board. For example, a keyless entry relay attack scoring $I = 4$ and $AF = 5$ yields:

$$
R = I \times AF = 4 \times 5 = 20
$$

This $R = 20$ mandates a **CAL 4** treatment, requiring ultra-wideband (UWB) ranging, time-of-flight cryptographic challenge-response, and cryptographic rollback protection.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mapping Risk Score to CAL (Cybersecurity Assurance Level)

The mapping from the discrete risk value $R$ to the Cybersecurity Assurance Level follows a **threshold function** specified in ISO/SAE 21434 Clause 6 and Annex H.

**Step 1 — Establish the discrete impact and feasibility scales.**

Per ISO/SAE 21434 Annex G, the impact scale is bounded:

$$
I \in \{1,\, 2,\, 3,\, 4\}
$$

The attack feasibility scale is bounded:

$$
AF \in \{1,\, 2,\, 3,\, 4,\, 5\}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the discrete risk product.**

$$
R = I \times AF
$$

The maximum theoretical risk value is:

$$
R_{max} = 4 \times 5 = 20
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the threshold mapping to assign CAL.**

The CAL threshold mapping (per ISO/SAE 21434 guidance) is:

$$
CAL(R) = \begin{cases}
1, & 1 \leq R \leq 4 \\
2, & 5 \leq R \leq 9 \\
3, & 10 \leq R \leq 15 \\
4, & 16 \leq R \leq 20
\end{cases}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Lower risk values produce lower assurance requirements, while higher risk values demand more rigorous verification depth — analogous to ASIL in ISO 26262.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Keyless Entry Relay Attack

**Step 1 — Item definition.**
Item: Passive Keyless Entry (PKE) system on a 2024 luxury sedan. Function: Auto-unlock the doors when the fob is within 1.5 m.

**Step 2 — Asset identification.**
Asset: Unauthenticated "unlock" signal between the fob and the BCM (Body Control Module).

**Step 3 — Threat scenario.**
A relay attack (also called a "relay station attack" or RSA) uses two attackers — one near the car, one near the fob (e.g., inside the owner's house). The "relay" device amplifies and forwards the fob's low-frequency (LF, 125 kHz) challenge signal over a long range (GSM/Wi-Fi), tricking the car into believing the fob is adjacent.

**Step 4 — Impact rating.**
Safety: **Moderate (3)** — vehicle theft, potential harm in driveaway scenarios.
Financial: **Severe (4)** — total vehicle loss, insurance fraud.
Operational: **Major (3)** — vehicle inoperable after theft.
Privacy: **Negligible (1)** — no personal data exfiltrated.

Worst-case per-axis impact:

$$
I = \max(3,\, 4,\, 3,\, 1) = 4
$$

**Step 5 — Attack feasibility.**
Per ISO/SAE 21434 Annex G, the attacker is profiled:
- Elapsed time to mount attack: < 1 day (low) → factor reduces feasibility rating.
- Specialist expertise required: Layman (low) → reduces.
- Knowledge of the item: Public → high feasibility.
- Window of opportunity: Easy (relay from outside the house) → high.
- Equipment: Standard (off-the-shelf SDR) → high.

Resulting attack feasibility rating:

$$
AF = 5
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Risk Value.**

$$
R = I \times AF = 4 \times 5 = 20
$$

**Step 7 — Determine CAL.**

$$
CAL(20) = 4
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The relay attack on legacy PKE consistently yields **CAL 4**, which mandates UWB-based distance bounding, cryptographic mutual authentication, and time-of-flight (ToF) verification — engineering controls that defeat signal-amplification attacks.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — TARA Output Summary for Keyless Entry

| Threat | Vulnerability | Impact $I$ | Feasibility $AF$ | Risk $R$ | CAL | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Relay attack | No distance bounding in LF | 4 | 5 | 20 | 4 | UWB ranging, ToF |
| Replay attack (captured token) | Static challenge | 3 | 4 | 12 | 3 | Rolling code (KeeLoq, Hitag2) |
| Jamming of LF band | No frequency hopping | 2 | 5 | 10 | 3 | Multi-band, FHSS |
| Fob cloning via OBD | Weak immobilizer PIN | 4 | 3 | 12 | 3 | ECU challenge-response |
| Eavesdropping on key programming | Unencrypted diagnostic session | 3 | 3 | 9 | 2 | Secure diagnostic (UDS 0x27 with PKI) |

> [!NOTE]
> This table is a **direct mapping of a KTU Part A or short-answer question** examiners use to test TARA competency. Memorize the structure: *Threat → Vulnerability → Impact → Feasibility → Risk → CAL → Mitigation*.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

All node identifiers below are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters. Node labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting. No reserved keywords are used as standalone node names.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Keyless Entry Relay Attack Surface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[VEHICLE BCM RECEIVER]:::highRisk --> B[LF CHALLENGE 125 KHZ]
    B --> C[FOB AUTHENTICATOR]
    C --> D[UWB RANGING CHECK CAL 4]
    D -->|TOF DELTA EXCEEDS 1.5 M| E[REJECT UNLOCK]
    D -->|TOF DELTA WITHIN 1.5 M| F[GRANT UNLOCK]
    G[ATTACKER RELAY NODE 1 NEAR CAR]:::attackerNode -->|AMPLIFY LF SIGNAL| H[LONG RANGE UPLINK GSM WIFI]
    H --> I[ATTACKER RELAY NODE 2 NEAR FOB]:::attackerNode
    I -->|INJECT LF TO FOB| C
    J[OBD II DIAGNOSTIC] --> K[IMMOBILIZER PIN READ]
    K -->|WEAK PIN| L[CLONE FOB TO START ENGINE]
    M[RF REPLAY CAPTURE] --> N[REPLAY TOKEN LATER]
    N -->|STATIC TOKEN| O[ILLEGAL UNLOCK]
    classDef highRisk fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef attackerNode fill:#a00000,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 ISO/SAE 21434 Lifecycle Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[CONCEPT PHASE CLAUSE 9]:::phase1 --> P2[PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT CLAUSE 10]
    P2 --> P3[CYBERSECURITY VALIDATION CLAUSE 11]
    P3 --> P4[PRODUCTION CLAUSE 12]
    P4 --> P5[OPERATIONS AND MAINTENANCE CLAUSE 13]
    P5 --> P6[END OF CYBERSECURITY SUPPORT AND DECOMMISSIONING CLAUSE 14]
    P0[ORGANIZATIONAL CYBERSECURITY MANAGEMENT CLAUSE 5]:::govern --> P1
    P0 --> P2
    P0 --> P3
    P0 --> P4
    P0 --> P5
    P0 --> P6
    P7[PROJECT DEPENDENT CYBERSECURITY MANAGEMENT CLAUSE 6]:::govern --> P1
    P7 --> P2
    P7 --> P3
    T[TARA CLAUSE 15]:::taraNode --> P1
    T --> P2
    T --> P3
    classDef phase1 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef govern fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef taraNode fill:#c62828,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Attack Vector Taxonomy (STRIDE-aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[ROOT ATTACKER MODEL] --> S[SPOOFING FOB IDENTITY]
    R --> T[TAMPERING CAN BUS OR ECU]
    R --> RE[REPUDIATION OF LOG FILES]
    R --> I[INFORMATION DISCLOSURE TELEMATICS]
    R --> D[DENIAL OF SERVICE OBD JAMMING]
    R --> E[ELEVATION OF PRIVILEGE ROOTING TCU]
    S --> S1[RELAY REPLAY CLONING]
    T --> T1[CAN INJECTION FIRMWARE MOD]
    I --> I1[TSN SNIFFING GNSS SPOOFING]
    D --> D1[RKE JAMMING 4G FLOOD]
    E --> E1[UDS 0x27 SEED KEY BYPASS]
```

### 4.5 Fallback: Block-Level Functional Architecture (PKE Air-Gap Defense)

For readers visualizing the modern UWB-based defense:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[FOB UWB TX] -->|TWO WAY RANGING TWR| V1[VEHICLE UWB RX]
    V1 -->|TOF COMPUTE| C1[CRYPTOCOMPUTE DELTA]
    C1 -->|DELTA LESS THAN 1.5 M| A1[AUTH OK AND UNLOCK]
    C1 -->|DELTA EXCEEDS 1.5 M| A2[REJECT AS RELAY]
    F1 -.->|LF CHALLENGE LEGACY| V1
    V1 -.->|CAN MESSAGE| BCM[BODY CONTROL MODULE]
    BCM --> ECU[IMMOBILIZER ECU HSM]
    ECU -->|AES 128 MUTUAL AUTH| F1
    classDef secure fill:#0b6e4f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef legacy fill:#9c6b00,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    class F1,V1,BCM,ECU,A1,C1 secure
    class A2 legacy
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **modern automotive keyless entry defense stack** layers UWB ranging + AES-128 mutual authentication + Hardware Security Module (HSM) inside the BCM/immobilizer. This multi-layer approach makes the $R = 20$ risk drop below CAL 4 thresholds.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Q:** Define the terms *threat*, *vulnerability*, and *risk* as applied to automotive cybersecurity. How are they related to each other?

**Model Answer:**

- **Threat:** A potential cause of an unwanted incident which may result in harm to a vehicle system (ISO/SAE 21434 Clause 3.61). Example: a relay attack against a keyless entry fob.
- **Vulnerability:** A weakness in an asset or control that can be exploited by a threat (Clause 3.66). Example: lack of distance-bounding in a legacy PKE system.
- **Risk:** The qualitative or quantitative measure of the probability of a threat exploiting a vulnerability, combined with the resulting impact (Clause 3.55).

**Relationship:** Risk is the *combination* of threat × vulnerability × impact. A threat without an exploitable vulnerability produces zero risk; a vulnerability without a threat also produces zero risk. Risk is the engineering output that drives the **Cybersecurity Assurance Level (CAL)**.

*(Examiner note: Award 1 mark each for the three definitions; bonus mark for articulating the multiplicative relationship.)*

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]`

**Q:** List the major global automotive cybersecurity standards and state one key objective of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Standard | Key Objective |
|---|---|
| **ISO/SAE 21434 (2021)** | Defines a process framework for managing cybersecurity risks across the E/E vehicle engineering lifecycle. |
| **UNECE WP.29 R155** | Mandates a Cyber Security Management System (CSMS) for type-approval of vehicles in EU, Japan, Korea. |
| **SAE J3061** | Provides a cybersecurity process reference and introduces the Cybersecurity Assurance Level (CAL) concept. |
| **ISO 24089 (2023)** | Governs software update engineering — companion to ISO/SAE 21434 for OTA. |
| **Auto-ISAC Best Practices** | Industry information sharing alliance for threat intelligence. |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Q (a)** Explain the **TARA (Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment)** methodology as defined in ISO/SAE 21434. List its key phases with brief descriptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

TARA is the systematic methodology specified in **ISO/SAE 21434 Clause 15** for identifying and evaluating cybersecurity threats to a vehicle E/E item. Its phases are:

1. **Item Definition (Clause 9 + 15.2):** Define the function, boundary, and operational environment. Example: "PKE system on a sedan."
2. **Asset Identification (15.3):** Enumerate the valuable data and components — unlock signal, immobilizer PIN, ECU firmware.
3. **Threat Scenario Identification (15.4):** Construct threat scenarios using threat actor profile, attack method, and pre-conditions.
4. **Impact Rating (15.5):** Score impact across safety, financial, operational, and privacy axes.
5. **Attack Path Analysis (15.6):** Decompose each threat scenario into ordered attack steps; rate each step's feasibility using Annex G factors.
6. **Risk Value Determination (15.7):** Compute the risk value as a function of impact and feasibility, then map to a CAL.
7. **Cybersecurity Goal and Claim Derivation:** Translate high-risk items into Cybersecurity Goals (CGs) and Claims (CCs).
8. **Treatment and Verification:** Define mitigations and verify with appropriate test depth per CAL.

The TARA output is the **Cybersecurity Interface Agreement (CSIA)** between OEM and supplier, and is the basis for type-approval documentation under R155.

---

**Q (b)** With a suitable example, explain the **keyless entry relay attack**. Discuss its threat, vulnerability, impact, and the modern mitigation using UWB. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Attack Description:** In a relay attack, two cooperating attackers use relay nodes to bridge the wireless link between the vehicle and the owner's key fob, which may be located tens of meters away (e.g., inside a home). The relay amplifies and re-transmits the LF (125 kHz) challenge from the car to the fob, and the fob's UHF response back to the car, tricking the BCM into granting unlock authorization.

**TARA Triplet:**
- **Threat:** Relay amplification by an external adversary.
- **Vulnerability:** No distance-bounding in legacy LF/UHF PKE — only a one-way authentication with no time-of-flight check.
- **Impact:** Vehicle theft (severe financial), safety hazard if drive-away occurs while children are inside (severe safety).

**Risk Computation:**

$$
R = I \times AF = 4 \times 5 = 20 \quad \Rightarrow \quad CAL = 4
$$

**Modern Mitigation — UWB Time-of-Flight (ToF):**
- The fob and vehicle each transmit and receive **Ultra-Wideband** pulses (channel 5, 6.5 GHz band).
- ToF is computed via **Two-Way Ranging (TWR)**.
- If the measured propagation delay implies a distance > 1.5 m, the unlock is **rejected** — even if the cryptographic challenge passes.
- Combined with **AES-128 mutual authentication** inside the fob's **Secure Element (SE)** and the BCM's **HSM**, the attack is no longer feasible.

**Residual Risk After Mitigation:**

$$
R_{residual} \approx 1 \times 1 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad CAL_{residual} = 1
$$

This is a **calibration example** frequently set as a Part B question in KTU university exams.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]`

**Q (a)** Compare and contrast **ISO/SAE 21434** and **UNECE WP.29 R155**. State their scope, mandatory nature, and relationship. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | ISO/SAE 21434 | UNECE WP.29 R155 |
|---|---|---|
| Type | International standard (process framework) | UN regulation (legal type-approval) |
| Mandatory? | Voluntary (industry consensus) | **Mandatory** in EU, Japan, Korea from July 2022 |
| Scope | Engineering lifecycle of E/E systems | Organization-wide Cyber Security Management System (CSMS) |
| Focus | How to engineer secure vehicles | How to manage cybersecurity across the OEM organization |
| Output | TARA report, Cybersecurity Case | CSMS certificate, type-approval |
| Lifecycle | Concept → EoL | Continuous (organization-wide) |
| Relation | Defines the engineering process that feeds R155 evidence | Requires ISO/SAE 21434-aligned processes for compliance |

**Relationship:** R155 is the **legal umbrella**; ISO/SAE 21434 is the **engineering implementation**. An OEM cannot get R155 type-approval without demonstrating that its engineering processes conform to ISO/SAE 21434. They are **complementary, not competing**.

---

**Q (b)** Describe the **seven major attack surfaces** in a modern connected vehicle. For each, give one real-world example attack. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **External Wireless Connectivity (BT/Wi-Fi/Cellular/GNSS):** Example — *BlueBorne* Bluetooth exploit, GNSS spoofing to mislead navigation.
2. **Keyless Entry Systems (RKE/PKE):** Example — *Relay attack* (covered above).
3. **In-Vehicle Network (CAN, Automotive Ethernet):** Example — *CAN bus injection* via the OBD-II port causing unintended acceleration (Miller & Valasek Jeep Cherokee 2015).
4. **Diagnostic Interface (OBD-II / UDS / DoIP):** Example — *Seed-Key bypass* allowing ECU re-flashing.
5. **ECU Firmware and OTA:** Example — *Malicious OTA update* bricking ECUs.
6. **Telematics / Backend / Mobile App:** Example — *Tesla API key theft* enabling remote unlock and start.
7. **Supply Chain (Tier-1 software):** Example — *Compromised third-party library* (e.g., SolarWinds-style attack on a Tier-1 HMI vendor).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse ISO/SAE 21434 with ISO 26262.** 21434 is cybersecurity; 26262 is functional safety. They share the concept of risk classification (CAL vs ASIL) but address fundamentally different failure modes.
> 2. **Do not list RKE attacks without specifying the mitigation.** Examiners award marks for **threat + vulnerability + impact + mitigation** — missing any one loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Do not use `|x|` notation in tables.** Use `abs(x)` or LaTeX `\vert x \vert`. The KTU answer sheet is hand-written, but in digital submissions this breaks markdown parsers.
> 4. **Always state the standard's issuing body and year** (e.g., "ISO/SAE 21434:2021") — vague references lose a mark.
> 5. **Remember the July 2022 R155 date** — examiners love chronological context questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ISO/SAE 21434 (2021)** = the engineering process standard; **R155** = the legal type-approval regulation; **SAE J3061** = the pre-cursor with CAL.
- **TARA** (Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment) is the heart of ISO/SAE 21434 — eight phases: Item Definition → Asset ID → Threat Scenarios → Impact → Attack Path → Risk Value → Goals → Treatment.
- **Risk Equation:** $R = I \times AF$, where $I \in \{1,2,3,4\}$ and $AF \in \{1,2,3,4,5\}$, giving $R \in [1, 20]$.
- **CAL Mapping:** $R \in [1,4] \to 1$; $[5,9] \to 2$; $[10,15] \to 3$; $[16,20] \to 4$.
- **Seven Attack Surfaces:** External wireless, Keyless entry, In-vehicle network, Diagnostic, Firmware/OTA, Telematics/Backend, Supply chain.
- **Keyless Entry Attacks:** Relay ($R=20$, CAL 4), Replay (rolling code mitigates), Jamming (FHSS mitigates), Fob cloning (HSM mitigates).
- **Modern PKE Defense:** UWB Two-Way Ranging + AES-128 mutual authentication + Secure Element/HSM.
- **Regulatory date:** UNECE R155 is **mandatory** from **July 2022** in EU, Japan, Korea.
- **Calibration pitfall:** Never conflate ASIL (safety) with CAL (cybersecurity) — they coexist but address different risks.
- **Threat modeling frameworks:** STRIDE, PASTA, and TARA (ISO/SAE 21434's preferred).
- **Key acronyms to memorize:** CSMS, TARA, CAL, CVSS, ToF, UWB, HSM, SE, CSIA, ECU, BCM, TCU, RKE, PKE.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision strategy (KTU exam day):** Memorize the 5×4 risk matrix grid, the CAL thresholds, the three standards table, and the relay-attack mitigation stack (LF + UHF + UWB + HSM). These four artifacts account for approximately 70% of the marks in this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigation and infotainment system
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT743 - VEHICLE SAFETY AND SECURITY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Navigation and infotainment system

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Vehicle Navigation System — Formal Definition

A **Vehicle Navigation System** is an integrated electronic subsystem that uses satellite-based positioning (typically GNSS), dead-reckoning sensors, digital map databases, and route-planning algorithms to determine the real-time geographic position of a vehicle and provide turn-by-turn guidance to a destination. As per ISO 15005 (dialogue management principles) and the KTU 2024 PEMUT743 syllabus, the system must ensure that the driver is guided safely with **minimal visual, cognitive, and manual distraction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Navigation = Localization + Map Matching + Path Planning + Human-Machine Interface (HMI) guidance. All four sub-systems must be present for a system to qualify as a "Navigation System" in a B.Tech valuation context.

## 1.2 Vehicle Infotainment System — Formal Definition

An **In-Vehicle Infotainment (IVI) System** is a convergent hardware-software platform that integrates **information** (navigation, telematics, ADAS visualization, vehicle diagnostics) and **entertainment** (audio, video, connectivity, smartphone projection) into a unified human-machine interface controlled from the driver's seat. The KTU syllabus groups this under Module 4 because IVI is the **primary human-facing interface** of modern safety and ADAS warning systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Term:** Modern OEMs (Hyundai, Tesla, Bosch, Harman) refer to the IVI stack as the **Head Unit (HU)** or **Digital Cockpit Domain Controller**, often running **AUTOSAR Adaptive**, **QNX Neutrino**, **Android Automotive OS (AAOS)**, or **Linux GENIVI**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Navigation Analogy — "The Tour Guide on Your Dashboard":**
Imagine you are blindfolded in an unfamiliar city. A friend walks beside you, holds a paper map, knows your exact coordinates from a satellite, and whispers in your ear: *"Walk 50 meters, then turn left at the traffic signal."* That friend is your **Navigation System**. The satellite is the **GNSS constellation**, the paper map is the **GIS database**, the friend's sense of balance when you stumble is the **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)**, and the whispered instruction is the **HMI guidance**.

**Infotainment Analogy — "The Smart Coffee Table on Wheels":**
Think of a modern car dashboard as a **smart coffee table with a TV, a phone, a radio, and a personal assistant** all built into it. The TV shows the map, the phone connects your music and contacts, the radio plays FM/HD Radio/streaming audio, and the personal assistant takes voice commands. This entire "table" is the **IVI system** — it informs you (info) and entertains you (tainment).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Standard GNSS Frequency Bands:** L1 (1575.42 MHz), L2 (1227.60 MHz), L5 (1176.45 MHz)
- **Speed of light (c):** **3 × 10⁸ m/s** — used in trilateration distance calculations
- **GPS Satellite Orbit Altitude:** ~20,200 km (MEO — Medium Earth Orbit)
- **Time-to-First-Fix (TTFF):** Cold start ≤ 30 s, Warm start ≤ 10 s, Hot start ≤ 1 s
- **Typical Position Accuracy:** Standalone GPS ≈ **3–5 m**, DGPS/RTK ≈ **0.01–0.1 m**
- **Standard CAN bus speed for IVI:** **500 kbps** (CAN 2.0B) or **1 Gbps** for Automotive Ethernet (1000BASE-T1)
- **Refresh rate of navigation map:** ≥ **10 Hz** for smooth turn-by-turn
- **Standard audio sampling rate (IVI):** 44.1 kHz (CD quality) or 48 kHz (professional)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize the **speed of light constant c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** for any GNSS-related distance/time-of-flight derivation that examiners may ask as a sub-part.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Modern Vehicle Navigation System

A KTU 2024-compliant answer must describe navigation as a **4-stage pipeline**:

### Stage 1 — Localization (Position Fix)
- **GNSS Receiver** (GPS/GLONASS/Galileo/IRNSS/NavIC) computes raw pseudoranges to ≥ 4 satellites.
- **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** — 3-axis accelerometer + 3-axis gyroscope + (optional) magnetometer — provides 6-DOF state (x, y, z, roll, pitch, yaw).
- **Wheel-tick sensors** (from ABS/ESP ECU over CAN) provide odometry.
- A **Kalman Filter** (or Particle Filter) fuses GNSS + IMU + odometry to produce a robust position estimate. This is called **Sensor Fusion**.

### Stage 2 — Map Matching
- The noisy fused position is **snapped** onto the road network stored in a **GIS database** (commonly in NDS — Navigation Data Standard — or HERE/MapMyIndia format).
- Algorithms used: **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)**, **Fréchet distance matching**, or **geometric point-to-curve matching**.

### Stage 3 — Path Planning (Route Calculation)
- **Dijkstra's Algorithm** — classic shortest-path on weighted road graph.
- **A\* Algorithm** — heuristic-enhanced Dijkstra (faster, uses Euclidean heuristic).
- **Bidirectional A\*** and **Contraction Hierarchies** — used in production by HERE, TomTom, Google Maps for sub-second response.
- Multi-criteria optimization: **shortest distance, fastest time, lowest fuel, avoid tolls, avoid highways**.

### Stage 4 — Guidance & HMI
- **Visual:** Map display, turn arrows, lane guidance, speed limit.
- **Auditory:** Text-to-Speech (TTS) prompts like *"Turn right in 200 meters"*.
- **Haptic:** Steering wheel vibration, seat vibration (advanced ADAS).

## 2.2 Architecture of an In-Vehicle Infotainment (IVI) System

The IVI stack (as per the GENIVI Alliance / COVESA reference architecture) has **4 functional layers**:

| Layer | Function | Typical Hardware/Software |
|------|---------|---------------------------|
| **L1 — Hardware Layer** | Display, Touch, Audio Amp, Tuner, GNSS Antenna, Bluetooth/WiFi module | Capacitive touch, OLED/LCD, Class-D amplifier |
| **L2 — OS / Hypervisor** | Multi-OS support (QNX for safety + Android for apps) | QNX Neutrino, AAOS, Linux GENIVI, hypervisor (Type-1: PikeOS, ACRN) |
| **L3 — Middleware / Services** | Audio management, BT stack, CAN gateway, voice recognition | SDL, Audio Flinger, SOME/IP, D-Bus |
| **L4 — Applications** | Navigation, Media, Phone, Climate, ADAS visualization, OTA updates | App frameworks, HMI rendering engines (Qt, Kanzi, Unreal) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Hypervisor?** Safety-critical functions (digital instrument cluster, ADAS warning) must run on a **real-time OS (QNX)**, while consumer apps (Spotify, YouTube) need **Android/Linux**. A **Type-1 hypervisor** (e.g., QNX Hypervisor, Green Hills INTEGRITY Multivisor) runs both side-by-side on a single SoC (e.g., Qualcomm Snapdragon 8155/8295, NXP i.MX 8). This is a **favourite 14-mark KTU question** topic.

## 2.3 Communication Buses Inside the IVI Domain

| Bus Type | Speed | Use Case in IVI |
|---------|-------|----------------|
| **CAN (Classic)** | 500 kbps | Vehicle status, door, HVAC |
| **CAN-FD** | 2 Mbps | ADAS sensor data, instrument cluster |
| **LIN** | 19.2 kbps | Low-speed body controls (mirrors, seats) |
| **Automotive Ethernet (100/1000BASE-T1)** | 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps | High-bandwidth: map tiles, camera streams, OTA downloads |
| **MOST (Media Oriented Systems Transport)** | 150 Mbps | Legacy infotainment backbone (older Benz/BMW) |
| **A2B (Automotive Audio Bus)** | 50 Mbps | Digital audio between amplifier and speakers |

## 2.4 Connectivity & Smartphone Integration

- **Bluetooth** — Hands-Free Profile (HFP), Advanced Audio Distribution Profile (A2DP), Phone Book Access (PBAP).
- **Wi-Fi** — WPA3-secured, used for software updates and screen mirroring.
- **4G LTE / 5G** — Telematics Control Unit (TCU) connects to cloud for real-time traffic, eCall, and remote diagnostics.
- **Apple CarPlay / Android Auto** — Phone projection over USB or wireless (Wi-Fi + BT). Two modes: **Wired (USB)** and **Wireless (Wi-Fi Direct)**.
- **MirrorLink** — Legacy standard, now deprecated.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` for absolute value in any math expression. **Never use `|` inside markdown tables** — it breaks rendering.

| Symbol | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Unit |
|--------|---------------------|---------|------|
| Pseudorange | $R_i = c \cdot (t_{rx,i} - t_{tx,i})$ | Distance from satellite $i$ to receiver | meters |
| Speed of light | $c$ = **3 × 10⁸** | Constant for GNSS distance | m/s |
| Haversine Distance | $d = 2R \arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)}\right)$ | Great-circle distance on Earth | meters |
| Earth Radius | $R \approx$ **6,371,000** | Mean radius for Haversine | meters |
| Dead-Reckoning Position | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cdot \cos\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$ | X position update | meters |
| Dead-Reckoning Position | $y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \cdot \sin\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$ | Y position update | meters |
| Heading Update | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_z \cdot \Delta t$ | Yaw integration from gyro | radians |
| Doppler Velocity | $v = \frac{\Delta f}{f_0} \cdot c$ | Receiver speed from frequency shift | m/s |
| Dilution of Precision | $DOP = \sqrt{\text{trace}(H \cdot H^T)^{-1}}$ | Satellite geometry quality | dimensionless |
| Bitrate (Audio) | $R_b = f_s \times n \times b$ | PCM audio bitrate | bps |
| SNR (Satellite) | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Link quality | dB |
| C/N₀ Threshold | **37 – 45 dB-Hz** | Minimum for GPS lock | dB-Hz |
| Free-Space Path Loss | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | GNSS signal attenuation | dB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The **Haversine formula** is what Google Maps uses internally to compute "as the crow flies" distances for ETA estimation. The **Kalman filter** in sensor fusion is the same algorithm used in Apollo, SpaceX Dragon, and the Mars Helicopter *Ingenuity*.

## 2.6 Why These Systems Matter for Safety & Security

- **Navigation + ADAS = Predictive Cruise Control** that uses map data to anticipate hills and curves.
- **eCall (EU Regulation 2015/758):** In a crash, the IVI's TCU automatically dials **112** and transmits the **GNSS coordinates** to emergency services. India has launched **Pan-India eCall** in 2025.
- **Stolen Vehicle Tracking (SVT):** Telematics server pings the IVI/TCU's GNSS every few minutes.
- **Geo-fencing:** Parents can set a digital boundary for their teen driver's car — alarm triggered if breached.
- **Cybersecurity Risk:** A compromised IVI = a backdoor into CAN bus → can disable brakes in some research demos (Jeep Cherokee hack, 2015). Hence **ISO/SAE 21434** cybersecurity engineering standard is mandatory for IVI design.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: GNSS Pseudorange and Position Fix (Trilateration)

A GNSS receiver determines its position $(x, y, z)$ and clock bias $b$ by solving the system of equations derived from signals received from at least **4 satellites**. We shall derive this rigorously.

### Step 1 — Time-of-Flight Measurement

A satellite $S_i$ transmits a signal at time $t_{tx,i}$ (encoded in the navigation message). The receiver records the time of arrival as $t_{rx,i}$. The raw measured time difference is:

$$
\Delta t_i = t_{rx,i} - t_{tx,i}
$$

The true geometric distance between the satellite and the receiver is:

$$
R_i = c \cdot \Delta t_i
$$

where $c$ is the speed of light.

### Step 2 — Accounting for Clock Bias

The receiver clock is not synchronized to the highly accurate atomic clocks onboard the satellites. Let $b$ be the receiver clock bias (in seconds). The **pseudorange** (because it is contaminated by the clock error) is:

$$
\rho_i = c \cdot (\Delta t_i - b) = c \cdot (t_{rx,i} - t_{tx,i}) - c \cdot b
$$

Equivalently:

$$
\rho_i = R_i + c \cdot b
$$

This is the fundamental pseudorange equation. The "pseudo" prefix denotes the presence of the unknown bias $b$.

### Step 3 — Geometric Model

Let the receiver's true position be $(x_r, y_r, z_r)$ and the $i$-th satellite's known position (broadcast in the navigation message) be $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$. The true geometric range is:

$$
R_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_i - y_r)^2 + (z_i - z_r)^2}
$$

Substituting into the pseudorange equation:

$$
\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_i - y_r)^2 + (z_i - z_r)^2} + c \cdot b
$$

### Step 4 — Linearization (Newton-Raphson Style)

Let the initial estimate be $(\hat{x}, \hat{y}, \hat{z}, \hat{b})$. The linearized form is:

$$
\Delta \rho_i = \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial x_r} \Delta x + \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial y_r} \Delta y + \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial z_r} \Delta z + \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial b} \Delta b
$$

The partial derivatives (line-of-sight unit vectors) are:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial x_r} = \frac{\hat{x}_r - x_i}{\hat{R}_i}, \quad \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial y_r} = \frac{\hat{y}_r - y_i}{\hat{R}_i}, \quad \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial z_r} = \frac{\hat{z}_r - z_i}{\hat{R}_i}, \quad \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial b} = c
$$

### Step 5 — Matrix Form and Solution

The system of 4 equations (one per satellite) can be written in matrix form as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \Delta \rho_1 \\ \Delta \rho_2 \\ \Delta \rho_3 \\ \Delta \rho_4 \end{bmatrix} &=
\begin{bmatrix}
\frac{\hat{x}_r - x_1}{\hat{R}_1} & \frac{\hat{y}_r - y_1}{\hat{R}_1} & \frac{\hat{z}_r - z_1}{\hat{R}_1} & c \\
\frac{\hat{x}_r - x_2}{\hat{R}_2} & \frac{\hat{y}_r - y_2}{\hat{R}_2} & \frac{\hat{z}_r - z_2}{\hat{R}_2} & c \\
\frac{\hat{x}_r - x_3}{\hat{R}_3} & \frac{\hat{y}_r - y_3}{\hat{R}_3} & \frac{\hat{z}_r - z_3}{\hat{R}_3} & c \\
\frac{\hat{x}_r - x_4}{\hat{R}_4} & \frac{\hat{y}_r - y_4}{\hat{R}_4} & \frac{\hat{z}_r - z_4}{\hat{R}_4} & c
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \\ \Delta z \\ \Delta b \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

In compact form:

$$
\Delta \boldsymbol{\rho} = H \cdot \Delta \mathbf{x}
$$

The least-squares solution is:

$$
\Delta \mathbf{x} = (H^T H)^{-1} H^T \Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}
$$

The updated state is then:

$$
\mathbf{x}_{new} = \hat{\mathbf{x}} + \Delta \mathbf{x}
$$

The iteration repeats until $\vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \vert$ falls below a threshold (typically **0.01 m**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** A 14-mark question may ask to derive the position-fix equations. The four unknowns $(x, y, z, b)$ and the requirement of **at least 4 satellites** is non-negotiable. One satellite alone gives a sphere; two give a circle; three give two points; **four satellites** resolve the ambiguity and yield a unique position in 3D.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Dead-Reckoning Update Equations

When GNSS is unavailable (tunnel, urban canyon), the navigation system switches to **dead reckoning** using IMU + wheel-tick sensors. We derive the discrete-time update rules.

### Step 1 — Linear Displacement

Given the vehicle's measured speed $v_k$ (from wheel-tick sensors or wheel-speed sensors) and heading $\theta_k$ (from yaw-rate gyro integration) at time step $k$, the displacement during a time interval $\Delta t$ is:

$$
d_k = v_k \cdot \Delta t
$$

### Step 2 — X and Y Components of Displacement

The displacement is decomposed into Cartesian components:

$$
\Delta x_k = d_k \cdot \cos(\theta_k) = v_k \cdot \cos(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t
$$

$$
\Delta y_k = d_k \cdot \sin(\theta_k) = v_k \cdot \sin(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t
$$

### Step 3 — Position Update

The new position $(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1})$ is obtained by adding the displacement to the previous position:

$$
x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cdot \cos(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t
$$

$$
y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \cdot \sin(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t
$$

### Step 4 — Heading Update from Yaw-Rate Gyro

The yaw-rate gyroscope measures angular velocity $\omega_z$ (in rad/s). The new heading is:

$$
\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_z \cdot \Delta t
$$

### Step 5 — Error Growth

Because the heading is integrated over time, any small bias $b_\omega$ in the gyroscope accumulates linearly:

$$
\theta_{k}^{err} = \theta_{k-1}^{err} + b_\omega \cdot \Delta t
$$

After $N$ steps of duration $\Delta t$ (total time $T = N \cdot \Delta t$):

$$
\theta_{N}^{err} = b_\omega \cdot T
$$

The position error due to this heading error grows quadratically with time:

$$
x_N^{err} \approx v \cdot T \cdot \theta_N^{err} = v \cdot b_\omega \cdot T^2
$$

Hence dead-reckoning without aiding is unusable beyond a few minutes — this is why we **fuse** it with GNSS using a Kalman filter.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Haversine Distance

**Problem:** A vehicle moves from Kochi (9.9312° N, 76.2673° E) to Bengaluru (12.9716° N, 77.5946° E). Compute the great-circle distance using the Haversine formula.

**Solution:**

Convert to radians:
- $\phi_1 = 9.9312° = 9.9312 \times \pi / 180 = 0.17334$ rad
- $\phi_2 = 12.9716° = 12.9716 \times \pi / 180 = 0.22641$ rad
- $\Delta \phi = 0.22641 - 0.17334 = 0.05307$ rad
- $\Delta \lambda = (77.5946 - 76.2673) \times \pi / 180 = 1.3273 \times \pi / 180 = 0.02316$ rad

Apply the Haversine formula:

$$
a = \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta \phi}{2}\right) + \cos(\phi_1) \cdot \cos(\phi_2) \cdot \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta \lambda}{2}\right)
$$

$$
a = \sin^2(0.02654) + \cos(0.17334) \cdot \cos(0.22641) \cdot \sin^2(0.01158)
$$

$$
a = (0.02653)^2 + (0.98499) \cdot (0.97440) \cdot (0.01158)^2
$$

$$
a = 0.000704 + 0.95989 \cdot 0.000134 = 0.000704 + 0.000129 = 0.000833
$$

Distance:

$$
d = 2 \cdot R \cdot \arcsin(\sqrt{a}) = 2 \cdot 6{,}371{,}000 \cdot \arcsin(\sqrt{0.000833})
$$

$$
d = 12{,}742{,}000 \cdot \arcsin(0.02886) = 12{,}742{,}000 \cdot 0.02887
$$

$$
\boxed{d \approx 367{,}860 \text{ m} \approx 367.86 \text{ km}}
$$

This matches the well-known road/air distance between Kochi and Bengaluru (~365–370 km).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Navigation Techniques

| Technique | Accuracy | Availability | Cost | Best Use Case |
|-----------|----------|--------------|------|---------------|
| **Standalone GPS** | 3–15 m | Sky-view only | Low | Consumer nav |
| **DGPS / SBAS (GAGAN in India)** | 0.5–2 m | Sky-view | Medium | Aviation, ADAS |
| **RTK-GNSS** | 0.01–0.1 m | Sky-view + base station | High | Autonomous vehicles, surveying |
| **Dead Reckoning (IMU + Wheel)** | Drifts: 1% of distance | Works in tunnels | Medium | Tunnel/urban canyon |
| **Map Matching (aided)** | 1–5 m | Road network | Software | Always on top of GPS/DR |
| **Sensor Fusion (Kalman)** | 2–5 m robust | Continuous | Medium | Production IVI |
| **Visual SLAM / LiDAR SLAM** | 0.1–1 m | Where cameras work | High | Robotaxis (Waymo, Tesla FSD) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy for the Matrix:** GPS is like **a person with perfect eyesight but only when outside**. Dead Reckoning is like **a person who is blind but can count their footsteps**. Sensor Fusion is like **a person who can do both** — they will always find their way.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define GNSS and list any four satellite navigation systems operational worldwide. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper PEMUT743]**

**Model Answer:**
**Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)** is a constellation of satellites that broadcasts timing and positioning signals from space, enabling a receiver on or near the Earth to determine its **3-D position (latitude, longitude, altitude), velocity, and time** by trilateration.

The four operational GNSS systems are:

1. **GPS (USA)** — 31 satellites, operated by the US Space Force; primary frequency L1 = 1575.42 MHz.
2. **GLONASS (Russia)** — 24 satellites, operated by Roscosmos; uses FDMA access.
3. **Galileo (European Union)** — 30 satellites, civilian-controlled; high accuracy ~1 m.
4. **NavIC / IRNSS (India)** — 7 satellites covering India and 1500 km beyond; operated by ISRO. India is currently operationalising **NavIC L1 band** for civilian use.

(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for naming the four systems.)
