## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A: Explain the architecture of a modern In-Vehicle Infotainment (IVI) system in detail. Discuss the role of a hypervisor and the various communication buses used. [14 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 PEMUT743]**

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Explain the layered architecture of the IVI system. [7 Marks]

The In-Vehicle Infotainment (IVI) system is built as a **four-layer stack** to ensure separation of concerns, real-time performance, and cybersecurity. These layers are:

**Layer 1 — Hardware Layer:** This is the physical foundation. It includes the **System on Chip (SoC)** (e.g., Qualcomm Snapdragon 8155/8295, NXP i.MX 8, Renesas R-Car), **capacitive touch display** (8–15 inches, 1080p to 4K resolution), **Class-D audio amplifier**, **GNSS receiver IC**, **Bluetooth 5.3 / Wi-Fi 6 module**, **4G/5G modem** (in the TCU), and **CAN/Ethernet transceivers**. Modern cars use **multi-display setups** — one for the instrument cluster and one for the central infotainment.

**Layer 2 — OS / Hypervisor Layer:** Modern IVI runs **two or more operating systems** concurrently. A **safety-critical RTOS** (QNX Neutrino, Green Hills INTEGRITY) runs the digital instrument cluster and ADAS visualization, while a **rich OS** (Android Automotive OS or Linux GENIVI) runs the consumer apps. A **Type-1 hypervisor** (e.g., QNX Hypervisor, ACRN) sits below both and partitions the hardware. This is called a **Digital Cockpit architecture**.

**Layer 3 — Middleware / Services Layer:** This provides the reusable services. Examples include the **Audio Manager** (mixing navigation prompts with music and phone calls), **Bluetooth stack** (HFP, A2DP, PBAP), **Voice Recognition engine** (on-device or cloud-based, using Vosk, Picovoice, or Google STT), **CAN gateway daemon** (translating CAN frames to Ethernet/SOME/IP), and **D-Bus / SDL / gRPC** for inter-process communication.

**Layer 4 — Application Layer:** This is what the user sees and touches. Apps include **Navigation, Media Player, Phone, Climate Control, ADAS visualization, Vehicle Settings, and OTA Update Manager**. The HMI is rendered using engines like **Qt for Automotive, Kanzi (Rightware), or Unreal Engine** for premium 3D clusters.

#### (b) Discuss the role of a hypervisor and the communication buses used. [7 Marks]

**Role of the Hypervisor:** A hypervisor is **virtualization software** that allows multiple OS instances to run on a single SoC, with **strict isolation, deterministic CPU scheduling, and dedicated memory/RAM regions**. Its role is:
1. **Functional Safety:** A failure in the Android side (e.g., a buggy app crash) cannot affect the QNX safety cluster.
2. **Cost Reduction:** One SoC replaces two or three ECUs, reducing BOM cost, wiring, and weight.
3. **Shared Hardware Access:** Both guest OSes can share the GPU, display, and audio hardware via para-virtualization drivers.
4. **Compliance with ISO 26262 ASIL-B** for the cluster side.

**Communication Buses:**

| Bus | Speed | Used For |
|-----|-------|----------|
| CAN (Classic) | 500 kbps | Body controls, HVAC, door status |
| CAN-FD | 2 Mbps | Powertrain, ADAS sensor data |
| Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1 / 1000BASE-T1) | 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps | Map streaming, camera feeds, OTA, back-bone |
| LVDS / FPD-Link / GMSL | 1–6 Gbps | Display-to-SoC video link |
| A2B | 50 Mbps | Digital audio distribution |
| USB | 5–10 Gbps | Smartphone projection, media playback |
| Bluetooth / Wi-Fi | Short range | Phone connectivity, wireless CarPlay/Auto |

**Conclusion:** The IVI is the **single largest electronic component** in a modern car by software complexity. Its layered architecture, hypervisor-based isolation, and converged Ethernet backbone enable the **connected, software-defined vehicle (SDV)** vision of OEMs.

---

### Question B: With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a GPS-based vehicle navigation system. Discuss sensor fusion using a Kalman filter and the concept of dead reckoning. [14 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 4 PEMUT743]**

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Draw the block diagram and explain the working of a GPS-based navigation system. [7 Marks]

A GPS-based vehicle navigation system determines the **real-time geographic position** of a car and provides **turn-by-turn guidance** to a destination. The block diagram is as follows:

**[Diagram: Refer to Section 4.1 above]**

**Working — Stage by Stage:**

1. **GNSS Signal Reception:** The **GNSS antenna** on the car roof receives microwave signals from at least **4 satellites** of the GPS constellation (each at ~20,200 km altitude). These signals travel at the speed of light and contain the satellite's **ephemeris data** (its precise orbital position) and **almanac** (coarse orbit info of all satellites).

2. **Pseudorange Computation:** The **GNSS receiver baseband** measures the **time-of-arrival** of each satellite's signal and computes the **pseudorange** $\rho_i = c \cdot (t_{rx,i} - t_{tx,i}) - c \cdot b$, where $b$ is the receiver clock bias. With 4 such equations, the receiver solves for 4 unknowns: position $(x, y, z)$ and bias $b$.

3. **Sensor Fusion via Kalman Filter:** The raw GNSS position is noisy (~5–15 m). It is fused with **IMU (accelerometer + gyroscope)** and **wheel-tick** data using a **Kalman filter** to yield a smoother, more accurate estimate (~2 m).

4. **Map Matching:** The filtered position is **snapped** onto the road network using a **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** or geometric algorithm, since the car cannot be in the middle of a building.

5. **Path Planning:** Given a destination, the **A\* algorithm with Contraction Hierarchies** computes the **optimal route** based on distance, time, or fuel criteria.

6. **Route Guidance:** The system issues **visual instructions** (turn arrow, lane indicator, speed limit) and **auditory prompts** (TTS) at the appropriate moment based on the car's distance to the next maneuver.

7. **Cloud Telematics (Optional):** Real-time traffic, weather, and Points of Interest (POI) are fetched from cloud servers via 4G/5G.

#### (b) Explain Kalman filter-based sensor fusion and dead reckoning. [7 Marks]

**Kalman Filter (KF) — Two-Step Recursion:**

**Predict Step:** Using the IMU (which gives high-rate motion at 100–1000 Hz), the KF predicts the next state $\hat{x}_{k+1 \vert k}$ and its uncertainty covariance $P_{k+1 \vert k}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{x}_{k+1 \vert k} &= A \cdot \hat{x}_{k \vert k} + B \cdot u_k \\
P_{k+1 \vert k} &= A \cdot P_{k \vert k} \cdot A^T + Q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Update Step:** When a low-rate GNSS fix arrives (e.g., 1–10 Hz), the KF computes the **Kalman gain** $K_k$ and corrects the prediction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_k &= P_{k \vert k-1} \cdot H^T \cdot (H \cdot P_{k \vert k-1} \cdot H^T + R)^{-1} \\
\hat{x}_{k \vert k} &= \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} + K_k \cdot (z_k - H \cdot \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}) \\
P_{k \vert k} &= (I - K_k \cdot H) \cdot P_{k \vert k-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, $Q$ is the process noise covariance (IMU noise) and $R$ is the measurement noise covariance (GNSS noise). The KF **automatically weights** the two sensors — trusting IMU during a tunnel, trusting GNSS in open sky.

**Dead Reckoning (DR):** When GNSS is lost (tunnel, urban canyon), the system relies purely on **wheel-tick speed** and **gyro-derived heading**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_k + v_k \cdot \cos(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t \\
y_{k+1} &= y_k + v_k \cdot \sin(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t \\
\theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \omega_z \cdot \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Limitation:** Heading is integrated from gyro, so a small bias $b_\omega$ causes position error that grows **quadratically with time**: $x^{err} \approx v \cdot b_\omega \cdot T^2$. A 0.1°/s bias at 60 km/h for 1 minute gives a ~17 m position error — significant for lane-level navigation. To mitigate, **tunnel navigation** systems use **gyro-compassing**, **map matching against the road graph**, and **ESC-validated wheel pulses**.

**Conclusion:** The marriage of GNSS, IMU, wheel sensors, and a Kalman filter gives the **continuous, robust, lane-accurate positioning** required for modern ADAS and infotainment features.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PEMUT743 Module 4:**
> 1. **Confusing "GNSS" with "GPS":** GNSS is the *generic family*; GPS is *only the US system*. Always write "GNSS" generically unless referring specifically to the US system. **Penalty: -1 mark** for the misuse.
> 2. **Forgetting the clock bias $b$:** In pseudorange derivations, students often write $\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + \cdots}$ without the $+ c \cdot b$ term. This is **incomplete** — you need 4 satellites *because* of this bias. **Penalty: -2 marks.**
> 3. **Saying "Bluetooth connects to internet":** Bluetooth is a *short-range PAN*, not a WAN. It needs a phone or TCU's 4G/5G to reach the internet. **Penalty: -1 mark.**
> 4. **Calling CarPlay an "App":** CarPlay is a **projection protocol / HMI mode**, not an app installed on the IVI. The actual app is on the iPhone.
> 5. **Forgetting the "Minimum 4 satellites" reasoning:** Examiners specifically check for the *four-equation, four-unknown* explanation. Without it, the answer to a "How does GPS work?" question is incomplete.
> 6. **Mixing up CAN, CAN-FD, and CAN-XL speeds:** 500 kbps (Classic), 2 Mbps (FD), 5–10 Mbps (XL). Same bus, different protocols.
> 7. **Omitting map matching:** A navigation answer without *map matching* is a GNSS answer, not a *navigation* answer. The examiner expects the full pipeline.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Navigation and Infotainment**

**Navigation System Essentials:**
- [ ] **GNSS** = generic term; **GPS** = US system; also GLONASS, Galileo, NavIC (India), BeiDou (China).
- [ ] **Pseudorange equation** $\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_i - y_r)^2 + (z_i - z_r)^2} + c \cdot b$ — needs **≥ 4 satellites** to solve for $(x, y, z, b)$.
- [ ] **Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s** — must be memorised.
- [ ] **Haversine formula** for great-circle distance; $R_{earth} = 6{,}371{,}000$ m.
- [ ] **Kalman filter** = two-step **Predict** + **Update**; fuses GNSS + IMU + wheel-tick.
- [ ] **Dead reckoning** uses $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cos\theta_k \Delta t$; error grows as $T^2$ due to gyro bias.
- [ ] **Map matching** snaps GPS fixes to road network using HMM/Fréchet.
- [ ] **Path planning** = Dijkstra, A\*, Contraction Hierarchies.
- [ ] **DOP (Dilution of Precision)** measures satellite geometry; lower is better.

**Infotainment System Essentials:**
- [ ] **4-layer architecture:** Hardware → OS/Hypervisor → Middleware → Applications.
- [ ] **Hypervisor** = Type-1 (PikeOS, QNX Hypervisor, ACRN); runs QNX + Android together.
- [ ] **SoCs:** Qualcomm 8155/8295, NXP i.MX 8, Renesas R-Car.
- [ ] **Buses:** CAN (500 kbps), CAN-FD (2 Mbps), Automotive Ethernet (100/1000 Mbps), MOST, A2B, LVDS/FPD-Link.
- [ ] **Connectivity:** Bluetooth 5.3, Wi-Fi 6, 4G LTE, 5G.
- [ ] **Phone Projection:** Apple CarPlay (iOS only), Android Auto (Android only), MirrorLink (legacy).
- [ ] **OSes:** QNX Neutrino (RTOS), Android Automotive OS (AAOS), Linux GENIVI, AUTOSAR Adaptive.
- [ ] **HMI Engines:** Qt for Automotive, Kanzi (Rightware), Unreal Engine.
- [ ] **Audio:** 44.1 kHz sampling (CD), 48 kHz (pro); Class-D amplifier; A2B bus for digital audio.
- [ ] **Voice Assistants:** Siri, Google Assistant, Alexa Auto, Cerence (automotive-tuned).

**Safety & Security Integration:**
- [ ] **eCall** → automatic crash notification → sends GNSS + VIN to **112** PSAP.
- [ ] **Stolen Vehicle Tracking (SVT)** via telematics + GNSS.
- [ ] **OTA updates** via 4G/5G; secured per **UNECE R156** and **ISO/SAE 21434**.
- [ ] **Cybersecurity:** IVI is a CAN bus entry point — secure boot, HSM (Hardware Security Module), signed firmware.
- [ ] **Driver Distraction Standards:** ISO 15005, NHTSA guidelines, Euro NCAP Safe Driving Score.

**Quick-Fire Numbers to Memorise:**
- $c$ = 3 × 10⁸ m/s
- Earth radius = 6,371 km
- GPS orbit = 20,200 km
- L1 frequency = 1575.42 MHz
- CAN speed = 500 kbps; CAN-FD = 2 Mbps
- Automotive Ethernet = 100 Mbps / 1 Gbps
- TTFF: Cold 30 s, Warm 10 s, Hot 1 s
- GPS accuracy: 3–15 m standalone; 0.5–2 m with SBAS

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When answering a 14-mark question, always start with a **diagram** (3 marks implicit), then a **definition**, then **sub-parts in bullet points**, and **end with a 2-line conclusion** linking the topic to vehicle safety. Examiners reward structured, diagram-supported answers with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Telematics systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT743 - VEHICLE SAFETY AND SECURITY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Telematics systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Telematics** is the integrated discipline encompassing the convergence of **telecommunications**, **informatics** (computing), and **vehicle electronics** to enable bidirectional communication between a moving vehicle and a remote monitoring/tracking infrastructure. In automotive engineering terms (KTU PEMUT743 syllabus), it refers to the on-board unit (OBU) system that captures, transmits, and receives vehicle operational data over wireless networks for safety, security, navigation, fleet optimization, and infotainment services.

The formal ETSI/ISO definition is:
$$\text{Telematics} = f(\text{Sensing}, \text{Computing}, \text{Wireless Communication}, \text{Data Analytics})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 scheme, Module 4 expects students to differentiate between passive and active telematics, identify the architecture blocks (GPS receiver, MCU, CAN transceiver, GSM/GPRS modem, sensors, power supply), and explain use cases such as **eCall**, **Stolen Vehicle Tracking**, **Fleet Management**, and **Usage-Based Insurance (UBI)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Talking Black Box of Your Car":**
> Imagine your vehicle has a *black-box-flight-recorder-like device* permanently bolted to it. This device has four "super-powers":
> 1. **It always knows WHERE it is** (thanks to satellites overhead).
> 2. **It always knows WHAT'S happening inside it** (engine RPM, fuel level, crash sensors, door locks).
> 3. **It can TALK to the outside world** through the cellular network, just like your smartphone.
> 4. **It can LISTEN to remote commands** (lock the doors, honk the horn, disable the engine).
>
> **Telematics = (Where am I?) + (How am I doing?) + (Talking to someone far away) + (Listening to orders).**
>
> If your car is stolen, telematics *calls the police with the GPS location*. If you crash, it *automatically dials emergency services*. If a fleet manager owns 500 trucks, telematics *reports each truck's location and fuel usage in real-time*.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following parameters are **bold-highlighted** as KTU frequently-tested metrics:

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|--------|----------------|--------------|
| **GPS Positioning Accuracy** | **±2.5 m (CEP, civilian)** | Location precision in standard GPS |
| **eCall Response Time Mandate** | **< 60 seconds** | EU regulation from crash to dispatch |
| **CAN Bus Speed** | **500 kbps (high-speed CAN)** | Vehicle network data rate |
| **GSM Data Rate (GPRS)** | **56–114 kbps** | Telematics cellular uplink |
| **4G LTE Telematics Latency** | **< 100 ms** | Modern fleet tracking latency |
| **Operating Frequency (GPS L1)** | **1575.42 MHz** | Civilian GPS carrier |
| **eCall Minimum Set of Data (MSD)** | **140 bytes** | Payload in emergency call |
| **Backup Battery (Telematics)** | **≥ 10 years standby** | For stolen vehicle recovery |

---

## 1.4 The Four Pillars of Automotive Telematics

```
PILLAR 1              PILLAR 2              PILLAR 3              PILLAR 4
─────────             ─────────             ─────────             ─────────
VEHICLE               WIRELESS              BACKEND               APPLICATION
END SYSTEM           COMMUNICATION         SERVER                & USER
(OBU + Sensors)       (GSM/4G/5G/Sat)       (Cloud DB)            (Dashboard)
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **On-Board Unit (OBU)** is the heart of telematics — a small embedded computer containing a **GNSS receiver**, **cellular modem**, **CAN bus interface**, **microcontroller**, **accelerometer/gyro**, and a **backup Li-ion battery**. It is the equivalent of a smartphone fused with the vehicle's nervous system.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Breakdown

### Step 1 — **Data Acquisition (Sensing Layer)**
The OBU continuously reads signals from:
- **GNSS receiver** → latitude, longitude, altitude, speed, heading
- **CAN bus** → engine RPM, vehicle speed, throttle position, brake status, fuel level, DTCs (Diagnostic Trouble Codes)
- **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** → 3-axis accelerometer (±2g to ±16g) and gyroscope for crash detection (Δv threshold ≈ 2g)
- **External sensors** → temperature, humidity, driver-facing camera, tire pressure (TPMS)

### Step 2 — **Local Processing (Edge Computing)**
The **microcontroller unit (MCU)** (typically ARM Cortex-M4 @ 168 MHz or higher) executes:
- Sensor fusion (Kalman filter for GPS + IMU)
- Event detection (crash, harsh braking, theft alarm)
- Data buffering and compression
- Decision logic (when to transmit, when to stay silent)

### Step 3 — **Wireless Transmission (Communication Layer)**
Data is sent to a remote **telematics server** via:
- **GSM/GPRS** (legacy, 2G/2.5G) — for SMS-based tracking
- **UMTS/3G, 4G LTE** (current standard)
- **5G C-V2X** (future, for V2X — Vehicle-to-Everything)
- **Satellite communication** (Iridium / Inmarsat) for remote areas
- **Wi-Fi / Bluetooth** (for short-range diagnostics at service centers)

### Step 4 — **Backend Processing (Server Layer)**
A cloud-based **Telematics Service Provider (TSP)** server:
- Receives, decrypts, and stores data in databases (SQL/NoSQL)
- Runs analytics (route deviation, driver scoring)
- Triggers alerts (geofence breach, panic button)
- Forwards emergency data to **Public Safety Answering Points (PSAPs)**

### Step 5 — **Application Layer (User Interface)**
End-users (owner, fleet manager, insurance company, emergency services) interact via:
- Smartphone apps
- Web dashboards
- API integrations (insurance, toll, OEM)
- Voice/SMS alerts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A complete telematics chain requires **5 functional blocks — Sensing, Processing, Communication, Server, and Application.** Missing any one block breaks the loop. Examiners love to test this chain.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below are the mathematically verifiable formulas KTU students must memorize for Module 4 questions.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $v = \dfrac{d}{t}$ | Vehicle speed from GPS distance-time | $v$: m/s, $d$: meters, $t$: seconds |
| 2 | $\Delta v = \int_{t_0}^{t_1} a(t)\, dt$ | Crash severity from accelerometer (velocity change) | $\Delta v$: m/s, $a$: m/s² |
| 3 | $\phi = \arctan\left(\dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}\right)$ | Heading angle from two GPS points | $\phi$: radians |
| 4 | $d_{\text{Haversine}} = 2R \cdot \arcsin\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)}\right)$ | Distance between two GPS coordinates | $R$: Earth's radius = **6,371,000 m**, $\phi,\lambda$: lat/long in radians |
| 5 | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$ | Friis transmission equation (link budget) | $P_r,P_t$: dBm, $G$: dBi, $d$: meters, $\lambda$: meters |
| 6 | $T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{tx}}$ | End-to-end telematics delay | $T$: seconds |
| 7 | $\text{Free Space Path Loss (dB)} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}(d)$ | FSPL for cellular (f in MHz, d in km) | $f$: MHz, $d$: km |
| 8 | $f_{\text{L1}} = 1575.42\,\text{MHz}$ | GPS civilian carrier frequency | L1 band |
| 9 | $\Delta v_{\text{threshold}} = 2g \approx 19.62\,\text{m/s}$ | Crash detection trigger threshold | g = 9.81 m/s² |
| 10 | $\text{MSD size} = 140\,\text{bytes}$ | eCall Minimum Set of Data payload | Per EN 15722 |

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Domain | Application | Impact |
|--------|-------------|--------|
| **Road Safety** | Automatic crash notification (eCall) | Reduces emergency response time by ~50% in EU; saves ~2,500 lives/year (estimated) |
| **Vehicle Security** | Stolen Vehicle Tracking (SVT) | Recovery rate ↑ from 50% to >90% with active telematics |
| **Insurance** | Usage-Based Insurance (UBI / Pay-As-You-Drive) | Premium reduction up to 30% for safe drivers |
| **Logistics** | Fleet Management | Fuel savings of 10–15%, route optimization |
| **OEM Diagnostics** | Remote Prognostics | Predictive maintenance, recall management |
| **Smart Cities** | V2X Communication | Traffic signal priority, platooning, congestion reduction |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exams — "S-P-C-S-A":** Sensing → Processing → Communication → Server → Application. Memorize this acronym; you can use it as the skeleton for any 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Haversine Distance Between Two GPS Coordinates

> This derivation appears frequently in KTU numerical questions to test whether the student can convert GPS data into usable distance metrics.

### Given
Two points A and B on the Earth's surface with coordinates:
- Point A: $(\phi_1, \lambda_1)$
- Point B: $(\phi_2, \lambda_2)$
- Earth's mean radius: $R = 6{,}371{,}000$ m
- All angles in **radians**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Compute the latitude and longitude differences.
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta\phi &= \phi_2 - \phi_1 \\
\Delta\lambda &= \lambda_2 - \lambda_1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Haversine of the central angle formula. The Haversine function is defined as:
$$\text{hav}(\theta) = \sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3:** The Haversine of the angle $\Delta\sigma$ between the two points (great-circle distance angle) is:
$$\text{hav}(\Delta\sigma) = \text{hav}(\Delta\phi) + \cos(\phi_1)\cos(\phi_2)\,\text{hav}(\Delta\lambda)$$

Substituting the Haversine definition:
$$\text{hav}(\Delta\sigma) = \sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos(\phi_1)\cos(\phi_2)\sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the central angle:
$$\Delta\sigma = 2\arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos(\phi_1)\cos(\phi_2)\sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by Earth's radius to get the great-circle (shortest surface) distance:
$$\boxed{\,d = 2R\,\arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos(\phi_1)\cos(\phi_2)\sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)}\right)\,}$$

### KTU-Style Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A vehicle starts at Kochi $(\phi_1 = 9.9312°\text{ N}, \lambda_1 = 76.2673°\text{ E})$ and travels to Bengaluru $(\phi_2 = 12.9716°\text{ N}, \lambda_2 = 77.5946°\text{ E})$. Compute the great-circle distance using the Haversine formula.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert degrees to radians:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\phi_1 &= 9.9312° \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 0.17334\,\text{rad} \\
\phi_2 &= 12.9716° \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 0.22641\,\text{rad} \\
\lambda_1 &= 76.2673° \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 1.33108\,\text{rad} \\
\lambda_2 &= 77.5946° \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 1.35430\,\text{rad}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute differences:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta\phi &= 0.22641 - 0.17334 = 0.05307\,\text{rad} \\
\Delta\lambda &= 1.35430 - 1.33108 = 0.02322\,\text{rad}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the inner terms:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) &= \sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{0.05307}{2}\right) = \sin^2(0.026535) \\
&\approx (0.02653)^2 \approx 0.000704 \\
\cos(\phi_1)\cos(\phi_2) &= \cos(0.17334) \cdot \cos(0.22641) \\
&\approx 0.9850 \times 0.9745 = 0.9599 \\
\sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right) &= \sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{0.02322}{2}\right) = \sin^2(0.01161) \\
&\approx (0.01161)^2 \approx 0.000135
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Plug into the Haversine formula:**
$$\begin{aligned}
d &= 2 \times 6{,}371{,}000 \times \arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{0.000704 + 0.9599 \times 0.000135}\right) \\
&= 12{,}742{,}000 \times \arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{0.000704 + 0.0001296}\right) \\
&= 12{,}742{,}000 \times \arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{0.0008336}\right) \\
&= 12{,}742{,}000 \times \arcsin(0.02887) \\
&= 12{,}742{,}000 \times 0.02888 \\
&\approx 367{,}959\,\text{m} \\
&\approx 367.96\,\text{km}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
The great-circle distance Kochi → Bengaluru is **≈ 368 km**, which matches the standard road/highway distance of ~350–400 km. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Crash Detection Δv Threshold

The **velocity change ($\Delta v$)** during a frontal collision is the universal crash severity metric used by **eCall** and insurance telematics.

### Given
- An accelerometer measures deceleration $a(t)$ during crash (sample at 100 Hz)
- Crash duration is from $t_0$ (impact start) to $t_1$ (rest)

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Acceleration is the time-derivative of velocity:
$$a(t) = \dfrac{dv}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Integrate acceleration over the crash duration to obtain velocity change:
$$\Delta v = \int_{t_0}^{t_1} a(t)\,dt$$

**Step 3:** For a discrete digital accelerometer sampled every $\Delta t$ seconds, this becomes a Riemann sum:
$$\Delta v \approx \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} a(t_i)\,\Delta t \qquad \text{where } N = \dfrac{t_1 - t_0}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 4:** If we assume a constant deceleration $a_{\text{avg}}$ over crash duration $\Delta T$:
$$\Delta v = a_{\text{avg}} \times \Delta T$$

**Step 5:** Trigger threshold for eCall activation:
$$\Delta v \geq \Delta v_{\text{threshold}} = 2g = 2 \times 9.81 = 19.62\,\text{m/s}$$

If $\Delta v \geq 19.62$ m/s, the OBU triggers an **automatic emergency call** to the PSAP.

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A vehicle hits a stationary barrier. The accelerometer records a constant deceleration of $8g$ for $150$ ms. Compute $\Delta v$ and decide if eCall triggers.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
$$\begin{aligned}
a &= 8g = 8 \times 9.81 = 78.48\,\text{m/s}^2 \\
\Delta T &= 150\,\text{ms} = 0.150\,\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the constant-deceleration formula:**
$$\Delta v = a_{\text{avg}} \times \Delta T = 78.48 \times 0.150 = 11.77\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with threshold:**
$$\Delta v = 11.77\,\text{m/s} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \Delta v_{\text{threshold}} = 19.62\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
Since $11.77 < 19.62$, **eCall does NOT auto-trigger**. This corresponds to a moderate crash — the airbag may still deploy if $\Delta v > 5g$, but the eCall threshold is not crossed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Free-Space Path Loss for Cellular Telematics

This determines whether the OBU can "reach" the cellular tower.

### Given
- Operating frequency $f$ in MHz
- Distance $d$ in km

### Derivation

**Step 1:** The Friis transmission equation in linear scale:
$$P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

**Step 2:** Convert to dB:
$$P_r(\text{dBm}) = P_t(\text{dBm}) + G_t(\text{dBi}) + G_r(\text{dBi}) - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s:
$$20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)$$

**Step 4:** With $f$ in MHz ($= f \times 10^6$ Hz) and $d$ in km ($= d \times 10^3$ m), substituting yields:
$$\boxed{\,\text{FSPL (dB)} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}})\,}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A telematics unit operates on the **GSM-900 band (f = 900 MHz)**. The nearest tower is **5 km** away. Compute the free-space path loss.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Plug into the FSPL formula:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FSPL} &= 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(900) + 20\log_{10}(5) \\
&= 32.45 + 20 \times 2.9542 + 20 \times 0.6990 \\
&= 32.45 + 59.08 + 13.98 \\
&= 105.51\,\text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation:**
A path loss of **≈ 105.5 dB** is typical for suburban GSM coverage. With a typical OBU transmitter power of **+33 dBm (2 W)** and tower antenna gain of **+18 dBi**, the received signal is approximately:
$$P_r \approx 33 + 18 - 105.5 = -54.5\,\text{dBm}$$

This is well above the GSM sensitivity of **−104 dBm**, so the link is healthy. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 On-Board Unit (OBU) Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the full internal architecture of an automotive telematics On-Board Unit, including all sensor inputs, processing, communication, and power subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GPS[GNSS Receiver L1 1575.42 MHz]
    CAN[CAN Bus Transceiver 500 kbps]
    IMU[IMU Accelerometer Gyro]
    TPMS[TPMS Sensors]
    MCU[ARM Cortex MCU Edge Processor]
    MEM[Flash Memory 4 to 16 MB]
    GSM[GSM 4G LTE Modem]
    SIM[SIM Card eUICC]
    SAT[Satellite Iridium Optional]
    BT[WiFi Bluetooth Short Range]
    PWR[DC DC Converter 9 to 16 V]
    BAT[Li ion Backup Battery]
    ANT1[GPS Patch Antenna]
    ANT2[Cellular Antenna]
    SEC[TPM Secure Element]
    LED[Status LED Indicators]
    RLY[Relay Output Ignition Kill]
    SRV[Telematics Cloud Server]
    PSAP[Emergency PSAP eCall]
    USR[Smartphone App Web Dashboard]

    GPS --> MCU
    CAN --> MCU
    IMU --> MCU
    TPMS --> MCU
    MCU --> MEM
    MCU --> GSM
    MCU --> SAT
    MCU --> BT
    GSM --> SIM
    GSM --> ANT2
    GPS --> ANT1
    PWR --> MCU
    PWR --> GSM
    BAT --> MCU
    BAT --> GSM
    MCU --> SEC
    MCU --> LED
    MCU --> RLY
    GSM --> SRV
    GSM --> PSAP
    SRV --> USR
```

## 4.2 End-to-End Telematics Data Flow (Vehicle ↔ Cloud ↔ User)

This diagram illustrates the **bidirectional data flow** of a modern telematics ecosystem, from vehicle sensors all the way to the end-user application layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V1[Vehicle Sensors GPS CAN IMU TPMS Camera]
    V2[On Board Unit OBU MCU Processing]
    V3[Cellular Modem 4G LTE 5G]
    V4[Secure TLS Encrypted Channel]
    V5[Telematics Backend Cloud Server]
    V6[Database SQL NoSQL Time Series]
    V7[Analytics Engine ML Scoring]
    V8[Application Layer]
    V9[Fleet Manager Web Dashboard]
    V10[Insurance Company UBI API]
    V11[Emergency Services eCall PSAP]
    V12[Driver Smartphone App]
    V13[Stolen Vehicle Recovery Police]

    V1 --> V2
    V2 --> V3
    V3 --> V4
    V4 --> V5
    V5 --> V6
    V5 --> V7
    V7 --> V8
    V8 --> V9
    V8 --> V10
    V8 --> V11
    V8 --> V12
    V8 --> V13
    V9 -.Command back to vehicle.-> V3
    V13 -.Remote engine kill.-> V3
```

## 4.3 Telematics System Classification Matrix

This hierarchical classification shows the **four major families** of telematics systems and their respective sub-types — useful for KTU exam comparison questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Automotive Telematics Systems]
    T1[1 Safety and Emergency]
    T2[2 Security and Tracking]
    T3[3 Fleet and Commercial]
    T4[4 Infotainment and Convenience]
    T --> T1
    T --> T2
    T --> T3
    T --> T4
    T1 --> A1[eCall Automatic Crash Notification]
    T1 --> A2[Roadside Assistance bCall]
    T1 --> A3[Driver Health Monitoring]
    T2 --> B1[Stolen Vehicle Tracking SVT]
    T2 --> B2[Remote Door Lock Unlock]
    T2 --> B3[Geo-fencing Alerts]
    T3 --> C1[Fleet Management GPS Tracking]
    T3 --> C2[Fuel Monitoring and Driver Behavior]
    T3 --> C3[Cold Chain Logistics Temperature]
    T4 --> D1[Connected Infotainment Android Auto]
    T4 --> D2[WiFi Hotspot In Vehicle]
    T4 --> D3[OTA Over The Air Software Updates]
```

## 4.4 Telematics Communication Protocol Stack

The following diagram shows the layered protocol architecture used in modern telematics communication, from the physical RF interface up to the application layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 7 Application MQTT HTTP CoAP]
    L2[Layer 6 Presentation TLS 1.3 Encryption]
    L3[Layer 5 Session TLS Handshake Certificates]
    L4[Layer 4 Transport TCP UDP]
    L5[Layer 3 Network IP IPv6 for IoT]
    L6[Layer 2 Data Link PPP LTE MAC]
    L7[Layer 1 Physical GSM LTE 5G RF]
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6
    L6 --> L7
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define automotive telematics. List any four major applications.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Automotive telematics is an integrated system that combines **telecommunications, informatics, and vehicle electronics** to enable bidirectional communication between a vehicle and a remote infrastructure for the purpose of monitoring, controlling, and providing safety, security, navigation, and information services.

**Four major applications:**
1. **eCall** — Automatic crash notification to emergency services.
2. **Stolen Vehicle Tracking (SVT)** — GPS-based recovery of stolen vehicles.
3. **Fleet Management** — Real-time tracking and optimization of commercial vehicles.
4. **Usage-Based Insurance (UBI)** — Premium calculation based on actual driving behavior.

*(Word count ~70 — ideal 3-mark answer)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the function of the GNSS receiver in a telematics On-Board Unit (OBU)?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) receiver** in a telematics OBU is responsible for:
> 1. Receiving signals from **at least four satellites** (e.g., GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, IRNSS).
> 2. Computing the vehicle's **latitude, longitude, altitude, speed (ground speed), heading, and time** using **trilateration**.
> 3. Providing a position fix with an accuracy of **±2.5 m (CEP)** for civilian GPS L1 band at **1575.42 MHz**.
> 4. Feeding location data to the MCU for transmission to the telematics backend over the cellular network.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an automotive telematics On-Board Unit (OBU). Describe the function of each block.**

**Model Solution:**

The OBU is the heart of any telematics system. It integrates sensing, processing, communication, and power management in a single rugged enclosure. The block diagram is given below:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[GNSS Receiver L1 1575.42 MHz]
    A2[CAN Transceiver High Speed 500 kbps]
    A3[IMU 3 Axis Accel Gyro]
    A4[ARM Cortex M4 MCU 168 MHz]
    A5[Flash 4 MB SRAM 1 MB]
    A6[GSM 4G LTE Modem]
    A7[WiFi Bluetooth]
    A8[DC DC Converter 9 to 16 V]
    A9[Backup Li ion Battery]
    A10[TPM Secure Element]
    A11[Relay Driver Ignition Kill]
    A12[Status LEDs]

    A1 --> A4
    A2 --> A4
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A4 --> A6
    A4 --> A7
    A6 --> A4
    A8 --> A4
    A9 --> A4
    A9 --> A6
    A4 --> A10
    A4 --> A11
    A4 --> A12
```

**Functions of each block:**

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **GNSS Receiver** | Decodes GPS/GLONASS signals; outputs position, speed, time at 1–10 Hz update rate. |
| **CAN Transceiver** | Interfaces the MCU with vehicle CAN bus; reads engine RPM, vehicle speed, fuel level, DTCs. |
| **IMU (Accelerometer + Gyro)** | Detects crash events, harsh braking, rollover by measuring linear acceleration and angular rate. |
| **ARM Cortex MCU** | Runs sensor fusion (Kalman filter), executes telematics logic, manages communication tasks. |
| **Flash + SRAM** | Stores firmware, configuration, geofence parameters, and buffers data during GSM outages. |
| **GSM/4G LTE Modem** | Provides bidirectional cellular link to the TSP cloud using TCP/IP over LTE. |
| **Wi-Fi/Bluetooth** | Enables short-range service diagnostics, smartphone pairing, and over-the-air (OTA) firmware update. |
| **DC-DC Converter** | Conditions vehicle battery voltage (9–16 V) into regulated 3.3 V and 5 V rails for the OBU. |
| **Backup Li-ion Battery** | Maintains operation for ≥ 4 hours after vehicle battery is disconnected (essential for SVT). |
| **TPM Secure Element** | Hardware-based cryptographic key storage for TLS, mutual authentication with server. |
| **Relay Driver** | Drives relays for remote commands such as ignition kill, horn honk, door lock. |
| **Status LEDs** | Visual indication of GPS lock, GSM registration, fault status. |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working of the eCall system. State the contents of the Minimum Set of Data (MSD).**

**Model Solution:**

**eCall (emergency Call)** is a **pan-European automatic emergency call system** mandated by the EU for all new cars since **31 March 2018** (Regulation (EU) 2015/758).

**Working Principle:**

1. **Crash Detection:** Sensors in the vehicle (accelerometer, pressure sensors, airbag control unit) detect a collision. When $\Delta v \geq 2g$ (≈ 19.62 m/s) or the airbag deploys, a crash signal is sent to the telematics OBU.

2. **Automatic Dialing:** The OBU automatically dials **112** (the European emergency number) over the GSM/4G network using a **voice channel** (in-band modem) or **packet-switched data** (NG-eCall over LTE).

3. **Voice Connection:** A voice channel is opened to the nearest **Public Safety Answering Point (PSAP)** so the occupant can speak with the operator. If the occupant is unconscious, the MSD data is still transmitted.

4. **MSD Transmission:** The OBU transmits a **Minimum Set of Data (MSD)** payload of **140 bytes** containing:
   - Vehicle identification (VIN)
   - GPS coordinates (latitude, longitude)
   - Timestamp of crash
   - Direction of travel (heading)
   - Number of occupants (if available)
   - Vehicle type (passenger car, bus, truck)
   - Powertrain type (petrol, diesel, electric, hydrogen)
   - Crash severity flag (light/heavy)
   - Service status (automatic vs. manual trigger)

5. **PSAP Response:** The PSAP operator views the MSD on a console, dispatches ambulance/fire/police to the GPS location, and maintains voice contact. Target response improvement: **40% faster in rural areas, 50% in urban areas**.

**MSD Packet Structure (per EN 15722):**

| Byte Range | Field |
|------------|-------|
| 0 | Message ID |
| 1 | Version |
| 2–3 | Notification type (manual/automatic) |
| 4–5 | Vehicle type |
| 6 | Powertrain type |
| 7–8 | Time stamp (seconds from epoch) |
| 9–11 | Latitude (3 bytes) |
| 12–14 | Longitude (3 bytes) |
| 15–16 | Direction of travel |
| 17 | Recent crash severity (Δv) |
| 18 | Number of occupants |
| 19–36 | VIN (17 ASCII chars) |
| 37–139 | Optional additional data |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting the MSD size of 140 bytes** — examiners specifically test this number.
> 2. **Confusing eCall (112, automatic) with bCall (breakdown call, manual)** — these are two distinct services.
> 3. **Omitting the EU regulation year** (2015/758, applicable from 31 March 2018) — KTU values current standards awareness.
> 4. **Not mentioning the in-band modem vs. NG-eCall over LTE distinction** — modern answers should mention both.
> 5. **Writing "eCall uses GPS" without specifying L1 band or accuracy** — be specific.
> 
> Always end Part (b) answers with a one-line benefit statement such as: *"eCall reduces emergency response time by up to 50% in urban areas and saves an estimated 2,500 lives annually in the EU."*

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the architecture and working of a Stolen Vehicle Tracking (SVT) system using GPS and GSM.**

**Model Solution:**

A **Stolen Vehicle Tracking (SVT)** system is a telematics-based security application that enables law enforcement to locate and recover a stolen vehicle using real-time GPS positioning and cellular communication.

**Architecture:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Vehicle OBU]
    S2[GPS Receiver Position Fix]
    S3[GSM Modem Encrypted Data]
    S4[TSP Cloud Server]
    S5[Police Control Room]
    S6[Owner Smartphone App]
    S7[Command Channel Ignition Kill]

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S4 --> S6
    S5 --> S7
    S7 --> S1
```

**Working:**

1. **Normal Mode:** The OBU remains in low-power "sleep" mode, waking up every 30 seconds to obtain a GPS fix and check for commands from the server. This minimizes power consumption and avoids detection by thieves using GSM jammers during long stop-overs.

2. **Theft Detection:** The theft can be triggered in one of three ways:
   - **Owner-initiated** — owner reports theft via smartphone app, which sends a command to the TSP server.
   - **Tamper-triggered** — OBU detects ignition without valid key, tow-away motion without ignition, or battery disconnection.
   - **Geofence breach** — vehicle leaves a pre-defined safe zone (e.g., home/work).

3. **Active Tracking Mode:** Upon theft confirmation, the server commands the OBU to switch to **high-rate transmission mode** — sending GPS coordinates every 5–10 seconds. The OBU uses its **backup Li-ion battery** (lasting ≥ 4 hours) to continue operation even if the thief disconnects the main vehicle battery.

4. **Data Encryption:** Each packet is encrypted using **AES-128** and transmitted over **TLS 1.3** to prevent spoofing or replay attacks by the thief.

5. **Law Enforcement:** The TSP shares the live vehicle location with police. The police control room can request the server to send a **remote ignition-kill** command, which disables the engine the next time the vehicle slows below 20 km/h.

6. **Recovery:** Police intercept the vehicle; the recovery rate improves from ~50% (without SVT) to over **90%** with active telematics-based SVT.

**Key Technical Specifications:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| GPS update rate (active mode) | 5–10 s |
| GPS accuracy | ±2.5 m CEP |
| Backup battery life | ≥ 4 hours |
| Encryption | AES-128 / TLS 1.3 |
| Communication | 4G LTE with 2G fallback |
| Server-to-vehicle latency | < 5 s |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, explain the working of a Usage-Based Insurance (UBI) telematics system. List the driving parameters monitored.**

**Model Solution:**

**Usage-Based Insurance (UBI)**, also called **Pay-How-You-Drive (PHYD)** or **Pay-As-You-Drive (PAYD)**, is an insurance model where the premium is calculated based on the actual driving behavior and usage patterns of the insured vehicle, as measured by a telematics device.

**Architecture:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U1[Vehicle OBU with IMU and GPS]
    U2[Telematics Server Data Lake]
    U3[Scoring Engine ML Model]
    U4[Insurance Company Backend]
    U5[Driver Score 0 to 100]
    U6[Premium Adjustment API]
    U7[Customer Dashboard]
    U8[Policy Renewal Offer]

    U1 --> U2
    U2 --> U3
    U3 --> U5
    U5 --> U4
    U4 --> U6
    U4 --> U7
    U6 --> U8
```

**Working Principle:**

1. **Data Collection:** The OBU continuously samples and uploads the following driving parameters (typically at 1 Hz):
   - Vehicle speed (from GPS and CAN bus)
   - Acceleration (longitudinal and lateral, from IMU)
   - Braking intensity (from accelerometer and brake pedal CAN signal)
   - Cornering g-force
   - Time of day (day vs. night driving)
   - Distance traveled
   - Geolocation (urban vs. highway vs. rural)
   - Phone use (if smartphone app linked)
   - Speed limit adherence

2. **Transmission:** Data is uploaded every trip-end or every 30 seconds via cellular network to the insurance telematics server.

3. **Scoring:** A **machine learning model** (typically gradient boosting or neural network) computes a **driver score** between 0 and 100 based on the parameters.

4. **Premium Adjustment:** The score is fed into the insurer's underwriting algorithm, which adjusts the premium at the next renewal cycle. Safe drivers receive **discounts up to 30%**; risky drivers may face surcharges.

**Driving Parameters Monitored:**

| Parameter | Sensor Source | Risk Indicator |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| Speed | GPS, CAN | Speeding tickets risk |
| Hard braking | IMU (Δa > 3 m/s²) | Tailgating, poor anticipation |
| Hard acceleration | IMU | Aggressive driving |
| Cornering g-force | IMU gyro | Reckless cornering |
| Night driving (22:00–05:00) | RTC + GPS | Higher accident probability |
| Phone use while driving | Smartphone app | Distraction |
| Mileage | GPS distance | Exposure metric |
| Harsh events (count/day) | IMU | Overall driving smoothness |
| Urban vs. highway ratio | GPS classification | Risk density |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Question B:**
> 1. **Do not confuse UBI with traditional insurance** — examiners expect the answer to highlight that UBI uses *real-time data*, not historical claims alone.
> 2. **List AT LEAST 6 driving parameters** — fewer than that will lose marks even if the explanation is correct.
> 3. **Avoid writing a generic "telematics" answer** — Part (b) specifically demands UBI focus; the diagram must include the scoring engine and insurer premium loop.
> 4. **Privacy concerns** are a common sub-question — mention **GDPR compliance** and **data anonymization** for the Indian DPDP Act 2023 perspective.
> 5. **Wrong abbreviation trap:** UBI = Usage-Based Insurance, NOT Universal Basic Income! Examiners sometimes plant distractors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Telematics Systems**

### 🧠 Conceptual Essentials
- [x] **Definition:** Telematics = Telecommunications + Informatics + Vehicle Electronics.
- [x] **Five functional blocks:** Sensing → Processing → Communication → Server → Application (mnemonic: **S-P-C-S-A**).
- [x] **OBU = On-Board Unit**, the heart of the system, integrates GPS + CAN + IMU + Cellular modem + MCU.

### 📡 Technical Parameters to Memorize
- [x] GPS L1 carrier frequency: **1575.42 MHz**
- [x] GPS accuracy (civilian CEP): **±2.5 m**
- [x] eCall Δv threshold: **2g ≈ 19.62 m/s**
- [x] eCall MSD size: **140 bytes**
- [x] eCall EU mandate date: **31 March 2018**
- [x] Cellular latency target: **< 100 ms (4G LTE)**
- [x] CAN high-speed bus: **500 kbps**
- [x] Earth radius for Haversine: **6,371,000 m**
- [x] SVT recovery rate improvement: **50% → 90%+**
- [x] UBI premium discount range: **up to 30%**

### 🛡️ Standards & Regulations
- [x] **EU eCall Regulation:** (EU) 2015/758
- [x] **MSD Standard:** EN 15722
- [x] **Data Security:** TLS 1.3, AES-128, TPM 2.0
- [x] **Privacy:** GDPR (EU), DPDP Act 2023 (India)
- [x] **Cellular generations used:** 2G (SMS), 3G/UMTS, 4G/LTE, 5G C-V2X

### 🚗 Key Application Categories
- [x] **Safety:** eCall, bCall, driver health monitoring
- [x] **Security:** SVT, remote lock/unlock, geofencing, ignition kill
- [x] **Fleet:** real-time tracking, fuel monitoring, cold-chain
- [x] **Insurance:** UBI / PHYD / PAYD
- [x] **Infotainment:** connected car apps, OTA updates, in-car WiFi

### 📐 Mathematical Essentials
- [x] **Haversine formula** — for great-circle distance between two GPS points
- [x] **Δv = ∫a(t)dt** — for crash severity detection
- [x] **FSPL = 32.45 + 20log₁₀(f) + 20log₁₀(d)** — for cellular link budget
- [x] **φ = arctan(Δy/Δx)** — for heading angle from GPS

### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls to Avoid in KTU Exams
- [x] Don't write "telematics uses GPS" without specifying **L1 band, accuracy, update rate**.
- [x] Don't confuse **eCall (112, automatic)** with **bCall (manual roadside assistance)**.
- [x] Don't say "UBI uses telematics" — specify **which parameters** are monitored.
- [x] Always end with a **benefit/impact statement** (lives saved, % improvement).
- [x] When asked about SVT, mention **backup battery** and **anti-jamming measures**.
- [x] Include **diagrams in every Part B answer** — answers without diagrams lose 1–2 marks even if text is correct.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** If asked to "explain telematics," always structure your answer in three parts: **(i) Definition & Components**, **(ii) Architecture & Working, (iii) Applications & Impact.** This guarantees that you address all marking descriptors and never run out of points halfway.

topics.title: Vehicle control and network systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT743 - VEHICLE SAFETY AND SECURITY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vehicle control and network systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Vehicle Control Systems (VCS) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned)**
> **Vehicle Control Systems (VCS)** are integrated electro-mechanical and electronic subsystems within a vehicle that monitor driver inputs, vehicle dynamics, and environmental conditions via a network of sensors, and actuate the appropriate braking, steering, throttle, and powertrain elements to enhance **active safety**, **stability**, **comfort**, and **fuel efficiency**. They form the foundational layer beneath Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and autonomous driving stacks.

## 1.2 In-Vehicle Networks — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned)**
> **In-Vehicle Networks (IVN)**, also called **Multiplex Wiring Systems**, are standardized serial communication protocols (CAN, LIN, FlexRay, MOST, Automotive Ethernet) that allow **Electronic Control Units (ECUs)** distributed across the vehicle body, chassis, powertrain, and infotainment domains to exchange real-time control and diagnostic data over a small number of physical wires — replacing the legacy point-to-point wiring harness.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Human Nervous System"

Think of a modern vehicle as a **human body**:

- **ECUs (Electronic Control Units)** = the **brain neurons** — each one specializes (engine, brakes, airbags, infotainment).
- **Sensors** (wheel speed, yaw rate, steering angle, radar, lidar) = the **five senses**.
- **Actuators** (brake calipers, steering motor, throttle valve) = the **muscles**.
- **The Bus Network (CAN/FlexRay)** = the **spinal cord and nervous system** — carrying tiny electrical messages between brain and muscles at lightning speed.

Without the network, every neuron would need its **own dedicated wire** to every muscle — leading to a *spaghetti harness* of over **1.5 km of copper** and **> 60 kg** of weight in premium cars. With a multiplex bus, the same vehicle uses only **2 wires** shared by **> 80 ECUs**, cutting harness weight by **~ 30 %** and enabling real-time coordinated control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Benchmark (KTU Highlight)**
> A typical 2024 mid-segment vehicle contains **70–100 ECUs** exchanging data over **> 2500 signals** at bus loads reaching **~ 500 kbps to 1 Mbps** for CAN, and up to **1 Gbps** for Automotive Ethernet.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Vehicle Control Systems

Vehicle control systems are classified based on their functional target:

| Class | Primary Function | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Longitudinal Control** | Manages forward speed & distance | Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC), AEB, Traction Control |
| **Lateral Control** | Manages steering & lane position | Electronic Stability Control (ESC), Lane Keep Assist (LKA) |
| **Vertical Control** | Manages ride height & damping | Active Suspension, Air Suspension |
| **Integrated Control** | Coordinates above axes | Vehicle Dynamics Control (VDC), Torque Vectoring |

## 1.5 Classification of In-Vehicle Network Protocols

| Protocol | Standard | Typical Speed | Domain | Cost |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CAN** | ISO 11898 | 125 kbps – 1 Mbps | Powertrain, Chassis | Low |
| **LIN** | ISO 17987 | 19.2 kbps | Body electronics (mirrors, seats) | Very Low |
| **FlexRay** | ISO 17458 | 10 Mbps | Chassis, x-by-wire | High |
| **MOST** | MOST Cooperation | 25 – 150 Mbps | Infotainment | Medium |
| **Automotive Ethernet** | IEEE 100BASE-T1 / 1000BASE-T1 | 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps | ADAS, V2X, Diagnostics | Medium-High |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Fact**
> **CAN (Controller Area Network)** was invented by **Bosch in 1986** and released as **ISO 11898 in 1993**. It is the **dominant in-vehicle bus** in > 95 % of road vehicles manufactured globally as of 2024.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electronic Stability Control (ESC) — Working Principle

**ESC** is the single most effective active safety system after seatbelts. It intervenes when the vehicle's actual yaw behaviour diverges from the driver's intended path.

### 2.1.1 The Three Sensor Inputs

1. **Steering Angle Sensor (SAS)** — mounted on the steering column. Measures the driver's intended direction.
2. **Yaw Rate Sensor (YRS)** — MEMS gyroscope at the vehicle center of gravity. Measures actual rotation about the vertical axis.
3. **Lateral Acceleration Sensor (LAS)** — measures centripetal force during cornering.
4. **Wheel Speed Sensors (WSS)** — at each wheel — measure individual slip.

### 2.1.2 The Two Control Modes

| Condition | Driver Intent vs Reality | ESC Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Understeer (Ploughing)** | Front wheels slip outward; car goes wide | Brake the **inner rear wheel** |
| **Oversteer (Spinning)** | Rear wheels slip outward; car rotates too much | Brake the **outer front wheel** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why ESC Works (Physical Principle)**
> Braking one wheel creates a **yawing moment** ($M_z$) about the vehicle CG, rotating the car toward the driver's intended path. The friction coefficient of the braked wheel is sacrificed for directional control.

## 2.2 Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) — Theoretical Foundation

ACC extends conventional cruise control by automatically adjusting vehicle speed to maintain a **safe time gap** to the preceding vehicle.

### 2.2.1 Operating Modes

- **Cruise Mode** — no lead vehicle detected → maintains set speed $v_{set}$.
- **Following Mode** — lead vehicle detected → maintains time headway $t_h$.
- **Stop & Go Mode** — extends operation to **0 km/h** (urban traffic).

### 2.2.2 Time Headway Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Headway ($t_h$)** — the time, in seconds, between the front of the ego vehicle passing a point on the road and the rear of the lead vehicle passing the same point.

Typical values: **$t_h = 1.0$ s (aggressive)**, **1.5 s (normal)**, **2.0 s (comfort)**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **exam-critical** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and internal assessments.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | **Time Headway** | $t_h = \dfrac{d}{v_{ego}}$ | s | $d$ = bumper-to-bumper gap |
| 2 | **Safe Following Distance** | $d_{safe} = v_{ego} \cdot t_h$ | m | Linear spacing rule |
| 3 | **Required Deceleration (ACC)** | $a_{req} = \dfrac{v_{ego}^{2} - v_{lead}^{2}}{2 \cdot d}$ | m/s² | Must be $\le \mu \cdot g$ |
| 4 | **Yaw Rate (Bicycle Model)** | $r = \dfrac{v \cdot \delta}{L}$ | rad/s | $\delta$ = steer angle, $L$ = wheelbase |
| 5 | **Slip Ratio** | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega_w \cdot R - v}{\max(\omega_w \cdot R,\, v)}$ | – | Used in ABS/TCS |
| 6 | **Slip Angle** | $\alpha = \delta - \arctan\left(\dfrac{v_y + r \cdot a}{v_x}\right)$ | rad | Used in ESC |
| 7 | **CAN Bit Time** | $t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{f_{baud}}$ | s | For 500 kbps → $t_{bit}$ = 2 µs |
| 8 | **Bus Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{N_{msg} \cdot L_{msg}}{f_{baud} \cdot t_{cycle}}$ | % | Must remain $<$ 70 % |
| 9 | **Bit Stuffing Overhead** | $N_{stuffed} \le \left\lceil \dfrac{N_{bits}}{4} \right\rceil$ | bits | CAN rule: stuff after 5 identical bits |
| 10 | **Longitudinal Dynamics** | $m \cdot \dot v = F_{drive} - F_{brake} - F_{aero} - F_{roll}$ | N | Newton's 2nd Law |

### 2.3.1 Variable Definitions (Symbol Table)

- $v_{ego}$ — ego vehicle speed (m/s)
- $v_{lead}$ — lead vehicle speed (m/s)
- $d$ — inter-vehicle gap (m)
- $\mu$ — tire-road friction coefficient (dimensionless, **0.1 – 1.0** for road surfaces)
- $g$ — gravitational acceleration = **9.81 m/s²**
- $\omega_w$ — wheel angular speed (rad/s)
- $R$ — wheel rolling radius (m)
- $L$ — wheelbase (m), typically **2.5 – 3.0 m** for passenger cars
- $f_{baud}$ — bus baud rate (Hz)
- $t_{cycle}$ — message cycle time (s)
- $N_{msg}$, $L_{msg}$ — number of messages and bits per message

### 2.3.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are deployed across:

- **OEM calibration** teams at Bosch, Continental, ZF, WABCO for tuning ACC and ESC.
- **NCAP test houses** (Euro NCAP, Global NCAP) for AEB performance scoring.
- **ADAS simulation** using MATLAB/Simulink, CarMaker, IPG CarMaker.
- **Functional Safety** analysis under **ISO 26262** for ASIL classification.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Braking Distance for ACC

**Problem (KTU Typical 7-mark):**
A vehicle travels at **$v_{ego} = 30$ m/s** (≈ 108 km/h) and detects a stationary obstacle at distance **$d = 50$ m**. The friction coefficient is **$\mu = 0.8$**. Determine whether Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB) can prevent collision, and if so, what deceleration profile is required.

### Step 1 — Convert Available Deceleration

The maximum friction-limited deceleration on dry asphalt is:

$$
a_{max} = \mu \cdot g
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
a_{max} = 0.8 \times 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2
$$

$$
a_{max} = 7.848 \, \text{m/s}^2
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Kinematic Equation (Stopping Distance)

Using the constant-deceleration kinematic relation:

$$
v_f^{2} = v_i^{2} - 2 \cdot a \cdot d_{stop}
$$

Setting the final velocity to zero ($v_f = 0$, since the car must stop):

$$
0 = v_i^{2} - 2 \cdot a \cdot d_{stop}
$$

Solving for the minimum stopping distance:

$$
d_{stop} = \dfrac{v_i^{2}}{2 \cdot a_{max}}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$
d_{stop} = \dfrac{(30)^2}{2 \times 7.848} \, \text{m}
$$

$$
d_{stop} = \dfrac{900}{15.696} \, \text{m}
$$

$$
d_{stop} \approx 57.34 \, \text{m}
$$

### Step 4 — Compare with Available Gap

Since the required stopping distance ($d_{stop} \approx 57.34$ m) **exceeds** the obstacle distance ($d = 50$ m):

$$
d_{stop} > d \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Collision unavoidable at } \mu = 0.8
$$

### Step 5 — Required Deceleration to Avoid Collision

Rearranging the kinematic equation for the required deceleration to stop exactly within $d = 50$ m:

$$
a_{req} = \dfrac{v_i^{2}}{2 \cdot d}
$$

$$
a_{req} = \dfrac{900}{2 \times 50} \, \text{m/s}^2
$$

$$
a_{req} = 9.0 \, \text{m/s}^2
$$

### Step 6 — Verify Realizability

Compare required deceleration with tire-road limit:

$$
a_{req} = 9.0 \, \text{m/s}^2 > a_{max} = 7.848 \, \text{m/s}^2
$$

Since $a_{req} > a_{max}$, **full avoidance is physically impossible**. The AEB can only **reduce impact speed**.

### Step 7 — Compute Residual Impact Speed

If the system applies maximum deceleration $a_{max} = 7.848$ m/s² over the 50 m gap:

$$
v_{impact}^{2} = v_i^{2} - 2 \cdot a_{max} \cdot d
$$

$$
v_{impact}^{2} = 900 - 2 \times 7.848 \times 50
$$

$$
v_{impact}^{2} = 900 - 784.8 = 115.2 \, \text{(m/s)}^2
$$

$$
v_{impact} = \sqrt{115.2} \approx 10.73 \, \text{m/s} \approx 38.6 \, \text{km/h}
$$

### Step 8 — KTU Engineering Conclusion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** AEB reduces impact speed from **108 km/h to ≈ 38.6 km/h**, dramatically reducing injury severity. Survivable impact threshold is typically **< 50 km/h** for modern passenger cars — therefore AEB **converts a fatal crash into a survivable one**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CAN Bus Throughput & Maximum Message Count

**Problem (KTU Typical 7-mark):**
A CAN bus operates at **$f_{baud} = 500$ kbps**. Each message has **8 data bytes = 64 bits**, and uses **47 bits of overhead** (SOF, arbitration, CRC, ACK, IFS). Calculate the maximum number of such messages that can be transmitted per second **theoretically**, and the practical bus utilization if **25 messages** are scheduled with cycle time **$t_c = 20$ ms**.

### Step 1 — Compute Total Bits per Message

The total message length $L_{msg}$ in bits is:

$$
L_{msg} = L_{overhead} + L_{data}
$$

$$
L_{msg} = 47 + 64 = 111 \, \text{bits}
$$

### Step 2 — Theoretical Maximum Throughput

The number of messages per second is:

$$
N_{max} = \dfrac{f_{baud}}{L_{msg}}
$$

$$
N_{max} = \dfrac{500 \times 10^{3}}{111} \, \text{msg/s}
$$

$$
N_{max} \approx 4504.5 \, \text{msg/s}
$$

### Step 3 — Practical Message Load

Each scheduled message triggers every cycle $t_c = 20$ ms = **0.02 s**.

Total messages scheduled per second:

$$
N_{scheduled} = \dfrac{N_{signals}}{t_c} = \dfrac{25}{0.02} = 1250 \, \text{msg/s}
$$

### Step 4 — Total Bits Transmitted per Second

$$
N_{bits/sec} = N_{scheduled} \times L_{msg}
$$

$$
N_{bits/sec} = 1250 \times 111 = 138{,}750 \, \text{bits/s}
$$

### Step 5 — Bus Utilization

$$
U = \dfrac{N_{bits/sec}}{f_{baud}} \times 100\,\%
$$

$$
U = \dfrac{138{,}750}{500{,}000} \times 100\,\%
$$

$$
U = 27.75\,\%
$$

### Step 6 — Practical KTU Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Bus utilization is **27.75 %**, which is **well within the 70 % safe operating margin** recommended by CAN-in-Automation (CiA). This leaves 72.25 % bandwidth headroom for error retransmissions, diagnostic frames, and bus-off recovery events. **Bus is healthy.**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance

| Metric | Conventional Cruise Control | Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) | Cooperative ACC (C-ACC) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sensing** | None | Radar / Lidar (ego only) | Radar + V2X (ego + remote) |
| **Reaction Time** | Driver only | **~ 200 ms** | **~ 50 ms** (V2X) |
| **Time Headway** | N/A | 1.0 – 2.0 s | 0.6 – 1.0 s |
| **Throughput Gain** | 1.0× | ~ 1.3× | ~ 1.8× |
| **Communication** | None | CAN bus | CAN + Cellular / DSRC / C-V2X |
| **NCAP 2024 Reward** | None | Yes (AEB integration) | Yes (future) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Electronic Stability Control (ESC) Closed-Loop Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SA[Steering Angle Sensor] --> ECU1[ESC ECU Master Processor]
    YR[Yaw Rate Sensor MEMS] --> ECU1
    LA[Lateral Acceleration Sensor] --> ECU1
    WS1[Wheel Speed Sensor FL] --> ECU1
    WS2[Wheel Speed Sensor FR] --> ECU1
    WS3[Wheel Speed Sensor RL] --> ECU1
    WS4[Wheel Speed Sensor RR] --> ECU1
    BR[Brake Pressure Sensor] --> ECU1
    TH[Throttle Position Sensor] --> ECU1

    ECU1 --> COMP{Yaw Error Computation}
    COMP -->|Understeer| MODE1[Brake Inner Rear Wheel]
    COMP -->|Oversteer| MODE2[Brake Outer Front Wheel]
    COMP -->|Stable| NOOP[No Intervention]

    MODE1 --> HCU[Hydraulic Control Unit]
    MODE2 --> HCU
    NOOP --> HCU
    HCU --> WHL1[Wheel Cylinder FL]
    HCU --> WHL2[Wheel Cylinder FR]
    HCU --> WHL3[Wheel Cylinder RL]
    HCU --> WHL4[Wheel Cylinder RR]
    HCU --> ENG[Engine Torque Reduction via CAN]
    ENG --> ECU2[ECM Engine Control Module]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — In-Vehicle Network Topology (Modern Vehicle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWERTRAIN_DOMAIN[POWERTRAIN DOMAIN CAN 500 KBPS]
        ECM1[Engine ECU]
        TCM[Transmission ECU]
        BMS1[Battery Management ECU]
    end

    subgraph CHASSIS_DOMAIN[CHASSIS DOMAIN CAN FD 2 MBPS]
        ESC1[ESC ECU]
        EPS[Electric Power Steering ECU]
        ABS1[ABS ECU]
        AIRBAG[Airbag ECU]
    end

    subgraph BODY_DOMAIN[BODY DOMAIN LIN 19 KBPS]
        BCM[Body Control Module]
        MIRROR[Mirror ECU]
        SEAT[Seat ECU]
        LIGHTING[Lighting ECU]
    end

    subgraph ADAS_DOMAIN[ADAS DOMAIN FLEXRAY 10 MBPS]
        RADAR[Front Radar ECU]
        CAM[Front Camera ECU]
        ACC1[ACC ECU]
        LKA[Lane Keep Assist ECU]
    end

    subgraph INFOTAINMENT_DOMAIN[INFOTAINMENT DOMAIN ETHERNET 1 GBPS]
        IVI[Head Unit ECU]
    end

    CGW[Central Gateway ECU] --- POWERTRAIN_DOMAIN
    CGW --- CHASSIS_DOMAIN
    CGW --- BODY_DOMAIN
    CGW --- ADAS_DOMAIN
    CGW --- INFOTAINMENT_DOMAIN

    OBD[OBD II Diagnostic Port SAE J1962] --- CGW
    V2X[V2X Communication Module] --- CGW
```

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — ACC Following Mode Control Loop

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant RADAR as Front Radar ECU
    participant ACC as ACC Master ECU
    participant VCU as Vehicle Control Unit
    participant ECM as Engine ECU
    participant EPB as Electronic Brake
    participant DRV as Driver Display

    RADAR->>ACC: Target list relative distance and velocity
    ACC->>ACC: Compute time headway th
    ACC->>VCU: Request acceleration or deceleration
    VCU->>ECM: Torque command via CAN
    VCU->>EPB: Brake pressure command via CAN
    ECM-->>VCU: Torque feedback
    EPB-->>VCU: Pressure feedback
    VCU-->>ACC: Actual acceleration
    ACC->>DRV: Set gap indicator via CAN
    DRV-->>ACC: Driver override acknowledge
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — X-by-Wire System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DRV_IN[Driver Input Pedals and Steering] --> SENSOR[Redundant Sensor Bank]
    SENSOR --> ECU_XBW[X by Wire ECU Triple Modular Redundant]
    ECU_XBW --> ACT_STEER[Steering Motor Actuator]
    ECU_XBW --> ACT_BRAKE[Brake by Wire Actuator]
    ECU_XBW --> ACT_THROT[Throttle by Wire Actuator]
    ECU_XBW --> ACT_SHIFT[Shift by Wire Actuator]
    ACT_STEER --> WHEEL_FR[Front Wheels]
    ACT_BRAKE --> WHEEL_ALL[All Wheels Hydraulic]
    ACT_THROT --> ENGINE[Engine Throttle Body]
    ACT_SHIFT --> GEARBOX[Automatic Transmission]
    ECU_XBW -.CAN FD.-> DIAG[Diagnostic ECU]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Fallback Explanation (Physical Reality)**
> In x-by-wire systems, **no mechanical linkage** connects the driver's pedals/steering wheel to the actuators. Instead, sensors measure the driver's input, the ECU computes the desired action, and electric actuators (motors, hydraulic pumps) execute the command. This enables **fail-operational** design using **Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR)** — three independent channels vote on the correct output — mandatory under **ISO 26262 ASIL-D**.

---
