# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`
**Define Time Headway and state its typical values used in Adaptive Cruise Control calibration.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> **Time Headway ($t_h$)** is defined as the time taken by the rear of the lead vehicle to reach a point on the road after the front of the ego vehicle has passed the same point. Mathematically, $t_h = d / v_{ego}$, where $d$ is the bumper-to-bumper gap and $v_{ego}$ is the ego vehicle speed.
>
> Typical KTU standard values:
> - Aggressive: **$t_h$ = 1.0 s**
> - Comfort: **$t_h$ = 1.5 s**
> - Cautious: **$t_h$ = 2.0 s**
>
> (1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark values = 3 marks)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Differentiate between CAN, LIN, and FlexRay in terms of baud rate and application domain.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Protocol | Baud Rate | Application Domain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CAN** | 125 kbps – 1 Mbps | Powertrain, chassis (high-speed control) |
| **LIN** | 19.2 kbps | Body electronics — mirrors, seats, windows |
| **FlexRay** | up to 10 Mbps | X-by-wire, safety-critical chassis (deterministic) |

(1 mark differentiation logic + 1 mark baud rate mapping + 1 mark domain mapping = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model QP Module 4 Choice A]`
**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an Electronic Stability Control (ESC) system. How does it differentiate between understeer and oversteer conditions?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition and Purpose (1 mark)**
ESC is an active safety system that continuously compares the driver's intended direction (from steering angle) with the vehicle's actual response (from yaw rate and lateral acceleration). It selectively brakes individual wheels to keep the vehicle on the driver's intended path.

**Step 2 — Sensor Inputs (2 marks)**
- Steering Angle Sensor (SAS): measures intended yaw.
- Yaw Rate Sensor (YRS): measures actual yaw.
- Lateral Acceleration Sensor (LAS): measures cornering force.
- Four Wheel Speed Sensors (WSS): measure individual wheel slip.

**Step 3 — Understeer vs Oversteer Logic (2 marks)**

| Condition | Detection | ESC Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Understeer** | $r_{actual} < r_{intended}$ | Brake inner rear wheel → creates corrective yaw |
| **Oversteer** | $r_{actual} > r_{intended}$ | Brake outer front wheel → creates counter-yaw |

**Step 4 — Block Diagram (refer Section 4.1) (1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Engineering Conclusion (1 mark)**
ESC reduces single-vehicle crash fatalities by **~ 38 %** and is **mandatory** under EU GSR (General Safety Regulation) from **July 2024** for all new passenger cars (Regulation (EU) 2019/2144).

---

**(b) A vehicle equipped with Adaptive Cruise Control travels at 25 m/s. The radar detects a target vehicle 30 m ahead moving at 18 m/s. The road friction coefficient is 0.7. Compute (i) the time headway, (ii) the required deceleration to maintain headway, and (iii) whether the system can execute the maneuver safely. Comment on the result.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Time Headway (2 marks)**

$$
t_h = \dfrac{d}{v_{ego}} = \dfrac{30}{25} = 1.2 \, \text{s}
$$

This falls within the **comfort band (1.0–1.5 s)** — ACC intervention is appropriate.

**Step 2 — Required Deceleration (2 marks)**

$$
a_{req} = \dfrac{v_{ego}^{2} - v_{lead}^{2}}{2 \cdot d}
$$

$$
a_{req} = \dfrac{(25)^2 - (18)^2}{2 \times 30} = \dfrac{625 - 324}{60}
$$

$$
a_{req} = \dfrac{301}{60} \approx 5.017 \, \text{m/s}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Available Deceleration (1 mark)**

$$
a_{max} = \mu \cdot g = 0.7 \times 9.81 = 6.867 \, \text{m/s}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Safety Check (1 mark)**

$$
a_{req} = 5.017 \, \text{m/s}^2 < a_{max} = 6.867 \, \text{m/s}^2 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Comment (1 mark)**
The maneuver is **safe** with a safety margin of **~ 27 %**. ACC will execute a smooth brake intervention. The deceleration **~ 0.51 g** is within passenger comfort limits (< 0.4 g preferred), so the intervention may be perceived as slightly firm — calibration can extend headway to **1.5 s** for smoother response.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** — forgetting to convert km/h to m/s when $v_{ego}$ is given in km/h (1 mark lost frequently).
> 2. **Incorrect formula selection** — confusing $a_{req} = v^2/(2d)$ with $a_{req} = (v_1^2 - v_2^2)/(2d)$; the latter applies only when both speeds are non-zero.
> 3. **Not converting $\mu \cdot g$** — forgetting that the deceleration limit is governed by the **friction circle**, not arbitrary acceleration.
> 4. **Missing final comment** — KTU 2024 scheme awards 1 mark for engineering interpretation; numerical-only answers lose marks.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model QP Module 4 Choice B]`
**(a) Describe the architecture and message frame format of the CAN protocol. Explain the function of each field in a standard CAN 2.0A frame.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — CAN Overview (1 mark)**
CAN (Controller Area Network) is a **multi-master, broadcast, message-prioritized** serial bus standardized under **ISO 11898**. It uses **non-return-to-zero (NRZ)** encoding with **bit stuffing**.

**Step 2 — CAN 2.0A Standard Frame Format (Table) (4 marks)**

| Field | Bits | Function |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **SOF** (Start of Frame) | 1 | Marks beginning of message; dominant bit |
| **Arbitration** | 11 | ID + RTR; lower ID = higher priority |
| **Control** | 6 | IDE, r0, DLC (data length code, 0–8 bytes) |
| **Data** | 0–64 | Payload bytes (max 8 for classic CAN) |
| **CRC** | 15 | Cyclic redundancy check + delimiter |
| **ACK** | 2 | Acknowledgement by any receiving node |
| **EOF** | 7 | End of frame — 7 recessive bits |

**Step 3 — Bit Stuffing Rule (1 mark)**
After **5 consecutive identical bits**, a stuff bit of opposite polarity is inserted by the transmitter and removed by the receiver. This ensures synchronization.

**Step 4 — Arbitration Logic (1 mark)**
During arbitration, all nodes transmit simultaneously. A node transmitting a recessive bit but reading dominant loses arbitration and backs off, becoming a receiver for that frame.

---

**(b) A CAN bus operates at 250 kbps. 40 signals are scheduled at 10 ms cycle time. Each message has 50 bits overhead and 64 bits of data. Calculate (i) total bus load, and (ii) whether the bus can support an additional 10 messages with 80-bit length at 20 ms cycle time without exceeding the 70 % utilization limit.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Existing Message Load (2 marks)**

Bits per existing message:

$$
L_{msg,1} = 50 + 64 = 114 \, \text{bits}
$$

Messages per second:

$$
N_1 = \dfrac{40}{0.010} = 4000 \, \text{msg/s}
$$

Bits per second:

$$
B_1 = 4000 \times 114 = 456{,}000 \, \text{bits/s}
$$

Bus utilization:

$$
U_1 = \dfrac{456{,}000}{250{,}000} \times 100\,\% = 182.4\,\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The existing bus is **already saturated** ($U_1$ exceeds 100 %). Adding more messages is mathematically impossible.

**Step 2 — Verify Practical Interpretation (2 marks)**
A utilization of 182.4 % means that the requested 40 messages **cannot be scheduled** at 10 ms on a 250 kbps CAN bus. Practical correction: reduce message rate, shorten cycle, or migrate to **CAN FD (5 Mbps)** or **CAN XL (10 Mbps)**.

**Step 3 — Hypothetical Additional Load (1 mark)**

If the 10 extra messages (80 bits, 20 ms cycle) were added on top:

$$
L_{msg,2} = 80 \, \text{bits}, \quad N_2 = \dfrac{10}{0.020} = 500 \, \text{msg/s}
$$

$$
B_2 = 500 \times 80 = 40{,}000 \, \text{bits/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Combined Hypothetical Utilization (1 mark)**

$$
U_{total} = \dfrac{456{,}000 + 40{,}000}{250{,}000} \times 100\,\% = 198.4\,\%
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Recommendation (1 mark)**
> [!NOTE]
> Migrate to **CAN FD** at **2 Mbps** → utilization drops to ~ 24.8 %, leaving ample headroom. Alternatively, apply **bus offloading** by routing low-priority body messages to a **LIN sub-network** behind a gateway ECU.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Cycle-time inversion** — students often divide cycle time into number of messages instead of the reverse. The correct relation is: **Messages/sec = Number of signals ÷ Cycle time.**
> 2. **Forgetting overhead** — overhead bits (SOF, arbitration, CRC, ACK, EOF, interframe space, stuff bits) are **mandatory** in CAN. Counting only the 64 data bytes underestimates utilization.
> 3. **Neglecting 70 % rule** — KTU expects the student to **interpret** the result, not just compute. Always state whether the bus is healthy, marginal, or overloaded.
> 4. **No engineering recommendation** — KTU 2024 scheme rewards **interpretive design thinking** (1 mark reserved).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Vehicle Control & Network Systems**

### Vehicle Control Systems
- ☐ **ESC** is the single most effective active safety system; mandatory under EU GSR 2019/2144 (effective July 2024).
- ☐ **Understeer** → brake **inner rear**; **Oversteer** → brake **outer front**.
- ☐ **ACC** uses **radar (76–77 GHz)** + **mono camera fusion** for target tracking.
- ☐ **AEB** reduces fatal crash risk by **~ 38 %** (Euro NCAP data).
- ☐ **Time Headway** typical values: 1.0 s (aggressive), 1.5 s (normal), 2.0 s (cautious).
- ☐ **X-by-wire** systems use **Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR)** to achieve ASIL-D under ISO 26262.

### In-Vehicle Networks
- ☐ **CAN** dominates powertrain/chassis; ISO 11898; 125 kbps–1 Mbps; invented by Bosch 1986.
- ☐ **CAN FD** boosts data field to 64 bytes and baud rate to **5 Mbps** — key 2024 trend.
- ☐ **LIN** is the cheapest sub-bus for body electronics; **19.2 kbps**; single-master.
- ☐ **FlexRay** supports **deterministic TDMA** scheduling for x-by-wire; **10 Mbps**.
- ☐ **Automotive Ethernet (IEEE 100/1000BASE-T1)** is the backbone for **ADAS and V2X** — up to **1 Gbps**.
- ☐ **Bus utilization rule**: **U < 70 %** = healthy, **70–80 %** = marginal, **> 80 %** = overload risk.
- ☐ **Central Gateway ECU** interconnects all in-vehicle sub-networks and provides firewalling.

### Key Numerical Benchmarks (Memorize)
- ☐ Standard wheelbase L = **2.5–3.0 m**
- ☐ Friction coefficient: dry asphalt μ ≈ **0.8–1.0**, wet ≈ **0.5–0.7**, snow ≈ **0.2**
- ☐ Maximum comfortable deceleration for ACC = **0.3 g ≈ 2.94 m/s²**
- ☐ Emergency AEB deceleration = **0.8–1.0 g**
- ☐ CAN bit stuffing overhead maximum ≈ **20 %**
- ☐ Maximum classic CAN payload = **8 bytes = 64 bits**
- ☐ V2X reaction time improvement: **~ 200 ms (radar) → ~ 50 ms (V2X)**

### KTU Frequently Tested Formulae
- ☐ $t_h = d / v_{ego}$
- ☐ $a_{req} = (v_1^2 - v_2^2) / (2d)$
- ☐ $a_{max} = \mu \cdot g$
- ☐ $U = (N_{msg} \cdot L_{msg}) / (f_{baud} \cdot t_{cycle})$
- ☐ $d_{stop} = v^2 / (2a)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always end numerical answers with an **engineering interpretation sentence**. KTU 2024 examiners allocate **15–20 %** of marks for **application-level reasoning**, not just arithmetic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security measures and practices in cybersecurity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT743 - VEHICLE SAFETY AND SECURITY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security measures and practices in cybersecurity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cybersecurity in Vehicles (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> Cybersecurity in the automotive domain refers to the disciplined application of **security measures, controls, processes, and best practices** designed to protect vehicular electronic systems, in-vehicle networks (CAN, LIN, Ethernet, FlexRay), V2X communication channels, telematics units, ECUs (Electronic Control Units), and connected services against unauthorized access, modification, destruction, or disclosure of information assets.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme curriculum for **PEMUT743**, "Security measures and practices" specifically refers to the **layered, defense-in-depth methodology** combining:

- **Preventive Controls** (Firewalls, Encryption, Authentication)
- **Detective Controls** (Intrusion Detection Systems / IDS, Logging, SIEM)
- **Corrective Controls** (Patch Management, Incident Response, Recovery)
- **Governance Practices** (Policies, Standards, Risk Management, Compliance)

These map directly to KTU Course Outcome **CO4**: *"Apply cybersecurity principles to safeguard automotive embedded systems, communication buses, and connected vehicle infrastructure."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Modern Bank Vault vs. A Connected Car**
> 
> Imagine a connected car is a **bank on wheels**. It carries:
> - **Valuable assets** → Driver/passenger personal data, location history, financial payment credentials
> - **Vault rooms** → ECUs, infotainment, ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems)
> - **Vault corridors** → CAN bus, Ethernet backbone
> - **External tellers** → Cloud services, mobile apps, V2X peers
> 
> **Cybersecurity measures** are the **guards, cameras, alarms, locks, and access protocols** that protect the vault, monitor who enters, and respond when something is wrong. Just as a bank doesn't rely on a single lock, an automotive system uses **Defense in Depth** — multiple overlapping security layers.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Cybersecurity — The **CIA Triad**

The cornerstone of all security measures is the preservation of three properties, often called the **CIA Triad**:

| Pillar | Definition | Automotive Example |
|--------|------------|--------------------|
| **Confidentiality** | Ensuring data is accessible only to authorized entities | Encrypting CAN messages so an attacker cannot read throttle/brake commands |
| **Integrity** | Ensuring data is not altered or tampered with in transit/storage | Using message authentication codes (MAC) on ECU firmware updates |
| **Availability** | Ensuring systems and data are accessible when needed | Protecting brake-by-wire ECUs from DoS attacks that could disable braking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Every cybersecurity measure in vehicles must be evaluated against the **CIA Triad**. A security control is incomplete if it fails any one of these three properties.

---

## 1.4 Additional Core Properties in Modern Vehicle Cybersecurity

> [!NOTE]
> Beyond CIA, modern automotive security (aligned with **ISO/SAE 21434**) emphasizes:

- **Authenticity** — Verifying the sender is genuine (e.g., legitimate ECU vs. rogue node).
- **Non-repudiation** — A sender cannot deny sending a message (digital signatures).
- **Accountability** — All actions are traceable to an entity (audit logs, secure boot logs).
- **Privacy** — Personal/sensitive data is handled per regulations (e.g., GDPR for EU drivers).
- **Safety-Security Co-engineering** — Security failures must not compromise functional safety (ISO 26262).

---

## 1.5 Classification of Security Measures

Security measures are typically classified into three broad categories, which form the basis of the **NIST Cybersecurity Framework** and are directly applicable to automotive systems per **UN R155** regulation:

### 1.5.1 Preventive (Proactive) Measures
> **Goal:** Stop attacks **before** they occur.
- **Examples in vehicles:** Firewalls on OBD-II port, secure boot, code signing, encryption, access control lists (ACLs), security gateways, network segmentation, multi-factor authentication for telematics.

### 1.5.2 Detective (Monitoring) Measures
> **Goal:** Identify attacks **in progress** or after the fact.
- **Examples in vehicles:** CAN Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS), anomaly detection, Security Information and Event Management (SIEM), audit logging, file integrity monitoring (FIM), honeypots in R&D environments.

### 1.5.3 Corrective (Reactive) Measures
> **Goal:** Restore systems and prevent recurrence after an incident.
- **Examples in vehicles:** Over-the-Air (OTA) firmware patching, incident response playbooks, root cause analysis, revocation of compromised keys, fail-operational fallback modes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often list "antivirus" as the only cybersecurity measure. In vehicles, the protective architecture is **multi-layered** — there is **no single silver bullet**.

---

## 1.6 Visual Summary of the Cybersecurity Framework

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│        CYBERSECURITY MEASURES & PRACTICES                │
│            (VEHICLE DOMAIN)                             │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  GOVERNANCE: ISO/SAE 21434, UN R155, Policies            │
│  RISK MGMT: TARA (Threat Analysis & Risk Assessment)     │
│  CONTROLS:  Preventive | Detective | Corrective         │
│  CIA TRIAD: Confidentiality | Integrity | Availability   │
│  PRACTICES: Patch Mgmt, Incident Response, Awareness     │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Security Model — Defense in Depth

Defense in depth is the **principal architectural philosophy** for vehicle cybersecurity. It assumes that any single control can fail, so multiple overlapping layers are deployed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):**
> *Defense in depth* is a multi-layered security strategy in which a series of security mechanisms are overlapped so that the failure of one control does not compromise the entire system. In vehicles, this means security at the **perimeter, network, ECU, application, and data** levels.

### The 7-Layer Automotive Defense Model

| Layer # | Layer Name | Typical Controls |
|---------|-----------|------------------|
| **L1** | **Perimeter / External Interface** | Telematics firewall, OBD-II lock, V2X message authentication, TPM |
| **L2** | **In-Vehicle Network** | CAN/LIN/Ethernet segmentation, gateways, network IDS |
| **L3** | **ECU Hardware** | Secure boot, hardware root of trust, HSM (Hardware Security Module), JTAG lock |
| **L4** | **Firmware / OS** | Code signing, secure OS (AUTOSAR), memory protection |
| **L5** | **Application** | Input validation, secure coding, sandboxing, least privilege |
| **L6** | **Data** | Encryption (AES-128/256), key management, secure storage |
| **L7** | **Governance / Process** | TARA, SBOM, incident response, training, compliance audits |

---

## 2.2 Cryptographic Foundations

Cryptography is the mathematical backbone of nearly all preventive and detective measures.

### 2.2.1 Symmetric Encryption
- **One shared secret key** for both encryption and decryption.
- **Algorithm examples:** AES-128, AES-256, ChaCha20.
- **Use in vehicles:** Bulk ECU-to-ECU data encryption, secure storage.

### 2.2.2 Asymmetric Encryption
- A **key pair**: a public key (shared openly) and a private key (kept secret).
- **Algorithm examples:** RSA-2048, ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography) — used in V2X PKI.
- **Use in vehicles:** V2X certificate exchange, firmware signing, TLS handshakes.

### 2.2.3 Hashing
- A **one-way function** that produces a fixed-size output (digest).
- **Algorithms:** SHA-256, SHA-3.
- **Use in vehicles:** Firmware integrity verification, password storage.

### 2.2.4 Message Authentication Code (MAC) / Digital Signature
- Provides both **integrity** and **authenticity**.
- **MAC algorithms:** HMAC-SHA256, CMAC (used in AUTOSAR SecOC).
- **Signatures:** ECDSA (used in V2X).

---

## 2.3 Authentication, Authorization, and Access Control

The **AAA Framework** (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting) is fundamental.

### 2.3.1 Authentication Methods in Vehicles
| Method | Description | Use Case |
|--------|-------------|----------|
| **Passwords / PINs** | Shared secret | Infotainment parental lock |
| **Challenge-Response** | ECU proves knowledge of a key without sending it | Immobilizer systems |
| **Digital Certificates** | X.509 certificates bound to a device | V2X, TLS for backend |
| **Hardware Tokens** | TPM, HSM, Secure Element | Key storage in ECUs |
| **Biometrics** | Fingerprint, face | Personalized driver settings |

### 2.3.2 Access Control Models
- **DAC (Discretionary Access Control):** Owner decides.
- **MAC (Mandatory Access Control):** System-enforced labels.
- **RBAC (Role-Based Access Control):** Permissions tied to roles (e.g., *diagnostic technician* vs. *driver*).
- **ABAC (Attribute-Based):** Decisions based on attributes (time, location, device health).

> [!NOTE]
> **AUTOSAR Classic & Adaptive** use **RBAC** extensively through the **Identity and Access Management (IAM)** module.

---

## 2.4 Network Security in Vehicles

| Bus / Network | Security Measures Applied |
|---------------|---------------------------|
| **CAN (Classical)** | SecOC (Secure Onboard Communication), CAN IDS, gateway filtering |
| **CAN FD** | Same as CAN + larger payload for MAC |
| **Automotive Ethernet** | MACsec (IEEE 802.1AE), TLS over DoIP (Diagnostics over IP) |
| **LIN** | Limited; relies on gateway isolation |
| **FlexRay** | Bus guardian, CRC, gateway enforcement |
| **V2X (C-V2X / DSRC)** | PKI-based digital signatures, certificate authority (CA), pseudonym certificates |

---

## 2.5 Risk Management — TARA (Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TARA is mandatory** under **ISO/SAE 21434** and is a high-yield KTU 2024 concept.

**TARA Steps:**
1. **Asset Identification** — What needs protection? (e.g., braking ECU, GPS data)
2. **Threat Scenario Identification** — What could go wrong? (e.g., replay attack on V2X)
3. **Impact Rating** — What is the damage? (Safety, Financial, Operational, Privacy — **S / F / O / P**)
4. **Attack Path Analysis** — How feasible is the attack? (Knowledge, Window, Equipment, Expertise — **KWE-E**)
5. **Risk Value Calculation** — Combine impact & feasibility.
6. **Treatment Decision** — Avoid, Reduce, Transfer, or Accept.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Confidentiality** | $C = E_k(M)$ | $C$ = ciphertext, $E$ = encryption function, $k$ = key, $M$ = plaintext | Achieved via symmetric or asymmetric encryption |
| 2 | **Decryption (Symmetric)** | $M = D_k(C)$ | $D$ = decryption function | Same key $k$ for encrypt/decrypt |
| 3 | **RSA Key Relation** | $n = p \cdot q$ | $p, q$ = large primes, $n$ = modulus | Foundation of RSA security |
| 4 | **Hash Function** | $h = H(M)$ | $h$ = digest (fixed size), $M$ = message | $H$ is one-way |
| 5 | **HMAC** | $HMAC(k,m) = H((k \oplus opad) \parallel H((k \oplus ipad) \parallel m))$ | $k$ = key, $m$ = message, $ipad/opad$ = constants | Integrity + Authenticity |
| 6 | **Digital Signature** | $\sigma = Sign_{priv}(H(M))$ ; Verify with $pub$ | $H$ = hash, $priv/pub$ = key pair | Provides non-repudiation |
| 7 | **Risk Value (Qualitative)** | $R = f(Impact, Likelihood)$ | $R$ = risk, dimensionless score | Often Impact $\times$ Likelihood on 1–5 scale |
| 8 | **TARA Impact Factors** | $I = \{S, F, O, P\}$ | $S$ = Safety, $F$ = Financial, $O$ = Operational, $P$ = Privacy | Each rated 1–5 |
| 9 | **TARA Attack Feasibility** | $AF = f(K, W, E, E')$ | $K$ = Knowledge, $W$ = Window, $E$ = Equipment, $E'$ = Expertise | ISO/SAE 21434 method |
| 10 | **Entropy of Key** | $H = \log_2(K)$ bits | $K$ = keyspace size | AES-256 $\Rightarrow$ $H = 256$ bits |
| 11 | **Brute Force Time (Worst Case)** | $T = \dfrac{2^n}{2 \cdot R}$ | $n$ = key bits, $R$ = tries/sec | Halved due to avg. case |
| 12 | **Password Entropy** | $H = L \cdot \log_2(C)$ | $L$ = length, $C$ = charset size | Used in password strength |
| 13 | **CAN Message Auth (SecOC)** | $MAC = CMAC_k(Payload \parallel Counter)$ | $k$ = fresh key, Counter = freshness value | Counters prevent replay |
| 14 | **Secure Boot Verification** | $Valid = (Sig_{pub}(Hash(FW)) == Stored)$ | $Sig$ = signature, $Hash$ = hash function | Boolean verification |
| 15 | **Availability (MTTF Metric)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures, $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair | System availability metric |

> [!WARNING]
> **Formatting Note:** All vertical bars `|` in this table have been replaced with `\mid` or removed to prevent markdown rendering issues.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

These measures are not theoretical — they are mandated and operational:

- **UNECE WP.29 R155** — Cybersecurity Management System (CSMS) certification **mandatory** for all new vehicle type approvals in EU from July 2022.
- **ISO/SAE 21434** — International standard for automotive cybersecurity engineering.
- **AUTOSAR SecOC** — Standardizes secure communication between ECUs.
- **NHTSA Cybersecurity Best Practices** — US guideline for OEMs and Tier-1 suppliers.
- **NIST CSF (Cybersecurity Framework)** — Adopted widely for vehicle backend/cloud systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always link your answers to **ISO/SAE 21434** or **UN R155** to demonstrate industry awareness. Examiners reward this.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Brute-Force Attack Resistance of an Encryption Key

This derivation shows **why key length matters** in vehicle cryptography (e.g., why AES-128 vs. AES-256 vs. 4-digit immobilizer PIN).

### Given:
- A key of length $n$ bits.
- An attacker tries $R$ keys per second.
- Assume worst-case: the correct key is the last one tried (or on average, half the keyspace is tried).

### To Derive:
- The **average time to break** the key by brute force.

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1:** Total number of possible keys in a keyspace of $n$ bits:
$$ N = 2^n $$

**Step 2:** In the **worst case**, the attacker must try all $N$ keys.
$$ T_{worst} = \frac{2^n}{R} $$

**Step 3:** In the **average case**, only half the keyspace is explored before success:
$$ T_{avg} = \frac{2^n}{2R} = \frac{2^{n-1}}{R} $$

**Step 4:** Convert to days, years, etc., depending on the unit of $R$:

$$ T_{years} = \frac{2^{n-1}}{R \times 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600} $$

### Example (KTU Numerical):

> An immobilizer uses a 4-digit PIN (each digit 0–9). An attacker can try 1000 PINs/sec. How long to break it on average?

**Step 1:** $n = 4$ digits, but the keyspace is $10^4 = 10{,}000$ possibilities.

$$ T_{avg} = \frac{10{,}000}{2 \times 1000} = 5 \text{ seconds} $$

> [!WARNING]
> This shows why **PIN-based immobilizers are weak** — modern immobilizers use **cryptographic challenge-response** with 128-bit keys.

### Example 2 (AES-128):

$$ T_{avg} = \frac{2^{127}}{R} $$

If $R = 10^{12}$ keys/sec (a powerful attacker):
$$ T_{avg} \approx \frac{1.7 \times 10^{38}}{10^{12}} = 1.7 \times 10^{26} \text{ seconds} \approx 5.4 \times 10^{18} \text{ years} $$

> This exceeds the age of the universe ($\approx 1.4 \times 10^{10}$ years), demonstrating **why AES-128 is computationally secure**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Password Entropy Calculation

Passwords are used in vehicle infotainment, telematics portals, and diagnostic tools. **Entropy** quantifies their strength.

### Formula:
$$ H = L \cdot \log_2(C) $$

Where:
- $L$ = password length
- $C$ = size of character set
- $H$ = entropy in bits

### Example:
- Password: `"V3hiclE!"` → $L = 9$ characters
- Charset: lowercase (26) + uppercase (26) + digits (10) + symbols (e.g., 10) = $C = 72$

$$ H = 9 \cdot \log_2(72) = 9 \times 6.17 = 55.5 \text{ bits} $$

> **Interpretation:** A 55-bit entropy password is **strong** (resistant to $\sim 2^{55}$ guesses, $\sim 3.6 \times 10^{16}$ tries).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: TARA Risk Score (Qualitative, KTU Style)

The TARA risk value combines **Impact** and **Attack Feasibility** per ISO/SAE 21434.

### Step 1: Impact Rating (sum of S, F, O, P scores)
Each factor is rated 1 (negligible) to 5 (catastrophic):
$$ I_{total} = S + F + O + P $$

### Step 2: Attack Feasibility
Calculated from the four $KWEE$ factors, each rated 1 (low) to 5 (high):
$$ AF = \frac{K + W + E + E'}{4} \text{ (or use ISO 21434 lookup table)} $$

### Step 3: Risk Matrix Mapping
Using a 5x5 risk matrix, locate the cell $(I_{total}, AF)$ to determine the **risk level**:

| Impact \ Feasibility | 1 (Low) | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 (High) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **20–25 (Severe)** | M | H | H | C | C |
| **15–19** | M | M | H | H | C |
| **10–14** | L | M | M | H | H |
| **5–9** | L | L | M | M | H |
| **1–4 (Negligible)** | L | L | L | M | M |

> Legend: **L** = Low, **M** = Medium, **H** = High, **C** = Critical

### Example:
A threat has $S=4, F=3, O=4, P=2 \Rightarrow I_{total} = 13$.
Attack feasibility $AF = 4$.
From the matrix: Risk = **H (High)** → risk treatment is required.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Security Controls by Attack Type

| Attack Type | CIA Property Violated | Preventive Control | Detective Control | Corrective Control |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Eavesdropping on CAN** | Confidentiality | Bus encryption (SecOC) | IDS anomaly detection | Re-key, segmentation |
| **Replay Attack on V2X** | Integrity / Authenticity | Freshness counters, signed messages | Duplicate detection | Certificate revocation |
| **Brute-Force Immobilizer** | Confidentiality | Cryptographic challenge-response | Failed-attempt logging | Account lockout, key reissue |
| **Malicious OTA Firmware** | Integrity / Authenticity | Code signing with HSM | Failed signature alert | Roll back to trusted image |
| **DoS on Brake ECU** | Availability | QoS, bus prioritization | Bus load monitoring | Fail-safe degraded mode |
| **OBD-II Injection** | All three | Gateway with firewall | OBD port monitoring | Disconnect, re-flash |
| **Rogue ECU on CAN** | Authenticity | ECU authentication | IDS frequency anomaly | ECU revocation |

---

## 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Numerical — Key Strength Comparison

**Question:** Compare the strength of a 56-bit DES key vs. a 128-bit AES key, assuming $R = 10^9$ tries/sec.

### Solution:

**DES (56-bit):**
$$ T_{avg} = \frac{2^{55}}{10^9} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{16}}{10^9} = 3.6 \times 10^{7} \text{ seconds} \approx 1.14 \text{ years} $$

**AES-128 (128-bit):**
$$ T_{avg} = \frac{2^{127}}{10^9} = \frac{1.7 \times 10^{38}}{10^9} = 1.7 \times 10^{29} \text{ seconds} \approx 5.4 \times 10^{21} \text{ years} $$

> **Conclusion:** AES-128 is $\approx 4.7 \times 10^{21}$ times stronger than DES in this scenario. This is why **DES is retired in automotive** and **AES is the standard**.

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Availability Metric (MTTF/MTBF)

For systems requiring high uptime (e.g., brake ECUs), availability is critical:

$$ A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} $$

### Example:
- $MTBF = 100{,}000$ hours
- $MTTR = 10$ hours
$$ A = \frac{100{,}000}{100{,}000 + 10} = 0.9999 = 99.99\% $$

This is the "**four nines**" availability — common requirement for safety-critical ECUs.

## 4.1 Defense-in-Depth Architecture for a Connected Vehicle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[External Threat Actors] --> node2[Perimeter Layer L1: Telematics Firewall and OBD Lock]
    node2 --> node3[Network Layer L2: CAN Gateway and IDS]

    node4 --> node5[Firmware OS L4: AUTOSAR SecOC and Code Signing]

    node8 -.Monitors.-> node4
    node9[Cloud Backend] --> node2
    node10[V2X Peers] --> node2
    node11[Mobile App] --> node2
```

**Interpretation:**
- Layers **L1–L7** are enforced simultaneously.
- Governance layer (L7) feeds policies back into every other layer.
- The architecture assumes any single layer can fail.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Secure Boot Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Vehicle Ignition ON] --> node2[Boot ROM Executes]
    node2 --> node3[Read Public Key from OTP]
    node3 --> node4[Verify First Stage Bootloader Signature]
    node4 --> node5[Load First Stage Bootloader]
    node5 --> node6[Verify Second Stage Bootloader Signature]
    node6 --> node7[Load Second Stage Bootloader]
    node7 --> node8[Verify OS and Application Signatures]
    node8 --> node9[Load OS and Applications]
    node9 --> node10[System Ready]
    node4 -- Verification Fails --> node11[Halt and Enter Safe Mode]
    node6 -- Verification Fails --> node11
    node8 -- Verification Fails --> node11
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation:** Each stage uses a **chain of trust** rooted in the immutable public key in **One-Time Programmable (OTP)** memory. This is a hardware root of trust.

---

## 4.5 CAN Bus SecOC Authentication Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ECUSender as Sender ECU
    participant Bus as CAN Bus
    participant ECUReceiver as Receiver ECU
    ECUSender->>ECUSender: Compute MAC = CMAC(Key, Payload + Freshness Counter)
    ECUSender->>Bus: Transmit Data + MAC + Counter
    Bus->>ECUReceiver: Forward Message
    ECUReceiver->>ECUReceiver: Recompute MAC with same Key and Counter
    ECUReceiver->>ECUReceiver: Compare MACs
    alt MACs Match
        ECUReceiver->>ECUReceiver: Accept Message
    else MAC Mismatch
        ECUReceiver->>ECUReceiver: Reject and Log IDS Event
    end
```

    node6 --> node7{Valid Certificate}

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Define the CIA Triad. Briefly explain how Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability are ensured in an automotive CAN bus.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model of cybersecurity comprising three properties:
- **Confidentiality** — Ensuring data is accessible only to authorized parties.
- **Integrity** — Ensuring data is not modified or tampered with during transit or storage.
- **Availability** — Ensuring systems and data remain accessible when required.

In a **CAN bus**:
- **Confidentiality** is limited on classical CAN (no native encryption), but is enhanced by **message encryption** or **secure gateways** to prevent eavesdropping.
- **Integrity** is ensured through **CRC checks**, **SecOC (Secure Onboard Communication)** with Message Authentication Codes (MAC), and **freshness counters** to prevent replay attacks.
- **Availability** is maintained via **bus arbitration priority** (e.g., braking messages get highest priority), **bus guardians**, and **redundant ECUs** to prevent DoS conditions.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
**List and briefly explain the three categories of security measures (Preventive, Detective, Corrective) with one automotive example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Preventive Measures** — Aim to *stop* attacks before they occur. *Automotive Example:* **Secure Boot with HSM** in ECUs prevents unauthorized firmware from executing.
2. **Detective Measures** — Aim to *identify* attacks during or after the fact. *Automotive Example:* **CAN Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** flags abnormal message frequencies indicating an injection attack.
3. **Corrective Measures** — Aim to *restore* and *harden* systems post-incident. *Automotive Example:* **Over-the-Air (OTA) firmware update** to patch a discovered vulnerability and revoke compromised keys.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Part B questions have **internal choice**. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are independent options.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Comprehensive Module 4 Style*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

> **Q. A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Defense-in-Depth model** for a connected vehicle. List **at least four layers** with their respective security controls. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. A (b)** Explain the **TARA (Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment)** methodology as per **ISO/SAE 21434**. List the steps and explain how Impact and Attack Feasibility are computed. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Q. A (a):**

The **Defense-in-Depth model** is a layered security architecture that assumes no single control is sufficient. It deploys overlapping security mechanisms at multiple levels so that a failure in one layer does not compromise the entire vehicle.

**Block Diagram:**
```
        ┌──────────────────────────┐
        │  L7: Governance (ISO 21434)│
        └────────────┬─────────────┘
                     │ (Policies)
        ┌────────────▼─────────────┐
        │  L1: Perimeter Firewall   │
        └────────────┬─────────────┘
                     │
        ┌────────────▼─────────────┐
        │  L2: Network Gateway+IDS  │
        └────────────┬─────────────┘
                     │
        ┌────────────▼─────────────┐
        │  L3: ECU (HSM, Secure Boot)│
        └────────────┬─────────────┘
                     │
        ┌────────────▼─────────────┐
        │  L4: Firmware (SecOC)     │
        └────────────┬─────────────┘
                     │
        ┌────────────▼─────────────┐
        │  L5: Application (RBAC)   │
        └────────────┬─────────────┘
                     │
        ┌────────────▼─────────────┐
        │  L6: Data (AES, Secure Storage)│
        └───────────────────────────┘
```

**Four Layers Explained:**
1. **Perimeter (L1):** Telematics control unit (TCU) firewall, OBD-II port authentication, V2X PKI verification.
2. **Network (L2):** Central gateway that filters/routers CAN, CAN-FD, and Ethernet traffic; IDS for anomaly detection.
3. **ECU Hardware (L3):** Hardware Security Module (HSM) for key storage, secure boot to verify firmware signatures.
4. **Application (L5):** Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) for diagnostic functions, sandboxed execution of third-party apps.

> **Conclusion:** Defense in depth ensures the attack surface is minimized, and an attacker must defeat **multiple** independent controls to compromise the vehicle.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q. A (b):**

**TARA (Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment)** is a structured methodology defined in **ISO/SAE 21434** to identify, classify, and treat cybersecurity risks in automotive systems.

**Steps of TARA:**

1. **Asset Identification:** Enumerate all items of value (e.g., braking ECU, GPS data, immobilizer, V2X credentials).
2. **Threat Scenario Identification:** Define what could go wrong (e.g., "*An attacker injects rogue messages on the CAN bus to spoof wheel speed*").
3. **Impact Rating:** For each scenario, assess the damage across four factors:
   - **S — Safety** (injury/death)
   - **F — Financial** (cost)
   - **O — Operational** (vehicle function loss)
   - **P — Privacy** (data leakage)
   Each rated 1 (negligible) to 5 (severe), then summed: $I_{total} = S + F + O + P$.

4. **Attack Feasibility:** Calculated using four parameters:
   - **K — Knowledge** required
   - **W — Window of opportunity**
   - **E — Equipment needed**
   - **E' — Expertise required**
   Each rated 1 to 5 and combined (often averaged) to give an overall feasibility score.

5. **Risk Determination:** Map the $(I_{total}, Feasibility)$ pair onto a 5×5 risk matrix to determine the level: **Low, Medium, High, or Critical**.

6. **Risk Treatment:** Based on the level:
   - **Avoid** — Eliminate the risk by removing the asset/feature.
   - **Reduce** — Apply security controls to lower risk.
   - **Transfer** — E.g., insurance or shared liability.
   - **Accept** — For low risks, document and accept.

> **Engineering Utility:** TARA is a **mandatory** input for cybersecurity concept development, required by **UN R155** for type approval in the EU and several other markets.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice for the Same Slot*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

> **Q. B (a)** Explain the **CIA Triad** in detail. With examples, show how each property is implemented in a connected vehicle's cybersecurity architecture. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. B (b)** Describe the working of **Secure Boot** and **SecOC (Secure Onboard Communication)** in automotive ECUs. Why are both required? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Q. B (a):**

The **CIA Triad** is the cornerstone of information security:

1. **Confidentiality** — Protecting data from unauthorized disclosure.
   - *Automotive Example:* **AES-128 encryption** of V2X messages ensures an eavesdropper cannot read a vehicle's location or speed.

2. **Integrity** — Ensuring data is not altered during transit.
   - *Automotive Example:* **SHA-256 hash** of firmware compared against a stored reference at boot, ensuring the binary has not been tampered with.

3. **Availability** — Ensuring services are accessible when required.
   - *Automotive Example:* **Bus priority and arbitration** in CAN ensures brake messages always get through, even under heavy bus load, preventing DoS-like conditions on critical ECUs.

4. **Supporting Properties (for full marks):** *Authenticity* (digital signatures verify the ECU is genuine), *Non-repudiation* (signatures prevent a sender from denying action), *Accountability* (audit logs trace all actions).

> **Architecture Mapping:** Modern vehicles implement all three via combined use of **Secure Boot + SecOC + Encryption + Firewalls + IDS**.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q. B (b):**

**Secure Boot** is a hardware-rooted process that ensures only **authentic, signed firmware** runs on an ECU.

**Working:**
1. The **Boot ROM** (immutable) executes first on power-up.
2. It holds a **public key** burned into OTP memory.
3. The Boot ROM verifies the **digital signature** of the first-stage bootloader.
4. If valid, the bootloader is executed; if not, the ECU enters a **safe/recovery mode**.
5. The chain of trust extends to verify the **OS and applications**, each signed by the OEM.
6. The chain terminates at the **Hardware Security Module (HSM)**, which stores root keys.

**SecOC (Secure Onboard Communication):** A module in **AUTOSAR** that adds **authentication and freshness** to messages on in-vehicle networks (especially CAN).

**Working:**
- The sender computes: $MAC = CMAC_k(Payload \parallel FreshnessCounter)$.
- The MAC and counter are appended to the data frame.
- The receiver recomputes the MAC with the **same shared key and counter** and compares.
- **Freshness counters** prevent **replay attacks** (where an old valid message is resent).

**Why Both Are Required:**
- **Secure Boot** protects the *ECU itself* from malicious firmware (system-level security).
- **SecOC** protects *inter-ECU communication* from tampering and spoofing (network-level security).
- Together, they establish a **rooted chain of trust** and **authenticated communication**, forming a comprehensive security posture aligned with **ISO/SAE 21434** and **UN R155**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Module 4 questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Security" with "Safety":** Security is about malicious intent; Safety is about accidental failure. Both must coexist (ISO 21434 + ISO 26262). Examiners *deduct marks* if you mix these.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention standards:** Always cite **ISO/SAE 21434, UN R155, AUTOSAR SecOC**. Generic answers without standards are penalized.
> 3. **Treating CIA as just three buzzwords:** Each must have a *concrete automotive example*. Marks are awarded for application, not just definition.
> 4. **Skipping the "Why":** Don't just list preventive/detective/corrective measures — explain *why* they belong in a vehicle, not just in IT.
> 5. **Ignoring TARA steps:** TARA questions are common. Missing any of the 6 steps costs partial credit.
> 6. **Not including block diagrams:** KTU examiners **expect a diagram** for any 7+ mark question. Without it, expect a 1–2 mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets:**

- **CIA Triad** = **Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability** (cornerstone of all security).
- **Defense in Depth** = multiple overlapping layers (L1 Perimeter → L7 Governance).
- **3 Categories of Controls** = **Preventive, Detective, Corrective**.
- **Preventive controls** include: Encryption, Authentication, Firewalls, Secure Boot, Code Signing.
- **Detective controls** include: IDS (CAN), SIEM, Anomaly Detection, Audit Logs.
- **Corrective controls** include: OTA Patching, Incident Response, Key Revocation, Rollback.
- **Key standards to memorize:**
  - **ISO/SAE 21434** — Cybersecurity engineering process
  - **UN R155** — CSMS certification (mandatory in EU)
  - **ISO 26262** — Functional safety (co-engineered with security)
  - **AUTOSAR SecOC** — Secure onboard communication protocol
- **TARA** has **6 steps**: Asset → Threat → Impact → Feasibility → Risk → Treatment.
- **Impact factors** = **S, F, O, P** (Safety, Financial, Operational, Privacy).
- **Feasibility factors** = **K, W, E, E'** (Knowledge, Window, Equipment, Expertise).
- **Risk = function of Impact × Feasibility.**
- **Brute force time** $\approx \frac{2^{n-1}}{R}$ seconds (average case).
- **AES-128** is the **de facto standard** for in-vehicle symmetric encryption.
- **AES-256 / RSA-2048 / ECC** are used for asymmetric needs.
- **Hashing** (SHA-256) ensures integrity, **MACs** add authenticity, **signatures** add non-repudiation.
- **Secure Boot** establishes a **chain of trust** rooted in immutable Boot ROM/OTP.
- **SecOC** uses **CMAC + freshness counter** to prevent replay attacks on CAN.
- **RBAC** is the dominant access control model in AUTOSAR.
- **V2X security** uses **PKI** with pseudonym certificates and short-lived keys for privacy.
- **Availability** of safety ECUs is typically **"four nines" (99.99%)** or higher.
- Always **link your answer to a real automotive subsystem** (braking ECU, ADAS, infotainment, TCU).
- **Modular writing** = **Definition → Standard → Example → Engineering Justification.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Potential consequences of cyber-attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT743 - VEHICLE SAFETY AND SECURITY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Potential consequences of cyber-attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In the context of automotive cybersecurity (UNECE WP.29 R155 / ISO/SAE 21434), the **potential consequences of cyber-attacks** refer to the cascading, multi-dimensional adverse impacts that result from the successful exploitation of vulnerabilities across a vehicle's electronic and networked systems. These consequences extend beyond mere data loss and manifest across four primary impact domains: **Safety, Financial, Privacy, and Operational** domains, with secondary ripples into Legal, Reputational, and National Security domains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**  
> *"Cyber-attack consequences are the tangible and intangible damages — ranging from physical injury and economic loss to data exfiltration and regulatory non-compliance — that occur when threat actors compromise the Confidentiality, Integrity, or Availability (CIA Triad) of in-vehicle networks, telematics, or back-end automotive cloud infrastructure."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Home Heist" Intuition

Imagine a modern smart home where every door, window, AC, and light bulb is connected to the internet.

- A **hacker who steals your password** is attacking **Confidentiality** (Privacy breach).
- A **hacker who changes your thermostat to a dangerous level** is attacking **Integrity** (Safety threat).
- A **hacker who locks you out of your own house remotely** is attacking **Availability** (Operational threat).

A connected car is a **"smart home on wheels moving at 100 km/h."** The same logic applies — but instead of just comfort, the attacker can influence **braking, steering, and acceleration**, making the consequences exponentially more severe.

## 1.3 Physical & Standard Metrics Used in Risk Quantification

| Metric | Standard Reference | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **ASIL (Automotive Safety Integrity Level)** | ISO 26262 | A, B, C, D — risk severity rating for automotive hazards |
| **CVSS Base Score** | FIRST.org | 0.0 – 10.0 severity scoring for vulnerabilities |
| **TARA** | ISO/SAE 21434 | Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment framework |
| **Impact Rating** | UNECE R155 Annex 5 | Low / Medium / High / Severe impact classification |
| **Attack Feasibility** | ISO/SAE 21434 | Very Low / Low / Medium / High / Very High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** Always mention the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) and the **STRIDE model** (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege) when classifying consequences — this is a guaranteed 2-mark trigger in KTU exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Attack Consequences — The 7-Domain Framework

Modern automotive cyber-attacks produce consequences that can be systematically mapped into **seven inter-linked domains**. Each domain is explained with its "Why it happens" and "How it cascades" logic.

### Domain 1: 🚗 Safety Consequences (The Most Critical)

- **Why it happens:** A successful attack on the **CAN bus, ECU, ADAS sensors, or braking system** can override driver control.
- **How it cascades:** Attacker → Compromises ADAS ECU → Injects forged sensor data → Vehicle misinterprets lane → Unintended acceleration / collision.
- **Outcome:** Loss of life, mass-casualty recalls, criminal liability for OEM.

### Domain 2: 💰 Financial Consequences

- **Why it happens:** Ransomware, theft of vehicle via relay attacks, warranty fraud, intellectual property (IP) theft of ECU source code.
- **How it cascades:** Attack → OEM production line halt → Revenue loss of **$1.5M – $22M per hour** in large OEMs.
- **Outcome:** Share price crash, recall costs (e.g., the 2015 Jeep recall cost **$1.3 Billion**).

### Domain 3: 🔐 Privacy & Data Exfiltration

- **Why it happens:** Telematics Control Units (TCUs) transmit GPS, driving patterns, biometric data, contact lists, and even in-cabin audio.
- **How it cascades:** Infotainment breach → Harvest 100 GB of user data → Sold on dark web.
- **Outcome:** GDPR fines (up to **4% of global annual turnover**), class-action lawsuits.

### Domain 4: ⚙️ Operational & Functional Disruption

- **Why it happens:** Denial-of-Service (DoS) attacks on CAN bus, immobilizer hack, OTA update corruption.
- **How it cascades:** Attacker floods CAN with error frames → Bus goes bus-off → All ECUs offline → Bricked vehicle.
- **Outcome:** Fleet grounding (e.g., entire taxi/rental fleets immobilized).

### Domain 5: 🏛️ Legal & Regulatory Consequences

- **Why it happens:** Failure to comply with **UNECE WP.29 R155** (Cyber Security Management System) and **R156** (OTA Software Updates).
- **How it cascades:** Attack → No CSMS evidence → OEM cannot sell type-approved vehicles in EU after **July 2024**.
- **Outcome:** Market withdrawal, criminal prosecution of CXOs.

### Domain 6: 📢 Reputational & Brand Damage

- **Why it happens:** Public disclosure of a vulnerability (e.g., via Twitter/X demo or academic paper).
- **How it cascades:** Viral video of "hacked Jeep" → Consumer trust collapse → Sales drop of **30–60%** in affected segment.
- **Outcome:** Long-term brand erosion, insurance premium hike.

### Domain 7: 🛡️ National Security & Critical Infrastructure

- **Why it happens:** Connected vehicles are part of the **Internet of Battlefield Things (IoBT)** and smart-city infrastructure.
- **How it cascades:** State-actor attack → Coordinated immobilization of 10,000 vehicles → Traffic gridlock / emergency response failure.
- **Outcome:** Classification as a national security incident; invocation of emergency cyber-defense protocols.

## 2.2 The Risk = f(Impact, Feasibility) Relationship

The fundamental formula used in every TARA (Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment) is:

$$R = f(I, F, E)$$

Where:
- $R$ = Risk Score (0 to 5 in ISO/SAE 21434)
- $I$ = Impact severity (0 = negligible, 4 = catastrophic / life-threatening)
- $F$ = Attack feasibility (0 = very low, 4 = very high)
- $E$ = Exposure factor (number of affected vehicles / attack surface breadth)

The **consequence level** is a direct sub-function of $I$ and $E$, while feasibility determines *whether* the consequence will actually manifest.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** When asked "What is the most severe consequence of a vehicle cyber-attack?", examiners expect the layered answer: **"Loss of human life due to compromised safety-critical ECUs, which then triggers financial recall, legal liability, and reputational collapse."** This 3-layer answer consistently scores full marks.

## 2.3 Mapping Consequences to the CIA Triad + STRIDE

| Cyber Dimension Violated | STRIDE Category | Real Vehicle Consequence | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Confidentiality | Information Disclosure | Driver GPS/biometric data exfiltrated | High |
| Integrity | Tampering / Spoofing | Forged CAN messages alter braking torque | Catastrophic |
| Availability | Denial of Service | Vehicle immobilized via bus-off attack | High |
| Non-Repudiation | Repudiation | False crash logs uploaded to OEM cloud | Medium |
| Authorization | Elevation of Privilege | Guest user gains root on IVI | High |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Consequence Cascade

## 3.1 The Attack-Consequence Chain — A Step-by-Step Logical Walkthrough

Since this topic does not involve algebraic derivations, the "derivation" is replaced by a **rigorous, step-by-step consequence propagation model** as taught in ISO/SAE 21434 §15 and UNECE R155 Annex 5.

### Step 1 — Identify the Asset
An **asset** is any in-vehicle or back-end component that stores, processes, or transmits data. Examples: ECU, gateway, TCU, OEM cloud, user smartphone app.

### Step 2 — Identify the Damage Scenario
A **damage scenario** is the description of the harm if the asset is compromised. Standard ISO/SAE 21434 damage categories are:
- **S** — Safety (injury or death of road users)
- **F** — Financial (monetary loss for user or OEM)
- **O** — Operational (vehicle functionality impaired)
- **P** — Privacy (personal data leaked)

### Step 3 — Map the Threat
Identify the threat actor and attack vector. Example: *"An unauthorized remote adversary exploits a hard-coded key in the TCU to inject malicious CAN frames."*

### Step 4 — Determine the Impact Rating ($I$)
ISO/SAE 21434 mandates a 0–4 scale:

$$
I_{rating} = 
\begin{cases}
0, & \text{Negligible (no discernible effect)} \\
1, & \text{Minor (limited data leakage)} \\
2, & \text{Moderate (loss of secondary function)} \\
3, & \text{Major (loss of primary safety function)} \\
4, & \text{Severe (loss of life / catastrophic harm)}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 5 — Determine the Attack Feasibility ($F$)
Based on the **CVSS-like automotive scoring**, the feasibility depends on:
- **Elapsed time** to perform the attack
- **Specialist expertise** required
- **Knowledge of the system** required
- **Window of opportunity**
- **Equipment required**

The combined feasibility score $F$ ranges from 0 (Very Low) to 4 (Very High).

### Step 6 — Compute the Risk Value ($R$)

$$
R = I + F
$$

The risk value is then mapped to one of five risk levels:

$$
R_{level} = 
\begin{cases}
1, & R \in [0,1] \quad \text{(Negligible)} \\
2, & R \in [2,3] \quad \text{(Low)} \\
3, & R \in [4,5] \quad \text{(Medium)} \\
4, & R \in [6,7] \quad \text{(High)} \\
5, & R \in [8,8] \quad \text{(Critical)}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 7 — Determine the Consequence Manifestation
For each risk level, the consequence propagation follows this cascade:

$$
\text{Asset Compromise} \rightarrow \text{Function Loss} \rightarrow \text{Damage Scenario} \rightarrow \text{Operational Impact} \rightarrow \text{Regulatory/Financial Outcome}
$$

### Step 8 — Risk Treatment Decision
Based on $R_{level}$:
- **Critical ($R=5$):** Immediate mitigation, possible vehicle stop-sale.
- **High ($R=4$):** Mandatory security patch within 30 days.
- **Medium ($R=3$):** Scheduled OTA update.
- **Low ($R=2$):** Monitoring and logging.
- **Negligible ($R=1$):** Document and accept.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Analytical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem Statement:**  
> An attacker exploits a buffer overflow in the Bluetooth stack of an infotainment unit to gain code execution. From there, the attacker pivots to the CAN bus and disables the Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) at highway speed. Apply the ISO/SAE 21434 consequence framework.

**Step 1 — Asset:** Infotainment ECU + ABS ECU.
**Step 2 — Damage Scenario:** **S + F + O** (Safety-critical braking failure, warranty recall, vehicle immobilization).
**Step 3 — Threat:** Remote unauthenticated adversary.
**Step 4 — Impact Rating:** $I = 4$ (Severe — loss of life probable at highway speed).
**Step 5 — Feasibility:** Medium-to-High because Bluetooth attacks are well-documented. Let $F = 3$.
**Step 6 — Risk Value:**

$$
R = I + F = 4 + 3 = 7
$$

**Step 7 — Risk Level:** $R = 7 \Rightarrow$ **High (Level 4)**.
**Step 8 — Consequence Cascade:**
1. Immediate recall of affected vehicle population.
2. UNECE R155 non-compliance investigation.
3. GDPR/PDPB privacy investigation if user data was exfiltrated.
4. Reputational stock-price impact of **5–15% within 48 hours**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** KTU expects you to **show the entire 8-step cascade** for full marks. Skipping Step 2 (Damage Scenario identification) is the most common cause of losing 3 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Consequence Cascade Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vulnerability in ECU / TCU / IVI] --> B[Exploit by Threat Actor]
    B --> C{Attack Successful?}
    C -->|Yes| D[CIA Triad Violation]
    C -->|No| E[Intrusion Detected and Blocked]
    D --> F1[Safety Impact - ASIL D]
    D --> F2[Financial Impact - Recall Cost]
    D --> F3[Privacy Impact - Data Exfiltration]
    D --> F4[Operational Impact - Vehicle Bricked]
    D --> F5[Legal Impact - UNECE R155 Non-Compliance]
    D --> F6[Reputational Impact - Brand Erosion]
    D --> F7[National Security Impact - Fleet Attack]
    F1 --> G[Consequence Manifestation in Real World]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    F6 --> G
    F7 --> G
    G --> H[Risk Treatment and Mitigation]
```

## 4.2 Attack Surface to Consequence Mapping Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AttackSurface[Attack Surface]
        AS1[Remote - OTA / Cellular]
        AS2[Short Range - Bluetooth / WiFi / RKE]
        AS3[Physical - OBD Port / USB]
        AS4[Supply Chain - Third Party ECU]
    end
    AttackSurface --> Vect[Attack Vector / TTP]
    Vect --> Target{Compromised Asset}
    Target --> T1[CAN Bus Injection]
    Target --> T2[ECU Firmware Modification]
    Target --> T3[Telematics Data Theft]
    Target --> T4[Gateway Pivoting]
    T1 --> Cons[Cyber Consequence]
    T2 --> Cons
    T3 --> Cons
    T4 --> Cons
    Cons --> Outcome[Safety, Financial, Privacy, Operational, Legal, Reputational, National Security]
```

## 4.3 Risk = Impact x Feasibility Consequence Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I0[Impact = 0 Negligible] --> R0
    I1[Impact = 1 Minor] --> R0
    I2[Impact = 2 Moderate] --> R1
    I3[Impact = 3 Major] --> R2
    I4[Impact = 4 Severe] --> R3
    F0[Feasibility = 0 Very Low] --> R0
    F1[Feasibility = 1 Low] --> R0
    F2[Feasibility = 2 Medium] --> R1
    F3[Feasibility = 3 High] --> R2
    F4[Feasibility = 4 Very High] --> R3
    R0[Risk Level 1 - 2: Negligible to Low]
    R1[Risk Level 2 - 3: Low to Medium]
    R2[Risk Level 3 - 4: High]
    R3[Risk Level 4 - 5: Critical]
    R0 --> Treat[Accept or Monitor]
    R1 --> Treat
    R2 --> Treat
    R3 --> Treat
    Treat --> Mitigation[Mandatory Patch / Stop-Sale / OTA Recall]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Q: List any three potential consequences of a cyber-attack on a modern connected vehicle.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Safety consequence** — Compromise of safety-critical ECUs (braking, steering) leading to accidents and loss of life.
2. **Financial consequence** — Costly vehicle recalls, ransomware payments, and revenue loss from production halts.
3. **Privacy consequence** — Exfiltration of personal data such as GPS history, biometric data, and contact lists via the infotainment or telematics unit.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write only *"data loss"* or *"hacking."* Examiners expect domain-specific keywords: **ASIL, ECU, telematics, recall, GDPR**. Use them.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Q: Differentiate between the impact of cyber-attacks on a passenger EV versus a commercial autonomous truck.**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | Passenger EV | Commercial Autonomous Truck |
|---|---|---|
| Safety Impact | Individual driver at risk | Mass casualty (36-wheeler crash) |
| Financial Impact | Personal warranty loss | Fleet-wide loss (~$50K–$200K per truck) |
| Operational Impact | Single vehicle | Entire logistics supply chain disrupted |
| Regulatory Impact | UNECE R155 individual | DOT/FMCSA + Homeland Security implications |
| Reputational Impact | Brand-specific | Industry-wide (logistics sector crash) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**Q: With reference to the ISO/SAE 21434 threat analysis framework:**
**(a)** Explain the seven major domains of cyber-attack consequences in modern vehicles. (7 Marks)
**(b)** A vulnerability in the Telematics Control Unit (TCU) allows an attacker to exfiltrate GPS data of 500,000 vehicles over 6 months. Using the consequence cascade model, analyze the Safety, Financial, Privacy, and Legal consequences. (7 Marks)

---

#### Part (a) Model Answer — Seven Domains of Consequences (7 Marks)

The seven major domains of consequences in modern vehicle cyber-attacks are:

**1. Safety Domain (1 Mark):** Compromise of braking, steering, or ADAS ECUs can lead to collisions, injuries, and fatalities. Severity classified per **ISO 26262 ASIL A–D**.

**2. Financial Domain (1 Mark):** Direct costs include recalls, lawsuits, and ransomware. The 2015 Jeep Cherokee recall is estimated to have cost Fiat-Chrysler over **$1.3 billion** including software patches and customer compensation.

**3. Privacy Domain (1 Mark):** TCUs and infotainment systems collect GPS, biometric, voice, and contact data. A breach violates **GDPR Article 9** (special category data) and India's **Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA) 2023**.

**4. Operational Domain (1 Mark):** DoS attacks on the CAN bus or immobilizer can brick a vehicle, leading to fleet grounding, towing costs, and rental-car shortages.

**5. Legal & Regulatory Domain (1 Mark):** Non-compliance with **UNECE WP.29 R155** (Cyber Security Management System) and **R156** (Software Update Management System) results in denial of type approval and market withdrawal from EU/India markets.

**6. Reputational Domain (1 Mark):** Public demonstrations of car hacking (e.g., Charlie Miller and Chris Valasek's 2015 Jeep demo) cause brand-level trust erosion and measurable **stock price drop of 5–15%** within 48 hours.

**7. National Security Domain (1 Mark):** Coordinated attacks on connected vehicles can disrupt smart-city traffic systems, emergency services, and military convoys — classified as **critical infrastructure** events.

---

#### Part (b) Model Answer — TCU GPS Exfiltration Case Study (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Asset Identification (1 Mark):**
The asset is the **Telematics Control Unit (TCU)** and the **OEM cloud backend** storing 500,000 vehicles' GPS data.

**Step 2 — Damage Scenario (1 Mark):**
Damage categories affected: **P (Privacy) primary, F (Financial) secondary, L (Legal) tertiary**.

**Step 3 — Consequence Cascade (4 Marks):**
- **Privacy Consequence:** GPS data over 6 months reveals home addresses, workplace locations, daily routines, and personal habits of 500,000 users. This is high-resolution behavioral profiling, sold on dark-web marketplaces.
- **Financial Consequence:** Class-action lawsuit settlements (average $5,000–$15,000 per user) imply a **theoretical liability of $2.5 Billion to $7.5 Billion**. OEM also incurs forensic investigation costs, PR firm fees, and re-engineering expenses.
- **Legal Consequence:** GDPR fines up to **4% of global annual turnover** (could exceed €1 billion for major OEMs). DPDPA 2023 imposes penalties up to **₹250 crore** per instance in India.
- **Operational Consequence:** OEM may be forced to take TCU fleet offline, disrupting connected services, e-Call, stolen-vehicle tracking, and insurance telematics.
- **Reputational Consequence:** Loss of customer trust in OEM's connected-car platform; potential churn of 20–30% subscribers from premium telematics packages.

**Step 4 — Risk Treatment (1 Mark):**
- Immediate disconnection of compromised API endpoints.
- Public disclosure within **72 hours** per GDPR Article 33.
- Mandatory OTA patch and credential rotation.
- Engagement of national CERT (e.g., **CERT-In** in India).

### Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**Q:**
**(a)** Define the CIA Triad and STRIDE model. Explain how each STRIDE category maps to a specific cyber-attack consequence in a vehicle. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Consider an attacker who exploits a weak cryptographic key in a keyless entry system to perform a **relay attack**, unlocking and stealing a luxury sedan. Compute the Risk Value using $R = I + F$, assume $I = 3$ and $F = 4$, determine the risk level, and list the cascading consequences. (7 Marks)

---

#### Part (a) Model Answer — CIA Triad & STRIDE Mapping (7 Marks)

**The CIA Triad (2 Marks):**
- **Confidentiality:** Ensuring data is accessible only to authorized entities.
- **Integrity:** Ensuring data is not altered in transit or at rest.
- **Availability:** Ensuring systems and data are accessible when needed.

**The STRIDE Model (1 Mark):**
A threat classification framework proposed by Microsoft with six categories: **S**poofing, **T**ampering, **R**epudiation, **I**nformation Disclosure, **D**enial of Service, **E**levation of Privilege.

**Mapping to Vehicle Cyber Consequences (4 Marks):**

| STRIDE | Vehicle Consequence Example | Domain Impacted |
|---|---|---|
| Spoofing | Forged CAN message with spoofed sender ID | Safety |
| Tampering | Modified ECU firmware via OBD port | Safety + Financial |
| Repudiation | Driver denies sending malicious OTA command | Legal |
| Information Disclosure | GPS/biometric data leaked from TCU | Privacy + Legal |
| Denial of Service | CAN bus flooded, vehicle immobilised | Operational |
| Elevation of Privilege | Infotainment guest user gains root shell | Safety + Operational |

---

#### Part (b) Model Answer — Relay Attack Risk Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Asset:** Keyless entry system + immobilizer ECU.
**Step 2 — Damage Scenario:** **F + S + O** — Vehicle theft, key cloning, possible unsafe vehicle operation if attacker re-uses keys.

**Step 3 — Risk Computation (3 Marks):**

$$
R = I + F
$$

$$
R = 3 + 4 = 7
$$

**Step 4 — Risk Level Mapping (1 Mark):**

Since $R = 7$, the risk falls in the **High (Level 4)** category per ISO/SAE 21434. This mandates immediate mitigation.

**Step 5 — Cascading Consequences (3 Marks):**
1. **Safety Consequence:** Stolen vehicle can be weaponized; cloned keys can bypass immobilizer, allowing ignition in unsafe conditions.
2. **Financial Consequence:** Insurance claims surge; OEM may be forced to recall and replace all vulnerable key fobs (costing **$200M–$500M** for a luxury brand).
3. **Reputational Consequence:** Consumer trust in the brand's security collapses, especially after viral media coverage.
4. **Legal Consequence:** OEM may face lawsuits for **negligent security design** under product liability law.
5. **Regulatory Consequence:** UNECE R155 audit may flag the cryptographic weakness as a non-conformity, threatening type approval.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. Do **not** describe consequences generically ("car gets hacked"). Always tie to a **specific ECU, function, and domain**.
> 2. For any 14-mark question, **always mention UNECE R155 and ISO/SAE 21434** explicitly — these are mandatory keywords.
> 3. For numerical/computational sub-parts, **show the formula**, the substitution, and the final mapped risk level. Skipping the risk-level mapping loses 1 mark.
> 4. In case-study questions, **always include the regulatory dimension** (UNECE R155, GDPR, DPDPA) — it is a hidden 2-mark discriminator.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the three pillars violated by any cyber-attack.
- **STRIDE** = Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege.
- **Seven Consequence Domains** = Safety, Financial, Privacy, Operational, Legal, Reputational, National Security.
- **Risk Formula:** $R = I + F$, where $I$ is Impact (0–4) and $F$ is Feasibility (0–4). Maximum $R = 8$ → **Critical**.
- **Damage Categories (ISO/SAE 21434):** S (Safety), F (Financial), O (Operational), P (Privacy).
- **Key Standards:** ISO/SAE 21434 (cybersecurity engineering), ISO 26262 (functional safety), UNECE R155 (CSMS), UNECE R156 (SUMS), GDPR, DPDPA 2023.
- **Real-world anchor cases to memorize:**
  - **2015 Jeep Cherokee Hack** (Miller & Valasek) → First remote vehicle cyber recall; $1.3B cost.
  - **2022 Tesla Jailbreak** → Researcher gained root access to infotainment.
  - **2020 Mercedes-Benz Keyless Theft** → Relay attack; mass vulnerability.
- **Cascade Logic:** Vulnerability → Exploit → CIA Violation → Multi-domain Consequence → Risk Treatment.
- **Most severe consequence:** Loss of human life, triggering legal, financial, and reputational collapse.
- **Regulatory trigger:** UNECE R155 compliance failure can block type approval in 50+ countries.
